+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/eGs3DOP1nhksegcQkmEj.1","tags":[]}

> Optimization is the art of pushing systems to their limits, finding the best solutions where efficiency and precision matter most. — S. Boyd and L. Vandenberghe

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/tWY0aBo5rOVaJtcVvBaU.1","tags":[]}

*Chapter 10 introduces numerical optimization methods for finding minima and maxima in one-dimensional and multidimensional problems. The chapter begins with fundamental concepts of objective functions, constraints, and optimization strategies, followed by bracketing methods, golden-section search, parabolic interpolation, and Brent’s method for one-dimensional optimization. Derivative-based techniques and line-search methods are then developed alongside multidimensional approaches including the downhill simplex method, Powell’s direction-set method, conjugate gradient methods, and quasi-Newton algorithms such as BFGS and L-BFGS. The chapter also examines linear programming through simplex and interior-point methods, stochastic global optimization using simulated annealing, and dynamic programming for structured decision problems. Throughout the chapter, mathematical theory is integrated with practical Rust implementations, providing readers with robust tools for optimization in science, engineering, machine learning, and operations research.*

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/RkCWi3XTmuHmOq9dwMs4.5","tags":[]}

# 10.1. Introduction

In mathematical optimization, the objective is to determine input values, commonly called *decision variables*, that minimize or maximize a given objective function. Let $f : \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on an $n$-dimensional Euclidean space. In a *minimization problem*, one seeks a point $x^\ast \in \mathbb{R}^n$ such that:

$$f(x^\ast) \le f(x) \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathbb{R}^n \tag{10.1.1}$$

The value $f(x^\ast)$ is called the *global minimum* of $f$. Similarly, a *maximization problem* seeks a point $x^\ast$ satisfying:

$$f(x^\ast) \ge f(x) \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathbb{R}^n \tag{10.1.2}$$

Any maximization problem can be converted into a minimization problem by considering the negative objective function $-f(x)$. For this reason, the discussion that follows focuses on minimization without loss of generality.

Two mathematical objects play a central role in optimization. The gradient,

$$\nabla f(x) = \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \ldots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right)^{\mathsf{T}} \tag{10.1.3}$$

indicates the direction of steepest increase of the function, while the Hessian matrix,

$$
\nabla^{2} f(x)
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{1}^{2}} 
& \cdots 
& \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{1}\partial x_{n}} \\[6pt]
\vdots 
& \ddots 
& \vdots \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{n}\partial x_{1}} 
& \cdots 
& \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{n}^{2}}
\end{pmatrix}
\tag{10.1.4}
$$

captures local curvature information. These quantities arise naturally in applications ranging from energy minimization in physics and engineering, to loss minimization in machine learning, to utility and profit optimization in economics.

### Local and Global Extrema

A fundamental challenge in optimization is the distinction between local and global extrema. A point $x_{\text{local}}$ is a *local minimum* if there exists a neighborhood $U$ of $x_{\text{local}}$ such that:

$$f(x_{\text{local}}) \le f(x) \quad \text{for all } x \in U \tag{10.1.5}$$

There may nevertheless exist a point $x^\ast$ elsewhere in the domain such that $f(x^\ast) < f(x_{\text{local}})$, in which case $x_{\text{local}}$ is not a global minimum. The same distinction applies to maxima.

For smooth functions, local extrema occur at critical points, defined by:

$$\nabla f(x) = 0 \tag{10.1.6}$$

However, not every critical point is a minimum. Depending on the eigenvalues of the Hessian matrix, a critical point may be a minimum, a maximum, or a saddle point. Saddle points are particularly important in high-dimensional problems, where they are abundant and can significantly impede convergence of naive optimization algorithms.

If the objective function $f$ is convex, any local minimum is also a global minimum, greatly simplifying optimization. Many practical objective functions, however, are non-convex and exhibit multiple local minima and maxima, making global optimization intrinsically difficult.

### Rust Implementation

Following the conceptual introduction to mathematical optimization in Section 10.1, Program 10.1.0 provides a concrete computational realization of the fundamental objects that underpin continuous optimization theory. In particular, it illustrates how an objective function $f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, its gradient $\nabla f(x)$ as defined in (10.1.3), and its Hessian matrix $\nabla^2 f(x)$ as defined in (10.1.4) can be represented and evaluated in practice. The program focuses on two-dimensional examples in order to make the geometric and analytical distinctions between convex and non-convex objectives explicit. By combining analytic expressions with finite-difference approximations, the implementation demonstrates how critical points defined by (10.1.6) arise in computation and how local curvature information determines whether such points correspond to minima, maxima, or saddle points. The example serves as a computational bridge between the abstract definitions of global and local extrema in (10.1.1) and (10.1.5) and the algorithms that will be developed in subsequent sections.

At the core of the implementation is a trait-based abstraction for two-dimensional objective functions. The `Objective2D` trait defines a common interface through which an objective function $f(x)$ can be evaluated at a given point $x \in \mathbb{R}^2$. Optional methods provide analytic expressions for the gradient $\nabla f(x)$ and Hessian $\nabla^2 f(x)$ when closed-form formulas are available. This design allows the same numerical infrastructure to be applied uniformly to different classes of objective functions, while preserving the mathematical structure introduced in (10.1.3) and (10.1.4).

To complement analytic derivatives, the program implements central finite-difference approximations for both the gradient and the Hessian. These routines numerically approximate the partial derivatives appearing in (10.1.3) and (10.1.4) using symmetric difference quotients. By comparing analytic and numerical derivatives at selected points, the program illustrates how differential information can be recovered in practice when explicit formulas are unavailable, and how discretization error manifests itself in finite-precision arithmetic.

Two representative objective functions are included to highlight the distinction between convex and non-convex optimization problems. The first is a convex quadratic function, whose Hessian matrix is constant and positive definite. In this case, the critical point obtained by solving $\nabla f(x) = 0$ corresponds to the unique global minimum, consistent with the theoretical guarantee that local and global minima coincide for convex functions. The second example is a non-convex Himmelblau-type function, which exhibits multiple local minima and regions of negative curvature. This example demonstrates how multiple critical points can satisfy (10.1.6) while differing fundamentally in their local optimality properties.

The program further includes routines for analyzing the Hessian matrix at a critical point by computing its eigenvalues. The signs of these eigenvalues are used to classify critical points as local minima, local maxima, or saddle points, directly reflecting the curvature-based interpretation discussed after (10.1.6). This classification highlights the practical importance of second-order information, particularly in non-convex problems where saddle points are prevalent and can impede naive optimization methods.

Finally, a coarse grid search is employed on bounded domains to illustrate the difference between local and global behavior. By sampling the objective function on a rectangular region, the program identifies approximate global minima on that region and contrasts them with analytically determined critical points. While such brute-force approaches are not scalable to high dimensions, they provide a clear and intuitive demonstration of the concepts of global and local extrema introduced at the beginning of the section.

```rust
// Program 10.1.0
//
// Mathematical Optimization: Objective, Gradient, Hessian, and Local vs Global Extrema
//
// This program demonstrates the core objects introduced in Section 10.1:
//
//   - Objective function f : R^n -> R.
//   - Gradient ∇f(x) as in (10.1.3).
//   - Hessian ∇²f(x) as in (10.1.4).
//   - Critical points defined by ∇f(x) = 0 as in (10.1.6).
//   - The distinction between local and global minima as discussed around (10.1.1) and (10.1.5).
//
// The implementation focuses on two-dimensional examples so that the Hessian is a 2×2 matrix,
// which allows an explicit eigenvalue-based classification of critical points.
//
// The program includes:
//   1) A convex quadratic objective (unique global minimum).
//   2) A non-convex Himmelblau-type objective (multiple local minima and saddle structure).
//
// It compares analytic gradients and Hessians to finite-difference approximations,
// then performs a coarse grid search on a bounded box to illustrate "global minimum on a region".

use std::f64;

type Vec2 = [f64; 2];
type Mat2 = [[f64; 2]; 2];

fn vec2_add(a: Vec2, b: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
    [a[0] + b[0], a[1] + b[1]]
}
fn vec2_sub(a: Vec2, b: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
    [a[0] - b[0], a[1] - b[1]]
}
fn vec2_scale(a: Vec2, s: f64) -> Vec2 {
    [a[0] * s, a[1] * s]
}
fn vec2_norm2(a: Vec2) -> f64 {
    (a[0] * a[0] + a[1] * a[1]).sqrt()
}

#[allow(dead_code)]
fn mat2_trace(a: Mat2) -> f64 {
    a[0][0] + a[1][1]
}
fn mat2_det(a: Mat2) -> f64 {
    a[0][0] * a[1][1] - a[0][1] * a[1][0]
}

/// Eigenvalues of a real symmetric 2×2 matrix:
/// A = [[a, b],[b, d]].
/// Returns (λ_min, λ_max).
fn symm2_eigenvalues(a: Mat2) -> (f64, f64) {
    let aa = a[0][0];
    let bb = 0.5 * (a[0][1] + a[1][0]); // enforce symmetry numerically
    let dd = a[1][1];

    let tr = aa + dd;
    let disc = (aa - dd) * (aa - dd) + 4.0 * bb * bb;
    let root = disc.sqrt();

    let l1 = 0.5 * (tr - root);
    let l2 = 0.5 * (tr + root);
    (l1.min(l2), l1.max(l2))
}

/// Classification of a critical point using the Hessian eigenvalues.
/// For smooth functions, at a point with ∇f(x)=0:
/// - both eigenvalues > 0: local minimum
/// - both eigenvalues < 0: local maximum
/// - mixed signs: saddle point
/// - near-zero eigenvalues: degenerate/flat directions
fn classify_critical_point(h: Mat2, tol: f64) -> &'static str {
    let (lmin, lmax) = symm2_eigenvalues(h);

    if lmin > tol && lmax > tol {
        "local minimum (positive definite Hessian)"
    } else if lmin < -tol && lmax < -tol {
        "local maximum (negative definite Hessian)"
    } else if lmin < -tol && lmax > tol {
        "saddle point (indefinite Hessian)"
    } else {
        "degenerate or nearly flat (eigenvalues near zero)"
    }
}

/// A two-dimensional objective with optional analytic gradient and Hessian.
trait Objective2D {
    fn name(&self) -> &'static str;
    fn f(&self, x: Vec2) -> f64;

    /// Analytic gradient if available.
    fn grad_analytic(&self, _x: Vec2) -> Option<Vec2> {
        None
    }

    /// Analytic Hessian if available.
    fn hess_analytic(&self, _x: Vec2) -> Option<Mat2> {
        None
    }
}

/// Central-difference gradient approximation to ∇f(x).
fn grad_fd<F>(f: F, x: Vec2, h: f64) -> Vec2
where
    F: Fn(Vec2) -> f64,
{
    let e0 = [1.0, 0.0];
    let e1 = [0.0, 1.0];

    let xph0 = vec2_add(x, vec2_scale(e0, h));
    let xmh0 = vec2_sub(x, vec2_scale(e0, h));
    let xph1 = vec2_add(x, vec2_scale(e1, h));
    let xmh1 = vec2_sub(x, vec2_scale(e1, h));

    let d0 = (f(xph0) - f(xmh0)) / (2.0 * h);
    let d1 = (f(xph1) - f(xmh1)) / (2.0 * h);
    [d0, d1]
}

/// Central-difference Hessian approximation to ∇²f(x).
/// Uses second differences for diagonal entries and mixed differences for off-diagonal.
fn hess_fd<F>(f: F, x: Vec2, h: f64) -> Mat2
where
    F: Fn(Vec2) -> f64,
{
    let e0 = [1.0, 0.0];
    let e1 = [0.0, 1.0];

    let f0 = f(x);

    // Diagonal second derivatives
    let xph0 = vec2_add(x, vec2_scale(e0, h));
    let xmh0 = vec2_sub(x, vec2_scale(e0, h));
    let d00 = (f(xph0) - 2.0 * f0 + f(xmh0)) / (h * h);

    let xph1 = vec2_add(x, vec2_scale(e1, h));
    let xmh1 = vec2_sub(x, vec2_scale(e1, h));
    let d11 = (f(xph1) - 2.0 * f0 + f(xmh1)) / (h * h);

    // Mixed second derivative
    let xpp = vec2_add(vec2_add(x, vec2_scale(e0, h)), vec2_scale(e1, h));
    let xpm = vec2_add(vec2_sub(x, vec2_scale(e0, h)), vec2_scale(e1, h));
    let xmp = vec2_sub(vec2_add(x, vec2_scale(e0, h)), vec2_scale(e1, h));
    let xmm = vec2_sub(vec2_sub(x, vec2_scale(e0, h)), vec2_scale(e1, h));

    let d01 = (f(xpp) - f(xpm) - f(xmp) + f(xmm)) / (4.0 * h * h);

    [[d00, d01], [d01, d11]]
}

/// Convex quadratic: f(x) = 1/2 x^T A x - b^T x, with symmetric positive definite A.
/// This objective has a unique global minimizer x* = A^{-1} b.
struct Quadratic {
    a: Mat2,
    b: Vec2,
}

impl Quadratic {
    fn new(a: Mat2, b: Vec2) -> Self {
        Self { a, b }
    }

    fn grad(&self, x: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
        // ∇f(x) = A x - b
        let ax0 = self.a[0][0] * x[0] + self.a[0][1] * x[1];
        let ax1 = self.a[1][0] * x[0] + self.a[1][1] * x[1];
        [ax0 - self.b[0], ax1 - self.b[1]]
    }

    fn hess(&self) -> Mat2 {
        // ∇²f(x) = A
        self.a
    }

    fn solve_minimizer(&self) -> Option<Vec2> {
        // Solve A x = b for 2×2.
        let det = mat2_det(self.a);
        if det.abs() < 1e-14 {
            return None;
        }
        let inv = [
            [self.a[1][1] / det, -self.a[0][1] / det],
            [-self.a[1][0] / det, self.a[0][0] / det],
        ];
        let x0 = inv[0][0] * self.b[0] + inv[0][1] * self.b[1];
        let x1 = inv[1][0] * self.b[0] + inv[1][1] * self.b[1];
        Some([x0, x1])
    }
}

impl Objective2D for Quadratic {
    fn name(&self) -> &'static str {
        "Convex quadratic"
    }

    fn f(&self, x: Vec2) -> f64 {
        // f(x) = 1/2 x^T A x - b^T x
        let ax0 = self.a[0][0] * x[0] + self.a[0][1] * x[1];
        let ax1 = self.a[1][0] * x[0] + self.a[1][1] * x[1];
        let xtax = x[0] * ax0 + x[1] * ax1;
        let btx = self.b[0] * x[0] + self.b[1] * x[1];
        0.5 * xtax - btx
    }

    fn grad_analytic(&self, x: Vec2) -> Option<Vec2> {
        Some(self.grad(x))
    }

    fn hess_analytic(&self, _x: Vec2) -> Option<Mat2> {
        Some(self.hess())
    }
}

/// Non-convex Himmelblau-type function:
/// f(x,y) = (x^2 + y - 11)^2 + (x + y^2 - 7)^2.
/// This objective is smooth but non-convex and has multiple local minima.
struct Himmelblau;

impl Himmelblau {
    fn grad(&self, x: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
        let (xx, yy) = (x[0], x[1]);
        let u = xx * xx + yy - 11.0;
        let v = xx + yy * yy - 7.0;

        // df/dx = 2u*(2x) + 2v*(1)
        // df/dy = 2u*(1) + 2v*(2y)
        let dfdx = 4.0 * xx * u + 2.0 * v;
        let dfdy = 2.0 * u + 4.0 * yy * v;
        [dfdx, dfdy]
    }

    fn hess(&self, x: Vec2) -> Mat2 {
        let (xx, yy) = (x[0], x[1]);
        let u = xx * xx + yy - 11.0;
        let v = xx + yy * yy - 7.0;

        // From:
        // dfdx = 4x*u + 2v
        // dfdy = 2u + 4y*v
        //
        // d2f/dx2 = 4u + 8x^2 + 2*(dv/dx) where dv/dx = 1
        //         = 4u + 8x^2 + 2
        // d2f/dy2 = 2*(du/dy) + 4v + 8y^2 where du/dy = 1
        //         = 2 + 4v + 8y^2
        // d2f/dxdy = d/dy(4x*u + 2v) = 4x*(du/dy) + 2*(dv/dy), dv/dy = 2y
        //         = 4x + 4y
        let dxx = 4.0 * u + 8.0 * xx * xx + 2.0;
        let dyy = 2.0 + 4.0 * v + 8.0 * yy * yy;
        let dxy = 4.0 * xx + 4.0 * yy;

        [[dxx, dxy], [dxy, dyy]]
    }
}

impl Objective2D for Himmelblau {
    fn name(&self) -> &'static str {
        "Non-convex Himmelblau-type"
    }

    fn f(&self, x: Vec2) -> f64 {
        let (xx, yy) = (x[0], x[1]);
        let u = xx * xx + yy - 11.0;
        let v = xx + yy * yy - 7.0;
        u * u + v * v
    }

    fn grad_analytic(&self, x: Vec2) -> Option<Vec2> {
        Some(self.grad(x))
    }

    fn hess_analytic(&self, x: Vec2) -> Option<Mat2> {
        Some(self.hess(x))
    }
}

/// Coarse grid search on a box [xmin,xmax]×[ymin,ymax].
/// This illustrates the idea of "global minimum on a region" by brute-force sampling.
fn grid_search_min<O: Objective2D>(
    obj: &O,
    xmin: f64,
    xmax: f64,
    ymin: f64,
    ymax: f64,
    n: usize,
) -> (Vec2, f64) {
    let mut best_x = [0.0, 0.0];
    let mut best_f = f64::INFINITY;

    for i in 0..=n {
        let tx = i as f64 / n as f64;
        let x = xmin + tx * (xmax - xmin);
        for j in 0..=n {
            let ty = j as f64 / n as f64;
            let y = ymin + ty * (ymax - ymin);
            let fx = obj.f([x, y]);
            if fx < best_f {
                best_f = fx;
                best_x = [x, y];
            }
        }
    }
    (best_x, best_f)
}

fn print_vec2(label: &str, v: Vec2) {
    println!("{label} [{:+.6e}, {:+.6e}]", v[0], v[1]);
}

fn print_mat2(label: &str, a: Mat2) {
    println!(
        "{label}\n  [[{:+.6e}, {:+.6e}],\n   [{:+.6e}, {:+.6e}]]",
        a[0][0], a[0][1], a[1][0], a[1][1]
    );
}

fn compare_derivatives<O: Objective2D>(obj: &O, x: Vec2) {
    println!("\n=== {} ===", obj.name());
    println!("x = [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x[0], x[1]);
    println!("f(x) = {:+.12e}", obj.f(x));

    let h = 1e-6;
    let g_fd = grad_fd(|z| obj.f(z), x, h);
    let h_fd = hess_fd(|z| obj.f(z), x, h);

    print_vec2("grad (finite-diff) =", g_fd);
    print_mat2("hess (finite-diff) =", h_fd);

    if let Some(g) = obj.grad_analytic(x) {
        print_vec2("grad (analytic)   =", g);
        let err = vec2_norm2(vec2_sub(g, g_fd));
        println!("||grad_analytic - grad_fd||_2 = {:.6e}", err);
    }

    if let Some(hess) = obj.hess_analytic(x) {
        print_mat2("hess (analytic)   =", hess);
        let (lmin, lmax) = symm2_eigenvalues(hess);
        println!("Hessian eigenvalues: λ_min = {:+.6e}, λ_max = {:+.6e}", lmin, lmax);
    }
}

fn main() {
    // Example 1: Convex quadratic with SPD A.
    // Choose A symmetric positive definite and b nonzero.
    let quad = Quadratic::new([[3.0, 1.0], [1.0, 2.0]], [1.0, 1.0]);

    // Example 2: Non-convex objective.
    let him = Himmelblau;

    // Compare derivatives at representative points.
    compare_derivatives(&quad, [0.0, 0.0]);
    compare_derivatives(&quad, [1.0, -1.0]);

    compare_derivatives(&him, [0.0, 0.0]);
    compare_derivatives(&him, [3.0, 2.0]);

    // For the convex quadratic, compute the analytic minimizer x* = A^{-1} b.
    println!("\n=== Quadratic: critical point and classification ===");
    if let Some(x_star) = quad.solve_minimizer() {
        let g = quad.grad(x_star);
        let hess = quad.hess();
        println!("x* (solution of ∇f(x)=0) = [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x_star[0], x_star[1]);
        print_vec2("∇f(x*) =", g);
        print_mat2("∇²f(x*) =", hess);
        let cls = classify_critical_point(hess, 1e-10);
        println!("classification: {cls}");
        println!("f(x*) = {:+.12e}", quad.f(x_star));
    } else {
        println!("Could not solve for x* because A appears singular.");
    }

    // Illustrate "global minimum on a region" by coarse grid search.
    println!("\n=== Coarse grid search: convex quadratic ===");
    let (xg, fg) = grid_search_min(&quad, -4.0, 4.0, -4.0, 4.0, 600);
    println!("region: [-4,4]×[-4,4], samples: (601×601)");
    println!("best point found: [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", xg[0], xg[1]);
    println!("best value found: {:+.12e}", fg);

    println!("\n=== Coarse grid search: non-convex objective ===");
    let (xg2, fg2) = grid_search_min(&him, -6.0, 6.0, -6.0, 6.0, 900);
    println!("region: [-6,6]×[-6,6], samples: (901×901)");
    println!("best point found: [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", xg2[0], xg2[1]);
    println!("best value found: {:+.12e}", fg2);

    // Classification at a point that is (approximately) critical for the non-convex objective.
    // We use the grid-search best point as a candidate and check ||∇f||.
    println!("\n=== Non-convex candidate critical point check ===");
    let g2 = him.grad(xg2);
    let g2_norm = vec2_norm2(g2);
    println!("candidate x = [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", xg2[0], xg2[1]);
    print_vec2("∇f(x) =", g2);
    println!("||∇f(x)||_2 = {:.6e}", g2_norm);
    let h2 = him.hess(xg2);
    print_mat2("∇²f(x) =", h2);
    let cls2 = classify_critical_point(h2, 1e-6);
    println!("classification (via Hessian): {cls2}");

    println!("\nDone.");
}
```

Program 10.1.0 demonstrates how the abstract definitions of objective functions, gradients, Hessians, and extrema introduced in Section 10.1 translate into concrete numerical procedures. By combining analytic formulas with finite-difference approximations, the program shows how critical points defined by (10.1.6) can be detected and classified in practice using curvature information from the Hessian matrix.

The contrast between the convex quadratic example and the non-convex Himmelblau-type function highlights a central theme of optimization theory. In convex settings, local analysis is sufficient to identify global optima, whereas in non-convex problems, multiple local minima and saddle points complicate the search for globally optimal solutions. These examples reinforce the theoretical discussion surrounding (10.1.1) and (10.1.5) and motivate the need for more sophisticated algorithms.

The modular structure of the code provides a foundation for subsequent sections of this chapter. Gradient-based methods, Newton and quasi-Newton schemes, and global optimization strategies can all be built upon the same representations of objective functions and derivative information introduced here. As such, Program 10.1.0 serves not only as an illustration of foundational concepts but also as a starting point for the algorithmic developments that follow.

## 10.1.1. Global Optimization Strategies

When an objective function possesses multiple local extrema, standard local optimization algorithms may converge to different solutions depending on the starting point. One common strategy for addressing this issue is to run local optimization methods from many different initial guesses and select the best solution obtained.

More systematic approaches involve global optimization methods, which aim to explore the domain more broadly and reduce sensitivity to initial conditions. Such methods are particularly important in complex, multimodal landscapes encountered in engineering design, parameter estimation, and molecular structure optimization (Sanchez Alvarez and Calaminici, 2025).

### Stochastic Global Optimization Methods

Stochastic global optimization methods have demonstrated significant success in modern practice. These methods introduce randomness into the search process, allowing occasional uphill moves that enable the algorithm to escape local minima that would trap deterministic methods.

A prominent example is *simulated annealing*, a random-search technique inspired by physical annealing processes in metallurgy. By permitting uphill moves with a probability that decreases over time, simulated annealing balances global exploration with local refinement. Such stochastic strategies have proven effective on difficult optimization problems with numerous local extrema, including high-dimensional design problems and molecular structure prediction (Sanchez Alvarez and Calaminici, 2025).

Other stochastic approaches include *genetic algorithms* and related evolutionary strategies, which perform global searches by mimicking mechanisms of natural selection. Hybrid methods that combine stochastic global exploration with deterministic local optimization have emerged as particularly effective. For example, recent work integrates genetic algorithms with the Nelder–Mead local search to achieve a balance between broad exploration and fine-grained refinement, yielding robust results on benchmark problems and parameter estimation tasks (Majhi and Mishra, 2025).

Because stochastic methods introduce randomness and differ fundamentally from deterministic descent algorithms, they are revisited later in this chapter in a dedicated section.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.1.1 on the limitations of local optimization in multimodal landscapes, Program 10.1.1 provides a practical implementation of several global optimization strategies designed to reduce sensitivity to initial conditions. In nonlinear optimization problems with multiple local extrema, deterministic local methods may converge to different solutions depending on their starting points, potentially missing better solutions elsewhere in the domain. This program illustrates how global exploration can be incorporated through stochastic and population-based techniques, and how these approaches can be combined with local refinement to improve robustness. By applying multiple strategies to a non-convex objective function with known multiple minima, the example concretely demonstrates the principles of global search, stochastic exploration, and hybrid optimization outlined in this section.

At the core of the implementation is a simple trait-based abstraction for two-dimensional objective functions. The `Objective2D` trait defines a uniform interface through which an objective function $f(x)$ can be evaluated at any point $x \in \mathbb{R}^2$. This abstraction allows the same optimization infrastructure to be reused across different algorithms without coupling the search logic to a specific functional form. The program employs a non-convex Himmelblau-type objective as a representative example of a multimodal landscape, consistent with the discussion of multiple local minima following Equation (10.1.5).

The first global strategy implemented is a multi-start local search approach. In this method, a deterministic local optimizer is run repeatedly from a collection of randomly generated initial guesses, and the best solution obtained across all runs is retained. The local optimizer used here is the Nelder–Mead simplex method, which does not require gradient information and is well suited to low-dimensional problems. This approach directly addresses the dependence on initialization inherent in local methods, while remaining conceptually simple and easy to implement.

The second strategy is simulated annealing, a stochastic global optimization method inspired by physical annealing processes. Candidate moves are generated randomly within the feasible domain, and moves that increase the objective function value are accepted with a probability that depends on a temperature parameter. This acceptance rule allows the algorithm to escape local minima early in the search, while gradually focusing on local refinement as the temperature decreases. The cooling schedule and proposal scale control the balance between exploration and exploitation, reflecting the trade-off described in the stochastic optimization discussion of Section 10.1.1.

The program also includes a compact hybrid evolutionary strategy that combines population-based global exploration with periodic local refinement. A population of candidate solutions is evolved using tournament selection, blend crossover, and mutation. At regular intervals, a small number of elite candidates are refined using the deterministic Nelder–Mead method. This hybrid structure illustrates how stochastic global methods and local optimization can be integrated into a single framework, as suggested by recent hybrid approaches discussed in the literature. The resulting algorithm leverages the exploratory power of evolutionary search while retaining the accuracy of local refinement near promising solutions.

The `main` function orchestrates and compares these three strategies. Each method is applied to the same objective function over a bounded search region, and their best solutions are reported and compared. By examining both the final objective values and the distances between the resulting solutions, the program highlights how different global strategies may converge to different local or global minima, even when applied to the same problem under identical constraints.

```rust
// Program 10.1.1
//
// Global Optimization Strategies: Multi-start Local Search, Simulated Annealing,
// and a Simple Hybrid Evolutionary plus Local Refinement Scheme
//
// This program supports the discussion in Section 10.1.1 by demonstrating three
// practical strategies for objective functions with multiple local extrema:
//
//  1) Multi-start local optimization: run a local method from many initial guesses
//     and keep the best result.
//  2) Simulated annealing: a stochastic global method that permits uphill moves
//     with probability controlled by a temperature schedule.
//  3) A lightweight hybrid: a population-based evolutionary search that periodically
//     refines elite candidates using a deterministic local method.
//
// The examples use a non-convex, multimodal objective in two variables so that
// the influence of initial conditions and the advantage of broader exploration
// are clear in a short, reproducible run.
//
// Build notes:
// - This is a single-file program with no external crates.
// - Randomness is produced by a small deterministic PRNG (LCG) for portability.

use std::f64;

type Vec2 = [f64; 2];

fn vec2_add(a: Vec2, b: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
    [a[0] + b[0], a[1] + b[1]]
}
fn vec2_sub(a: Vec2, b: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
    [a[0] - b[0], a[1] - b[1]]
}
fn vec2_scale(a: Vec2, s: f64) -> Vec2 {
    [a[0] * s, a[1] * s]
}
fn vec2_centroid(a: Vec2, b: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
    [(a[0] + b[0]) * 0.5, (a[1] + b[1]) * 0.5]
}
fn clamp(x: f64, lo: f64, hi: f64) -> f64 {
    if x < lo {
        lo
    } else if x > hi {
        hi
    } else {
        x
    }
}
fn clamp2(x: Vec2, lo: Vec2, hi: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
    [clamp(x[0], lo[0], hi[0]), clamp(x[1], lo[1], hi[1])]
}
fn l2_norm(a: Vec2) -> f64 {
    (a[0] * a[0] + a[1] * a[1]).sqrt()
}

/// Minimal deterministic PRNG for portability (64-bit LCG).
/// This is not cryptographically secure and is used only for stochastic optimization.
struct Rng64 {
    state: u64,
}
impl Rng64 {
    fn new(seed: u64) -> Self {
        Self { state: seed }
    }
    fn next_u64(&mut self) -> u64 {
        // LCG parameters used commonly in practice; sufficient for demonstration.
        self.state = self
            .state
            .wrapping_mul(6364136223846793005u64)
            .wrapping_add(1442695040888963407u64);
        self.state
    }
    fn next_f64(&mut self) -> f64 {
        // Convert to [0,1). Use top 53 bits to fill mantissa.
        let u = self.next_u64() >> 11;
        (u as f64) * (1.0 / ((1u64 << 53) as f64))
    }
    fn uniform(&mut self, lo: f64, hi: f64) -> f64 {
        lo + (hi - lo) * self.next_f64()
    }
    fn uniform2(&mut self, lo: Vec2, hi: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
        [self.uniform(lo[0], hi[0]), self.uniform(lo[1], hi[1])]
    }
}

/// Objective function interface for 2D optimization.
trait Objective2D {
    fn name(&self) -> &'static str;
    fn f(&self, x: Vec2) -> f64;
}

/// Himmelblau-type objective:
/// f(x,y) = (x^2 + y - 11)^2 + (x + y^2 - 7)^2.
/// This function has multiple local minima and is commonly used to illustrate multimodality.
struct Himmelblau;
impl Objective2D for Himmelblau {
    fn name(&self) -> &'static str {
        "Himmelblau-type"
    }
    fn f(&self, x: Vec2) -> f64 {
        let (xx, yy) = (x[0], x[1]);
        let u = xx * xx + yy - 11.0;
        let v = xx + yy * yy - 7.0;
        u * u + v * v
    }
}

/// Nelder–Mead local optimizer in 2D.
/// This method is deterministic and often effective for small dimensions, but can
/// converge to different local minima depending on the initial guess.
fn nelder_mead_2d<O: Objective2D>(
    obj: &O,
    x0: Vec2,
    lo: Vec2,
    hi: Vec2,
    initial_step: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
    tol_f: f64,
) -> (Vec2, f64) {
    // Simplex: three points in 2D.
    let mut p0 = clamp2(x0, lo, hi);
    let mut p1 = clamp2([x0[0] + initial_step, x0[1]], lo, hi);
    let mut p2 = clamp2([x0[0], x0[1] + initial_step], lo, hi);

    let mut f0 = obj.f(p0);
    let mut f1 = obj.f(p1);
    let mut f2 = obj.f(p2);

    // Standard coefficients.
    let alpha = 1.0; // reflection
    let gamma = 2.0; // expansion
    let rho = 0.5;   // contraction
    let sigma = 0.5; // shrink

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        // Order by function value: p0 best, p2 worst (after sorting).
        // Manual sorting for three points to keep code compact.
        if f1 < f0 {
            std::mem::swap(&mut p0, &mut p1);
            std::mem::swap(&mut f0, &mut f1);
        }
        if f2 < f0 {
            std::mem::swap(&mut p0, &mut p2);
            std::mem::swap(&mut f0, &mut f2);
        }
        if f2 < f1 {
            std::mem::swap(&mut p1, &mut p2);
            std::mem::swap(&mut f1, &mut f2);
        }

        // Convergence check using function spread in the simplex.
        let f_spread = (f2 - f0).abs();
        if f_spread < tol_f {
            break;
        }

        // Centroid of best two points.
        let c = vec2_centroid(p0, p1);

        // Reflection.
        let pr = clamp2(vec2_add(c, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(c, p2), alpha)), lo, hi);
        let fr = obj.f(pr);

        if fr < f0 {
            // Expansion.
            let pe = clamp2(vec2_add(c, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(pr, c), gamma)), lo, hi);
            let fe = obj.f(pe);
            if fe < fr {
                p2 = pe;
                f2 = fe;
            } else {
                p2 = pr;
                f2 = fr;
            }
        } else if fr < f1 {
            // Accept reflection.
            p2 = pr;
            f2 = fr;
        } else {
            // Contraction.
            let pc = if fr < f2 {
                // Outside contraction.
                clamp2(vec2_add(c, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(pr, c), rho)), lo, hi)
            } else {
                // Inside contraction.
                clamp2(vec2_add(c, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(p2, c), -rho)), lo, hi)
            };
            let fc = obj.f(pc);

            if fc < f2 {
                p2 = pc;
                f2 = fc;
            } else {
                // Shrink toward best point.
                p1 = clamp2(vec2_add(p0, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(p1, p0), sigma)), lo, hi);
                p2 = clamp2(vec2_add(p0, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(p2, p0), sigma)), lo, hi);
                f1 = obj.f(p1);
                f2 = obj.f(p2);
            }
        }
    }

    // Return best point.
    if f1 < f0 {
        std::mem::swap(&mut p0, &mut p1);
        std::mem::swap(&mut f0, &mut f1);
    }
    if f2 < f0 {
        std::mem::swap(&mut p0, &mut p2);
        std::mem::swap(&mut f0, &mut f2);
    }
    (p0, f0)
}

/// Multi-start strategy: run a local method from many random initial guesses
/// and select the best solution obtained.
fn multistart_local<O: Objective2D>(
    obj: &O,
    rng: &mut Rng64,
    lo: Vec2,
    hi: Vec2,
    starts: usize,
    nm_step: f64,
    nm_max_iter: usize,
    nm_tol_f: f64,
) -> (Vec2, f64) {
    let mut best_x = rng.uniform2(lo, hi);
    let mut best_f = obj.f(best_x);

    for _ in 0..starts {
        let x0 = rng.uniform2(lo, hi);
        let (x, fx) = nelder_mead_2d(obj, x0, lo, hi, nm_step, nm_max_iter, nm_tol_f);
        if fx < best_f {
            best_f = fx;
            best_x = x;
        }
    }
    (best_x, best_f)
}

/// Simulated annealing in a bounded box.
/// A candidate move is accepted if it improves f, or if it worsens f with probability
/// exp(-(Δf)/T), where T decreases over time.
fn simulated_annealing<O: Objective2D>(
    obj: &O,
    rng: &mut Rng64,
    lo: Vec2,
    hi: Vec2,
    x_init: Vec2,
    t0: f64,
    cooling: f64,
    steps_per_temp: usize,
    temps: usize,
    proposal_scale0: f64,
) -> (Vec2, f64) {
    let mut x = clamp2(x_init, lo, hi);
    let mut fx = obj.f(x);

    let mut best_x = x;
    let mut best_f = fx;

    let mut t = t0;

    for k in 0..temps {
        // Reduce proposal scale slowly with temperature.
        let proposal_scale = proposal_scale0 * (t / t0).max(1e-6);

        for _ in 0..steps_per_temp {
            // Propose a random step in each coordinate.
            let dx = rng.uniform(-proposal_scale, proposal_scale);
            let dy = rng.uniform(-proposal_scale, proposal_scale);
            let x_new = clamp2([x[0] + dx, x[1] + dy], lo, hi);
            let f_new = obj.f(x_new);

            let df = f_new - fx;

            let accept = if df <= 0.0 {
                true
            } else {
                // Metropolis criterion.
                let p = (-df / t).exp();
                rng.next_f64() < p
            };

            if accept {
                x = x_new;
                fx = f_new;

                if fx < best_f {
                    best_f = fx;
                    best_x = x;
                }
            }
        }

        // Cooling schedule T_{k+1} = cooling * T_k.
        t *= cooling;

        // A safety stop if temperature becomes numerically negligible.
        if k > 10 && t < 1e-12 {
            break;
        }
    }

    (best_x, best_f)
}

/// A compact hybrid method:
/// - Maintain a population sampled in the search box.
/// - Select parents by tournament selection.
/// - Create offspring by blend crossover plus mutation.
/// - Periodically refine elites using Nelder–Mead local optimization.
fn hybrid_ea_with_local_refine<O: Objective2D>(
    obj: &O,
    rng: &mut Rng64,
    lo: Vec2,
    hi: Vec2,
    pop_size: usize,
    generations: usize,
    tournament_k: usize,
    crossover_alpha: f64,
    mutation_sigma: f64,
    elite_refine: usize,
    refine_every: usize,
) -> (Vec2, f64) {
    #[derive(Clone)]
    struct Individual {
        x: Vec2,
        f: f64,
    }

    let mut pop: Vec<Individual> = (0..pop_size)
        .map(|_| {
            let x = rng.uniform2(lo, hi);
            let f = obj.f(x);
            Individual { x, f }
        })
        .collect();

    let mut best = pop[0].clone();
    for ind in &pop {
        if ind.f < best.f {
            best = ind.clone();
        }
    }

    let tournament = |rng: &mut Rng64, pop: &[Individual], k: usize| -> Individual {
        let mut best = pop[(rng.next_f64() * pop.len() as f64) as usize].clone();
        for _ in 1..k {
            let cand = pop[(rng.next_f64() * pop.len() as f64) as usize].clone();
            if cand.f < best.f {
                best = cand;
            }
        }
        best
    };

    for gen in 0..generations {
        // Sort population so elites are easy to access.
        pop.sort_by(|a, b| a.f.partial_cmp(&b.f).unwrap());

        if pop[0].f < best.f {
            best = pop[0].clone();
        }

        // Periodic elite refinement with a deterministic local optimizer.
        if refine_every > 0 && (gen % refine_every == 0) {
            for i in 0..elite_refine.min(pop.len()) {
                let x0 = pop[i].x;
                let (x_ref, f_ref) = nelder_mead_2d(obj, x0, lo, hi, 0.5, 400, 1e-12);
                pop[i].x = x_ref;
                pop[i].f = f_ref;
            }
        }

        // Create next generation with elitism: keep top few unchanged.
        let elite_keep = (pop_size / 10).max(1);
        let mut next_pop: Vec<Individual> = pop[..elite_keep].to_vec();

        while next_pop.len() < pop_size {
            let p1 = tournament(rng, &pop, tournament_k);
            let p2 = tournament(rng, &pop, tournament_k);

            // Blend crossover in each coordinate:
            // child = (1 - a)*p1 + a*p2, with a sampled from [-alpha, 1+alpha].
            let a0 = rng.uniform(-crossover_alpha, 1.0 + crossover_alpha);
            let a1 = rng.uniform(-crossover_alpha, 1.0 + crossover_alpha);

            let mut child = [
                (1.0 - a0) * p1.x[0] + a0 * p2.x[0],
                (1.0 - a1) * p1.x[1] + a1 * p2.x[1],
            ];

            // Mutation: add bounded noise.
            child[0] += rng.uniform(-mutation_sigma, mutation_sigma);
            child[1] += rng.uniform(-mutation_sigma, mutation_sigma);

            child = clamp2(child, lo, hi);
            let f = obj.f(child);
            next_pop.push(Individual { x: child, f });
        }

        pop = next_pop;
    }

    // Final best.
    pop.sort_by(|a, b| a.f.partial_cmp(&b.f).unwrap());
    if pop[0].f < best.f {
        best = pop[0].clone();
    }
    (best.x, best.f)
}

fn main() {
    // Objective and search region.
    let obj = Himmelblau;
    let lo: Vec2 = [-6.0, -6.0];
    let hi: Vec2 = [6.0, 6.0];

    // Deterministic seed for reproducible results.
    let mut rng = Rng64::new(20260201u64);

    println!("Objective: {}", obj.name());
    println!("Search box: x in [{:.1}, {:.1}], y in [{:.1}, {:.1}]", lo[0], hi[0], lo[1], hi[1]);
    println!();

    // 1) Multi-start local optimization (Nelder–Mead).
    let starts = 40;
    let (x_ms, f_ms) = multistart_local(&obj, &mut rng, lo, hi, starts, 0.8, 800, 1e-12);
    println!("Multi-start local search");
    println!("  starts: {}", starts);
    println!("  best x: [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x_ms[0], x_ms[1]);
    println!("  best f: {:+.12e}", f_ms);
    println!();

    // 2) Simulated annealing.
    // Start from a random point to emphasize reduced sensitivity to initialization.
    let x0_sa = rng.uniform2(lo, hi);
    let (x_sa, f_sa) = simulated_annealing(
        &obj,
        &mut rng,
        lo,
        hi,
        x0_sa,
        5.0,      // t0
        0.90,     // cooling
        1500,     // steps per temperature
        50,       // number of temperatures
        1.5,      // initial proposal scale
    );
    println!("Simulated annealing");
    println!("  init x: [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x0_sa[0], x0_sa[1]);
    println!("  best x: [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x_sa[0], x_sa[1]);
    println!("  best f: {:+.12e}", f_sa);
    println!();

    // 3) Hybrid evolutionary search with periodic local refinement of elites.
    let (x_h, f_h) = hybrid_ea_with_local_refine(
        &obj,
        &mut rng,
        lo,
        hi,
        60,    // population size
        80,    // generations
        3,     // tournament size
        0.25,  // crossover alpha for blend
        0.30,  // mutation sigma
        4,     // elites to refine
        10,    // refine every N generations
    );
    println!("Hybrid evolutionary search with local refinement");
    println!("  best x: [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x_h[0], x_h[1]);
    println!("  best f: {:+.12e}", f_h);
    println!();

    // Compare the three results directly.
    // The smallest value among them is the final reported candidate for the best solution found.
    let mut best_x = x_ms;
    let mut best_f = f_ms;
    let mut best_label = "multi-start";

    if f_sa < best_f {
        best_f = f_sa;
        best_x = x_sa;
        best_label = "simulated annealing";
    }
    if f_h < best_f {
        best_f = f_h;
        best_x = x_h;
        best_label = "hybrid evolutionary";
    }

    println!("Best solution found (across strategies): {}", best_label);
    println!("  x*: [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", best_x[0], best_x[1]);
    println!("  f(x*): {:+.12e}", best_f);

    // A small diagnostic: verify that the reported points are feasible.
    let feasible = best_x[0] >= lo[0] && best_x[0] <= hi[0] && best_x[1] >= lo[1] && best_x[1] <= hi[1];
    println!("  feasible in box: {}", feasible);

    // Optional: report distance between the three outcomes to highlight sensitivity to initialization.
    let d_ms_sa = l2_norm(vec2_sub(x_ms, x_sa));
    let d_ms_h = l2_norm(vec2_sub(x_ms, x_h));
    let d_sa_h = l2_norm(vec2_sub(x_sa, x_h));
    println!();
    println!("Outcome separation (Euclidean distance):");
    println!("  ||x_ms - x_sa|| = {:.6e}", d_ms_sa);
    println!("  ||x_ms - x_h || = {:.6e}", d_ms_h);
    println!("  ||x_sa - x_h || = {:.6e}", d_sa_h);
}
```

Program 10.1.1 demonstrates how global optimization strategies can mitigate the limitations of purely local search methods in problems with multiple local extrema. The multi-start approach shows how repeated local optimization can improve robustness through redundancy, while simulated annealing illustrates the role of controlled randomness in escaping local traps. The hybrid evolutionary method further demonstrates how global exploration and local refinement can be combined to achieve reliable performance across complex landscapes.

The results obtained for the multimodal test function emphasize an important conceptual point: global optimization does not necessarily produce a unique solution when multiple global minima exist. Instead, different strategies may converge to different basins of attraction that are equivalent in objective value. This behavior reinforces the distinction between local and global extrema introduced earlier in Section 10.1 and motivates the careful interpretation of optimization outcomes in practical applications.

The modular structure of the implementation provides a foundation for more advanced global optimization techniques developed later in this chapter. Extensions may include adaptive cooling schedules, more sophisticated evolutionary operators, or the systematic coupling of stochastic global methods with gradient-based local solvers. As such, Program 10.1.1 serves as a concrete starting point for understanding and experimenting with global optimization strategies in finite-precision numerical environments.

## 10.1.2. Constrained and Unconstrained Optimization

Optimization problems are commonly classified as either constrained or unconstrained, depending on whether the decision variables are required to satisfy explicit restrictions. In constrained optimization, the objective is to minimize or maximize a function $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to one or more conditions of the form:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad h_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0,$$

which represent equality and inequality constraints, respectively. These constraints restrict the feasible set to a subset of the ambient space, often introducing geometric structure such as manifolds, polyhedra, or convex regions. The presence of constraints fundamentally alters the nature of optimality, since extrema may occur on the boundary of the feasible region rather than at stationary points of the objective function.

A particularly important subclass of constrained problems is linear programming, in which both the objective function and all constraints are linear. Linear programs arise in resource allocation, scheduling, network flows, and economic modeling, and they benefit from a rich theoretical foundation. Specialized algorithms such as the simplex method and modern interior-point methods exploit the convex polyhedral structure of the feasible region to achieve high efficiency and reliability. More general constrained problems, including nonlinear programming, require additional theoretical tools such as Lagrange multipliers, Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions, and penalty or barrier formulations to characterize and compute optimal solutions.

In contrast, unconstrained optimization considers problems in which the variables are free to vary over an open domain, typically a subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$, without explicit restrictions. In this setting, optimality is characterized by properties of the objective function itself, most notably the vanishing of its gradient at local extrema and the behavior of its higher-order derivatives. Unconstrained problems arise naturally when constraints are absent, when they have been eliminated by reformulation, or when they are incorporated implicitly through regularization terms added to the objective function.

This chapter focuses primarily on unconstrained optimization for continuous and differentiable functions. Despite the apparent simplicity of this setting, unconstrained problems exhibit a wide range of numerical behaviors, including ill-conditioning, nonconvexity, multiple local minima, flat regions, and saddle points. As a result, no single algorithm is universally effective. Different methods are required depending on whether derivative information is available, whether the objective function is convex or highly nonlinear, and whether robustness or rapid local convergence is the primary concern.

In many practical situations, however, explicit derivative information is unavailable, unreliable, or prohibitively expensive to compute. Objective functions may arise from complex simulations, experimental measurements, black-box models, or legacy codes for which gradients and Hessians cannot be obtained accurately. Even when derivatives exist in theory, numerical noise, discontinuities, or stochastic effects may render gradient-based methods ineffective. These considerations motivate the study of *derivative-free optimization methods*, which rely solely on function evaluations to guide the search for extrema. The following sections introduce such methods and examine how they complement classical derivative-based techniques within the broader framework of unconstrained optimization.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.1.2 on the conceptual and practical differences between constrained and unconstrained optimization, Program 10.1.2 provides a concrete numerical illustration of how constraints fundamentally alter the structure of an optimization problem. While unconstrained optimization seeks stationary points of the objective function in the ambient space, constrained problems restrict admissible solutions to a feasible set defined by equality and inequality conditions, often forcing optimal solutions to lie on lower-dimensional manifolds or boundary regions. This program demonstrates how constrained problems can be reformulated as unconstrained ones through penalty and barrier techniques, allowing derivative-free optimization methods to be applied even when explicit constraint handling is unavailable. By comparing unconstrained minimization with penalty-based and barrier-based reformulations, the example highlights how feasibility, optimality, and numerical behavior interact in practical constrained optimization.

At the core of the implementation is a simple two-dimensional objective function together with one equality constraint and one inequality constraint. The base objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ is minimized directly in the unconstrained case, providing a reference solution that illustrates how unconstrained optima may violate feasibility conditions. The equality constraint $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ and the inequality constraint $h(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ are defined as separate functions, reflecting the standard formulation of constrained optimization problems introduced at the beginning of Section 10.1.2.

The unconstrained optimization stage applies a derivative-free Nelder–Mead simplex method to the base objective function alone. Because no restrictions are imposed, the algorithm converges to the stationary point of $f$, regardless of whether that point satisfies the constraints. This behavior illustrates the fundamental limitation of unconstrained methods when applied directly to constrained problems, as discussed in the context surrounding the constraint definitions.

To incorporate constraints into the optimization process, the program next implements a quadratic penalty reformulation. In this approach, the constrained problem is replaced by an unconstrained objective consisting of the original function augmented by penalty terms proportional to the squared constraint violations. Equality constraints are penalized symmetrically through squared residuals, while inequality constraints are penalized only when violated. By progressively increasing the penalty parameter, the algorithm is forced to trade off objective minimization against constraint satisfaction, driving the solution toward the feasible set. The staged penalty loop in the code demonstrates how feasibility improves gradually as the penalty strength increases, while also illustrating the numerical limitations of finite penalty parameters.

The program then introduces a log barrier formulation for the inequality constraint, combined with a quadratic penalty for the equality constraint. The barrier term prevents the optimizer from leaving the interior of the feasible region by driving the objective to infinity as the inequality constraint boundary is approached. Unlike penalty methods, barrier methods enforce strict feasibility throughout the optimization process, requiring an initial interior point and gradually relaxing the barrier parameter to approach the constraint boundary. This implementation illustrates how constrained optima on the boundary of the feasible set can be approximated from within, consistent with classical interior-point ideas.

All optimization stages rely on the same derivative-free Nelder–Mead algorithm, emphasizing that penalty and barrier techniques can convert constrained problems into forms amenable to unconstrained solvers. This design choice reinforces the motivation for derivative-free methods discussed at the end of Section 10.1.2, particularly in settings where gradient and Hessian information is unavailable, unreliable, or prohibitively expensive to compute.

```rust
// Program 10.1.2
//
// Constrained and Unconstrained Optimization: Penalty and Barrier Reformulations,
// with Derivative-Free Search by Nelder–Mead
//
// This program supports Section 10.1.2 by contrasting unconstrained optimization
// with constrained optimization, and by showing how constraints can be incorporated
// into an unconstrained objective using penalty and barrier ideas.
//
// We consider a simple 2D problem with a nonlinear inequality constraint and a linear
// equality constraint:
//
//   Minimize      f(x) = (x1 - 1)^2 + (x2 - 2)^2
//   Subject to    h(x) = x1 + x2 - 2 <= 0          (inequality constraint)
//                  g(x) = x1 - x2 = 0             (equality constraint)
//
// The unconstrained minimizer of f is (1,2), but it violates both constraints.
// Under the constraints, the solution lies on the feasible set boundary and/or
// manifold, illustrating that constrained optima do not generally occur at stationary
// points of f in the ambient space.
//
// The program demonstrates three approaches:
//
//  1) Unconstrained baseline: directly minimize f.
//  2) Quadratic penalty method: minimize f(x) + ρ * g(x)^2 + ρ * max(0,h(x))^2,
//     increasing ρ to enforce feasibility progressively.
//  3) Log barrier method (inequality only): minimize f(x) - μ * log(-h(x)) on the
//     interior h(x) < 0, decreasing μ to approach the boundary.
//
// All minimizations are performed using a derivative-free Nelder–Mead implementation,
// illustrating why derivative-free methods are useful when gradients are unavailable
// or unreliable.
//
// Notes:
// - No external crates are required.
// - The barrier method requires a strictly feasible starting point (h(x) < 0).
// - Equality constraints are handled with a quadratic penalty (typical in practice).

use std::f64;

type Vec2 = [f64; 2];
#[allow(dead_code)]
type Mat2 = [[f64; 2]; 2];

fn vec2_add(a: Vec2, b: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
    [a[0] + b[0], a[1] + b[1]]
}
fn vec2_sub(a: Vec2, b: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
    [a[0] - b[0], a[1] - b[1]]
}
fn vec2_scale(a: Vec2, s: f64) -> Vec2 {
    [a[0] * s, a[1] * s]
}
fn vec2_centroid(a: Vec2, b: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
    [(a[0] + b[0]) * 0.5, (a[1] + b[1]) * 0.5]
}
fn clamp(x: f64, lo: f64, hi: f64) -> f64 {
    if x < lo {
        lo
    } else if x > hi {
        hi
    } else {
        x
    }
}
fn clamp2(x: Vec2, lo: Vec2, hi: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
    [clamp(x[0], lo[0], hi[0]), clamp(x[1], lo[1], hi[1])]
}
#[allow(dead_code)]
fn norm2(x: Vec2) -> f64 {
    (x[0] * x[0] + x[1] * x[1]).sqrt()
}

fn max0(x: f64) -> f64 {
    if x > 0.0 { x } else { 0.0 }
}

/// A small deterministic PRNG (64-bit LCG) to pick starting points reproducibly.
struct Rng64 {
    state: u64,
}
impl Rng64 {
    fn new(seed: u64) -> Self {
        Self { state: seed }
    }
    fn next_u64(&mut self) -> u64 {
        self.state = self
            .state
            .wrapping_mul(6364136223846793005u64)
            .wrapping_add(1442695040888963407u64);
        self.state
    }
    fn next_f64(&mut self) -> f64 {
        let u = self.next_u64() >> 11;
        (u as f64) * (1.0 / ((1u64 << 53) as f64))
    }
    fn uniform(&mut self, lo: f64, hi: f64) -> f64 {
        lo + (hi - lo) * self.next_f64()
    }
    fn uniform2(&mut self, lo: Vec2, hi: Vec2) -> Vec2 {
        [self.uniform(lo[0], hi[0]), self.uniform(lo[1], hi[1])]
    }
}
#[allow(dead_code)]
/// Two-dimensional objective function interface.
trait Objective2D {
    fn name(&self) -> &'static str;
    fn f(&self, x: Vec2) -> f64;
}

/// Nelder–Mead local optimizer in 2D, constrained to a bounding box.
/// This is used as a derivative-free method, relying only on function evaluations.
fn nelder_mead_2d<F>(
    f: F,
    x0: Vec2,
    lo: Vec2,
    hi: Vec2,
    initial_step: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
    tol_f: f64,
) -> (Vec2, f64)
where
    F: Fn(Vec2) -> f64,
{
    // Initialize simplex (3 points in 2D).
    let mut p0 = clamp2(x0, lo, hi);
    let mut p1 = clamp2([x0[0] + initial_step, x0[1]], lo, hi);
    let mut p2 = clamp2([x0[0], x0[1] + initial_step], lo, hi);

    let mut f0 = f(p0);
    let mut f1 = f(p1);
    let mut f2 = f(p2);

    // Standard coefficients.
    let alpha = 1.0; // reflection
    let gamma = 2.0; // expansion
    let rho = 0.5;   // contraction
    let sigma = 0.5; // shrink

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        // Sort (p0 best, p2 worst).
        if f1 < f0 {
            std::mem::swap(&mut p0, &mut p1);
            std::mem::swap(&mut f0, &mut f1);
        }
        if f2 < f0 {
            std::mem::swap(&mut p0, &mut p2);
            std::mem::swap(&mut f0, &mut f2);
        }
        if f2 < f1 {
            std::mem::swap(&mut p1, &mut p2);
            std::mem::swap(&mut f1, &mut f2);
        }

        let spread = (f2 - f0).abs();
        if spread < tol_f {
            break;
        }

        let c = vec2_centroid(p0, p1);

        // Reflect worst point through centroid.
        let pr = clamp2(vec2_add(c, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(c, p2), alpha)), lo, hi);
        let fr = f(pr);

        if fr < f0 {
            // Expand.
            let pe = clamp2(vec2_add(c, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(pr, c), gamma)), lo, hi);
            let fe = f(pe);
            if fe < fr {
                p2 = pe;
                f2 = fe;
            } else {
                p2 = pr;
                f2 = fr;
            }
        } else if fr < f1 {
            // Accept reflection.
            p2 = pr;
            f2 = fr;
        } else {
            // Contract.
            let pc = if fr < f2 {
                // Outside contraction.
                clamp2(vec2_add(c, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(pr, c), rho)), lo, hi)
            } else {
                // Inside contraction.
                clamp2(vec2_add(c, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(p2, c), -rho)), lo, hi)
            };
            let fc = f(pc);

            if fc < f2 {
                p2 = pc;
                f2 = fc;
            } else {
                // Shrink toward best.
                p1 = clamp2(vec2_add(p0, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(p1, p0), sigma)), lo, hi);
                p2 = clamp2(vec2_add(p0, vec2_scale(vec2_sub(p2, p0), sigma)), lo, hi);
                f1 = f(p1);
                f2 = f(p2);
            }
        }
    }

    // Return best.
    if f1 < f0 {
        std::mem::swap(&mut p0, &mut p1);
        std::mem::swap(&mut f0, &mut f1);
    }
    if f2 < f0 {
        std::mem::swap(&mut p0, &mut p2);
        std::mem::swap(&mut f0, &mut f2);
    }
    (p0, f0)
}

// ---------- Problem definition (objective and constraints) ----------

/// Base objective f(x) to be minimized.
fn base_objective(x: Vec2) -> f64 {
    // f(x) = (x1 - 1)^2 + (x2 - 2)^2
    let dx1 = x[0] - 1.0;
    let dx2 = x[1] - 2.0;
    dx1 * dx1 + dx2 * dx2
}

/// Equality constraint g(x) = 0.
fn g_eq(x: Vec2) -> f64 {
    // g(x) = x1 - x2
    x[0] - x[1]
}

/// Inequality constraint h(x) <= 0.
fn h_ineq(x: Vec2) -> f64 {
    // h(x) = x1 + x2 - 2 <= 0
    x[0] + x[1] - 2.0
}

/// Quadratic penalty reformulation for both equality and inequality constraints:
///   F_ρ(x) = f(x) + ρ * g(x)^2 + ρ * max(0,h(x))^2
fn penalty_objective(x: Vec2, rho: f64) -> f64 {
    let f = base_objective(x);
    let g = g_eq(x);
    let h = h_ineq(x);

    f + rho * g * g + rho * max0(h) * max0(h)
}

/// Log barrier for inequality plus quadratic penalty for equality:
///   B_{μ,ρ}(x) = f(x) - μ log(-h(x)) + ρ g(x)^2, defined only when h(x) < 0.
fn barrier_objective(x: Vec2, mu: f64, rho_eq: f64) -> f64 {
    let h = h_ineq(x);
    if h >= 0.0 {
        // Outside the feasible interior, return a huge value to discourage steps.
        return 1e30 + 1e6 * h * h;
    }
    let f = base_objective(x);
    let g = g_eq(x);
    f - mu * (-h).ln() + rho_eq * g * g
}

/// A convenience printer for feasibility diagnostics.
fn print_feasibility(label: &str, x: Vec2) {
    let f = base_objective(x);
    let g = g_eq(x);
    let h = h_ineq(x);
    println!("{label}");
    println!("  x = [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x[0], x[1]);
    println!("  f(x) = {:+.12e}", f);
    println!("  g(x) = {:+.12e}  (equality)", g);
    println!("  h(x) = {:+.12e}  (inequality; feasible if <= 0)", h);
    println!("  feasible (ineq)? {}", h <= 0.0);
    println!();
}

fn main() {
    // Bounding box used to keep the derivative-free simplex steps well-behaved.
    // This is not the constraint set; it is merely a computational safeguard.
    let lo: Vec2 = [-6.0, -6.0];
    let hi: Vec2 = [6.0, 6.0];

    println!("Program 10.1.2: Constrained vs Unconstrained Optimization");
    println!("Base objective: f(x) = (x1-1)^2 + (x2-2)^2");
    println!("Constraints: g(x)=x1-x2=0,  h(x)=x1+x2-2<=0");
    println!();

    // A reproducible initial point.
    let mut rng = Rng64::new(20260201u64);
    let x0 = rng.uniform2(lo, hi);

    // ---- 1) Unconstrained baseline ----
    let (x_u, fu) = nelder_mead_2d(|x| base_objective(x), x0, lo, hi, 0.8, 1200, 1e-12);
    println!("Unconstrained minimization (derivative-free Nelder–Mead)");
    println!("  start x0 = [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x0[0], x0[1]);
    println!("  x* = [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x_u[0], x_u[1]);
    println!("  f(x*) = {:+.12e}", fu);
    println!();
    print_feasibility("Feasibility at unconstrained solution:", x_u);

    // ---- 2) Quadratic penalty method ----
    // Increase rho progressively to enforce constraints.
    println!("Quadratic penalty method (increasing ρ)");
    let mut x_p = x_u;
    let mut rho = 1.0;

    for stage in 0..6 {
        let rho_stage = rho;
        let (x_stage, f_stage) = nelder_mead_2d(
            |x| penalty_objective(x, rho_stage),
            x_p,
            lo,
            hi,
            0.7,
            1400,
            1e-12,
        );

        println!("  stage {}: ρ = {:.3e}", stage + 1, rho_stage);
        println!("    x = [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x_stage[0], x_stage[1]);
        println!("    penalized objective = {:+.12e}", f_stage);
        println!("    g(x) = {:+.12e}", g_eq(x_stage));
        println!("    h(x) = {:+.12e}", h_ineq(x_stage));
        println!("    feasible (ineq)? {}", h_ineq(x_stage) <= 0.0);
        println!();

        x_p = x_stage;
        rho *= 10.0;
    }

    print_feasibility("Feasibility at final penalty-stage solution:", x_p);

    // ---- 3) Log barrier method (inequality) + penalty (equality) ----
    // Requires an interior starting point: h(x) < 0.
    // We construct one by projecting toward the interior if needed.
    let mut x_b = x0;
    if h_ineq(x_b) >= -0.1 {
        // Push toward interior along the direction (-1,-1).
        x_b = clamp2([x_b[0] - 2.0, x_b[1] - 2.0], lo, hi);
    }
    // Ensure interior. If still not interior, use a known strictly feasible point.
    if h_ineq(x_b) >= -0.1 {
        x_b = [-1.0, -1.0]; // satisfies h = -4 < 0
    }

    println!("Log barrier method (decreasing μ) with equality penalty");
    println!("  interior start x0 = [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x_b[0], x_b[1]);
    println!("  h(x0) = {:+.12e} (must be < 0 for barrier)", h_ineq(x_b));
    println!();

    let mut mu = 1.0;
    let rho_eq = 1e3; // keep equality reasonably enforced throughout
    for stage in 0..6 {
        let mu_stage = mu;
        let (x_stage, f_stage) = nelder_mead_2d(
            |x| barrier_objective(x, mu_stage, rho_eq),
            x_b,
            lo,
            hi,
            0.5,
            1600,
            1e-12,
        );

        println!("  stage {}: μ = {:.3e}", stage + 1, mu_stage);
        println!("    x = [{:+.6}, {:+.6}]", x_stage[0], x_stage[1]);
        println!("    barrier objective = {:+.12e}", f_stage);
        println!("    g(x) = {:+.12e}", g_eq(x_stage));
        println!("    h(x) = {:+.12e}", h_ineq(x_stage));
        println!("    feasible (ineq)? {}", h_ineq(x_stage) <= 0.0);
        println!();

        x_b = x_stage;
        mu *= 0.2; // decrease barrier parameter
    }

    print_feasibility("Feasibility at final barrier-stage solution:", x_b);

    // Summary: compare three solutions by base objective and feasibility.
    println!("Summary (base objective values and feasibility):");
    println!(
        "  unconstrained: f={:+.6e}, g={:+.2e}, h={:+.2e}",
        base_objective(x_u),
        g_eq(x_u),
        h_ineq(x_u)
    );
    println!(
        "  penalty-final: f={:+.6e}, g={:+.2e}, h={:+.2e}",
        base_objective(x_p),
        g_eq(x_p),
        h_ineq(x_p)
    );
    println!(
        "  barrier-final: f={:+.6e}, g={:+.2e}, h={:+.2e}",
        base_objective(x_b),
        g_eq(x_b),
        h_ineq(x_b)
    );

    println!();
    println!("Done.");
}
```

Program 10.1.2 demonstrates how constrained optimization problems can be addressed using unconstrained numerical techniques through penalty and barrier reformulations. By comparing the unconstrained solution with those obtained from penalty and barrier methods, the program illustrates how feasibility constraints reshape the optimization landscape and alter the location and nature of optimal solutions.

The quadratic penalty approach highlights the gradual enforcement of constraints and the trade-off between accuracy and numerical conditioning as penalty parameters increase. In contrast, the log barrier method illustrates strict interior feasibility and controlled convergence toward constraint boundaries. Together, these approaches reflect two classical and complementary strategies for handling constraints in numerical optimization.

The example reinforces the theoretical discussion in Section 10.1.2 by showing that constrained optima need not satisfy the stationarity conditions of the original objective function in the ambient space. Instead, optimality emerges from an interaction between objective minimization and constraint enforcement. The modular structure of the implementation provides a foundation for later sections on Lagrange multipliers, Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions, and interior-point methods, and illustrates how constrained optimization fits naturally within the broader framework of unconstrained numerical algorithms.

## 10.1.3. Derivative-Free Methods

Unconstrained optimization algorithms may be classified according to whether they use derivative information. Derivative-free methods rely solely on evaluations of the objective function $f(x)$. These methods are indispensable when the function is not differentiable, when derivatives are prohibitively expensive to compute, or when the objective function is obtained from a black-box simulation or physical experiment.

In one dimension, standard derivative-free techniques include golden-section search and Brent’s method, the latter combining golden-section search with parabolic interpolation to improve convergence while retaining robustness. In multiple dimensions, widely used derivative-free methods include the downhill simplex method (Nelder–Mead), which explores the search space using a simplex of points. Nelder–Mead is robust and often effective for difficult functions, but it can be slow and does not guarantee convergence in theory. Nevertheless, it continues to be widely used and enhanced within hybrid optimization frameworks (Majhi and Mishra, 2025).

Another derivative-free approach is Powell’s direction-set method, which successively minimizes the objective function along a set of directions, typically employing a one-dimensional minimization routine such as Brent’s method. Powell’s method is often faster than Nelder–Mead on smooth problems but may be less robust on irregular landscapes.

## 10.1.4. Derivative-Based Methods

When gradient information is available, derivative-based methods generally converge more rapidly than derivative-free alternatives. In one dimension, knowledge of $f'(x)$ allows efficient root-finding and derivative-based line-search techniques.

In multiple dimensions, access to the gradient $\nabla f(x)$ enables methods such as steepest descent, conjugate gradient (CG) methods, and quasi-Newton methods. Conjugate gradient methods are particularly attractive for large-scale problems because they require only $O(n)$ memory and often converge in far fewer iterations than simple gradient descent.

Quasi-Newton methods, including the Davidon–Fletcher–Powell and BFGS algorithms, construct approximations to the Hessian matrix to guide the search. These methods require $O(n^2)$ storage but are often highly efficient in practice. Limited-memory variants such as L-BFGS reduce storage requirements and are widely used in large-scale applications, including machine learning.

Even when analytical gradients are unavailable, quasi-Newton methods can be applied using finite-difference approximations. Experience reported in recent studies indicates that, provided the problem dimension is not excessively large, such approaches often outperform purely derivative-free methods in overall efficiency (Vieira and Lisboa, 2024).

## 10.1.5. Practical Perspective

No single optimization algorithm is best for all problems. Performance depends strongly on the properties of the objective function, including smoothness, dimensionality, noise, and multimodality. A practical strategy is therefore to experiment with several methods. For one-dimensional problems, bracketing followed by golden-section or Brent’s method is often effective. For multidimensional problems without derivatives, Nelder–Mead or Powell’s method provides a reasonable starting point. When gradients are available, conjugate gradient methods are well suited to large problems, while quasi-Newton methods such as BFGS often achieve rapid convergence for problems of moderate size.

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/WTSzOVGaW7w3Rkw6qZmd.6","tags":[]}

# 10.2. Initially Bracketing a Minimum

Before applying any one-dimensional line-search or minimization algorithm, it is prudent to first bracket the location of a minimum. Bracketing a minimum means identifying three points $a$, $b$, and $c$, ordered such that:

$$a < b < c \quad \text{or} \quad a > b > c,$$

with the property:

$$f(b) < f(a) \quad \text{and} \quad f(b) < f(c) \tag{10.2.1}$$

In other words, the function value at the middle point (b) is lower than at its two neighbors, indicating that a local minimum lies somewhere in the open interval ((a,c)).

This concept is analogous to *bracketing a root* of an equation, where one seeks an interval in which the function changes sign. For minimization, however, the criterion is that the function decreases and then increases, forming a “valley.” Once such a triplet $(a,b,c)$ has been found, subsequent minimization algorithms can safely focus on narrowing the interval $(a,c)$ to isolate the minimum.

One reason for bracketing a minimum is *algorithmic safety.* If the approximate location of the minimum is unknown, algorithms that proceed without an initial bracket may converge to an unintended local minimum or fail altogether if they step outside a reasonable region of the domain. Ensuring that the interval $(a,c)$ contains a downhill–uphill transition provides confidence that a minimum exists within that interval.

Some modern optimization routines accept a single initial guess and internally perform a bracketing step before refining the solution. Nevertheless, performing this step explicitly is advantageous, as it gives the user direct control over the search region and avoids unintended excursions. Once a valid bracket has been established, all subsequent computations are confined to that interval.

## 10.2.1. Basic Bracketing Strategy

Once the objective of bracketing has been established, the remaining question is how to construct such a triplet efficiently when only function evaluations are available. In general, there is no universally optimal procedure for selecting initial bracketing points, since the local behavior of the objective function is unknown a priori. Nevertheless, simple exploratory heuristics often perform well, especially in one-dimensional settings where function evaluations are relatively inexpensive.

Assume that an initial guess $x_0$ is available, together with a small positive step size $\delta > 0$ that sets the initial scale of exploration. The first action is to evaluate the function at a nearby point displaced from the initial guess,

$$f(x_0 + \delta) \tag{10.2.2}$$

This evaluation serves as a probe that reveals whether the function decreases or increases in the positive direction, thereby providing coarse directional information without requiring derivative data.

Two distinct situations may occur, corresponding to whether this initial probe moves in a favorable direction.

### Uphill or flat step

If,

$$f(x_0 + \delta) \ge f(x_0), \tag{10.2.3}$$

then the function does not decrease when moving to the right of $x_0$. In this case, the natural response is to explore the opposite direction by evaluating:

$$f(x_0 - \delta) \tag{10.2.4}$$

A decrease in this direction indicates that the function slopes downward on one side of $x_0$ and upward on the other, providing immediate evidence that a minimum is enclosed nearby. The triplet,

$$a = x_0 + \delta, \quad b = x_0, \quad c = x_0 - \delta \tag{10.2.5}$$

then forms a valid bracket, with the middle point lower than both endpoints.

If neither direction yields a decrease, the outcome is ambiguous rather than a failure. The step size $\delta$ may be poorly chosen relative to the local curvature of the function, or the initial guess may already be close to a stationary point or a flat region. In practice, this situation is often handled by reducing $\delta$, testing additional nearby points, or accepting $x_0$ as a provisional minimizer subject to further verification.

### Downhill step

If instead

$$f(x_0 + \delta) < f(x_0), \tag{10.2.6}$$

then the function decreases in the positive direction, indicating that the minimizer lies further along that direction. In this case, the initial step has correctly identified the downhill slope of the function, but the minimum has not yet been bracketed. The appropriate strategy is therefore to continue moving in the same direction, typically by increasing the step length progressively, until a point is found where the function value increases again. The first such increase signals that the search has crossed the bottom of the valley, at which point a valid bracketing triplet can be assembled.

This asymmetric treatment of uphill and downhill steps reflects an important practical principle: bracketing is not about locating the minimum precisely, but about identifying a region in which the qualitative behavior of the function guarantees its existence. The procedure deliberately sacrifices precision for robustness, relying on minimal assumptions and a small number of function evaluations. As such, it forms a reliable foundation for subsequent one-dimensional minimization algorithms that refine the bracket and converge rapidly to the minimizer.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.2 on the role of initial bracketing in one-dimensional minimization, Program 10.2.1 provides a concrete implementation of a basic bracketing strategy based solely on function evaluations. In numerical optimization, reliable convergence of line-search and interval-reduction methods depends critically on first identifying an interval that is guaranteed to contain a local minimum. This program operationalizes the theoretical condition expressed in Equation (10.2.1) by probing the objective function near an initial guess and adaptively expanding the search region until a valid downhill–uphill transition is detected. The implementation emphasizes robustness rather than precision, illustrating how a small number of strategically chosen function evaluations can establish a safe search interval even when no derivative information is available.

At the core of the implementation is the function `bracket_minimum`, which encapsulates the logic described in Section 10.2.1 for constructing a bracketing triplet $(a,b,c)$ satisfying the condition of Equation (10.2.1). The function accepts an objective function supplied as a callable object, an initial guess $x_0$, and a small positive step size $\delta$ that sets the initial exploration scale. The procedure begins by evaluating the function at (x_0) and at the displaced point $x_0 + \delta$, corresponding to the probing step described in Equation (10.2.2). This single evaluation provides directional information about the local behavior of the objective function without requiring derivative data.

If the initial probe satisfies the inequality in Equation (10.2.3), indicating that the function does not decrease when moving to the right of $x_0$, the algorithm immediately evaluates the symmetric point $x_0 - \delta$ as in Equation (10.2.4). When both neighboring values exceed $f(x_0)$, the algorithm concludes that the initial guess already lies between two higher points and directly forms the symmetric bracket defined in Equation (10.2.5). This case corresponds to an immediate and inexpensive bracketing outcome, often encountered when the initial guess lies near a local minimizer.

If, on the other hand, the initial probe satisfies the inequality in Equation (10.2.6), the algorithm identifies a downhill direction but recognizes that the minimum has not yet been enclosed. In this case, the responsibility is delegated to the helper function `expand_until_uphill`, which repeatedly advances in the downhill direction while geometrically increasing the step size. This expansion phase continues until the function value increases again, signaling that the bottom of the valley has been crossed. The first such increase produces three consecutive points with the middle point lower than both neighbors, ensuring that the bracketing condition of Equation (10.2.1) is satisfied.

The `expand_until_uphill` function embodies an important practical principle emphasized in the text: bracketing is concerned with qualitative behavior rather than precise localization. By allowing the step length to grow multiplicatively, the algorithm quickly escapes shallow regions and avoids excessive evaluations in flat or slowly varying parts of the objective function.
