At the same time, explicit checks for finite values and a maximum iteration count guard against pathological cases and undefined function behavior.

The `ordered_bracket` utility function ensures that the returned triplet is consistently ordered in the sense required by the definition of a bracket, either $a<b<c$ or $a>b>c$, while preserving the middle point as the lowest function value. This separation of concerns clarifies the algorithmic structure and makes the correctness of the bracketing condition transparent.

The `main` function demonstrates the algorithm on both a simple convex quadratic function and a mildly nonconvex objective. In each case, the program prints the resulting bracketing triplet, the corresponding function values, and the number of function evaluations required. A direct numerical check verifies that the returned triplet satisfies the inequality of Equation (10.2.1), reinforcing the conceptual link between the mathematical definition of a bracket and its computational realization.

```rust
// Program 10.2.1: Initially bracketing a minimum (Section 10.2)
//
// This program constructs a bracketing triplet (a,b,c) such that
// f(b) < f(a) and f(b) < f(c) (see (10.2.1)), using only function evaluations.
// It follows the probing idea in (10.2.2) and branches on the cases
// (10.2.3) (uphill or flat to the right) and (10.2.6) (downhill to the right).
//
// No external crates are required.

use std::fmt;

/// A successful bracketing result with points and function values.
#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub struct Bracket {
    pub a: f64,
    pub b: f64,
    pub c: f64,
    pub fa: f64,
    pub fb: f64,
    pub fc: f64,
    pub evals: usize,
}

impl fmt::Display for Bracket {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        writeln!(f, "Bracket:")?;
        writeln!(f, "  a = {:+.10e}, f(a) = {:+.10e}", self.a, self.fa)?;
        writeln!(f, "  b = {:+.10e}, f(b) = {:+.10e}", self.b, self.fb)?;
        writeln!(f, "  c = {:+.10e}, f(c) = {:+.10e}", self.c, self.fc)?;
        writeln!(f, "  evals = {}", self.evals)?;
        Ok(())
    }
}

/// Errors that can occur while attempting to bracket a minimum.
#[derive(Debug)]
pub enum BracketError {
    NonFiniteInput(&'static str),
    NonFiniteValue(&'static str),
    MaxItersReached { evals: usize },
}

/// Ensure the bracketing triplet is ordered a < b < c and consistent with (10.2.1).
fn ordered_bracket(a: f64, fa: f64, b: f64, fb: f64, c: f64, fc: f64, evals: usize) -> Bracket {
    if a <= b && b <= c {
        Bracket { a, b, c, fa, fb, fc, evals }
    } else if c <= b && b <= a {
        Bracket { a: c, b, c: a, fa: fc, fb, fc: fa, evals }
    } else {
        let mut pts = [(a, fa), (b, fb), (c, fc)];
        pts.sort_by(|p, q| p.0.partial_cmp(&q.0).unwrap());
        Bracket {
            a: pts[0].0,
            fa: pts[0].1,
            b: pts[1].0,
            fb: pts[1].1,
            c: pts[2].0,
            fc: pts[2].1,
            evals,
        }
    }
}

/// Attempt to bracket a local minimum starting from x0 with initial step delta > 0.
///
/// Strategy (Section 10.2.1):
/// 1) Evaluate f(x0) and probe f(x0 + delta) as in (10.2.2).
/// 2) If f(x0 + delta) >= f(x0) (case (10.2.3)), probe f(x0 - delta) as in (10.2.4).
///    - If both sides are >= f(x0), we have a nearby symmetric bracket (x0-delta, x0, x0+delta).
///    - If the left side decreases, the downhill direction is left, so expand leftward until an increase.
/// 3) If f(x0 + delta) < f(x0) (case (10.2.6)), expand rightward until an increase is observed.
/// 4) On success return (a,b,c) satisfying (10.2.1).
pub fn bracket_minimum(
    f: &(dyn Fn(f64) -> f64),
    x0: f64,
    delta: f64,
    grow: f64,
    max_iters: usize,
) -> Result<Bracket, BracketError> {
    if !x0.is_finite() {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("x0 is not finite"));
    }
    if !delta.is_finite() || delta <= 0.0 {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("delta must be finite and > 0"));
    }
    if !grow.is_finite() || grow <= 1.0 {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("grow must be finite and > 1"));
    }
    if max_iters == 0 {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("max_iters must be > 0"));
    }

    let mut evals = 0usize;

    let f0 = f(x0);
    evals += 1;
    if !f0.is_finite() {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteValue("f(x0) is not finite"));
    }

    // Probe to the right: f(x0 + delta) (equation (10.2.2)).
    let x1 = x0 + delta;
    let f1 = f(x1);
    evals += 1;
    if !f1.is_finite() {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteValue("f(x0 + delta) is not finite"));
    }

    // Case (10.2.3): uphill or flat to the right.
    if f1 >= f0 {
        // Probe to the left: f(x0 - delta) (equation (10.2.4)).
        let x2 = x0 - delta;
        let f2 = f(x2);
        evals += 1;
        if !f2.is_finite() {
            return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteValue("f(x0 - delta) is not finite"));
        }

        // If both sides are not better than x0, then b = x0 is already lower than both endpoints.
        if f2 >= f0 {
            return Ok(ordered_bracket(x2, f2, x0, f0, x1, f1, evals));
        }

        // Otherwise we found downhill to the left: expand leftward until an increase.
        return expand_until_uphill(f, x0, f0, x2, f2, -delta, grow, max_iters, evals);
    }

    // Case (10.2.6): downhill to the right, expand rightward until an increase.
    expand_until_uphill(f, x0, f0, x1, f1, delta, grow, max_iters, evals)
}

/// Expand geometrically along a downhill direction until the function turns uphill,
/// then return a bracketing triplet (a,b,c) with the middle point lowest.
///
/// Inputs:
/// - x_prev, f_prev: a point before the downhill step (often x0)
/// - x_curr, f_curr: the current downhill point (one step away from x_prev)
/// - step: signed step direction and initial magnitude
fn expand_until_uphill(
    f: &(dyn Fn(f64) -> f64),
    mut x_prev: f64,
    mut f_prev: f64,
    mut x_curr: f64,
    mut f_curr: f64,
    mut step: f64,
    grow: f64,
    max_iters: usize,
    mut evals: usize,
) -> Result<Bracket, BracketError> {
    for _ in 0..max_iters {
        step *= grow;
        let x_next = x_curr + step;
        let f_next = f(x_next);
        evals += 1;
        if !f_next.is_finite() {
            return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteValue(
                "f(x_next) is not finite during expansion",
            ));
        }

        // Turned uphill: (x_prev, x_curr, x_next) brackets a minimum with b = x_curr.
        if f_next >= f_curr {
            if !(f_curr < f_prev && f_curr < f_next) {
                return Err(BracketError::MaxItersReached { evals });
            }
            return Ok(ordered_bracket(
                x_prev, f_prev, x_curr, f_curr, x_next, f_next, evals,
            ));
        }

        // Still downhill: advance.
        x_prev = x_curr;
        f_prev = f_curr;
        x_curr = x_next;
        f_curr = f_next;
    }

    Err(BracketError::MaxItersReached { evals })
}

fn main() {
    // Example 1: A simple convex function with a minimum near x = 2.
    let f1 = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    // Example 2: A mildly nonconvex function; bracketing should still find a local valley.
    let f2 = |x: f64| (x - 1.5) * (x - 1.5) + 0.2 * (5.0 * x).cos();

    let cases: [(&str, &dyn Fn(f64) -> f64, f64, f64); 3] = [
        ("Quadratic", &f1, 0.0, 0.25),
        ("Nonconvex", &f2, 0.0, 0.10),
        ("Nonconvex (different x0)", &f2, 2.5, 0.10),
    ];

    for (name, f, x0, delta) in cases {
        println!("Case: {}", name);
        match bracket_minimum(f, x0, delta, 2.0, 50) {
            Ok(br) => {
                let cond = br.fb < br.fa && br.fb < br.fc; // (10.2.1)
                println!("{}", br);
                println!("  satisfies (10.2.1): {}", cond);
            }
            Err(e) => {
                println!("  bracketing failed: {:?}", e);
            }
        }
        println!();
    }
}
```

Program 10.2.1 illustrates a practical and robust approach to bracketing a minimum using only function evaluations. Rather than attempting to approximate the minimizer directly, the algorithm focuses on establishing a reliable interval in which the qualitative behavior of the objective function guarantees the existence of a local minimum. This strategy reflects the central theme of Section 10.2: sacrificing precision in the early stages of optimization in favor of stability and algorithmic safety.

The examples demonstrate that even for nonconvex functions, a simple exploratory procedure can successfully identify a valid bracket with a small and predictable number of evaluations. By cleanly separating probing, directional decisions, and interval expansion, the implementation highlights the logical structure underlying many modern line-search routines, which often incorporate similar bracketing steps internally.

Once such a bracket has been established, more refined minimization methods, such as golden-section search or parabolic interpolation, can be applied with confidence. As such, the program serves both as a standalone diagnostic tool and as a foundational component for more advanced one-dimensional optimization algorithms developed in subsequent sections.

## 10.2.2. Expanding the Search Interval

When a downhill direction has been identified, the step size is increased progressively to locate where the function begins to rise again. A common strategy is *exponential expansion*, in which successive points are evaluated at:

$$x_k = x_0 + 2^k \delta, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \tag{10.2.7}$$

until the condition

$$f(x_k) > f(x_{k-1}) \tag{10.2.8}$$

is satisfied.

At this point, the minimum has been passed. A valid bracketing triplet is then given by:

$$a = x_{k-2}, \quad b = x_{k-1}, \quad c = x_k, \tag{10.2.9}$$

provided that

$$f(a) > f(b) < f(c) \tag{10.2.10}$$

This exponential growth strategy guarantees that if the minimum lies within a distance $M$ of the initial guess, it will be bracketed in $O(\log M)$ function evaluations.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.2.1 on identifying a downhill search direction, Program 10.2.2 presents a concrete implementation of exponential interval expansion for bracketing a one-dimensional minimum. Once a direction of descent has been established, the remaining task is to determine where the objective function begins to increase again, thereby enclosing the minimum within a finite interval. In numerical optimization, this step is essential for ensuring that subsequent interval-reduction methods operate within a region that is guaranteed to contain a local minimizer. The program implements the exponential growth strategy described in Section 10.2.2, progressively enlarging the step size according to Equation (10.2.7) until the rise condition of Equation (10.2.8) is satisfied. This approach balances robustness and efficiency, ensuring logarithmic complexity with respect to the distance to the minimum while relying solely on function evaluations.

At the core of the implementation is the function `expand_exponential`, which realizes the exponential expansion strategy described in Section 10.2.2. The function assumes that a downhill direction has already been identified, typically through the probing procedure outlined in Section 10.2.1, and uses this directional information to guide the expansion. Starting from an initial guess $x_0$ and a base step size $\delta$, successive trial points are generated according to Equation (10.2.7), with the step length doubling at each iteration. This geometric progression ensures that the search interval grows rapidly enough to cross the minimum even when it lies far from the initial guess.

The algorithm monitors the objective function values at consecutive expansion points and terminates as soon as the rise condition of Equation (10.2.8) is detected. This condition indicates that the search has passed the bottom of the valley and entered an uphill region. At this point, the minimum is guaranteed to lie between the last two downhill points and the first uphill point. The function then assembles the bracketing triplet $(a,b,c)$ as specified in Equation (10.2.9) and verifies numerically that the valley condition of Equation (10.2.10) is satisfied. This explicit check reinforces the conceptual link between the mathematical definition of a bracket and its computational realization.

A key design feature of the implementation is its strict separation between directional expansion and interval validation. The helper routine that orders the returned triplet ensures that the points are presented consistently as either $a<b<c$ or $a>b>c$, independent of whether the expansion proceeded to the left or to the right. This normalization simplifies subsequent processing and emphasizes that bracketing is fundamentally about enclosing a minimum, not about preserving the original exploration order. At the same time, safeguards such as finite-value checks and a maximum iteration count prevent pathological behavior when the objective function is ill-behaved or lacks a well-defined local minimum in the explored region.

The `main` function demonstrates the exponential expansion procedure on both convex and mildly nonconvex objective functions. In each case, a downhill direction is specified explicitly, reflecting the typical workflow in which this routine is called only after directional probing has been performed. The program reports the resulting bracketing triplet, the associated function values, and the total number of function evaluations required. A direct numerical test confirms that the returned triplet satisfies the inequality of Equation (10.2.10), providing concrete evidence that the theoretical conditions of Section 10.2.2 are met in practice.

```rust
// Program 10.2.2: Expanding the search interval by exponential growth (Section 10.2.2)
//
// This program implements the exponential expansion strategy described in Section 10.2.2.
// Once a downhill direction has been identified, it evaluates points of the form (10.2.7)
// and stops when the rise condition (10.2.8) is satisfied. It then returns the bracketing
// triplet (10.2.9) and checks the valley condition (10.2.10).
//
// No external crates are required.

use std::fmt;

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub struct Bracket {
    pub a: f64,
    pub b: f64,
    pub c: f64,
    pub fa: f64,
    pub fb: f64,
    pub fc: f64,
    pub evals: usize,
}

impl fmt::Display for Bracket {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        writeln!(f, "Bracket:")?;
        writeln!(f, "  a = {:+.10e}, f(a) = {:+.10e}", self.a, self.fa)?;
        writeln!(f, "  b = {:+.10e}, f(b) = {:+.10e}", self.b, self.fb)?;
        writeln!(f, "  c = {:+.10e}, f(c) = {:+.10e}", self.c, self.fc)?;
        writeln!(f, "  evals = {}", self.evals)?;
        Ok(())
    }
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub enum ExpandError {
    NonFiniteInput(&'static str),
    NonFiniteValue(&'static str),
    NotDownhill, // the provided direction is not downhill at the first step
    MaxItersReached { evals: usize },
}

/// Return the triplet ordered so that a < b < c (or reversed if the search moved left).
fn ordered_bracket(a: f64, fa: f64, b: f64, fb: f64, c: f64, fc: f64, evals: usize) -> Bracket {
    if a <= b && b <= c {
        Bracket { a, b, c, fa, fb, fc, evals }
    } else if c <= b && b <= a {
        Bracket { a: c, b, c: a, fa: fc, fb, fc: fa, evals }
    } else {
        let mut pts = [(a, fa), (b, fb), (c, fc)];
        pts.sort_by(|p, q| p.0.partial_cmp(&q.0).unwrap());
        Bracket {
            a: pts[0].0,
            fa: pts[0].1,
            b: pts[1].0,
            fb: pts[1].1,
            c: pts[2].0,
            fc: pts[2].1,
            evals,
        }
    }
}

/// Exponential expansion in a known downhill direction.
///
/// Inputs:
/// - f: objective function
/// - x0: initial guess
/// - delta: initial step scale (must be > 0)
/// - direction: +1.0 means expand to the right, -1.0 means expand to the left
/// - max_k: maximum number of expansion steps k
///
/// Algorithm:
/// - Generate points x_k according to (10.2.7): x_k = x0 + 2^k * delta, for k = 1, 2, ...
///   using the supplied sign in `direction`.
/// - Stop when (10.2.8) holds: f(x_k) > f(x_{k-1}).
/// - Return (a,b,c) as in (10.2.9): a = x_{k-2}, b = x_{k-1}, c = x_k,
///   and verify the valley condition (10.2.10) numerically.
pub fn expand_exponential(
    f: &(dyn Fn(f64) -> f64),
    x0: f64,
    delta: f64,
    direction: f64,
    max_k: usize,
) -> Result<Bracket, ExpandError> {
    if !x0.is_finite() {
        return Err(ExpandError::NonFiniteInput("x0 is not finite"));
    }
    if !delta.is_finite() || delta <= 0.0 {
        return Err(ExpandError::NonFiniteInput("delta must be finite and > 0"));
    }
    if !direction.is_finite() || (direction != 1.0 && direction != -1.0) {
        return Err(ExpandError::NonFiniteInput("direction must be +1.0 or -1.0"));
    }
    if max_k < 2 {
        return Err(ExpandError::NonFiniteInput("max_k must be >= 2"));
    }

    let mut evals = 0usize;

    let f0 = f(x0);
    evals += 1;
    if !f0.is_finite() {
        return Err(ExpandError::NonFiniteValue("f(x0) is not finite"));
    }

    // k = 1: x1 = x0 + 2^1 * delta (equation (10.2.7))
    let x1 = x0 + direction * (2.0 * delta);
    let f1 = f(x1);
    evals += 1;
    if !f1.is_finite() {
        return Err(ExpandError::NonFiniteValue("f(x1) is not finite"));
    }

    // We assume a downhill direction has already been identified.
    // For consistency with Section 10.2.2, require f(x1) < f(x0) before expanding.
    if !(f1 < f0) {
        return Err(ExpandError::NotDownhill);
    }

    // k = 2: x2 = x0 + 2^2 * delta (equation (10.2.7))
    let mut x_prev2 = x0;
    let mut f_prev2 = f0;

    let mut x_prev1 = x1;
    let mut f_prev1 = f1;

    let mut pow2 = 4.0; // 2^2
    let mut xk = x0 + direction * (pow2 * delta);
    let mut fk = f(xk);
    evals += 1;
    if !fk.is_finite() {
        return Err(ExpandError::NonFiniteValue("f(x2) is not finite"));
    }

    // Iterate for k = 2, 3, ... until the rise condition (10.2.8) holds.
    for _k in 2..=max_k {
        // Check (10.2.8): f(x_k) > f(x_{k-1})
        if fk > f_prev1 {
            // Return (10.2.9): a = x_{k-2}, b = x_{k-1}, c = x_k.
            // Verify (10.2.10) numerically: f(a) > f(b) < f(c).
            if !(f_prev2 > f_prev1 && f_prev1 < fk) {
                // If this fails, the objective is not behaving like a clean valley here.
                return Err(ExpandError::MaxItersReached { evals });
            }
            return Ok(ordered_bracket(
                x_prev2, f_prev2, x_prev1, f_prev1, xk, fk, evals,
            ));
        }

        // Shift window: (k-2,k-1,k) becomes (k-1,k,k+1).
        x_prev2 = x_prev1;
        f_prev2 = f_prev1;
        x_prev1 = xk;
        f_prev1 = fk;

        // Next point: multiply by 2 again so that 2^(k+1) is used in (10.2.7).
        pow2 *= 2.0;
        xk = x0 + direction * (pow2 * delta);
        fk = f(xk);
        evals += 1;
        if !fk.is_finite() {
            return Err(ExpandError::NonFiniteValue("f(x_k) is not finite during expansion"));
        }
    }

    Err(ExpandError::MaxItersReached { evals })
}

fn main() {
    // Example 1: A convex function. Starting at x0 = 0, the downhill direction is to the right.
    let f1 = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    // Example 2: A mildly nonconvex function. A local minimum is still bracketed by expansion.
    let f2 = |x: f64| (x - 1.5) * (x - 1.5) + 0.2 * (5.0 * x).cos();

    // Choose x0 and delta and specify a known downhill direction.
    // In practice, the downhill direction is determined by the probe comparisons in Section 10.2.1.
    let cases = [
        ("Convex (right)", &f1 as &dyn Fn(f64) -> f64, 0.0, 0.10, 1.0),
        ("Nonconvex (right)", &f2 as &dyn Fn(f64) -> f64, 0.0, 0.05, 1.0),
        ("Nonconvex (left)", &f2 as &dyn Fn(f64) -> f64, 2.5, 0.05, -1.0),
    ];

    for (name, f, x0, delta, dir) in cases {
        println!("Case: {}", name);
        match expand_exponential(f, x0, delta, dir, 60) {
            Ok(br) => {
                let cond_10210 = br.fa > br.fb && br.fb < br.fc; // (10.2.10)
                println!("{}", br);
                println!("  satisfies (10.2.10): {}", cond_10210);
            }
            Err(e) => {
                println!("  expansion failed: {:?}", e);
            }
        }
        println!();
    }
}
```

Program 10.2.2 demonstrates how exponential expansion can be used to robustly and efficiently bracket a minimum once a downhill direction has been identified. By increasing the step size geometrically, the algorithm guarantees that a minimum located within a finite distance of the initial guess will be enclosed using only $O(\log M)$ function evaluations, as discussed in Section 10.2.2. This efficiency makes exponential expansion particularly attractive in settings where function evaluations are moderately expensive but derivative information is unavailable or unreliable.

The examples illustrate that the resulting bracket need not be symmetric and may span a wide interval, especially for nonconvex functions. This behavior is entirely consistent with the goal of bracketing, which prioritizes reliability over tight localization. Once a valid bracket has been established, more precise minimization algorithms can be applied safely within the enclosed interval. In this sense, exponential expansion serves as a critical bridge between exploratory probing and high-accuracy line-search methods, forming a foundational component of robust one-dimensional optimization workflows.

## 10.2.3. Practical Implementation Considerations

In practical algorithms, bracketing is usually carried out by an explicit exploratory phase rather than by assuming that a suitable triplet is already available. A common approach begins with an initial point $a$ and a second point $b = a + \delta$, where $\delta$ is a user-chosen step length that sets both the search direction and the initial scale. The objective function is evaluated at these points, and as long as $f(b) < f(a)$, the interval is shifted outward by setting $a \leftarrow b$ and selecting a new $b$ farther along the same direction. This outward expansion is often implemented by multiplying the step length by a constant factor greater than one, allowing the algorithm to rapidly move away from the initial guess while minimizing unnecessary function evaluations.

The expansion process continues until a point is reached where the function value increases, that is, until $f(b) > f(a)$. At this stage, the algorithm has detected a change in monotonic behavior, indicating that the minimum lies somewhere between the previous and current points. Together with the preceding point, these evaluations define a bracketing triplet $(a, b, c)$ satisfying $f(a) > f(b) < f(c)$. This triplet provides a guaranteed interval within which a local minimum exists, forming the foundation for subsequent interval-reduction methods such as golden-section search or parabolic interpolation.

Several practical issues arise in this procedure. The choice of the initial step size $\delta$ affects efficiency but not correctness. If $\delta$ is too small, many iterations may be required before a bracket is found, while an excessively large $\delta$ may skip over narrow minima or lead to evaluations far from the region of interest. Robust implementations therefore balance rapid expansion with safeguards such as limits on the maximum interval length or the number of expansion steps.

It is also important to emphasize that the function need not be unimodal over the entire real line. The bracketing phase merely isolates one region where the function decreases and then increases, thereby identifying a single basin of attraction containing a local minimum. Different initial points or search directions may lead to different brackets and, consequently, to different local minima. When the objective function is multimodal and the global minimum is sought, bracketing must be combined with higher-level strategies such as multiple restarts, stochastic exploration, or other global optimization techniques discussed in later sections.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.2.3 on practical strategies for bracketing a minimum, Program 10.2.3 provides a concrete implementation of an exploratory bracketing procedure based on outward interval shifting. In practical numerical optimization, one rarely begins with a pre-existing bracketing triplet. Instead, the algorithm must actively explore the objective function starting from an initial point and progressively enlarge the search region until a change in monotonic behavior is detected. This program implements such an exploratory phase by repeatedly shifting the interval in a downhill direction and increasing the step length multiplicatively, as described in the section text. The resulting procedure balances rapid exploration with robustness, ensuring that a valid bracketing interval is identified using only a modest number of function evaluations.

At the core of the implementation is the function `bracket_by_outward_shift`, which encapsulates the practical bracketing logic described in Section 10.2.3. The function begins with an initial point $a_0$ and a second point $b = a_0 + \delta$, where the step length $\delta$ determines both the initial search direction and the scale of exploration. The objective function is evaluated at these points, and their relative values determine whether the algorithm proceeds immediately in the chosen direction or first reverses direction as a safeguard. This initial adjustment reflects the exploratory nature of practical bracketing and avoids the assumption that the user-supplied step always points downhill.

Once a downhill direction has been established, the algorithm enters an outward shifting phase. As long as the inequality $f(b) < f(a)$ holds, the interval is shifted forward by assigning the current point $b$ to $a$ and selecting a new point farther along the same direction. The step length is multiplied by a constant factor greater than one at each iteration, allowing the algorithm to move rapidly away from the initial guess while keeping the number of function evaluations small. This multiplicative growth implements the expansion principle discussed in Section 10.2.3 and is essential for efficiency when the minimum lies far from the starting point.

The expansion process terminates when the function value at the newly proposed point exceeds that at the previous point, indicating that the minimum has been passed. At this stage, the last three evaluated points define a bracketing triplet satisfying the inequality $f(a) > f(b) < f(c)$. The implementation explicitly checks this condition before returning the result, ensuring that the mathematical definition of a valid bracket is enforced at the computational level. A normalization step then orders the triplet consistently as either $a < b < c$ or $a > b > c$, independent of the direction in which the exploration proceeded. This ordering simplifies subsequent interval-reduction methods and emphasizes that the purpose of bracketing is to enclose a minimum, not to preserve the original traversal order.

Several practical safeguards are incorporated into the implementation. Finite-value checks prevent undefined behavior when the objective function is not well behaved, while limits on the number of expansion steps and on the maximum span of the interval guard against runaway excursions far from the region of interest. These measures reflect common practices in robust numerical software and highlight that correctness in bracketing depends not only on mathematical logic but also on defensive programming choices.

The `main` function demonstrates the behavior of the algorithm on both convex and multimodal objective functions. By varying the initial point and step direction, the examples illustrate how different exploratory paths can lead to different valid brackets, each isolating a distinct basin of attraction. This behavior directly reflects the discussion in Section 10.2.3 that bracketing identifies a local minimum rather than a global one, and that different starting conditions may produce different outcomes.

```rust
// Program 10.2.3: Practical bracketing by outward interval shifting (Section 10.2.3)
//
// This program implements the practical exploratory bracketing procedure described in Section 10.2.3.
// Starting from an initial point a and a second point b = a + δ, it evaluates f(a) and f(b).
// As long as f(b) < f(a), the search shifts outward by setting a ← b and choosing a new b
// farther along the same direction, typically by multiplying the step length by a factor > 1.
// The process terminates when f(b) > f(a), at which point the minimum has been passed and a
// bracketing triplet (a, b, c) with f(a) > f(b) < f(c) can be constructed from the last three points.
//
// No external crates are required.

use std::fmt;

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub struct Bracket {
    pub a: f64,
    pub b: f64,
    pub c: f64,
    pub fa: f64,
    pub fb: f64,
    pub fc: f64,
    pub evals: usize,
}

impl fmt::Display for Bracket {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        writeln!(f, "Bracket:")?;
        writeln!(f, "  a = {:+.10e}, f(a) = {:+.10e}", self.a, self.fa)?;
        writeln!(f, "  b = {:+.10e}, f(b) = {:+.10e}", self.b, self.fb)?;
        writeln!(f, "  c = {:+.10e}, f(c) = {:+.10e}", self.c, self.fc)?;
        writeln!(f, "  evals = {}", self.evals)?;
        Ok(())
    }
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub enum BracketError {
    NonFiniteInput(&'static str),
    NonFiniteValue(&'static str),
    DegenerateStep,
    MaxItersReached { evals: usize },
    MaxSpanExceeded { evals: usize, span: f64 },
}

/// Return the triplet ordered consistently as a < b < c (or reversed for descending x).
fn ordered_bracket(a: f64, fa: f64, b: f64, fb: f64, c: f64, fc: f64, evals: usize) -> Bracket {
    if a <= b && b <= c {
        Bracket { a, b, c, fa, fb, fc, evals }
    } else if c <= b && b <= a {
        Bracket { a: c, b, c: a, fa: fc, fb, fc: fa, evals }
    } else {
        let mut pts = [(a, fa), (b, fb), (c, fc)];
        pts.sort_by(|p, q| p.0.partial_cmp(&q.0).unwrap());
        Bracket {
            a: pts[0].0,
            fa: pts[0].1,
            b: pts[1].0,
            fb: pts[1].1,
            c: pts[2].0,
            fc: pts[2].1,
            evals,
        }
    }
}

/// Practical exploratory bracketing by outward interval shifting (Section 10.2.3).
///
/// Inputs:
/// - f: objective function
/// - a0: initial point a
/// - delta: initial step length δ (sets initial direction and scale)
/// - grow: multiplicative expansion factor (> 1)
/// - max_steps: maximum number of outward shifts/expansions
/// - max_span: optional safety limit on |b - a0| to prevent runaway excursions
///
/// Procedure:
/// - Start with a = a0, b = a0 + δ.
/// - Evaluate f(a), f(b).
/// - While f(b) < f(a), shift outward:
///     c ← a, a ← b, b ← b + step, step ← grow * step
/// - Stop when f(b) > f(a). Then the last three points (c, a, b) satisfy f(c) > f(a) < f(b),
///   providing a bracketing triplet (a,b,c) in the sense of Section 10.2.
///
/// Notes:
/// - This routine isolates a single basin of attraction. Different a0 or δ may produce different
///   brackets for multimodal objectives, consistent with the discussion in Section 10.2.3.
pub fn bracket_by_outward_shift(
    f: &(dyn Fn(f64) -> f64),
    a0: f64,
    delta: f64,
    grow: f64,
    max_steps: usize,
    max_span: Option<f64>,
) -> Result<Bracket, BracketError> {
    if !a0.is_finite() {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("a0 is not finite"));
    }
    if !delta.is_finite() || delta == 0.0 {
        return Err(BracketError::DegenerateStep);
    }
    if !grow.is_finite() || grow <= 1.0 {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("grow must be finite and > 1"));
    }
    if max_steps == 0 {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("max_steps must be > 0"));
    }
    if let Some(ms) = max_span {
        if !ms.is_finite() || ms <= 0.0 {
            return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("max_span must be finite and > 0"));
        }
    }

    let mut evals = 0usize;

    // Initial points: a = a0, b = a0 + δ.
    let mut a = a0;
    let mut b = a0 + delta;
    let mut step = delta;

    let mut fa = f(a);
    evals += 1;
    if !fa.is_finite() {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteValue("f(a0) is not finite"));
    }

    let mut fb = f(b);
    evals += 1;
    if !fb.is_finite() {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteValue("f(a0 + delta) is not finite"));
    }

    // Track the point preceding a. We set c = a initially and will overwrite it on the first shift.
    let mut c = a;
    let mut fc = fa;

    // If we are not going downhill initially, reverse direction by flipping δ once.
    // This is a practical safeguard: Section 10.2.3 emphasizes exploratory behavior rather than
    // assuming a correct direction is already provided.
    if fb >= fa {
        step = -step;
        b = a + step;
        fb = f(b);
        evals += 1;
        if !fb.is_finite() {
            return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteValue("f(a0 - delta) is not finite"));
        }
    }

    // Outward shifting and expansion.
    for _ in 0..max_steps {
        // Safety: prevent runaway excursions away from the region of interest.
        if let Some(ms) = max_span {
            let span = (b - a0).abs();
            if span > ms {
                return Err(BracketError::MaxSpanExceeded { evals, span });
            }
        }

        // If the function has started to rise, we have passed the minimum.
        if fb > fa {
            // The last three points are (c, a, b). The middle point a is the lowest.
            // We expect: f(c) > f(a) < f(b). If c == a (no shift happened), then we do not yet
            // have a three-point bracket and must continue.
            if c != a && (fc > fa && fa < fb) {
                return Ok(ordered_bracket(c, fc, a, fa, b, fb, evals));
            }
            // Otherwise continue expanding to obtain a proper three-point bracket.
        }

        // Still downhill or flat: shift outward.
        c = a;
        fc = fa;

        a = b;
        fa = fb;

        // Increase step length and propose a new b farther along the same direction.
        step *= grow;
        b = a + step;

        fb = f(b);
        evals += 1;
        if !fb.is_finite() {
            return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteValue("f(b) is not finite during expansion"));
        }
    }

    Err(BracketError::MaxItersReached { evals })
}

fn main() {
    // A convex example (single basin).
    let f1 = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    // A mildly multimodal example to illustrate basin selection.
    let f2 = |x: f64| (x - 1.5) * (x - 1.5) + 0.2 * (5.0 * x).cos();

    // Each run demonstrates that different starting points and step directions can lead to
    // different brackets for multimodal objectives, consistent with Section 10.2.3.
    let cases: [(&str, &dyn Fn(f64) -> f64, f64, f64); 4] = [
        ("Convex (a0=0, delta=+0.1)", &f1, 0.0, 0.10),
        ("Convex (a0=3, delta=-0.1)", &f1, 3.0, -0.10),
        ("Multimodal (a0=0, delta=+0.05)", &f2, 0.0, 0.05),
        ("Multimodal (a0=2.5, delta=-0.05)", &f2, 2.5, -0.05),
    ];

    for (name, f, a0, delta) in cases {
        println!("Case: {}", name);
        match bracket_by_outward_shift(f, a0, delta, 1.6, 80, Some(1000.0)) {
            Ok(br) => {
                // Verify the bracketing inequality f(a) > f(b) < f(c) as stated in Section 10.2.3.
                let cond = br.fa > br.fb && br.fb < br.fc;
                println!("{}", br);
                println!("  satisfies f(a) > f(b) < f(c): {}", cond);
            }
            Err(e) => {
                println!("  bracketing failed: {:?}", e);
            }
        }
        println!();
    }
}
```

Program 10.2.3 demonstrates how a practical bracketing strategy can be implemented by combining outward interval shifting with controlled step growth. The algorithm reliably identifies a region in which the objective function decreases and then increases, thereby guaranteeing the existence of a local minimum within the returned interval. This approach embodies the key principle emphasized in Section 10.2.3: bracketing is an exploratory phase whose primary goal is robustness rather than precision.

The examples show that the resulting bracket need not be narrow or symmetric, particularly for multimodal functions. This is not a limitation but a deliberate design choice, as the role of the bracketing phase is to provide a safe starting interval for more refined minimization methods. Once such an interval has been established, techniques such as golden-section search or parabolic interpolation can be applied with confidence. The modular structure of the implementation also makes it straightforward to integrate this bracketing routine into larger optimization frameworks or to augment it with global strategies such as multiple restarts when searching for global minima.

## 10.2.4. Enhancements and Heuristics

Some bracketing algorithms incorporate *parabolic extrapolation* when more than two points are available. Given three points $(x_{k-2}, x_{k-1}, x_k)$ with known function values and satisfying:

$$f(x_{k-2}) > f(x_{k-1}) < f(x_k), \tag{10.2.11}$$

one may fit a parabola through these points and estimate the location of its minimum. Jumping directly to this estimated point can sometimes bracket the minimum more quickly than rigid exponential expansion, particularly if the function is approximately quadratic near the minimum.

Such extrapolation is heuristic. If the function is highly asymmetric or nonsmooth, the estimate may overshoot the true minimum. Consequently, practical implementations often combine cautious expansion with occasional extrapolated steps to balance robustness and efficiency.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.2.4 on enhancing basic bracketing strategies, Program 10.2.4 provides a practical implementation of bracketing with cautious parabolic extrapolation. In numerical optimization, purely geometric expansion can be unnecessarily conservative when the objective function is smooth and approximately quadratic near a local minimum. Parabolic extrapolation offers a way to exploit this local structure by estimating the minimizer of a quadratic model fitted through three points that already form a valley. This program integrates such extrapolation as a heuristic acceleration mechanism, while preserving the primary goal of the bracketing phase: returning a guaranteed interval that satisfies the valley condition. The implementation emphasizes robustness by combining extrapolation with explicit safeguards that prevent overshoot and ensure that the returned triplet remains suitable for subsequent interval-reduction methods.

At the core of the implementation is the function `bracket_with_parabolic_extrapolation`, which extends the outward expansion strategy developed in Sections 10.2.2 and 10.2.3. The function begins by establishing an initial downhill direction using two nearby evaluations and then introduces a third point through controlled expansion. Once three points are available, the algorithm checks whether they satisfy the valley inequality expressed in Equation (10.2.11). Only when this condition holds does the algorithm consider applying a parabolic extrapolation step.

The parabolic extrapolation is implemented by fitting a quadratic interpolant through the three available points and estimating the location of its minimum. This estimate is treated as a candidate point rather than as a definitive improvement. To maintain robustness, the candidate is accepted only if it lies strictly within the current interval and yields a lower function value than the existing middle point. These conditions reflect the heuristic nature of extrapolation emphasized in Section 10.2.4 and prevent extreme jumps when the objective function is asymmetric, flat, or nonsmooth.

A key feature of the implementation is the explicit rebuilding of a valid bracket when an extrapolated point is accepted. Rather than returning a potentially degenerate triplet, the algorithm evaluates neighboring points on each side of the new candidate and constructs a fresh three-point bracket that again satisfies the inequality of Equation (10.2.11). This step ensures that the fundamental contract of the bracketing phase is preserved: regardless of whether extrapolation is used, the routine always returns a triplet that encloses a local minimum in the required sense.

Several practical safeguards are embedded throughout the procedure. Finite-value checks guard against undefined function evaluations, while bounds on the maximum number of expansion steps and the maximum excursion from the initial point prevent runaway behavior. The algorithm also falls back gracefully to the existing bracket whenever extrapolation fails to produce a clear improvement. Together, these measures illustrate how heuristic enhancements can be incorporated into a bracketing routine without sacrificing correctness or predictability.

The `main` function demonstrates the behavior of the algorithm on both convex and mildly nonconvex objective functions. In the convex case, parabolic extrapolation rapidly identifies the exact minimizer and rebuilds a symmetric bracket around it. In the nonconvex cases, extrapolation may or may not be beneficial, and the algorithm adapts accordingly by either refining the bracket or relying on cautious expansion. These examples reinforce the point that extrapolation is an optional accelerator rather than a replacement for bracketing logic.

```rust
// Program 10.2.4: Bracketing with cautious parabolic extrapolation (Section 10.2.4)
//
// This program implements a practical one-dimensional bracketing routine enhanced with a
// parabolic extrapolation heuristic. When three points (x_{k-2}, x_{k-1}, x_k) are available
// and satisfy the valley inequality (10.2.11),
//     f(x_{k-2}) > f(x_{k-1}) < f(x_k),
// a quadratic interpolant through these points can be used to estimate the location of its
// minimum. Jumping to this estimated point may reduce the number of function evaluations when
// the objective is approximately quadratic near the minimizer.
//
// Because parabolic extrapolation is heuristic and may overshoot on asymmetric or nonsmooth
// functions, this implementation is deliberately cautious:
// 1) The extrapolated point is only accepted if it lies strictly inside the current interval
//    and improves upon the current middle value.
// 2) If accepted, the algorithm rebuilds a guaranteed three-point bracket around the new point
//    so that the returned triplet always satisfies (10.2.11).
// 3) If the extrapolation is unusable, the algorithm falls back to controlled outward expansion.
//
// The routine is intended as a textbook-grade reference implementation. It returns a bracketing
// triplet (a,b,c) with a<b<c (or a>b>c) and f(a) > f(b) < f(c), ready for subsequent interval-
// reduction methods such as golden-section search or parabolic interpolation.
//
// No external crates are required.

use std::fmt;

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub struct Bracket {
    pub a: f64,
    pub b: f64,
    pub c: f64,
    pub fa: f64,
    pub fb: f64,
    pub fc: f64,
    pub evals: usize,
}

impl fmt::Display for Bracket {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        writeln!(f, "Bracket:")?;
        writeln!(f, "  a = {:+.10e}, f(a) = {:+.10e}", self.a, self.fa)?;
        writeln!(f, "  b = {:+.10e}, f(b) = {:+.10e}", self.b, self.fb)?;
        writeln!(f, "  c = {:+.10e}, f(c) = {:+.10e}", self.c, self.fc)?;
        writeln!(f, "  evals = {}", self.evals)?;
        Ok(())
    }
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub enum BracketError {
    NonFiniteInput(&'static str),
    NonFiniteValue(&'static str),
    DegenerateStep,
    MaxItersReached { evals: usize },
    MaxSpanExceeded { evals: usize, span: f64 },
}

/// Return the triplet ordered so that a < b < c (or reversed if descending).
fn ordered_bracket(a: f64, fa: f64, b: f64, fb: f64, c: f64, fc: f64, evals: usize) -> Bracket {
    if a <= b && b <= c {
        Bracket { a, b, c, fa, fb, fc, evals }
    } else if c <= b && b <= a {
        Bracket { a: c, b, c: a, fa: fc, fb, fc: fa, evals }
    } else {
        // Defensive sorting for rare non-monotone inputs.
        let mut pts = [(a, fa), (b, fb), (c, fc)];
        pts.sort_by(|p, q| p.0.partial_cmp(&q.0).unwrap());
        Bracket {
            a: pts[0].0,
            fa: pts[0].1,
            b: pts[1].0,
            fb: pts[1].1,
            c: pts[2].0,
            fc: pts[2].1,
            evals,
        }
    }
}

/// Clamp x to [lo, hi] assuming lo <= hi.
fn clamp(x: f64, lo: f64, hi: f64) -> f64 {
    if x < lo {
        lo
    } else if x > hi {
        hi
    } else {
        x
    }
}

/// Parabolic minimizer through three points (x0,f0), (x1,f1), (x2,f2).
///
/// Returns None if the quadratic model is ill-conditioned or nearly linear.
fn parabolic_minimizer(x0: f64, f0: f64, x1: f64, f1: f64, x2: f64, f2: f64) -> Option<f64> {
    // This is an implementation detail: it fits a quadratic interpolant and finds its stationary point.
    let x10 = x1 - x0;
    let x12 = x1 - x2;

    let d0 = f1 - f0;
    let d2 = f1 - f2;

    let denom = (x10 * d2) - (x12 * d0);
    if !denom.is_finite() || denom.abs() < 1e-14 {
        return None;
    }

    let numer = (x10 * x10 * d2) - (x12 * x12 * d0);
    let shift = 0.5 * numer / denom;
    let x_star = x1 - shift;

    if x_star.is_finite() {
        Some(x_star)
    } else {
        None
    }
}

/// Evaluate f(x) and check for finiteness.
fn eval_f(f: &(dyn Fn(f64) -> f64), x: f64, evals: &mut usize) -> Result<f64, BracketError> {
    let fx = f(x);
    *evals += 1;
    if fx.is_finite() {
        Ok(fx)
    } else {
        Err(BracketError::NonFiniteValue("f(x) is not finite"))
    }
}

/// Rebuild a guaranteed three-point bracket around a new center x_new.
///
/// Given an existing enclosing interval [xl, xr] with a valley at x_new, this routine ensures
/// a returned triplet (a,b,c) satisfying (10.2.11) by evaluating points on each side if needed.
fn rebuild_bracket_around(
    f: &(dyn Fn(f64) -> f64),
    xl: f64,
    xr: f64,
    x_new: f64,
    f_new: f64,
    evals: &mut usize,
) -> Result<Bracket, BracketError> {
    let lo = xl.min(xr);
    let hi = xl.max(xr);

    // Choose provisional neighbors inside (lo, hi). If x_new is close to a boundary, pull the neighbor inward.
    let left = if x_new - lo > 0.0 { lo } else { (lo + x_new) * 0.5 };
    let right = if hi - x_new > 0.0 { hi } else { (hi + x_new) * 0.5 };

    // Evaluate endpoints; if either endpoint is not higher than the center, pull it inward once.
    let mut a = left;
    let mut fa = eval_f(f, a, evals)?;

    if fa <= f_new {
        a = 0.5 * (a + x_new);
        fa = eval_f(f, a, evals)?;
    }

    let mut c = right;
    let mut fc = eval_f(f, c, evals)?;

    if fc <= f_new {
        c = 0.5 * (c + x_new);
        fc = eval_f(f, c, evals)?;
    }

    // After at most one inward adjustment on each side, enforce the bracket condition.
    if !(fa > f_new && f_new < fc) {
        // If the function is extremely flat or pathological, we may still fail.
        // In that case, return an error so the caller can fall back to expansion.
        return Err(BracketError::MaxItersReached { evals: *evals });
    }

    Ok(ordered_bracket(a, fa, x_new, f_new, c, fc, *evals))
}

/// Bracketing with cautious parabolic extrapolation (Section 10.2.4).
///
/// Inputs:
/// - f: objective function
/// - x0: initial point
/// - delta: initial step length δ (sets initial direction and scale)
/// - grow: multiplicative expansion factor (> 1)
/// - max_steps: maximum number of expansion steps
/// - max_span: optional bound on |x - x0| to prevent runaway excursions
///
/// Output:
/// - A bracket (a,b,c) satisfying (10.2.11), with consistent ordering.
pub fn bracket_with_parabolic_extrapolation(
    f: &(dyn Fn(f64) -> f64),
    x0: f64,
    delta: f64,
    grow: f64,
    max_steps: usize,
    max_span: Option<f64>,
) -> Result<Bracket, BracketError> {
    if !x0.is_finite() {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("x0 is not finite"));
    }
    if !delta.is_finite() || delta == 0.0 {
        return Err(BracketError::DegenerateStep);
    }
    if !grow.is_finite() || grow <= 1.0 {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("grow must be finite and > 1"));
    }
    if max_steps < 2 {
        return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("max_steps must be >= 2"));
    }
    if let Some(ms) = max_span {
        if !ms.is_finite() || ms <= 0.0 {
            return Err(BracketError::NonFiniteInput("max_span must be finite and > 0"));
        }
    }

    let mut evals = 0usize;

    // Initial evaluation.
    let mut x_a = x0;
    let mut f_a = eval_f(f, x_a, &mut evals)?;

    // Second point sets initial direction.
    let mut step = delta;
    let mut x_b = x_a + step;
    let mut f_b = eval_f(f, x_b, &mut evals)?;

    // Exploratory safeguard: if not downhill, reverse direction once.
    if f_b >= f_a {
        step = -step;
        x_b = x_a + step;
        f_b = eval_f(f, x_b, &mut evals)?;
    }

    // Third point for the first three-point pattern.
    step *= grow;
    let mut x_c = x_b + step;
    let mut f_c = eval_f(f, x_c, &mut evals)?;

    for _ in 0..max_steps {
        // Safety bound on excursion size.
        if let Some(ms) = max_span {
            let span = (x_a - x0).abs().max((x_b - x0).abs()).max((x_c - x0).abs());
            if span > ms {
                return Err(BracketError::MaxSpanExceeded { evals, span });
            }
        }

        // If we have a valley triplet already, consider parabolic extrapolation.
        if f_a > f_b && f_b < f_c {
            // Attempt a cautious parabolic step inside (x_a, x_c).
            if let Some(x_star_raw) = parabolic_minimizer(x_a, f_a, x_b, f_b, x_c, f_c) {
                let lo = x_a.min(x_c);
                let hi = x_a.max(x_c);

                // Clamp to stay strictly inside the current interval to avoid overshoot.
                // A small margin prevents returning endpoints that collapse the valley inequality.
                let margin = 1e-12 * (hi - lo).max(1.0);
                let x_star = clamp(x_star_raw, lo + margin, hi - margin);

                let f_star = eval_f(f, x_star, &mut evals)?;

                // Accept only if it improves on the current middle point.
                if f_star < f_b {
                    // Rebuild a guaranteed bracket around the improved point.
                    // If rebuilding fails (extreme flatness), fall back to the existing bracket.
                    if let Ok(br) = rebuild_bracket_around(f, x_a, x_c, x_star, f_star, &mut evals)
                    {
                        return Ok(br);
                    }
                }
            }

            // If extrapolation is not used or not helpful, return the current valid bracket.
            return Ok(ordered_bracket(x_a, f_a, x_b, f_b, x_c, f_c, evals));
        }

        // Otherwise continue outward shifting: keep moving in the downhill direction.
        // If f_b < f_a, shift (a,b,c) forward; if not, we also shift to keep searching.
        x_a = x_b;
        f_a = f_b;
        x_b = x_c;
        f_b = f_c;

        step *= grow;
        x_c = x_b + step;
        f_c = eval_f(f, x_c, &mut evals)?;
    }

    Err(BracketError::MaxItersReached { evals })
}

fn main() {
    // Convex function: extrapolation is often accurate near the minimum.
    let f1 = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    // Mildly nonconvex function: extrapolation may be less reliable, so caution is important.
    let f2 = |x: f64| (x - 1.5) * (x - 1.5) + 0.2 * (5.0 * x).cos();

    let cases: [(&str, &dyn Fn(f64) -> f64, f64, f64); 4] = [
        ("Convex (x0=0, delta=+0.10)", &f1, 0.0, 0.10),
        ("Convex (x0=3, delta=-0.10)", &f1, 3.0, -0.10),
        ("Nonconvex (x0=0, delta=+0.05)", &f2, 0.0, 0.05),
        ("Nonconvex (x0=2.5, delta=-0.05)", &f2, 2.5, -0.05),
    ];

    for (name, f, x0, delta) in cases {
        println!("Case: {}", name);
        match bracket_with_parabolic_extrapolation(f, x0, delta, 1.6, 80, Some(1000.0)) {
            Ok(br) => {
                let cond = br.fa > br.fb && br.fb < br.fc; // (10.2.11)
                println!("{}", br);
                println!("  satisfies (10.2.11): {}", cond);
            }
            Err(e) => {
                println!("  bracketing failed: {:?}", e);
            }
        }
        println!();
    }
}
```

Program 10.2.4 demonstrates how heuristic enhancements can be integrated into the bracketing phase without undermining its reliability. By selectively applying parabolic extrapolation only when a clear valley has been detected and by rebuilding a guaranteed bracket around any accepted extrapolated point, the algorithm balances efficiency with robustness. This design directly reflects the discussion in Section 10.2.4, where extrapolation is presented as a useful but nonessential tool.

The examples illustrate that when the objective function is close to quadratic, extrapolation can significantly reduce the number of function evaluations required to identify a suitable bracket. At the same time, the safeguarded implementation ensures that pathological cases do not compromise the validity of the result. Once a reliable bracketing triplet has been obtained, more precise minimization techniques can be applied confidently within the enclosed interval. As such, this program provides a practical bridge between exploratory bracketing and high-accuracy one-dimensional minimization methods.

## 10.2.5. Section Remarks

Bracketing a minimum is a critical preparatory step for one-dimensional optimization. Although it requires additional function evaluations upfront, it significantly improves the reliability of subsequent minimization algorithms. By ensuring that a local minimum lies within a known interval, bracketing guards against pathological behavior and floating-point issues. As with root-finding, the guiding principle is simple but effective: always bracket the solution before attempting to isolate it.

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/MbL5C1dGC3RzBTEKZs5b.7","tags":[]}

# 10.3. Golden Section Search in One Dimension

Once a minimum has been bracketed by a triplet $(a, b, c)$ satisfying,

$$f(a) > f(b) < f(c), \tag{10.3.1}$$

the problem shifts from *existence* to *localization*. The minimizer is known to lie somewhere within the interval $(a, c)$, but this interval may still be too wide to provide a useful approximation. The objective of the next stage is therefore to reduce the size of the enclosing interval systematically, while preserving the guarantee that the minimum remains inside it.

Golden-section search is a classical derivative-free method designed precisely for this task. It provides a simple, reliable mechanism for shrinking a bracketing interval using only function evaluations, without assuming differentiability or access to gradient information. Conceptually, the method plays a role similar to the bisection method in root-finding: at each iteration, the current interval is subdivided, one subinterval is eliminated based on function comparisons, and the remaining interval is guaranteed to contain the solution.

There is, however, an important distinction between the two settings. Bisection relies on a sign change of the function and uses the midpoint of the interval, whereas golden-section search assumes only that the objective function is unimodal on the interval $(a, c)$. Rather than using the midpoint, it places evaluation points at carefully chosen interior locations so that the reduction in interval length is optimized in a worst-case sense. This choice ensures that progress is steady and predictable, regardless of the detailed shape of the function.

The defining feature of golden-section search is that the same proportion of the interval is discarded at every step, independent of which side is eliminated. This leads to a fixed convergence rate and avoids the inefficiencies that can arise when evaluation points must be recomputed from scratch at each iteration. As a result, the method is particularly attractive in settings where function evaluations are expensive or noisy, and robustness is more important than rapid local convergence.

## 10.3.1. Interval Reduction Logic

Given a bracketing triplet $(a, b, c)$ with $f(b)$ minimal, the minimizer must lie in the open interval $(a, c)$. The fundamental operation of golden-section search is to introduce a new interior point $x \in (a, c)$, evaluate $f(x)$, and then discard the portion of the interval that cannot contain the minimum.

Two outcomes are possible:

If $f(x) \ge f(b),$ then the function does not decrease beyond $b$ in the direction of $x$. In this case, the minimum cannot lie between $b$ and $x$, and the interval can be safely reduced by replacing the original bracket $(a, b, c)$ with $(a, b, x)$. The enclosing interval thus shrinks from $(a, c)$ to $(a, x)$.

If instead $f(x) < f(b)$, then the new point yields a lower function value than the current middle point. This indicates that the minimum lies further in the direction of $x$, and the appropriate update is to replace the bracket by $(b, x, c)$, thereby discarding the interval $(a, b)$.

In both cases, the width of the interval containing the minimum is reduced while maintaining the unimodal structure required for further iterations. The central question, addressed by the golden-section construction, is how to choose the position of $x$ so that this reduction is maximized in the worst case. The answer leads to a specific ratio for subdividing the interval, ensuring that each iteration produces the greatest guaranteed decrease in interval length without introducing unnecessary function evaluations.

This interval-reduction logic forms the backbone of golden-section search and underlies its efficiency, simplicity, and enduring usefulness in one-dimensional derivative-free optimization.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.3 on reducing a bracketing interval once the existence of a minimum has been established, Program 10.3.1 provides a practical implementation of golden-section search for one-dimensional minimization. After a valid bracketing triplet satisfying Equation (10.3.1) has been obtained, the central task is no longer to detect the presence of a minimum, but to localize it accurately within a progressively shrinking interval. Golden-section search addresses this task using only function evaluations, without relying on derivatives or smoothness assumptions beyond unimodality. The program demonstrates how a fixed, carefully chosen subdivision ratio leads to predictable interval reduction and guarantees steady progress toward the minimizer in finite-precision arithmetic.

At the core of the implementation is the `golden_section_search` function, which operates on an initial bracketing triplet $(a,b,c)$ satisfying the inequality in Equation (10.3.1). The function first validates and normalizes this triplet so that the search interval is consistently represented as $[a,c]$ with increasing abscissas. This normalization allows the subsequent interval-reduction logic to be expressed cleanly and independently of the direction in which the original bracket was constructed.

The golden-section algorithm introduces two interior points within the current interval, positioned according to the golden-section ratio derived in Section 10.3.1. At each iteration, the function values at these interior points are compared. Depending on the outcome, one subinterval is discarded in accordance with the logic described following Equation (10.3.1): if the function value at the new point does not improve upon the current best, the interval segment beyond that point is eliminated; otherwise, the interval is shifted inward toward the region of lower function values. In both cases, the enclosing interval shrinks while continuing to contain the minimizer under the unimodality assumption.

A key feature of golden-section search, reflected directly in the implementation, is that only one new function evaluation is required per iteration after initialization. Because one of the interior points can be reused when the interval is updated, the algorithm avoids redundant evaluations and achieves a fixed convergence rate that is independent of the detailed shape of the objective function. This predictable behavior is particularly valuable in applications where function evaluations are expensive or noisy.

The implementation also carefully tracks the best sampled point encountered during the search and ensures that the final reported minimizer lies within the final enclosing interval. Termination occurs when the width of the interval falls below a prescribed tolerance or when a maximum number of iterations is reached. These stopping conditions balance numerical accuracy against computational cost and reflect standard practice in robust derivative-free optimization routines.

The `main` function illustrates the use of golden-section search on two representative objective functions: a strictly convex quadratic and a mildly nonconvex function with local structure. In both cases, the initial bracketing triplet satisfies Equation (10.3.1), and the algorithm systematically refines the interval until a tight enclosure of the minimizer is obtained. The printed results confirm that the returned estimate lies within the final interval and that the interval width is consistent with the chosen tolerance.

```rust
// Program 10.3.1: Golden-section search in one dimension (Section 10.3)
//
// This program implements golden-section search for localizing a minimum once a bracketing
// triplet (a,b,c) has been found satisfying the condition (10.3.1):
//     f(a) > f(b) < f(c).
//
// The method assumes the objective is unimodal on the interval (a,c). It repeatedly shrinks
// the enclosing interval while preserving the guarantee that the minimizer remains inside.
// Only function evaluations are used. No derivatives are required.
//
// This version is warning-free and guarantees that the returned estimate x_min lies inside
// the final interval [left, right].
//
// No external crates are required.

use std::fmt;

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub struct Bracket {
    pub a: f64,
    pub b: f64,
    pub c: f64,
    pub fa: f64,
    pub fb: f64,
    pub fc: f64,
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub struct GoldenResult {
    pub x_min: f64,   // best point returned (guaranteed in [left,right])
    pub f_min: f64,   // f(x_min)
    pub left: f64,    // final enclosing interval left endpoint
    pub right: f64,   // final enclosing interval right endpoint
    pub evals: usize, // total function evaluations in golden_section_search
    pub iters: usize, // number of interval-reduction iterations
}

impl fmt::Display for GoldenResult {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        writeln!(f, "Golden-section result:")?;
        writeln!(f, "  x_min  = {:+.12e}", self.x_min)?;
        writeln!(f, "  f_min  = {:+.12e}", self.f_min)?;
        writeln!(f, "  left   = {:+.12e}", self.left)?;
        writeln!(f, "  right  = {:+.12e}", self.right)?;
        writeln!(f, "  width  = {:+.12e}", self.right - self.left)?;
        writeln!(f, "  evals  = {}", self.evals)?;
        writeln!(f, "  iters  = {}", self.iters)?;
        Ok(())
    }
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub enum GoldenError {
    NonFiniteInput(&'static str),
    NonFiniteValue(&'static str),
    InvalidBracket(&'static str),
}

/// Evaluate f(x) and require finiteness.
fn eval_f(f: &(dyn Fn(f64) -> f64), x: f64) -> Result<f64, GoldenError> {
    let fx = f(x);
    if fx.is_finite() {
        Ok(fx)
    } else {
        Err(GoldenError::NonFiniteValue("f(x) is not finite"))
    }
}

/// Validate a bracketing triplet and normalize it to increasing-x order.
/// Returns (left, mid, right, f_mid).
fn normalize_bracket(br: Bracket) -> Result<(f64, f64, f64, f64), GoldenError> {
    let all = [br.a, br.b, br.c, br.fa, br.fb, br.fc];
    if all.iter().any(|v| !v.is_finite()) {
        return Err(GoldenError::NonFiniteInput("bracket fields must be finite"));
    }

    let inc = br.a < br.b && br.b < br.c;
    let dec = br.a > br.b && br.b > br.c;
    if !(inc || dec) {
        return Err(GoldenError::InvalidBracket(
            "bracket must satisfy a < b < c or a > b > c",
        ));
    }

    // Check (10.3.1): f(a) > f(b) < f(c).
    if !(br.fa > br.fb && br.fb < br.fc) {
        return Err(GoldenError::InvalidBracket(
            "bracket must satisfy f(a) > f(b) < f(c) (see (10.3.1))",
        ));
    }

    if inc {
        Ok((br.a, br.b, br.c, br.fb))
    } else {
        // Reverse to increasing-x order. The middle point remains br.b.
        Ok((br.c, br.b, br.a, br.fb))
    }
}

/// Golden-section search on a bracketing interval.
///
/// Inputs:
/// - f: objective function
/// - br: bracketing triplet satisfying (10.3.1)
/// - tol: absolute tolerance for the final interval width
/// - max_iters: cap on the number of reduction steps
///
/// Output:
/// - GoldenResult with final interval [left,right] and x_min guaranteed in that interval.
pub fn golden_section_search(
    f: &(dyn Fn(f64) -> f64),
    br: Bracket,
    tol: f64,
    max_iters: usize,
) -> Result<GoldenResult, GoldenError> {
    if !tol.is_finite() || tol <= 0.0 {
        return Err(GoldenError::NonFiniteInput("tol must be finite and > 0"));
    }
    if max_iters == 0 {
        return Err(GoldenError::NonFiniteInput("max_iters must be > 0"));
    }

    // Normalize bracket to an increasing-x interval [left, right].
    let (mut left, mid, mut right, f_mid) = normalize_bracket(br)?;

    // Golden ratio constants.
    let phi = (1.0 + 5.0_f64.sqrt()) * 0.5; // φ
    let inv_phi = 1.0 / phi;                // 1/φ ≈ 0.618...

    // Initial interior points.
    let mut x1 = right - inv_phi * (right - left);
    let mut x2 = left + inv_phi * (right - left);

    let mut evals = 0usize;

    let mut f1 = eval_f(f, x1)?;
    evals += 1;
    let mut f2 = eval_f(f, x2)?;
    evals += 1;

    let mut iters = 0usize;

    while (right - left) > tol && iters < max_iters {
        iters += 1;

        // Interval reduction logic (Section 10.3.1):
        // compare function values and discard the subinterval that cannot contain the minimizer.
        if f1 <= f2 {
            // Discard (x2, right): new interval is [left, x2].
            right = x2;

            // Reuse x1 as the new x2 and compute the new x1.
            x2 = x1;
            f2 = f1;

            x1 = right - inv_phi * (right - left);
            f1 = eval_f(f, x1)?;
            evals += 1;
        } else {
            // Discard (left, x1): new interval is [x1, right].
            left = x1;

            // Reuse x2 as the new x1 and compute the new x2.
            x1 = x2;
            f1 = f2;

            x2 = left + inv_phi * (right - left);
            f2 = eval_f(f, x2)?;
            evals += 1;
        }
    }

    // Choose a final estimate guaranteed inside [left, right].
    // The interior points x1 and x2 are always inside the current interval.
    let (mut x_best, mut f_best) = if f1 <= f2 { (x1, f1) } else { (x2, f2) };

    // Optionally consider the original bracketing middle point only if it lies inside.
    // This preserves consistency in floating-point arithmetic.
    if mid >= left && mid <= right && f_mid < f_best {
        x_best = mid;
        f_best = f_mid;
    }

    Ok(GoldenResult {
        x_min: x_best,
        f_min: f_best,
        left,
        right,
        evals,
        iters,
    })
}

fn main() {
    // Example objective functions. In practice, (a,b,c) would come from a bracketing routine
    // such as those developed in Section 10.2.

    // Convex quadratic with minimizer at x = 2.
    let f1 = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    // Mildly nonconvex objective with local basins.
    let f2 = |x: f64| (x - 1.5) * (x - 1.5) + 0.2 * (5.0 * x).cos();

    // Brackets chosen to satisfy (10.3.1). These could be produced by Program 10.2.3 or 10.2.4.
    let br1 = Bracket {
        a: 0.9256,
        b: 2.0,
        c: 2.629536,
        fa: f1(0.9256),
        fb: f1(2.0),
        fc: f1(2.629536),
    };

    let br2 = Bracket {
        a: 0.79048,
        b: 1.4538308698,
        c: 2.1536288,
        fa: f2(0.79048),
        fb: f2(1.4538308698),
        fc: f2(2.1536288),
    };

    let tol = 1e-8;
    let max_iters = 200;

    println!("Case: Convex quadratic");
    match golden_section_search(&f1, br1, tol, max_iters) {
        Ok(res) => {
            println!("{}", res);
            println!(
                "  interval encloses x_min: {}",
                res.left <= res.x_min && res.x_min <= res.right
            );
        }
        Err(e) => println!("  golden-section search failed: {:?}", e),
    }
    println!();

    println!("Case: Mildly nonconvex");
    match golden_section_search(&f2, br2, tol, max_iters) {
        Ok(res) => {
            println!("{}", res);
            println!(
                "  interval encloses x_min: {}",
                res.left <= res.x_min && res.x_min <= res.right
            );
        }
        Err(e) => println!("  golden-section search failed: {:?}", e),
    }
}
```

Program 10.3.1 demonstrates how golden-section search provides a reliable and conceptually simple mechanism for localizing a minimum once a valid bracket has been established. By discarding a fixed fraction of the interval at each step and reusing previously evaluated points, the method achieves steady convergence with minimal overhead and without requiring derivative information.

The examples highlight the strengths of golden-section search in both convex and mildly nonconvex settings. While the method does not exploit curvature information and therefore converges more slowly than higher-order techniques, its robustness and predictability make it an essential component of one-dimensional derivative-free optimization. In practice, golden-section search often serves as a dependable fallback or as a refinement stage following more aggressive bracketing or extrapolation strategies, ensuring accurate localization of minima under minimal assumptions.

## 10.3.2. Derivation of the Golden Ratio

Let the original interval length be $c-a$, and suppose the middle point $b$ divides the interval such that:

$$w = \frac{b-a}{c-a}, \quad 1-w = \frac{c-b}{c-a} \tag{10.3.2}$$

Without loss of generality, assume $w < \tfrac{1}{2}$, so the right subinterval $(b,c)$ is longer. The new evaluation point $x$ is chosen in this larger segment. Let,

$$z = \frac{x-b}{c-a} \tag{10.3.3}$$

If $f(x) \ge f(b)$, the remaining interval is $(a,x)$, whose relative length is $w+z$. If $f(x) < f(b)$, the remaining interval is $(b,c)$, whose relative length is $1-w$. To minimize the worst-case interval length, these two possibilities are set equal:

$$w + z = 1 - w \tag{10.3.4}$$

Additionally, the algorithm is designed to be *self-similar*, meaning that the relative position of the new interior point in the reduced interval should match the original relative position $w$. This leads to the condition:

$$\frac{z}{1-w} = w \tag{10.3.5}$$

Substituting $z = 1 - 2w$ from (10.3.4) into (10.3.5) gives:

$$\frac{1 - 2w}{1 - w} = w \tag{10.3.6}$$

Multiplying through and rearranging yields the quadratic equation:

$$w^2 - 3w + 1 = 0 \tag{10.3.7}$$

Solving this equation gives:

$$w = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}  \tag{10.3.8}$$

Only the solution in the interval $(0,1)$ is admissible:

$$w = \frac{3 - \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 0.381966 \tag{10.3.9}$$

The complementary fraction is:

$$1 - w \approx 0.618034 \tag{10.3.10}$$

These values are related to the *golden ratio,*

$$\varphi = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \tag{10.3.11}$$

The values obtained in (10.3.9)–(10.3.11) determine a unique subdivision of the interval with an important structural property. When the interval is reduced according to the rules of Section 10.3.1, the relative positions of the interior points remain unchanged. As a result, after the initial step, the same geometric configuration repeats at every iteration. This self-similarity ensures that only one new function evaluation is required per iteration and that the enclosing interval contracts by a fixed factor independent of the outcome of the comparison. These properties lead directly to the golden-section algorithm described in the next subsection.

### Rust Implementation

Following the derivation in Section 10.3.2 of the unique subdivision ratio determined by equations (10.3.7)–(10.3.11), Program 10.3.2 provides a concrete implementation of the golden-section construction and its use in one-dimensional minimization. The derivation shows that enforcing both equal worst-case interval reduction and self-similarity leads to a fixed pair of interior subdivision fractions related to the golden ratio. This program translates those theoretical constants into executable form and demonstrates how they give rise to an efficient bracketing algorithm that requires only one new function evaluation per iteration. By combining the explicit computation of the golden constants with a reusable golden-section search routine, the program illustrates how the abstract geometric arguments of Section 10.3.2 directly inform practical numerical algorithms.

At the core of the implementation is the `GoldenConstants` structure, which encapsulates the numerical values derived in equations (10.3.9)–(10.3.11). The golden ratio $\varphi$ from (10.3.11), the shorter subdivision fraction $w$ from (10.3.9), and its complement $1-w$ from (10.3.10) are computed explicitly from $\sqrt{5}$. Storing these values in a dedicated structure makes the mathematical origin of the constants transparent and avoids hard-coded numerical literals, ensuring that the implementation mirrors the analytical derivation.

The function `golden_section_minimize` implements the interval-reduction logic described in Section 10.3.1 using the constants obtained from Section 10.3.2. Given an initial bracket $(a,c)$, two interior points are placed according to the subdivision defined by $1-w$, which corresponds to the longer fraction identified in (10.3.10). At each iteration, the function compares the values $f(x_1)$ and $f(x_2)$ and discards the subinterval that cannot contain the minimizer. This decision logic reflects the worst-case interval comparison discussed around equation (10.3.4), while the reuse of one interior point at every step is a direct consequence of the self-similarity condition formalized in (10.3.5).

A key feature of the implementation is that only a single new function evaluation is required per iteration. When one subinterval is discarded, one of the interior points and its associated function value is reused, and only the second interior point is recomputed. This behavior is precisely the computational benefit guaranteed by the golden-section geometry established in Section 10.3.2. The loop terminates when the enclosing interval length falls below a prescribed tolerance or when a maximum iteration count is reached, ensuring both numerical reliability and bounded computational cost.

The `main` function serves a dual illustrative purpose. First, it verifies the numerical consistency of the constants by printing the values of $\varphi$, $w$, and $1-w$ and checking their expected algebraic relationships. Second, it demonstrates the golden-section algorithm on two representative objective functions. A simple quadratic function is used to confirm rapid and stable convergence to a known minimizer, while a smooth non-polynomial example illustrates that the method remains effective for more general unimodal functions. Together, these examples highlight how the abstract derivation of the golden ratio leads directly to a robust and efficient minimization procedure in finite-precision arithmetic.

```rust
// Program 10.3.2. Golden Ratio Constants and Golden-Section Search
//
// This program operationalizes the derivation in Section 10.3.2 by computing the unique
// subdivision fractions w and 1-w from (10.3.7)–(10.3.11), and then using them to run a
// golden-section minimization on a unimodal function over an interval (a, c).
//
// Mathematical context:
// - The interior subdivision fraction w is defined by (10.3.2) and is chosen to satisfy
//   the equal worst-case reduction condition (10.3.4) and the self-similarity condition (10.3.5).
// - Eliminating z using (10.3.4) and substituting into (10.3.5) yields (10.3.6), which rearranges
//   to the quadratic (10.3.7). The admissible root gives w in (10.3.9), and 1-w in (10.3.10).
// - The golden ratio phi in (10.3.11) is related to these constants, and the resulting geometry
//   ensures that each iteration needs only one new function evaluation.
//
// Notes on implementation:
// - We store r = 1 - w, which is the contraction factor (10.3.10). In standard form,
//   r = (sqrt(5) - 1)/2 = 1/phi, and w = 1 - r.
// - The algorithm maintains a bracket [a, c] and two interior points x1, x2 with cached values.
//   Each iteration discards one subinterval and reuses one cached evaluation.

use std::f64;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct GoldenConstants {
    /// Golden ratio phi = (1 + sqrt(5)) / 2 from (10.3.11)
    phi: f64,
    /// w = (3 - sqrt(5)) / 2 from (10.3.9), the shorter fraction
    w: f64,
    /// r = 1 - w from (10.3.10), the longer fraction and contraction factor
    r: f64,
}

impl GoldenConstants {
    fn new() -> Self {
        let sqrt5 = 5.0_f64.sqrt();

        // (10.3.11)
        let phi = (1.0 + sqrt5) / 2.0;

        // (10.3.9)
        let w = (3.0 - sqrt5) / 2.0;

        // (10.3.10)
        let r = 1.0 - w;

        Self { phi, w, r }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct GoldenResult {
    /// Approximate minimizer location
    x_min: f64,
    /// Function value at x_min
    f_min: f64,
    /// Final interval endpoints
    a: f64,
    c: f64,
    /// Number of iterations performed
    iters: usize,
}

/// Golden-section search for a unimodal function on [a, c].
///
/// Termination:
/// - Stops when interval length |c - a| <= tol, or when max_iter is reached.
///
/// Requirements:
/// - f should be unimodal on [a, c] for the bracketing logic to be valid.
fn golden_section_minimize<F>(
    f: F,
    mut a: f64,
    mut c: f64,
    tol: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> GoldenResult
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    assert!(c > a, "Require c > a.");
    assert!(tol > 0.0, "Require tol > 0.");
    assert!(max_iter > 0, "Require max_iter > 0.");

    let gc = GoldenConstants::new();

    // Two interior points in [a, c] chosen to preserve the self-similar configuration.
    // Using r = 1 - w, we can place:
    //   x1 = c - r (c - a)
    //   x2 = a + r (c - a)
    // This yields a symmetric reuse pattern where only one new point is introduced per step.
    let mut x1 = c - gc.r * (c - a);
    let mut x2 = a + gc.r * (c - a);

    let mut f1 = f(x1);
    let mut f2 = f(x2);

    let mut iters = 0usize;

    while (c - a).abs() > tol && iters < max_iter {
        iters += 1;
```
