```rust
        if f1 <= f2 {
            // Minimum lies in [a, x2]; discard [x2, c].
            // Reuse x1 (and f1); x2 becomes the old x1; introduce one new x1.
            c = x2;
            x2 = x1;
            f2 = f1;

            x1 = c - gc.r * (c - a);
            f1 = f(x1);
        } else {
            // Minimum lies in [x1, c]; discard [a, x1].
            // Reuse x2 (and f2); x1 becomes the old x2; introduce one new x2.
            a = x1;
            x1 = x2;
            f1 = f2;

            x2 = a + gc.r * (c - a);
            f2 = f(x2);
        }
    }

    // Choose the better of the two interior points as the reported minimizer.
    let (x_min, f_min) = if f1 <= f2 { (x1, f1) } else { (x2, f2) };

    GoldenResult {
        x_min,
        f_min,
        a,
        c,
        iters,
    }
}

fn main() {
    // 1) Compute constants from (10.3.9)–(10.3.11).
    let gc = GoldenConstants::new();
    println!("Golden constants derived from (10.3.9)–(10.3.11):");
    println!("  phi  = {:.15}", gc.phi);
    println!("  w    = {:.15}", gc.w);
    println!("  1-w  = {:.15}", gc.r);
    println!("  Check: w in (0,1) = {}", gc.w > 0.0 && gc.w < 1.0);
    println!("  Check: 1/phi      = {:.15}", 1.0 / gc.phi);
    println!("  Check: (1-w) - 1/phi = {:.3e}", gc.r - (1.0 / gc.phi));
    println!();

    // 2) Demonstrate golden-section minimization on a simple unimodal function.
    // Example A: f(x) = (x - 2)^2 + 1, minimum at x = 2.
    let f_quad = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    let res_a = golden_section_minimize(f_quad, -5.0, 7.0, 1e-10, 200);
    println!("Example A: f(x) = (x - 2)^2 + 1 on [-5, 7]");
    println!(
        "  x_min ≈ {:.12}, f_min ≈ {:.12}, iters = {}",
        res_a.x_min, res_a.f_min, res_a.iters
    );
    println!(
        "  final bracket: [{:.12}, {:.12}], length ≈ {:.3e}",
        res_a.a,
        res_a.c,
        (res_a.c - res_a.a).abs()
    );
    println!();

    // Example B: f(x) = (x - 0.3)^2 + 0.05*cos(8x), still unimodal on a small enough interval.
    // We choose an interval where the quadratic dominates and the function remains unimodal.
    let f_smooth = |x: f64| (x - 0.3) * (x - 0.3) + 0.05 * (8.0 * x).cos();

    let res_b = golden_section_minimize(f_smooth, -0.5, 1.0, 1e-10, 300);
    println!("Example B: f(x) = (x - 0.3)^2 + 0.05*cos(8x) on [-0.5, 1.0]");
    println!(
        "  x_min ≈ {:.12}, f_min ≈ {:.12}, iters = {}",
        res_b.x_min, res_b.f_min, res_b.iters
    );
    println!(
        "  final bracket: [{:.12}, {:.12}], length ≈ {:.3e}",
        res_b.a,
        res_b.c,
        (res_b.c - res_b.a).abs()
    );
}
```

Program 10.3.2 demonstrates how the derivation of the golden ratio in Section 10.3.2 translates seamlessly into an efficient numerical algorithm. By enforcing equal worst-case interval reduction and self-similarity, the golden-section method achieves predictable convergence behavior independent of the specific shape of the objective function, provided unimodality holds on the initial interval.

The numerical examples confirm the theoretical properties of the method. The quadratic test case illustrates steady contraction of the interval toward the exact minimizer, while the smooth non-polynomial example shows that the algorithm remains robust even when the objective function deviates from simple analytic forms. In both cases, the fixed contraction factor determined by (10.3.9)–(10.3.11) ensures reliable progress without the need for derivative information.

The modular structure of the implementation makes it straightforward to embed the golden-section search as a component in more sophisticated optimization frameworks. In later sections, this bracketing strategy can be combined with interpolation-based methods or hybrid global–local algorithms, where the golden-section search provides a dependable mechanism for interval reduction and localization of minima.

## 10.3.3. The Golden-Section Algorithm

With the interval-reduction logic established in Section 10.3.1 and the optimal subdivision ratio derived in Section 10.3.2, the golden-section algorithm can now be stated as a concrete iterative procedure. Starting from a bracketing triplet $(a,b,c)$ satisfying $f(a) > f(b) < f(c)$, the algorithm repeatedly applies the same reduction step, shrinking the enclosing interval $(a,c)$ while maintaining a valid bracket for the minimum.

At each iteration, a new interior point $x$ is introduced in the larger of the two subintervals $(a,b)$ or $(b,c)$. Its location is determined using the golden-section ratio so that the relative spacing of points matches the proportions derived previously. After the first iteration, the set of points $(a,b,x,c)$ naturally falls into the golden configuration, and all subsequent points can be generated by simple reuse and relabeling rather than recomputation from scratch.

The algorithm then compares the function values at the two interior points. If $f(x) \ge f(b)$, the interval $(b,c)$ is discarded and the new bracket becomes $(a,b,x)$. If instead $f(x) < f(b)$, the interval $(a,b)$ is discarded and the new bracket becomes $(b,x,c)$. In either case, the unimodal structure of the function on the reduced interval is preserved, and the minimum is guaranteed to remain enclosed.

A crucial practical feature of this construction is that only one new function evaluation is required per iteration. One of the interior points from the previous step becomes an endpoint or retained interior point in the next bracket, allowing its function value to be reused. This property is not incidental but follows directly from the self-similarity enforced by the golden ratio. It is this reuse of information that distinguishes golden-section search from naive interval subdivision schemes and makes it particularly efficient when function evaluations dominate computational cost.

Each iteration reduces the length of the enclosing interval by a fixed factor equal to $1-w \approx 0.618$. Consequently, the convergence is linear, with a contraction rate that is known *a priori* and does not depend on the smoothness, curvature, or scaling of the objective function. The method therefore provides a predictable and robust mechanism for localization once a bracket has been established.

Golden-section search terminates when the interval length $|c-a|$ falls below a prescribed tolerance or when successive function values become indistinguishable within machine precision. At termination, the best available interior point serves as an approximation to the minimizer. Although the method does not exploit derivative information and cannot achieve superlinear convergence, its reliability and simplicity make it a foundational algorithm in one-dimensional optimization and a natural building block for hybrid schemes such as Brent’s method.

### Rust Implementation

Following the formulation of the golden-section algorithm in Section 10.3.3, Program 10.3.3 provides a concrete implementation of the interval-reduction procedure derived in Sections 10.3.1 and 10.3.2. Once a valid bracketing triplet $(a,b,c)$ satisfying the unimodality condition has been established, the algorithm proceeds by repeatedly shrinking the enclosing interval using the fixed subdivision ratio determined by equations (10.3.9)–(10.3.11). This program translates the abstract geometric construction of the golden section into an explicit iterative method, demonstrating how self-similarity and information reuse lead to a robust and predictable minimization strategy. The implementation emphasizes the practical advantages of the method, in particular the fact that only a single new function evaluation is required at each iteration.

At the core of the implementation is the `GoldenConstants` structure, which encapsulates the numerical constants derived in Section 10.3.2. The golden ratio $\varphi$ from equation (10.3.11), the shorter subdivision fraction $w$ from equation (10.3.9), and its complement $1-w$ from equation (10.3.10) are computed explicitly from $\sqrt{5}$. Storing these values in a dedicated structure ensures that the algorithm remains tightly coupled to its analytical foundation and avoids reliance on opaque numerical literals.

The algorithm operates on a `Bracket` structure representing an initial triplet $(a,b,c)$ that satisfies the bracketing condition $f(a) > f(b) < f(c)$. This condition guarantees that the minimum lies within the interval $(a,c)$ and that the function is unimodal on this range. The constructor of the `Bracket` type explicitly evaluates and stores the function values at all three points, both to validate the bracketing condition and to make the initial state of the algorithm transparent.

The main computational routine, `golden_section_from_bracket`, implements the iterative reduction logic described in Section 10.3.3. From the initial endpoints $a$ and $c$, two interior points are placed according to the golden-section ratio defined by equation (10.3.10). At each iteration, the function values at these interior points are compared. If the value at the newly introduced point exceeds that at the retained interior point, the subinterval that cannot contain the minimum is discarded. Otherwise, the complementary subinterval is removed. In both cases, one interior point and its cached function value are reused, and only a single new function evaluation is performed in the next iteration.

This reuse of information is a direct consequence of the self-similarity condition derived in equation (10.3.5). After the first iteration, the remaining points always fall into the same golden configuration, allowing the algorithm to proceed by simple relabeling rather than recomputation. Each iteration reduces the length of the enclosing interval by the fixed factor $1-w \approx 0.618$, yielding linear convergence with a contraction rate that is known in advance and independent of the detailed shape of the objective function.

The `main` function demonstrates the algorithm on a simple unimodal test function with a known minimizer. It begins by constructing a valid bracketing triplet and then applies the golden-section search until the interval length $|c-a|$ falls below a prescribed tolerance. Diagnostic output reports the number of iterations, the total number of function evaluations, and the final interval width, making the convergence behavior explicit and allowing direct verification of the theoretical properties discussed in Section 10.3.3.

```rust
// Program 10.3.3. The Golden-Section Algorithm
//
// This program implements golden-section search starting from a bracketing triplet
// (a, b, c) satisfying f(a) > f(b) < f(c). It repeatedly shrinks the enclosing
// interval (a, c) using the golden ratio subdivision derived in Section 10.3.2,
// while maintaining a valid bracket for the minimum.
//
// Practical features emphasized in Section 10.3.3:
// - Only one new function evaluation per iteration, due to point reuse.
// - Fixed linear contraction factor equal to 1 - w ≈ 0.618 (Equation (10.3.10)).
// - Termination when |c - a| falls below a prescribed tolerance.

use std::f64;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct GoldenConstants {
    /// phi = (1 + sqrt(5)) / 2 (10.3.11)
    phi: f64,
    /// w = (3 - sqrt(5)) / 2 (10.3.9)
    w: f64,
    /// r = 1 - w (10.3.10), the contraction factor
    r: f64,
}

impl GoldenConstants {
    fn new() -> Self {
        let sqrt5 = 5.0_f64.sqrt();
        let phi = (1.0 + sqrt5) / 2.0; // (10.3.11)
        let w = (3.0 - sqrt5) / 2.0;   // (10.3.9)
        let r = 1.0 - w;               // (10.3.10)
        Self { phi, w, r }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct Bracket {
    a: f64,
    b: f64,
    c: f64,
    fa: f64,
    fb: f64,
    fc: f64,
}

impl Bracket {
    /// Construct and validate a bracketing triplet (a, b, c) with f(a) > f(b) < f(c).
    fn new<F>(f: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64) -> Self
    where
        F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
    {
        assert!(a < b && b < c, "Require a < b < c.");

        let fa = f(a);
        let fb = f(b);
        let fc = f(c);

        assert!(
            fa > fb && fc > fb,
            "Require bracketing condition f(a) > f(b) < f(c)."
        );

        Self { a, b, c, fa, fb, fc }
    }

    fn length(&self) -> f64 {
        self.c - self.a
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct GoldenResult {
    x_min: f64,
    f_min: f64,
    a: f64,
    c: f64,
    iters: usize,
    evals: usize,
}

/// Golden-section search from a valid bracketing triplet.
///
/// Termination (as stated in Section 10.3.3):
/// - Stop when |c - a| <= tol, or when max_iter is reached.
fn golden_section_from_bracket<F>(
    f: F,
    bracket: Bracket,
    tol: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> GoldenResult
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    assert!(tol > 0.0, "Require tol > 0.");
    assert!(max_iter > 0, "Require max_iter > 0.");

    let gc = GoldenConstants::new();

    let mut a = bracket.a;
    let mut c = bracket.c;

    // Establish the golden configuration with two interior points.
    // Using r = 1 - w (10.3.10), define:
    //   x1 = c - r(c - a)
    //   x2 = a + r(c - a)
    // giving a < x1 < x2 < c.
    let mut x1 = c - gc.r * (c - a);
    let mut x2 = a + gc.r * (c - a);

    let mut f1 = f(x1);
    let mut f2 = f(x2);

    // We already evaluated f at a, b, c when building the bracket, plus f at x1, x2 here.
    let mut evals = 5usize;

    let mut iters = 0usize;

    while (c - a).abs() > tol && iters < max_iter {
        iters += 1;

        if f1 <= f2 {
            // Minimum lies in [a, x2]; discard (x2, c).
            c = x2;

            // Reuse x1 as the new x2; only one new evaluation is needed.
            x2 = x1;
            f2 = f1;

            x1 = c - gc.r * (c - a);
            f1 = f(x1);
            evals += 1;
        } else {
            // Minimum lies in [x1, c]; discard (a, x1).
            a = x1;

            // Reuse x2 as the new x1; only one new evaluation is needed.
            x1 = x2;
            f1 = f2;

            x2 = a + gc.r * (c - a);
            f2 = f(x2);
            evals += 1;
        }
    }

    let (x_min, f_min) = if f1 <= f2 { (x1, f1) } else { (x2, f2) };

    GoldenResult {
        x_min,
        f_min,
        a,
        c,
        iters,
        evals,
    }
}

fn main() {
    // Example objective function: unimodal quadratic with exact minimum at x = 2.
    let f = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    // Starting bracketing triplet (a, b, c) with f(a) > f(b) < f(c).
    let a = -1.0;
    let b = 2.5;
    let c = 6.0;

    let bracket = Bracket::new(&f, a, b, c);

    // Stopping parameters.
    let tol = 1e-10;
    let max_iter = 500;

    let res = golden_section_from_bracket(f, bracket, tol, max_iter);

    println!("Program 10.3.3: Golden-Section Algorithm");
    println!("Initial bracketing triplet (a, b, c) with f(a) > f(b) < f(c):");
    println!(
        "  a = {:.6}, f(a) = {:.6}",
        bracket.a, bracket.fa
    );
    println!(
        "  b = {:.6}, f(b) = {:.6}",
        bracket.b, bracket.fb
    );
    println!(
        "  c = {:.6}, f(c) = {:.6}",
        bracket.c, bracket.fc
    );
    println!("  initial length |c - a| = {:.6}", bracket.length());
    println!();

    println!("Result:");
    println!("  x_min ≈ {:.12}", res.x_min);
    println!("  f_min ≈ {:.12}", res.f_min);
    println!("  iterations = {}", res.iters);
    println!("  function evaluations = {}", res.evals);
    println!(
        "  final interval: [{:.12}, {:.12}], length ≈ {:.3e}",
        res.a,
        res.c,
        (res.c - res.a).abs()
    );

    let gc = GoldenConstants::new();
    println!();
    println!("Golden constants (Section 10.3.2):");
    println!("  phi = {:.15}", gc.phi);
    println!("  w   = {:.15}", gc.w);
    println!("  1-w = {:.15}  (fixed contraction factor)", gc.r);
}
```

Program 10.3.3 illustrates the golden-section algorithm as a practical realization of the interval-reduction principles developed in Sections 10.3.1 and 10.3.2. By enforcing a fixed subdivision ratio derived from the golden ratio, the method achieves steady and predictable contraction of the search interval without requiring derivative information or higher-order smoothness assumptions.

The numerical example confirms the theoretical characteristics of the algorithm. The enclosing interval shrinks by a constant factor at each iteration, and the total number of function evaluations grows linearly with the number of iterations, reflecting the reuse of previously computed values. Although the convergence is only linear, the reliability and simplicity of the method make it an effective localization tool once a bracket has been established.

The modular structure of the implementation makes the golden-section search a natural building block for more advanced one-dimensional optimization techniques. In particular, hybrid algorithms such as Brent’s method combine golden-section steps with interpolation-based acceleration, retaining the robustness of the golden-section framework while achieving faster convergence when additional smoothness information can be exploited.

## 10.3.4. Convergence and Optimality

Golden-section search converges linearly, with the length of the enclosing interval reduced by a constant factor at each iteration. This rate is slower than that of methods exhibiting superlinear or quadratic convergence, such as Newton’s method or other derivative-based schemes. The difference reflects a fundamental trade-off: faster convergence rates typically rely on access to derivative information and on stronger smoothness assumptions, whereas golden-section search operates using only function evaluations and requires no information beyond unimodality on the bracketing interval.

This modest convergence rate is offset by a high degree of robustness. Because the algorithm does not depend on gradients or curvature, it remains reliable in situations where derivatives are unavailable, expensive to compute, contaminated by noise, or poorly scaled. The predictable contraction of the interval also makes its behavior easy to analyze and control, which is particularly valuable in black-box optimization settings.

From a theoretical standpoint, golden-section search enjoys a strong optimality property. It can be shown that, among all derivative-free methods that rely solely on sequential function evaluations and assume only unimodality, no algorithm can guarantee a better worst-case reduction of the enclosing interval per function evaluation. In this minimax sense, the contraction factor derived from the golden ratio is optimal. Any alternative choice of subdivision ratio would lead to a larger worst-case remaining interval after one iteration, regardless of how the function behaves within the unimodal class.

Recent research has investigated ways to improve upon classical golden-section search by introducing additional assumptions about the objective function. When convexity is known in advance, modified line search strategies can exploit this structure to achieve faster convergence rates than those guaranteed by unimodality alone (Orseau and Hutter, 2023). Other work has examined alternative sectioning ratios tailored to specific subclasses of unimodal functions, reporting reductions in the number of function evaluations required to reach a prescribed accuracy (Kodnyanko, 2025). These developments do not contradict the optimality of the golden-section method in the minimax setting but instead illustrate how additional structural information can be leveraged to go beyond its classical guarantees.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.3.4 on the convergence properties and optimality of golden-section search, Program 10.3.4 provides a concrete numerical demonstration of these theoretical results. While the preceding sections establish that golden-section search converges linearly with a fixed contraction factor determined by the golden ratio, this program makes that behavior explicit by tracking the evolution of the enclosing interval across iterations. In addition, the program contrasts golden-section search with alternative derivative-free interval subdivision schemes to illustrate why the golden ratio yields an optimal worst-case reduction per function evaluation under the sole assumption of unimodality. The implementation therefore serves not as a new algorithmic variant, but as a diagnostic and explanatory tool that connects abstract convergence theory directly to observable numerical behavior.

At the core of the implementation is the `GoldenConstants` structure, which encodes the subdivision parameters derived in Section 10.3.2. The values of the golden ratio $\varphi$ from equation (10.3.11), the shorter fraction $w$ from equation (10.3.9), and the contraction factor $1-w$ from equation (10.3.10) are computed explicitly and reused throughout the program. Making these constants explicit ensures that the observed convergence behavior can be directly traced back to the analytical derivation of the golden-section ratio.

The program operates on a validated `Bracket` structure representing an initial triplet ((a,b,c)) that satisfies the unimodality condition $f(a) > f(b) < f(c)$. This guarantees that the minimum lies within the enclosing interval $(a,c)$ and that interval reduction steps preserve correctness. The stored function values at the bracket points are also used to account accurately for the total number of function evaluations, which is central to the optimality discussion in Section 10.3.4.

The function `golden_section_trace` implements golden-section search exactly as described in Section 10.3.3, while additionally recording the length of the enclosing interval at each iteration. By storing these lengths, the program is able to compute an empirical contraction ratio and compare it with the theoretical factor $1-w$ from equation (10.3.10). Because only one new function evaluation is performed per iteration after initialization, the measured contraction rate corresponds directly to contraction per function evaluation, which is the quantity of interest in the minimax optimality analysis.

To provide a meaningful point of comparison, the program also implements a family of symmetric two-point sectioning methods in the function `two_point_section_trace`. These methods evaluate the objective function at two newly chosen interior points on every iteration and discard one subinterval based on the comparison. Although such schemes can achieve stronger contraction per iteration for suitable choices of the subdivision parameter, they require two function evaluations per iteration. The program therefore reports both the observed contraction per iteration and the implied contraction per function evaluation, making explicit the trade-off discussed in Section 10.3.4.

The `main` function orchestrates the comparison by applying golden-section search and several two-point schemes to the same unimodal test function and initial bracket. It reports iteration counts, function evaluation counts, final interval lengths, and average contraction ratios. This side-by-side comparison illustrates that, while alternative schemes may appear competitive when measured per iteration, golden-section search achieves a smaller worst-case remaining interval per function evaluation, consistent with its theoretical optimality among unimodal, derivative-free methods.

```rust
// Program 10.3.4. Convergence and Optimality of Golden-Section Search
//
// This program illustrates the linear convergence of golden-section search and the
// optimality of its contraction factor in the minimax, derivative-free unimodal setting
// described in Section 10.3.4.
//
// It compares golden-section search (one new evaluation per iteration after initialization)
// against a family of symmetric two-point sectioning schemes that use two new evaluations
// per iteration, highlighting contraction per function evaluation.

use std::f64;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct GoldenConstants {
    /// phi = (1 + sqrt(5)) / 2 (10.3.11)
    phi: f64,
    /// w = (3 - sqrt(5)) / 2 (10.3.9)
    w: f64,
    /// r = 1 - w (10.3.10), the contraction factor
    r: f64,
}

impl GoldenConstants {
    fn new() -> Self {
        let sqrt5 = 5.0_f64.sqrt();
        let phi = (1.0 + sqrt5) / 2.0; // (10.3.11)
        let w = (3.0 - sqrt5) / 2.0;   // (10.3.9)
        let r = 1.0 - w;               // (10.3.10)
        Self { phi, w, r }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct Bracket {
    a: f64,
    b: f64,
    c: f64,
    fa: f64,
    fb: f64,
    fc: f64,
}

impl Bracket {
    fn new<F>(f: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64) -> Self
    where
        F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
    {
        assert!(a < b && b < c, "Require a < b < c.");

        let fa = f(a);
        let fb = f(b);
        let fc = f(c);

        assert!(
            fa > fb && fc > fb,
            "Require bracketing condition f(a) > f(b) < f(c)."
        );

        Self { a, b, c, fa, fb, fc }
    }

    fn length(&self) -> f64 {
        self.c - self.a
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct Trace {
    lengths: Vec<f64>,
    iters: usize,
    evals: usize,
    x_min: f64,
    f_min: f64,
    a: f64,
    c: f64,
}

fn golden_section_trace<F>(f: F, bracket: Bracket, tol: f64, max_iter: usize) -> Trace
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    assert!(tol > 0.0);
    assert!(max_iter > 0);

    let gc = GoldenConstants::new();
    let mut a = bracket.a;
    let mut c = bracket.c;

    let mut x1 = c - gc.r * (c - a);
    let mut x2 = a + gc.r * (c - a);
    let mut f1 = f(x1);
    let mut f2 = f(x2);

    let mut evals = 5usize; // a,b,c plus x1,x2
    let mut iters = 0usize;

    let mut lengths = Vec::with_capacity(max_iter + 1);
    lengths.push((c - a).abs());

    while (c - a).abs() > tol && iters < max_iter {
        iters += 1;

        if f1 <= f2 {
            c = x2;
            x2 = x1;
            f2 = f1;

            x1 = c - gc.r * (c - a);
            f1 = f(x1);
            evals += 1;
        } else {
            a = x1;
            x1 = x2;
            f1 = f2;

            x2 = a + gc.r * (c - a);
            f2 = f(x2);
            evals += 1;
        }

        lengths.push((c - a).abs());
    }

    let (x_min, f_min) = if f1 <= f2 { (x1, f1) } else { (x2, f2) };

    Trace {
        lengths,
        iters,
        evals,
        x_min,
        f_min,
        a,
        c,
    }
}

fn two_point_section_trace<F>(
    f: F,
    bracket: Bracket,
    alpha: f64,
    tol: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> Trace
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    assert!(alpha > 0.0 && alpha < 0.5, "Require alpha in (0, 1/2).");
    assert!(tol > 0.0);
    assert!(max_iter > 0);

    let mut a = bracket.a;
    let mut c = bracket.c;

    let mut evals = 3usize; // a,b,c already evaluated in bracket
    let mut iters = 0usize;

    let mut lengths = Vec::with_capacity(max_iter + 1);
    lengths.push((c - a).abs());

    let mut best_x = bracket.b;
    let mut best_f = bracket.fb;

    while (c - a).abs() > tol && iters < max_iter {
        iters += 1;

        let x1 = a + alpha * (c - a);
        let x2 = c - alpha * (c - a);

        let f1 = f(x1);
        let f2 = f(x2);
        evals += 2;

        if f1 <= f2 {
            c = x2;
            if f1 < best_f {
                best_f = f1;
                best_x = x1;
            }
        } else {
            a = x1;
            if f2 < best_f {
                best_f = f2;
                best_x = x2;
            }
        }

        lengths.push((c - a).abs());
    }

    Trace {
        lengths,
        iters,
        evals,
        x_min: best_x,
        f_min: best_f,
        a,
        c,
    }
}

fn average_contraction_ratio(lengths: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    if lengths.len() < 2 {
        return f64::NAN;
    }
    let mut sum = 0.0;
    let mut count = 0usize;
    for k in 0..(lengths.len() - 1) {
        let l0 = lengths[k];
        let l1 = lengths[k + 1];
        if l0 > 0.0 && l1 > 0.0 {
            sum += l1 / l0;
            count += 1;
        }
    }
    if count == 0 { f64::NAN } else { sum / (count as f64) }
}

fn main() {
    let f = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    let bracket = Bracket::new(&f, -1.0, 2.5, 6.0);

    let tol = 1e-10;
    let max_iter = 500;

    let gc = GoldenConstants::new();
    let golden = golden_section_trace(f, bracket, tol, max_iter);

    let alphas = [0.10, 0.25, 0.40, 0.49];

    println!("Program 10.3.4: Convergence and Optimality");
    println!();

    println!("Golden constants from (10.3.9)–(10.3.11):");
    println!("  phi  (10.3.11) = {:.15}", gc.phi);
    println!("  w    (10.3.9)  = {:.15}", gc.w);
    println!("  1-w  (10.3.10) = {:.15}", gc.r);
    println!("  check: (1/phi) - (1-w) = {:.3e}", (1.0 / gc.phi) - gc.r);
    println!();

    println!("Initial bracket:");
    println!("  a = {:.6}, f(a) = {:.6}", bracket.a, bracket.fa);
    println!("  b = {:.6}, f(b) = {:.6}", bracket.b, bracket.fb);
    println!("  c = {:.6}, f(c) = {:.6}", bracket.c, bracket.fc);
    println!("  initial length |c-a| = {:.6}", bracket.length());
    println!();

    let golden_avg = average_contraction_ratio(&golden.lengths);
    println!("Golden-section search (one new evaluation per iteration):");
    println!("  theoretical contraction factor (10.3.10): 1-w = {:.15}", gc.r);
    println!("  observed average contraction ratio:        {:.15}", golden_avg);
    println!("  iterations = {}", golden.iters);
    println!("  function evaluations = {}", golden.evals);
    println!("  x_min ≈ {:.12}, f_min ≈ {:.12}", golden.x_min, golden.f_min);
    println!(
        "  final interval length ≈ {:.3e}",
        (golden.c - golden.a).abs()
    );
    println!();

    println!("Two-point sectioning (two new evaluations per iteration):");
    for &alpha in &alphas {
        let run = two_point_section_trace(f, bracket, alpha, tol, max_iter);
        let avg = average_contraction_ratio(&run.lengths);

        let per_iter = 1.0 - alpha;
        let per_eval_effective = per_iter.sqrt();

        println!("  alpha = {:.2}", alpha);
        println!("    worst-case contraction per iteration: 1-alpha = {:.6}", per_iter);
        println!("    effective per-evaluation factor:      sqrt(1-alpha) = {:.6}", per_eval_effective);
        println!("    observed average contraction ratio:   {:.6}", avg);
        println!("    iterations = {}, evaluations = {}", run.iters, run.evals);
        println!(
            "    final interval length ≈ {:.3e}, x_min ≈ {:.12}",
            (run.c - run.a).abs(),
            run.x_min
        );
    }

    println!();
    println!("Interpretation:");
    println!("  Golden-section contracts by about {:.3} per new function evaluation.", gc.r);
    println!("  In the symmetric two-point family, each iteration costs two evaluations, so the");
    println!("  effective per-evaluation contraction is sqrt(1-alpha), which remains larger than");
    println!("  the golden value even as alpha approaches 1/2.");
}
```

Program 10.3.4 provides empirical confirmation of the convergence and optimality properties of golden-section search discussed in Section 10.3.4. The recorded interval lengths demonstrate clear linear convergence, with the enclosing interval shrinking by a constant factor that closely matches the theoretical value derived from the golden ratio. This predictable contraction behavior explains both the robustness and the analytical transparency of the method.

The comparison with symmetric two-point sectioning schemes highlights the essential optimality result. Although such methods can achieve more aggressive interval reduction per iteration, their reliance on two new function evaluations per step leads to inferior worst-case contraction when measured per evaluation. In contrast, golden-section search balances interval reduction and information reuse in a way that is provably optimal under unimodality alone.

These results reinforce the role of golden-section search as a foundational algorithm in one-dimensional optimization. While additional assumptions such as convexity or smoothness can be exploited to obtain faster convergence, the golden-section method remains a benchmark for robustness and efficiency in black-box settings, and a natural reference point for evaluating more sophisticated line-search strategies.

## 10.3.5. Stopping Criteria and Numerical Precision

Golden-section search reduces the interval width monotonically. A natural stopping criterion is therefore based on the interval length:

$$|c - a| < \tau (|b| + 1), \tag{10.3.12}$$

where $\tau$ is a prescribed tolerance.

However, numerical precision imposes practical limits on how accurately the minimizer can be located. Near a smooth minimum at $b$, a Taylor expansion gives:

$$f(b + h) \approx f(b) + \tfrac{1}{2} f''(b) h^2 \tag{10.3.15}$$

When $h^2$ becomes comparable to machine epsilon, further reductions in the interval no longer yield meaningful improvements in $f(b)$. In double precision arithmetic, this typically limits reliable localization of the minimizer to roughly eight decimal digits in $x$.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.3.5 on stopping criteria and the role of numerical precision in golden-section search, Program 10.3.5 provides a practical implementation that makes these considerations explicit. While golden-section search guarantees monotonic reduction of the enclosing interval, the choice of when to terminate the iteration has a direct impact on both efficiency and numerical reliability. This program implements an interval-based stopping rule tied to the scale of the minimizer and supplements it with a precision-aware safeguard that reflects the limitations of finite-precision arithmetic. Together, these mechanisms illustrate how theoretically motivated stopping conditions interact with floating-point effects in practical optimization.

At the core of the implementation is the same golden-section interval-reduction logic developed in Sections 10.3.2 and 10.3.3, combined with an explicit stopping test based on equation (10.3.12). The algorithm monitors the length of the enclosing interval $(a,c)$ and terminates once this length becomes small relative to the scale of the current minimizer estimate. By scaling the tolerance with $|b|+1$, the stopping condition adapts naturally to problems where the minimizer is far from the origin, avoiding overly strict absolute tolerances in large-scale settings.

The main search routine, `golden_section_with_stops`, applies this interval-based criterion as its primary termination condition. As in earlier implementations, the golden-section ratio ensures that only one new function evaluation is required per iteration after initialization, preserving the efficiency advantages of the method. The algorithm always prefers this mathematically defined stopping rule, ensuring that the convergence guarantees of golden-section search remain intact whenever the requested tolerance is compatible with floating-point resolution.

To illustrate the effects of numerical precision discussed around equation (10.3.15), the implementation also includes a secondary precision-aware stopping condition. Near a smooth minimum, improvements in the function value scale quadratically with the displacement $h$, as indicated by the Taylor expansion in (10.3.15). When $h^2$ becomes comparable to machine epsilon, further reductions in the interval no longer produce representable improvements in the function value. The program detects this regime by comparing the interval width to a scale proportional to $\sqrt{\varepsilon}$, where $\varepsilon$ denotes machine epsilon for double-precision arithmetic.

The `StopReason` enumeration records whether termination occurred due to the interval criterion (10.3.12), a precision-limited regime implied by (10.3.15), or an iteration cap. This explicit classification allows the user to distinguish between mathematically sufficient convergence and convergence limited by floating-point effects. The diagnostic output printed at the end of the run further reinforces this interpretation by comparing the final interval width and its square to machine epsilon.

The `main` function demonstrates these ideas on a smooth quadratic test problem with a well-conditioned minimum. By varying the tolerance parameter $\tau$, one can observe two distinct behaviors: for moderate tolerances, the algorithm terminates according to the interval-length condition, while for extremely small tolerances it halts at the precision limit, illustrating the practical bound on achievable localization in double precision. This makes the abstract discussion of numerical precision in Section 10.3.5 directly observable in a concrete computational setting.

```rust
// Program 10.3.5. Stopping Criteria and Numerical Precision in Golden-Section Search
//
// This program implements golden-section search with the interval-based stopping criterion
// in Equation (10.3.12):
//
//   |c - a| < tau (|b| + 1).   (10.3.12)
//
// It also includes a secondary stopping condition that illustrates the practical limit
// imposed by floating-point arithmetic near a smooth minimum. Using the Taylor model
// in Equation (10.3.15),
//
//   f(b + h) ≈ f(b) + (1/2) f''(b) h^2,   (10.3.15)
//
// meaningful improvements in f(b) cease once h^2 is comparable to machine epsilon. In
// double precision this corresponds to h on the order of sqrt(EPSILON), which typically
// limits reliable localization of x to about eight decimal digits.
//
// Important design choice:
// - The interval criterion (10.3.12) is always checked first.
// - The precision-based stop is only allowed to trigger when tau is set below the
//   representable x-resolution scale ~ sqrt(EPSILON), so it does not prematurely stop
//   ordinary runs.

use std::f64;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct GoldenConstants {
    /// phi = (1 + sqrt(5)) / 2 (10.3.11)
    phi: f64,
    /// w = (3 - sqrt(5)) / 2 (10.3.9)
    w: f64,
    /// r = 1 - w (10.3.10), the contraction factor
    r: f64,
}

impl GoldenConstants {
    fn new() -> Self {
        let sqrt5 = 5.0_f64.sqrt();
        let phi = (1.0 + sqrt5) / 2.0; // (10.3.11)
        let w = (3.0 - sqrt5) / 2.0;   // (10.3.9)
        let r = 1.0 - w;               // (10.3.10)
        Self { phi, w, r }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct Bracket {
    a: f64,
    b: f64,
    c: f64,
    fa: f64,
    fb: f64,
    fc: f64,
}

impl Bracket {
    /// Construct and validate a bracketing triplet (a, b, c) with f(a) > f(b) < f(c).
    fn new<F>(f: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64) -> Self
    where
        F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
    {
        assert!(a < b && b < c, "Require a < b < c.");

        let fa = f(a);
        let fb = f(b);
        let fc = f(c);

        assert!(
            fa > fb && fc > fb,
            "Require bracketing condition f(a) > f(b) < f(c)."
        );

        Self { a, b, c, fa, fb, fc }
    }

    fn length(&self) -> f64 {
        self.c - self.a
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
enum StopReason {
    IntervalCriterion, // (10.3.12) satisfied
    PrecisionLimit,    // tau below ~sqrt(EPSILON) scale, further x-localization is not meaningful
    MaxIterations,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct GoldenPrecisionResult {
    x_min: f64,
    f_min: f64,
    a: f64,
    c: f64,
    iters: usize,
    evals: usize,
    stop_reason: StopReason,
}

/// Golden-section search from a valid bracketing triplet (a, b, c).
///
/// Primary stop: interval length criterion (10.3.12):
///   |c - a| < tau (|b| + 1)
///
/// Secondary stop (precision limit illustration):
/// - If tau is set smaller than sqrt(EPSILON), the interval criterion may demand a width
///   that cannot be resolved meaningfully in double precision.
/// - In that case, the algorithm stops once |c - a| is below ~sqrt(EPSILON) (|b| + 1),
///   which corresponds to the scale implied by (10.3.15).
fn golden_section_with_stops<F>(
    f: F,
    bracket: Bracket,
    tau: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> GoldenPrecisionResult
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    assert!(tau > 0.0, "Require tau > 0.");
    assert!(max_iter > 0, "Require max_iter > 0.");

    let gc = GoldenConstants::new();
    let sqrt_eps = f64::EPSILON.sqrt();

    let mut a = bracket.a;
    let mut c = bracket.c;

    // Golden configuration (a, x1, x2, c) with r = 1 - w (10.3.10).
    let mut x1 = c - gc.r * (c - a);
    let mut x2 = a + gc.r * (c - a);
    let mut f1 = f(x1);
    let mut f2 = f(x2);

    // Total evaluations: f(a), f(b), f(c) computed in Bracket::new plus f(x1), f(x2).
    let mut evals = 5usize;
    let mut iters = 0usize;

    // Best-so-far (for reporting and stable output).
    let mut best_x = bracket.b;
    let mut best_f = bracket.fb;

    loop {
        let (cand_x, cand_f) = if f1 <= f2 { (x1, f1) } else { (x2, f2) };
        if cand_f < best_f {
            best_f = cand_f;
            best_x = cand_x;
        }

        // Use the current best interior point as b in (10.3.12).
        let b_for_stop = best_x;
        let interval = (c - a).abs();
        let scale_x = b_for_stop.abs() + 1.0;

        // Primary stopping criterion (10.3.12).
        if interval < tau * scale_x {
            return GoldenPrecisionResult {
                x_min: best_x,
                f_min: best_f,
                a,
                c,
                iters,
                evals,
                stop_reason: StopReason::IntervalCriterion,
            };
        }

        // Precision-limited stopping: only relevant when tau is smaller than sqrt(EPSILON).
        // This corresponds to the (10.3.15) discussion that improvements in f(b) scale like h^2,
        // so x-localization beyond h ~ sqrt(EPSILON) becomes numerically meaningless.
        if tau < sqrt_eps && interval < sqrt_eps * scale_x {
            return GoldenPrecisionResult {
                x_min: best_x,
                f_min: best_f,
                a,
                c,
                iters,
                evals,
                stop_reason: StopReason::PrecisionLimit,
            };
        }

        if iters >= max_iter {
            return GoldenPrecisionResult {
                x_min: best_x,
                f_min: best_f,
                a,
                c,
                iters,
                evals,
                stop_reason: StopReason::MaxIterations,
            };
        }

        // One golden-section iteration (one new evaluation due to reuse).
        iters += 1;

        if f1 <= f2 {
            // Minimum lies in [a, x2]; discard (x2, c).
            c = x2;

            // Reuse x1 as the new x2.
            x2 = x1;
            f2 = f1;

            // Introduce one new interior point.
            x1 = c - gc.r * (c - a);
            f1 = f(x1);
            evals += 1;
        } else {
            // Minimum lies in [x1, c]; discard (a, x1).
            a = x1;

            // Reuse x2 as the new x1.
            x1 = x2;
            f1 = f2;

            // Introduce one new interior point.
            x2 = a + gc.r * (c - a);
            f2 = f(x2);
            evals += 1;
        }
    }
}

fn main() {
    // Smooth test function with a well-conditioned minimum at x = 2.
    // For this quadratic, the Taylor model in (10.3.15) has the exact scaling
    // f(b + h) - f(b) = h^2, so the precision discussion is easy to observe.
    let f = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    // Bracketing triplet (a, b, c) with f(a) > f(b) < f(c).
    let bracket = Bracket::new(&f, -1.0, 2.5, 6.0);

    // Choose tau for (10.3.12). For illustration:
    // - tau = 1e-8   often matches the practical ~8 decimal digit x-localization limit.
    // - tau = 1e-12  is smaller than that practical limit; the precision stop may trigger.
    let tau = 1e-12;
    let max_iter = 2000;

    let gc = GoldenConstants::new();
    println!("Program 10.3.5: Stopping Criteria and Numerical Precision");
    println!();

    println!("Golden constants (Section 10.3.2):");
    println!("  phi  (10.3.11) = {:.15}", gc.phi);
    println!("  w    (10.3.9)  = {:.15}", gc.w);
    println!("  1-w  (10.3.10) = {:.15}", gc.r);
    println!("  sqrt(EPSILON)  ≈ {:.3e}", f64::EPSILON.sqrt());
    println!();

    println!("Stopping criterion (10.3.12): |c - a| < tau (|b| + 1)");
    println!("  tau = {:.3e}", tau);
    println!();

    println!("Initial bracket:");
    println!("  a = {:.6}, f(a) = {:.6}", bracket.a, bracket.fa);
    println!("  b = {:.6}, f(b) = {:.6}", bracket.b, bracket.fb);
    println!("  c = {:.6}, f(c) = {:.6}", bracket.c, bracket.fc);
    println!("  initial length |c-a| = {:.6}", bracket.length());
    println!();

    let res = golden_section_with_stops(f, bracket, tau, max_iter);

    println!("Result:");
    println!("  x_min ≈ {:.15}", res.x_min);
    println!("  f_min ≈ {:.15}", res.f_min);
    println!("  iterations = {}", res.iters);
    println!("  function evaluations = {}", res.evals);
    println!(
        "  final interval: [{:.15}, {:.15}], length ≈ {:.3e}",
        res.a,
        res.c,
        (res.c - res.a).abs()
    );
    println!("  stop reason = {:?}", res.stop_reason);

    // Precision diagnostic related to (10.3.15):
    // Use h as half the final interval width as a characteristic localization scale.
    let h = 0.5 * (res.c - res.a).abs();
    let eps = f64::EPSILON;
    println!();
    println!("Precision diagnostic (related to (10.3.15)):");
    println!("  h ≈ half-interval length ≈ {:.3e}", h);
    println!("  h^2 ≈ {:.3e}", h * h);
    println!("  machine epsilon (f64::EPSILON) ≈ {:.3e}", eps);
    println!("  h^2 / EPSILON ≈ {:.3e}", (h * h) / eps);

    // If h is below ~sqrt(EPSILON) * (|b| + 1), then by (10.3.15) the expected
    // improvement in f(b) is on the order of EPSILON or smaller, and additional
    // reductions will not yield meaningful changes in f within double precision.
}
```

Program 10.3.5 demonstrates how stopping criteria in golden-section search must balance theoretical convergence guarantees with the realities of finite-precision arithmetic. The interval-based condition derived from equation (10.3.12) provides a natural and scale-aware termination rule that aligns closely with the geometric structure of the algorithm. When the requested tolerance is compatible with floating-point resolution, this criterion ensures predictable and reliable localization of the minimizer.

The additional precision-aware stopping mechanism highlights an important practical limitation. As the interval width approaches the scale implied by equation (10.3.15), further reductions cease to yield meaningful improvements in the objective function value. This behavior explains why, in double-precision arithmetic, the minimizer can typically be localized to only a finite number of significant digits, regardless of how aggressively the interval is reduced.

By making both stopping mechanisms explicit and observable, the program reinforces the broader lesson that numerical optimization algorithms must be designed with careful attention to stopping conditions. Effective termination criteria are not merely a matter of convenience but are essential for achieving reliable results in finite-precision environments.

## 10.3.6. Practical Relevance

Despite being outperformed on smooth and well-behaved problems by interpolation-based methods such as Brent’s method, golden-section search remains an important and widely used tool in practice. Its value lies not in asymptotic speed, but in reliability. Because it relies solely on function evaluations and assumes only unimodality on the search interval, it continues to perform predictably in situations where derivative information is unavailable, unreliable, or prohibitively expensive to obtain.

Golden-section search is particularly well suited to problems involving noisy objective functions, where small perturbations can render derivative estimates meaningless or destabilize interpolation-based schemes. It is also attractive when each function evaluation is costly, for example when the objective involves a large simulation, a numerical solution of differential equations, or an external black-box model. In such settings, the algorithm’s strict control over the number of function evaluations and its guaranteed interval contraction provide a clear and transparent trade-off between accuracy and computational effort.

More broadly, golden-section search illustrates a central principle of numerical optimization: carefully chosen sampling strategies can reduce uncertainty in a systematic and provably optimal way, even in the absence of detailed information about the objective function. The use of self-similar interval reduction and information reuse anticipates ideas that recur in more advanced algorithms, including safeguarded line searches, trust-region methods, and hybrid schemes that combine global robustness with local acceleration. For this reason, golden-section search remains not only a practical method in its own right, but also a conceptual foundation for much of modern one-dimensional optimization.

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/I158BwDJPeDng8B0r3Nt.5","tags":[]}

# 10.4. Parabolic Interpolation and Brent’s Method in One Dimension

In one-dimensional optimization without derivatives, the objective is to locate the minimum of a unimodal function efficiently within a bounded interval. It is assumed that a minimum has already been bracketed, that is, points $a<b<c$ have been found such that:

$$f(b) < f(a), \qquad f(b) < f(c) \tag{10.4.1}$$

Golden-section search provides a robust mechanism for reducing such an interval and is guaranteed to converge even for poorly behaved functions. However, it is deliberately conservative, effectively assuming a worst-case functional shape and reducing the interval at a fixed rate of approximately $0.618$ per iteration. In many practical situations, the objective function is smooth near the minimum and is well approximated locally by a quadratic. In such cases, convergence can be accelerated by exploiting this structure rather than shrinking the interval pessimistically at a constant ratio.

This observation motivates *inverse parabolic interpolation*, in which a parabola is fitted through three sampled points of the objective function and the vertex of that parabola is used as a prediction for the location of the minimum. If the function is exactly quadratic, a single parabolic step can almost identify the minimizer exactly. Even when the function is only approximately quadratic, such interpolation often yields a much better estimate than a fixed-ratio interval reduction.

## 10.4.1. Mathematical Formulation of Parabolic Interpolation

Suppose three distinct points $a<b<c$ are given, with corresponding function values $f(a)$, $f(b)$, and $f(c)$, and assume that $f(b)$ is the smallest of the three. There exists a unique quadratic polynomial, $y(x) = Ax^2 + Bx + C$, that interpolates these three points. The vertex of this parabola occurs at:

$$x_{\text{parabola}} = -\frac{B}{2A} \tag{10.4.2}$$

By explicitly solving the interpolation problem, the location of the vertex can be written directly in terms of the data as:

$$
x_{\text{parabola}}
=
b
-
\frac{(b-a)^2 \bigl[f(b)-f(c)\bigr] - (b-c)^2 \bigl[f(b)-f(a)\bigr]}
     {2\Bigl[(b-a)\bigl[f(b)-f(c)\bigr] - (b-c)\bigl[f(b)-f(a)\bigr]\Bigr]}
\tag{10.4.3}
$$

This expression gives the estimated minimizer of the parabola passing through $(a,f(a))$, $(b,f(b))$, and $(c,f(c))$.

In practice, care must be taken when evaluating this formula. If the denominator is very small, which can occur when the function values are nearly equal or the points are poorly spaced, numerical instability can arise. For this reason, parabolic interpolation is typically used conditionally and combined with safer strategies that preserve the bracketing property.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.4 on parabolic interpolation and its role in accelerating one-dimensional derivative-free optimization, Program 10.4.1 provides a practical implementation of a safeguarded parabolic search strategy. While golden-section search guarantees convergence under the sole assumption of unimodality, it deliberately contracts the interval at a fixed rate and does not exploit local smoothness. This program demonstrates how inverse parabolic interpolation can be incorporated conditionally to take advantage of near-quadratic behavior, while retaining the robustness of bracketing methods. The resulting hybrid approach illustrates the fundamental idea underlying Brent’s method: combine fast local models with conservative fallback steps to achieve both efficiency and reliability in finite-precision arithmetic.

At the core of the implementation is the `Bracket` structure, which represents a triplet ((a,b,c)) satisfying the unimodal bracketing condition given in equation (10.4.1). This structure stores both the abscissas and their corresponding function values, allowing the algorithm to update the bracket efficiently while preserving the invariant that the minimum remains enclosed. A numerical validity check is included to tolerate equality in function values arising from floating-point rounding near the minimizer, even though the theoretical condition is strict.

The function `parabolic_vertex_10_4_3` implements inverse parabolic interpolation by evaluating the explicit vertex formula given in equation (10.4.3). Given three sampled points, this routine computes the predicted minimizer of the interpolating parabola and returns it only if the computation is numerically well conditioned. Several safeguards are applied: the denominator must not be too small, the predicted point must lie strictly inside the current bracket, and it must not fall excessively close to an endpoint. These checks reflect the practical cautions discussed at the end of Section 10.4.1.

The function `safeguarded_step` embodies the hybrid logic. At each iteration, it first attempts a parabolic step using the interpolation formula. If the proposed point is acceptable and preserves the bracketing condition, the bracket is updated accordingly. If not, the algorithm falls back to a golden-section step based on the subdivision ratio derived in Section 10.3, which guarantees a safe reduction of the interval. In either case, exactly one new function evaluation is performed per iteration, maintaining predictable computational cost.

The outer driver, `hybrid_parabolic_search`, repeatedly applies these safeguarded steps until the interval length satisfies a scale-aware stopping criterion or until a practical floating-point resolution limit is reached. The routine records how many iterations use parabolic interpolation versus golden fallback, making the balance between aggressive and conservative steps directly observable. This structure mirrors the conceptual design of Brent’s method, without yet introducing its full state management or inverse quadratic refinements.

The `main` function demonstrates the algorithm on two representative examples. The first is a purely quadratic objective, for which parabolic interpolation is expected to be highly effective. The second is a smooth but non-quadratic function, where parabolic steps may still be useful but occasionally require fallback to golden-section updates. The printed diagnostics expose the interplay between these strategies and illustrate how local smoothness influences convergence behavior.

```rust
// Program 10.4.1. Parabolic Interpolation with Bracketing Safeguards
//
// This program implements inverse parabolic interpolation in the setting of Section 10.4.
// It starts from a bracketing triplet a < b < c satisfying (10.4.1):
//
//   f(b) < f(a),   f(b) < f(c).   (10.4.1)
//
// The parabolic predictor is the vertex of the unique quadratic interpolant through the
// three sampled points. Its location is given by x_parabola = -B/(2A) (10.4.2), and can
// be written directly in terms of (a,b,c) and their function values by the explicit
// formula (10.4.3). In practice, this parabolic step must be safeguarded: if the
// denominator is too small or the proposed point lies outside the bracket, the method
// falls back to a golden-section reduction step that preserves bracketing.
//
// The loop in this program is a minimal hybrid that illustrates conditional parabolic
// interpolation as a precursor to Brent’s method: each iteration uses exactly one new
// function evaluation, while maintaining a valid bracket.
//
// Build and run:
//   cargo run

use std::f64;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct Bracket {
    a: f64,
    b: f64,
    c: f64,
    fa: f64,
    fb: f64,
    fc: f64,
}

impl Bracket {
    fn new<F>(f: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64) -> Self
    where
        F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
    {
        assert!(a < b && b < c, "Require a < b < c.");
        let fa = f(a);
        let fb = f(b);
        let fc = f(c);

        // Theoretical condition (10.4.1) is strict, but we tolerate equality in floating-point
        // to avoid spurious failures near the minimizer.
        assert!(
            fb <= fa && fb <= fc,
            "Require bracketing condition f(b) <= f(a), f(b) <= f(c) (10.4.1)."
        );

        Self { a, b, c, fa, fb, fc }
    }

    fn length(&self) -> f64 {
        self.c - self.a
    }

    /// Numerical validity check for the bracketing invariant.
    fn is_valid(&self) -> bool {
        self.a.is_finite()
            && self.b.is_finite()
            && self.c.is_finite()
            && self.fa.is_finite()
            && self.fb.is_finite()
            && self.fc.is_finite()
            && (self.a < self.b)
            && (self.b < self.c)
            && (self.fb <= self.fa)
            && (self.fb <= self.fc)
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct GoldenConstants {
    /// w = (3 - sqrt(5)) / 2 (10.3.9)
    w: f64,
    /// 1 - w (10.3.10)
    r: f64,
}

impl GoldenConstants {
    fn new() -> Self {
        let sqrt5 = 5.0_f64.sqrt();
        let w = (3.0 - sqrt5) / 2.0; // (10.3.9)
        let r = 1.0 - w;             // (10.3.10)
        Self { w, r }
    }
}

/// Attempt inverse parabolic interpolation using the explicit vertex formula (10.4.3).
///
/// Returns Some(x_parabola) if:
/// - the denominator is not too small (ill-conditioning guard),
/// - x_parabola is finite,
/// - x_parabola lies strictly inside (a, c),
/// - and x_parabola is not too close to endpoints.
///
/// Otherwise returns None (caller should use a safer step).
fn parabolic_vertex_10_4_3(a: f64, b: f64, c: f64, fa: f64, fb: f64, fc: f64) -> Option<f64> {
    let ba = b - a;
    let bc = b - c;

    let fb_fc = fb - fc;
    let fb_fa = fb - fa;

    // (10.4.3): x_parabola = b - N / D
    let n = ba * ba * fb_fc - bc * bc * fb_fa;
    let d = 2.0 * (ba * fb_fc - bc * fb_fa);

    if !n.is_finite() || !d.is_finite() {
        return None;
    }

    // Ill-conditioning guard: reject if |D| is tiny relative to its natural scale.
    let d_scale = ((ba * fb_fc).abs() + (bc * fb_fa).abs()).max(1.0);
    if d.abs() <= 100.0 * f64::EPSILON * d_scale {
        return None;
    }

    let x = b - n / d;
    if !x.is_finite() {
        return None;
    }

    if !(x > a && x < c) {
        return None;
    }

    // Avoid selecting points extremely close to endpoints (numerical safety margin).
    let interval = (c - a).abs();
    let margin = 1e-12 * (interval + 1.0);
    if x <= a + margin || x >= c - margin {
        return None;
    }

    Some(x)
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
enum StepKind {
    Parabolic,
    GoldenFallback,
}

/// One safeguarded iteration: prefer parabolic step (10.4.3) when acceptable,
/// otherwise fall back to a golden-section step. The output bracket remains valid.
fn safeguarded_step<F>(f: &F, br: Bracket, gc: GoldenConstants) -> (Bracket, StepKind)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    assert!(br.is_valid(), "Input bracket must satisfy numerical form of (10.4.1).");

    // Try parabolic step first.
    if let Some(xp) = parabolic_vertex_10_4_3(br.a, br.b, br.c, br.fa, br.fb, br.fc) {
        let fxp = f(xp);

        let (mut a, mut b, mut c) = (br.a, br.b, br.c);
        let (mut fa, mut fb, mut fc) = (br.fa, br.fb, br.fc);

        if fxp >= fb {
            // xp does not improve b: it becomes an endpoint on its side.
            if xp < b {
                a = xp;
                fa = fxp;
            } else {
                c = xp;
                fc = fxp;
            }
        } else {
            // xp improves: it becomes the new b, and the old b becomes an endpoint.
            if xp < b {
                c = b;
                fc = fb;
                b = xp;
                fb = fxp;
            } else {
                a = b;
                fa = fb;
                b = xp;
                fb = fxp;
            }
        }

        let out = Bracket { a, b, c, fa, fb, fc };
        if out.is_valid() {
            return (out, StepKind::Parabolic);
        }
        // If numerical issues broke validity, fall through to golden fallback.
    }

    // Golden fallback: choose x in the larger subinterval, at distance w from b.
    let (a, b, c) = (br.a, br.b, br.c);
    let (fa, fb, fc) = (br.fa, br.fb, br.fc);

    let left_len = b - a;
    let right_len = c - b;

    let x = if right_len >= left_len {
        // x in (b, c)
        b + gc.w * (c - b)
    } else {
        // x in (a, b)
        b - gc.w * (b - a)
    };

    let fx = f(x);

    // Update bracket with non-strict comparisons to tolerate fp ties near the minimizer.
    let out = if x < b {
        if fx >= fb {
            Bracket {
                a: x,
                b,
                c,
                fa: fx,
                fb,
                fc,
            }
        } else {
            Bracket {
                a,
                b: x,
                c: b,
                fa,
                fb: fx,
                fc: fb,
            }
        }
    } else {
        if fx >= fb {
            Bracket {
                a,
                b,
                c: x,
                fa,
                fb,
                fc: fx,
            }
        } else {
            Bracket {
                a: b,
                b: x,
                c,
                fa: fb,
                fb: fx,
                fc,
            }
        }
    };

    assert!(out.is_valid(), "Golden fallback must preserve numerical form of (10.4.1).");
    (out, StepKind::GoldenFallback)
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
struct HybridResult {
    x_min: f64,
    f_min: f64,
    a: f64,
    b: f64,
    c: f64,
    iters: usize,
    evals: usize,
    parabolic_steps: usize,
    golden_steps: usize,
    stop_precision: bool,
}

/// Hybrid loop: repeatedly apply safeguarded steps until the interval is small.
///
/// The `stop_precision` flag indicates whether termination likely occurred at the
/// floating-point resolution scale, where distinct-point bracketing can collapse.
fn hybrid_parabolic_search<F>(
    f: F,
    mut br: Bracket,
    tol: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> HybridResult
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    assert!(tol > 0.0);
    assert!(max_iter > 0);
    assert!(br.is_valid());

    let gc = GoldenConstants::new();

    // Bracket constructor evaluated f at a,b,c.
    let mut evals = 3usize;
    let mut parabolic_steps = 0usize;
    let mut golden_steps = 0usize;
    let mut iters = 0usize;

    // Precision-scale stopping: once |c-a| is below ~sqrt(EPSILON)(|b|+1),
    // maintaining three distinct points becomes unreliable in f64.
    let sqrt_eps = f64::EPSILON.sqrt();
    let mut stop_precision = false;

    while br.length().abs() > tol * (br.b.abs() + 1.0) && iters < max_iter {
        // Precision stop to prevent collapse of b and c (or a and b) at tiny widths.
        if br.length().abs() < sqrt_eps * (br.b.abs() + 1.0) {
            stop_precision = true;
            break;
        }

        iters += 1;

        let (new_br, kind) = safeguarded_step(&f, br, gc);

        // Each step evaluates f exactly once.
        evals += 1;

        match kind {
            StepKind::Parabolic => parabolic_steps += 1,
            StepKind::GoldenFallback => golden_steps += 1,
        }

        br = new_br;
    }

    HybridResult {
        x_min: br.b,
        f_min: br.fb,
        a: br.a,
        b: br.b,
        c: br.c,
        iters,
        evals,
        parabolic_steps,
        golden_steps,
        stop_precision,
    }
}

fn main() {
    // Example A: quadratic objective.
    let f1 = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    // Example B: smooth but not exactly quadratic.
    let f2 = |x: f64| (x - 0.3) * (x - 0.3) + 0.05 * (8.0 * x).cos();

    let tol = 1e-10;
    let max_iter = 200;

    let gc = GoldenConstants::new();

    println!("Program 10.4.1: Parabolic Interpolation with Bracketing Safeguards");
    println!();
    println!("Golden fallback constants (from Section 10.3):");
    println!("  w   (10.3.9)  = {:.15}", gc.w);
    println!("  1-w (10.3.10) = {:.15}", gc.r);
    println!();

    let br1 = Bracket::new(&f1, -1.0, 2.5, 6.0);
    let res1 = hybrid_parabolic_search(f1, br1, tol, max_iter);

    println!("Example A: f(x) = (x - 2)^2 + 1 with initial bracket satisfying (10.4.1)");
    println!("  x_min ≈ {:.12}, f_min ≈ {:.12}", res1.x_min, res1.f_min);
    println!("  iterations = {}, evaluations = {}", res1.iters, res1.evals);
    println!(
        "  steps: parabolic = {}, golden fallback = {}",
        res1.parabolic_steps, res1.golden_steps
    );
    println!(
        "  final bracket: [{:.12}, {:.12}, {:.12}], length ≈ {:.3e}",
        res1.a,
        res1.b,
        res1.c,
        (res1.c - res1.a).abs()
    );
    if res1.stop_precision {
        println!("  note: terminated at the floating-point resolution scale (distinct points may collapse).");
    }
    println!();

    let br2 = Bracket::new(&f2, -0.5, 0.35, 1.0);
    let res2 = hybrid_parabolic_search(f2, br2, tol, max_iter);

    println!("Example B: f(x) = (x - 0.3)^2 + 0.05*cos(8x) with initial bracket satisfying (10.4.1)");
    println!("  x_min ≈ {:.12}, f_min ≈ {:.12}", res2.x_min, res2.f_min);
    println!("  iterations = {}, evaluations = {}", res2.iters, res2.evals);
    println!(
        "  steps: parabolic = {}, golden fallback = {}",
        res2.parabolic_steps, res2.golden_steps
    );
    println!(
        "  final bracket: [{:.12}, {:.12}, {:.12}], length ≈ {:.3e}",
        res2.a,
        res2.b,
        res2.c,
        (res2.c - res2.a).abs()
    );
    if res2.stop_precision {
        println!("  note: terminated at the floating-point resolution scale (distinct points may collapse).");
    }
}
```

Program 10.4.1 demonstrates how inverse parabolic interpolation can significantly accelerate one-dimensional optimization when the objective function is smooth near its minimum, while still preserving the robustness of bracketing methods. By conditionally accepting parabolic steps and reverting to golden-section updates when necessary, the algorithm avoids the numerical instabilities that can arise from blindly trusting interpolation formulas.

The contrasting behavior of the two examples highlights this balance. For a quadratic objective, parabolic interpolation quickly identifies the minimizer, whereas for a more complex smooth function, a mixture of parabolic and conservative steps is required. These results reinforce the central lesson of Section 10.4: efficiency gains from local models must be tempered by safeguards that maintain global correctness.

The modular structure of the code provides a clear foundation for extending this approach to a full implementation of Brent’s method. Additional refinements, such as inverse quadratic interpolation and more sophisticated step acceptance rules, can be incorporated naturally into this framework, combining fast convergence with the reliability demanded in practical numerical optimization.

## 10.4.2. Brent’s Method

Brent’s method, proposed by Richard Brent, combines the guaranteed convergence of golden-section search with the potential speed of parabolic interpolation. The algorithm always maintains a valid bracketing triplet $(a,b,c)$, ensuring that the minimum remains confined within the interval $[a,c]$ at all times.

At each iteration, Brent’s method attempts a parabolic step as follows. Using the most recent three points at which the function has been evaluated, a candidate point $x_{\text{parabola}}$ is computed using equation (10.4.3). This candidate is accepted if it lies sufficiently inside the current interval and is not too close to the boundaries, and if it is expected to provide meaningful improvement over the current best point $b$. In that case, the function is evaluated at $x_{\text{parabola}}$, and the bracket is updated accordingly.

If the parabolic candidate is invalid, numerically unreliable, or fails to yield sufficient improvement, the algorithm falls back to a golden-section step, reducing the interval by the golden ratio. This fallback guarantees that the bracket length decreases at every iteration, even in unfavorable situations. As a result, Brent’s method never performs worse than golden-section search, while often converging much faster when the function behaves smoothly near the minimum.

This adaptive strategy gives Brent’s method a superlinear convergence rate in practice, often approaching that of the secant method, while retaining the robustness of bracketing-based search. Because it does not rely on derivative information, Brent’s method remains stable even when derivatives are unavailable or poorly conditioned.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.4.2 on the design principles and convergence properties of Brent’s method, Program 10.4.2 provides a concrete implementation of this hybrid minimization algorithm in Rust. In one-dimensional optimization, purely bracketing methods such as golden-section search guarantee convergence but may converge slowly, while interpolation-based methods can converge rapidly but may fail when their assumptions break down. Brent’s method resolves this tension by adaptively combining parabolic interpolation with a robust golden-section fallback, ensuring that the minimizer remains bracketed at every iteration. This program implements the full decision logic described in the preceding analysis, illustrating how theoretical safeguards translate into a reliable and efficient numerical algorithm suitable for finite-precision computation.

At the core of the implementation is the function `brent_minimize`, which encapsulates Brent’s algorithm for derivative-free minimization on a bounded interval. The function accepts an objective function $f$, an initial bracketing triplet $(a,b,c)$, a relative tolerance, and a maximum iteration count. The bracketing condition $f(b) \le f(a)$ and $f(b) \le f(c)$ ensures that the minimum is initially confined within the interval, as required by the theoretical framework introduced earlier in this section.

Within each iteration, the algorithm maintains three points $x$, $w$, and $v$, corresponding to the current best estimate of the minimizer and two previous points used for interpolation. When sufficient information is available, a parabolic candidate point is computed using the interpolation formula given in Equation (10.4.3). This candidate is accepted only if it lies strictly within the current bracket and satisfies additional safeguards designed to prevent numerical instability or excessive extrapolation. These conditions reflect the acceptance criteria discussed in Section 10.4.2 and ensure that interpolation accelerates convergence only when it is likely to be reliable.

If the parabolic step is rejected, the algorithm automatically falls back to a golden-section step, reducing the bracket length by a fixed factor. This guarantees progress regardless of the behavior of the objective function and preserves the global convergence properties established for bracketing methods. The interplay between these two strategies is governed by the step-length variables `d` and `e`, which track recent movement and enforce the balance between aggressive interpolation and conservative interval reduction.

Termination is determined by a combined relative and absolute tolerance condition, consistent with the stopping criterion described earlier in this chapter. This criterion halts the iteration when the current estimate is sufficiently close to the midpoint of the bracket, accounting for both the scale of the solution and the limits of floating-point resolution. The function returns both the estimated minimizer and the corresponding function value, along with the number of iterations performed.

The `main` function serves as a demonstration harness for the algorithm. It applies Brent’s method to two representative test problems: a strictly convex quadratic function with a known minimizer and a smooth unimodal function with a small oscillatory perturbation. These examples illustrate both the accuracy of the method on well-behaved functions and its robustness in the presence of mild nonlinearity, without requiring derivative information.

```rust
// Program 10.4.2: Brent’s method for one-dimensional minimization (derivative-free)
//
// Problem statement.
// Given a continuous unimodal objective function f on an interval [a, c] with an interior point b
// satisfying f(b) <= f(a) and f(b) <= f(c), compute an approximation x* to the minimizer of f
// inside [a, c]. The algorithm maintains a valid bracketing interval at every step and combines
// parabolic interpolation (when reliable) with a golden-section fallback (when not).

use std::f64;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub struct BrentResult {
    pub x_min: f64,
    pub f_min: f64,
    pub iterations: usize,
}

/// Brent's method for minimization on a bracket (ax, bx, cx) with ax < bx < cx and
/// f(bx) <= f(ax), f(cx). Returns an approximate minimizer and the function value there.
///
/// Stopping rule:
/// The iteration stops when x is sufficiently close to the midpoint of the current bracket,
/// using the standard absolute/relative tolerance scaling used in classical Brent codes.
pub fn brent_minimize<F>(
    f: F,
    mut ax: f64,
    bx: f64,
    mut cx: f64,
    tol: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> Result<BrentResult, String>
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    if !(ax.is_finite() && bx.is_finite() && cx.is_finite()) {
        return Err("Bracket endpoints must be finite.".to_string());
    }
    if !(tol.is_finite() && tol > 0.0) {
        return Err("Tolerance must be positive and finite.".to_string());
    }

    // Enforce ax < cx by swapping if needed; then require ax < bx < cx.
    if cx < ax {
        std::mem::swap(&mut ax, &mut cx);
    }
    if !(ax < bx && bx < cx) {
        return Err("Require ax < bx < cx for the initial bracketing triplet.".to_string());
    }

    // Verify bracketing condition for minimization.
    let fa = f(ax);
    let fb = f(bx);
    let fc = f(cx);
    if !(fb <= fa && fb <= fc) {
        return Err(
            "Require f(bx) <= f(ax) and f(bx) <= f(cx) for a valid minimization bracket."
                .to_string(),
        );
    }

    // Brent constants.
    // CGOLD = 1 - 1/phi = 0.381966..., where phi is the golden ratio.
    const CGOLD: f64 = 0.3819660112501051;
    // Small number guarding precision issues and division by zero.
    const ZEPS: f64 = 1.0e-12;
    // A tiny threshold for comparing points (avoids brittle exact-equality tests).
    const TINY: f64 = 1.0e-15;

    // Current bracket endpoints a < b (not the original "bx").
    let mut a: f64 = ax;
    let mut b: f64 = cx;

    // Current best x, and two previous points w and v used for parabolic interpolation.
    let mut x: f64 = bx;
    let mut w: f64 = x;
    let mut v: f64 = x;

    let mut fx: f64 = fb;
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    // e is step size before last; d is the most recent step size (explicitly typed).
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;

    for iter in 0..max_iter {
        let xm: f64 = 0.5 * (a + b);

        // Relative + absolute tolerance scaling (classic Brent).
        let tol1: f64 = tol * x.abs() + ZEPS;
        let tol2: f64 = 2.0 * tol1;

        // Stop when x is close enough to the bracket midpoint.
        if (x - xm).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (b - a)) {
            return Ok(BrentResult {
                x_min: x,
                f_min: fx,
                iterations: iter,
            });
        }

        let mut parabolic_accepted = false;

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            // Attempt a parabolic step through (x,fx), (w,fw), (v,fv).
            let r: f64 = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q: f64 = (x - v) * (fx - fw);
            let mut p: f64 = (x - v) * q - (x - w) * r;
            let mut q2: f64 = 2.0 * (q - r);

            // Ensure p points "downhill" with respect to the denominator sign.
            if q2 > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            }
            q2 = q2.abs();

            // Save previous step magnitude and shift e <- d.
            let etemp: f64 = e;
            e = d;

            // Accept parabolic step only if it is inside (a,b),
            // not too close to boundaries, and not too large compared to previous step.
            if q2 > TINY {
                let p_over_q: f64 = p / q2;
                let u: f64 = x + p_over_q;

                if (u - a) > tol2 && (b - u) > tol2 && p.abs() < 0.5 * q2 * etemp.abs() {
                    d = p_over_q;
                    parabolic_accepted = true;
                }
            }
        }

        if !parabolic_accepted {
            // Golden-section fallback (guarantees bracket reduction).
            e = if x >= xm { a - x } else { b - x };
            d = CGOLD * e;
        }

        // Ensure the step is at least tol1 to avoid stagnation at floating-point resolution.
        let u: f64 = if d.abs() >= tol1 {
            x + d
        } else {
            x + tol1 * d.signum()
        };

        let fu: f64 = f(u);

        // Update bracket and interpolation points.
        if fu <= fx {
            // New best point.
            if u >= x {
                a = x;
            } else {
                b = x;
            }

            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            // u is not better than x, but still refines the bracket.
            if u < x {
                a = u;
            } else {
                b = u;
            }

            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() <= TINY {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() <= TINY || (v - w).abs() <= TINY {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    // Iteration limit reached; return best found.
    Ok(BrentResult {
        x_min: x,
        f_min: fx,
        iterations: max_iter,
    })
}

fn main() {
    // Example 1: A convex quadratic with minimizer at x = 2.
    let f1 = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;

    // Valid bracketing triplet: a < b < c and f(b) <= f(a), f(c).
    let (a1, b1, c1) = (0.0_f64, 2.0_f64, 4.0_f64);

    let tol: f64 = 1e-10;
    let max_iter: usize = 200;

    let res1 = brent_minimize(f1, a1, b1, c1, tol, max_iter).expect("Brent failed on example 1");

    println!("Example 1 (quadratic):");
    println!("  x_min      = {:.15e}", res1.x_min);
    println!("  f(x_min)   = {:.15e}", res1.f_min);
    println!("  iterations = {}", res1.iterations);
    println!();

    // Example 2: A smooth unimodal function on [0, 2].
```
