```rust
                fallback_used,
                fallback_bisection,
                fallback_golden,
                final_a: a,
                final_b: b,
                final_c: c,
                final_width: width,
                termination: TerminationReason::IntervalTol,
            });
        }

        if dfb.abs() <= opt.df_tol {
            return Ok(SafeguardedResult {
                x_min: b,
                f_min: fb,
                iterations: iter,
                evals_f: fe.evals_f,
                evals_df: fe.evals_df,
                secant_accepted,
                secant_rejected,
                fallback_used,
                fallback_bisection,
                fallback_golden,
                final_a: a,
                final_b: b,
                final_c: c,
                final_width: width,
                termination: TerminationReason::DerivativeTol,
            });
        }

        // Identify the downhill side using the sign of f'(b) (Section 10.5.1).
        // If f'(b) > 0, search left: (a, b). If f'(b) < 0, search right: (b, c).
        let use_left_interval = dfb > 0.0;
        let (l, r, dfl, dfr) = if use_left_interval {
            (a, b, dfa, dfb)
        } else {
            (b, c, dfb, dfc)
        };

        // If the active interval is too small to represent strict interior points, terminate.
        if strict_interior_bounds(l, r, opt.tiny).is_none() {
            return Ok(SafeguardedResult {
                x_min: b,
                f_min: fb,
                iterations: iter,
                evals_f: fe.evals_f,
                evals_df: fe.evals_df,
                secant_accepted,
                secant_rejected,
                fallback_used,
                fallback_bisection,
                fallback_golden,
                final_a: a,
                final_b: b,
                final_c: c,
                final_width: (c - a).abs(),
                termination: TerminationReason::IntervalTol,
            });
        }

        // Attempt secant on f' (Equation 10.5.5 or analog).
        let denom = dfr - dfl;
        let scale = dfl.abs().max(dfr.abs()).max(1.0);
        let denom_ok = denom.is_finite() && denom.abs() >= opt.denom_rel_tol * scale && denom.abs() >= opt.tiny;

        let mut used_secant = false;
        let mut x_trial = f64::NAN;

        if denom_ok {
            let x_secant = secant_candidate(l, r, dfl, dfr);

            // Geometric safeguards: inside the active interval and not near its boundaries.
            let inside_ok = is_well_inside(l, r, x_secant, opt.interior_margin_frac, opt.tiny);

            // Predicted improvement safeguard: secant must not be negligible at current scale.
            // Use a simple local linear model magnitude: |f'(b)| * |step|.
            // The scale is |f(b)| + 1, so the threshold behaves sensibly near f(b)=0.
            let step_mag = (x_secant - b).abs();
            let predicted_drop = dfb.abs() * step_mag;
            let predicted_ok = predicted_drop >= opt.min_predicted_drop_frac * (fb.abs() + 1.0);

            if inside_ok && predicted_ok {
                x_trial = x_secant;
                used_secant = true;
            }
        }

        // If secant is rejected, use a conservative fallback that guarantees interval reduction.
        if !used_secant {
            secant_rejected += 1;
            fallback_used += 1;

            x_trial = match opt.fallback {
                FallbackStep::Bisection => {
                    fallback_bisection += 1;
                    0.5 * (l + r)
                }
                FallbackStep::GoldenSection => {
                    fallback_golden += 1;
                    golden_fallback(l, r)
                }
            };

            // Enforce strict interior placement for the fallback as well.
            let (lo, hi) = strict_interior_bounds(l, r, opt.tiny).unwrap();
            x_trial = x_trial.clamp(lo, hi);
        } else {
            secant_accepted += 1;
            // Enforce strict interior placement even for accepted secant points.
            let (lo, hi) = strict_interior_bounds(l, r, opt.tiny).unwrap();
            x_trial = x_trial.clamp(lo, hi);
        }

        // Evaluate at the trial point.
        let fx = fe.eval_f(x_trial);
        let dfx = fe.eval_df(x_trial);

        // Update the bracketing triplet strictly using function values.
        if fx <= fb {
            if use_left_interval {
                // Active interval was (a,b). Replace c with old b, then b with x_trial.
                c = b;
                fc = fb;
                dfc = dfb;

                b = x_trial;
                fb = fx;
                dfb = dfx;
            } else {
                // Active interval was (b,c). Replace a with old b, then b with x_trial.
                a = b;
                fa = fb;
                dfa = dfb;

                b = x_trial;
                fb = fx;
                dfb = dfx;
            }
        } else {
            // No improvement. Shrink the active endpoint toward x_trial.
            if use_left_interval {
                a = x_trial;
                fa = fx;
                dfa = dfx;
            } else {
                c = x_trial;
                fc = fx;
                dfc = dfx;
            }
        }

        // Safety checks: maintain ordering and bracketing condition.
        if !(a < b && b < c) {
            return Err("Internal error: bracketing triplet lost ordering.".to_string());
        }
        if !(fb <= fa && fb <= fc) {
            return Err("Internal error: bracketing condition violated.".to_string());
        }
    }

    Ok(SafeguardedResult {
        x_min: b,
        f_min: fb,
        iterations: opt.max_iter,
        evals_f: fe.evals_f,
        evals_df: fe.evals_df,
        secant_accepted,
        secant_rejected,
        fallback_used,
        fallback_bisection,
        fallback_golden,
        final_a: a,
        final_b: b,
        final_c: c,
        final_width: (c - a).abs(),
        termination: TerminationReason::MaxIter,
    })
}

fn main() {
    // Options chosen to highlight the safeguard and fallback logic discussed in Section 10.5.3.
    // Switch fallback between Bisection and GoldenSection to compare conservative behaviors.
    let opt = SafeguardedOptions {
        interval_tol: 1e-12,
        df_tol: 1e-12,
        max_iter: 300,
        interior_margin_frac: 0.1,
        denom_rel_tol: 1e-12,
        min_predicted_drop_frac: 1e-12,
        fallback: FallbackStep::Bisection,
        tiny: 1e-14,
    };

    // Test 1: convex quadratic.
    let f1 = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;
    let df1 = |x: f64| 2.0 * (x - 2.0);

    let (a1, b1, c1) = (0.0_f64, 2.2_f64, 4.0_f64);

    let r1 = safeguarded_search_with_fallbacks(f1, df1, a1, b1, c1, opt)
        .expect("Safeguarded search failed on test 1");

    println!("Test 1: convex quadratic (secant + safeguards + fallback)");
    println!("  x_min              = {:.16e}", r1.x_min);
    println!("  f(x_min)           = {:.16e}", r1.f_min);
    println!("  iterations         = {}", r1.iterations);
    println!("  evals f            = {}", r1.evals_f);
    println!("  evals f'           = {}", r1.evals_df);
    println!("  secant accepted    = {}", r1.secant_accepted);
    println!("  secant rejected    = {}", r1.secant_rejected);
    println!("  fallback used      = {}", r1.fallback_used);
    println!("  fallback bisection = {}", r1.fallback_bisection);
    println!("  fallback golden    = {}", r1.fallback_golden);
    println!(
        "  final [a,b,c]      = [{:.16e}, {:.16e}, {:.16e}]",
        r1.final_a, r1.final_b, r1.final_c
    );
    println!("  final width        = {:.16e}", r1.final_width);
    println!("  termination        = {:?}", r1.termination);
    println!();

    // Test 2: smooth unimodal with oscillatory term.
    let f2 = |x: f64| (x - 0.3).powi(2) + 0.05 * (5.0 * x).sin();
    let df2 = |x: f64| 2.0 * (x - 0.3) + 0.25 * (5.0 * x).cos();

    let (a2, b2, c2) = (0.0_f64, 0.5_f64, 2.0_f64);

    let r2 = safeguarded_search_with_fallbacks(f2, df2, a2, b2, c2, opt)
        .expect("Safeguarded search failed on test 2");

    println!("Test 2: smooth unimodal + oscillation (secant + safeguards + fallback)");
    println!("  x_min              = {:.16e}", r2.x_min);
    println!("  f(x_min)           = {:.16e}", r2.f_min);
    println!("  iterations         = {}", r2.iterations);
    println!("  evals f            = {}", r2.evals_f);
    println!("  evals f'           = {}", r2.evals_df);
    println!("  secant accepted    = {}", r2.secant_accepted);
    println!("  secant rejected    = {}", r2.secant_rejected);
    println!("  fallback used      = {}", r2.fallback_used);
    println!("  fallback bisection = {}", r2.fallback_bisection);
    println!("  fallback golden    = {}", r2.fallback_golden);
    println!(
        "  final [a,b,c]      = [{:.16e}, {:.16e}, {:.16e}]",
        r2.final_a, r2.final_b, r2.final_c
    );
    println!("  final width        = {:.16e}", r2.final_width);
    println!("  termination        = {:?}", r2.termination);
}
```

Program 10.5.3 illustrates how safeguards and fallback strategies are essential for making derivative-based one-dimensional search reliable in practice. While the secant step can offer substantial acceleration under favorable conditions, it is inherently extrapolative and must be constrained by geometric and numerical checks. By rejecting unsafe proposals and reverting to conservative interval-reducing steps, the algorithm avoids stagnation and preserves global convergence guarantees.

The numerical examples emphasize that derivative information should be viewed as an accelerator rather than a decision-maker. When the derivative behaves smoothly, secant steps can dominate and lead to rapid convergence. When it does not, fallback strategies ensure continued progress without compromising stability. This hybrid philosophy mirrors the design principles encountered earlier in Brent’s method and underscores the importance of safeguarding in practical optimization algorithms.

The modular structure of the implementation makes it straightforward to experiment with alternative fallback strategies or acceptance criteria. As such, this program provides a flexible foundation for more advanced hybrid methods that combine derivative-based acceleration with robust bracketing, which are explored further in subsequent sections.

**Note:** In the quadratic example, the algorithm terminates by the derivative-based criterion once a stationary point is detected, even though the enclosing interval remains relatively wide. This behavior is expected: for functions with linear derivatives, the secant step can identify the minimizer in very few iterations. Because all updates preserve the bracketing condition, early termination by the derivative criterion does not compromise correctness, although it may occur before substantial interval contraction.

## 10.5.4. Secant versus Higher-Order Interpolation

Given access to both function and derivative values, it is natural to ask whether higher-order interpolation schemes could provide further acceleration. For example, using function values and first derivatives at two points, one can construct a unique cubic polynomial through Hermite interpolation and compute the minimum of this cubic as a trial point.

In practice, however, such higher-order approaches tend to be less reliable than the secant method. Cubic and higher-degree polynomials are prone to oscillatory behavior between interpolation points and are highly sensitive to noise or round-off errors in the derivative data. Even small inaccuracies in $f'(x)$ can produce large shifts in the location of the interpolated minimum, leading to unstable or erratic steps.

By contrast, the secant method relies on a linear approximation of the derivative, which is considerably more robust. It avoids overfitting local information and produces steps that are less sensitive to small perturbations in the data. Although its theoretical convergence order is lower than that of an ideal cubic model, the secant approach typically delivers sufficient acceleration once the iterates are close to the minimum, while maintaining predictable behavior in finite-precision arithmetic.

For these reasons, practical one-dimensional optimization algorithms that use derivative information almost always favor safeguarded secant steps over higher-order interpolation. This choice reflects a recurring theme in numerical optimization: moderate-order models, when combined with strong safeguards, often outperform more sophisticated schemes in real computational environments.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.5.4 on the trade-off between model complexity and numerical robustness in derivative-based one-dimensional search, Program 10.5.4 provides a concrete comparison between safeguarded secant steps on the first derivative and higher-order cubic Hermite interpolation. While it is tempting to exploit additional derivative information to construct more accurate local models, such approaches can be fragile in finite-precision arithmetic. This program implements both strategies within the same bracketing framework and applies identical safeguards and fallback rules, allowing their practical behavior to be compared directly. The examples illustrate how moderate-order models often provide a better balance between acceleration and stability than more elaborate interpolation schemes.

At the core of the implementation is the function `run_search`, which performs safeguarded one-dimensional minimization on an initial bracketing triplet $(a,b,c)$ satisfying the condition in Equation (10.5.2). The function accepts both the objective $f(x)$ and its first derivative $f'(x)$, along with a selector indicating whether trial points should be generated using a secant step on the derivative or a cubic Hermite interpolant. Throughout the computation, the bracketing invariant is maintained strictly, and all updates to $(a,b,c)$ are based solely on function values.

When the secant strategy is selected, a trial point is computed by applying the secant method to the derivative on the active subinterval, as defined by Equation (10.5.5) or its symmetric counterpart. This step approximates the location at which a linear model of $f'(x)$ would vanish, providing superlinear convergence when the derivative behaves smoothly. The proposal is accepted only if it lies well inside the active subinterval and satisfies basic numerical stability checks; otherwise, it is rejected.

When the cubic strategy is selected, the program constructs a cubic Hermite interpolant that matches both function values and first derivatives at the endpoints of the active subinterval. The stationary points of this cubic are computed analytically, and a candidate minimizer is extracted if it corresponds to a genuine interior minimum. As discussed in Section 10.5.4, this higher-order construction is more sensitive to perturbations in the derivative data, and the code therefore applies the same geometric and numerical safeguards before accepting the resulting trial point.

If either proposal fails its acceptance tests, the algorithm falls back to a conservative bisection step that guarantees reduction of the enclosing interval. This fallback mechanism ensures steady progress even when derivative-based models are unreliable. The program records the number of accepted proposals, rejected proposals, and fallback steps, making it possible to quantify how often each interpolation strategy contributes meaningful progress.

The `main` function demonstrates the behavior of both approaches on three test problems. A strictly convex quadratic illustrates the baseline performance when derivative information is exact and well behaved. A smooth unimodal function with an oscillatory component highlights the interaction between local interpolation and global safeguards. Finally, a variant with deliberately perturbed derivative data illustrates the increased sensitivity of cubic interpolation compared with the secant method. In all cases, the reported iteration counts, evaluation totals, and final brackets are consistent with the theoretical discussion in Section 10.5.4.

```rust
// Program 10.5.4: Secant versus Higher-Order (Cubic Hermite) Interpolation with Safeguards
//
// Problem statement.
// Minimize a smooth objective f(x) given both f and f'(x). Starting from a bracketing triplet
// (a, b, c) satisfying f(b) <= f(a) and f(b) <= f(c) (Equation 10.5.2), we compare two
// derivative-assisted trial-point proposals on the active subinterval selected by the sign of f'(b)
// (Section 10.5.1):
//
// (1) Secant on the derivative (Section 10.5.2). On (l, r), propose x_secant by applying the
//     secant formula to f'(x) (Equation 10.5.5 or its (b,c) analog).
//
// (2) Cubic Hermite model. On (l, r), build the unique cubic that matches f and f' at both
//     endpoints (Hermite interpolation), then propose the minimizer of that cubic on (l, r).
//
// Safeguards.
// Both proposals are screened. A trial point is rejected if it lies outside the active interval,
// is too close to its boundaries, is numerically unstable, or (for the cubic) if the stationary
// points do not yield a valid interior minimizer. Rejected proposals fall back to a conservative
// bisection step that guarantees interval reduction.
//
// Bracketing updates.
// After evaluating the trial point, the triplet (a, b, c) is updated strictly using function
// values, never derivative values, so the enclosure of the minimum is preserved even if f'(x)
// is noisy or misleading.

use std::f64;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub enum TerminationReason {
    IntervalTol,
    DerivativeTol,
    MaxIter,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub enum ProposalKind {
    SecantOnDerivative,
    CubicHermite,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub struct CompareOptions {
    pub interval_tol: f64,
    pub df_tol: f64,
    pub max_iter: usize,
    pub interior_margin_frac: f64,
    pub denom_rel_tol: f64,
    pub tiny: f64,
    /// If true, require both small width and small |f'(b)| to terminate.
    pub require_both: bool,
}

impl Default for CompareOptions {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            interval_tol: 1e-12,
            df_tol: 1e-12,
            max_iter: 300,
            interior_margin_frac: 0.1,
            denom_rel_tol: 1e-12,
            tiny: 1e-14,
            require_both: true,
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub struct RunStats {
    pub x_min: f64,
    pub f_min: f64,
    pub iterations: usize,
    pub evals_f: usize,
    pub evals_df: usize,
    pub accepted: usize,
    pub rejected: usize,
    pub fallback: usize,
    pub final_a: f64,
    pub final_b: f64,
    pub final_c: f64,
    pub final_width: f64,
    pub termination: TerminationReason,
}

pub struct FuncWithDeriv<F, G>
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
    G: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    f: F,
    df: G,
    pub evals_f: usize,
    pub evals_df: usize,
}

impl<F, G> FuncWithDeriv<F, G>
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
    G: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    pub fn new(f: F, df: G) -> Self {
        Self {
            f,
            df,
            evals_f: 0,
            evals_df: 0,
        }
    }

    #[inline]
    pub fn eval_f(&mut self, x: f64) -> f64 {
        self.evals_f += 1;
        (self.f)(x)
    }

    #[inline]
    pub fn eval_df(&mut self, x: f64) -> f64 {
        self.evals_df += 1;
        (self.df)(x)
    }
}

fn validate_triplet(a: f64, b: f64, c: f64) -> Result<(), String> {
    if !(a.is_finite() && b.is_finite() && c.is_finite()) {
        return Err("Bracket points must be finite.".to_string());
    }
    if !(a < b && b < c) {
        return Err("Require a < b < c.".to_string());
    }
    Ok(())
}

/// Strict interior bounds for (l, r). Returns None if no strict interior can be represented.
fn strict_interior_bounds(l: f64, r: f64, tiny: f64) -> Option<(f64, f64)> {
    let w = (r - l).abs();
    let eps = (1e-15 * w).max(tiny);
    let lo = l + eps;
    let hi = r - eps;
    if lo < hi { Some((lo, hi)) } else { None }
}

fn is_well_inside(l: f64, r: f64, x: f64, margin_frac: f64, tiny: f64) -> bool {
    if !x.is_finite() {
        return false;
    }
    let w = (r - l).abs();
    if w <= 0.0 {
        return false;
    }
    let margin = (margin_frac * w).max(tiny);
    x > l + margin && x < r - margin
}

fn bisection_fallback(l: f64, r: f64, tiny: f64) -> f64 {
    let (lo, hi) = strict_interior_bounds(l, r, tiny).unwrap();
    (0.5 * (l + r)).clamp(lo, hi)
}

/// Secant proposal for a root of f'(x) on (l, r):
/// x = r - f'(r)/(f'(r)-f'(l)) * (r-l)
fn propose_secant_on_derivative(
    l: f64,
    r: f64,
    dfl: f64,
    dfr: f64,
    opt: &CompareOptions,
) -> Option<f64> {
    let denom = dfr - dfl;
    let scale = dfl.abs().max(dfr.abs()).max(1.0);
    if !denom.is_finite() || denom.abs() < opt.denom_rel_tol * scale || denom.abs() < opt.tiny {
        return None;
    }
    let x = r - (dfr / denom) * (r - l);
    if is_well_inside(l, r, x, opt.interior_margin_frac, opt.tiny) {
        Some(x)
    } else {
        None
    }
}

/// Compute the minimizer of the cubic Hermite interpolant on (l, r), if it exists and is interior.
///
/// We map x = l + t h, where h = r-l and t in [0,1]. The Hermite interpolant is:
/// p(t) = y0 H00(t) + y1 H01(t) + h m0 H10(t) + h m1 H11(t),
/// with y0=f(l), y1=f(r), m0=f'(l), m1=f'(r).
///
/// Its derivative dp/dt is a quadratic. We solve dp/dt = 0 and select a root t in (0,1) that yields
/// a local minimum (dp''/dt^2 > 0). Then x = l + t h.
fn propose_cubic_hermite_minimizer(
    l: f64,
    r: f64,
    y0: f64,
    y1: f64,
    m0: f64,
    m1: f64,
    opt: &CompareOptions,
) -> Option<f64> {
    let h = r - l;
    if !h.is_finite() || h <= 0.0 {
        return None;
    }

    // dp/dt = A t^2 + B t + C.
    // Derived by differentiating Hermite basis:
    // A = 6(y0 - y1) + 3h(m0 + m1)
    // B = -6(y0 - y1) - 4h m0 - 2h m1
    // C = h m0
    let a = 6.0 * (y0 - y1) + 3.0 * h * (m0 + m1);
    let b = -6.0 * (y0 - y1) - 4.0 * h * m0 - 2.0 * h * m1;
    let c = h * m0;

    if !(a.is_finite() && b.is_finite() && c.is_finite()) {
        return None;
    }

    // Solve quadratic A t^2 + B t + C = 0.
    let mut candidates: Vec<f64> = Vec::new();

    if a.abs() < opt.tiny {
        // Linear case: B t + C = 0
        if b.abs() < opt.tiny {
            return None;
        }
        let t = -c / b;
        candidates.push(t);
    } else {
        let disc = b * b - 4.0 * a * c;
        if disc < 0.0 || !disc.is_finite() {
            return None;
        }
        let sqrt_disc = disc.sqrt();

        // Use a stable quadratic formula variant.
        let q = if b >= 0.0 {
            -0.5 * (b + sqrt_disc)
        } else {
            -0.5 * (b - sqrt_disc)
        };
        if q.abs() < opt.tiny {
            return None;
        }
        let t1 = q / a;
        let t2 = c / q;
        candidates.push(t1);
        candidates.push(t2);
    }

    // Select interior root that corresponds to a local minimum: d2p/dt2 = 2A t + B > 0.
    // Then map back to x and enforce "well inside" safeguard.
    let mut best_x: Option<f64> = None;

    for &t in &candidates {
        if !t.is_finite() {
            continue;
        }
        if t <= 0.0 || t >= 1.0 {
            continue;
        }
        let second = 2.0 * a * t + b;
        if !second.is_finite() || second <= 0.0 {
            continue;
        }
        let x = l + t * h;
        if is_well_inside(l, r, x, opt.interior_margin_frac, opt.tiny) {
            best_x = Some(x);
            break;
        }
    }

    best_x
}

/// One run of the safeguarded search using a chosen proposal kind.
/// Bracketing updates use only function values.
pub fn run_search<F, G>(
    f: F,
    df: G,
    mut a: f64,
    mut b: f64,
    mut c: f64,
    opt: CompareOptions,
    kind: ProposalKind,
) -> Result<RunStats, String>
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
    G: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    validate_triplet(a, b, c)?;

    let mut fe = FuncWithDeriv::new(f, df);

    // Cache f-values.
    let mut fa = fe.eval_f(a);
    let mut fb = fe.eval_f(b);
    let mut fc = fe.eval_f(c);

    if !(fb <= fa && fb <= fc) {
        return Err("Initial triplet must satisfy f(b) <= f(a) and f(b) <= f(c).".to_string());
    }

    // Cache derivative values.
    let mut dfa = fe.eval_df(a);
    let mut dfb = fe.eval_df(b);
    let mut dfc = fe.eval_df(c);

    let mut accepted = 0usize;
    let mut rejected = 0usize;
    let mut fallback = 0usize;

    for iter in 0..opt.max_iter {
        let width = (c - a).abs();
        let width_ok = width <= opt.interval_tol;
        let df_ok = dfb.abs() <= opt.df_tol;

        if opt.require_both {
            if width_ok && df_ok {
                return Ok(RunStats {
                    x_min: b,
                    f_min: fb,
                    iterations: iter,
                    evals_f: fe.evals_f,
                    evals_df: fe.evals_df,
                    accepted,
                    rejected,
                    fallback,
                    final_a: a,
                    final_b: b,
                    final_c: c,
                    final_width: width,
                    termination: TerminationReason::IntervalTol,
                });
            }
        } else {
            if width_ok {
                return Ok(RunStats {
                    x_min: b,
                    f_min: fb,
                    iterations: iter,
                    evals_f: fe.evals_f,
                    evals_df: fe.evals_df,
                    accepted,
                    rejected,
                    fallback,
                    final_a: a,
                    final_b: b,
                    final_c: c,
                    final_width: width,
                    termination: TerminationReason::IntervalTol,
                });
            }
            if df_ok {
                return Ok(RunStats {
                    x_min: b,
                    f_min: fb,
                    iterations: iter,
                    evals_f: fe.evals_f,
                    evals_df: fe.evals_df,
                    accepted,
                    rejected,
                    fallback,
                    final_a: a,
                    final_b: b,
                    final_c: c,
                    final_width: width,
                    termination: TerminationReason::DerivativeTol,
                });
            }
        }

        // Choose active interval by sign of f'(b) (Section 10.5.1).
        let use_left = dfb > 0.0;
        let (l, r) = if use_left { (a, b) } else { (b, c) };

        // If strict interior cannot be represented, stop.
        if strict_interior_bounds(l, r, opt.tiny).is_none() {
            return Ok(RunStats {
                x_min: b,
                f_min: fb,
                iterations: iter,
                evals_f: fe.evals_f,
                evals_df: fe.evals_df,
                accepted,
                rejected,
                fallback,
                final_a: a,
                final_b: b,
                final_c: c,
                final_width: (c - a).abs(),
                termination: TerminationReason::IntervalTol,
            });
        }

        let proposal = match (kind, use_left) {
            (ProposalKind::SecantOnDerivative, true) => {
                propose_secant_on_derivative(a, b, dfa, dfb, &opt)
            }
            (ProposalKind::SecantOnDerivative, false) => {
                propose_secant_on_derivative(b, c, dfb, dfc, &opt)
            }
            (ProposalKind::CubicHermite, true) => {
                propose_cubic_hermite_minimizer(a, b, fa, fb, dfa, dfb, &opt)
            }
            (ProposalKind::CubicHermite, false) => {
                propose_cubic_hermite_minimizer(b, c, fb, fc, dfb, dfc, &opt)
            }
        };

        let mut x_trial = if let Some(x) = proposal {
            accepted += 1;
            x
        } else {
            rejected += 1;
            fallback += 1;
            bisection_fallback(l, r, opt.tiny)
        };

        // Enforce strict interior in the active interval.
        let (lo, hi) = strict_interior_bounds(l, r, opt.tiny).unwrap();
        x_trial = x_trial.clamp(lo, hi);

        // Evaluate at trial point.
        let fx = fe.eval_f(x_trial);
        let dfx = fe.eval_df(x_trial);

        // Bracketing update using function values only.
        if fx <= fb {
            if use_left {
                c = b;
                fc = fb;
                dfc = dfb;

                b = x_trial;
                fb = fx;
                dfb = dfx;
            } else {
                a = b;
                fa = fb;
                dfa = dfb;

                b = x_trial;
                fb = fx;
                dfb = dfx;
            }
        } else {
            if use_left {
                a = x_trial;
                fa = fx;
                dfa = dfx;
            } else {
                c = x_trial;
                fc = fx;
                dfc = dfx;
            }
        }

        if !(a < b && b < c) {
            return Err("Internal error: bracketing triplet lost ordering.".to_string());
        }
        if !(fb <= fa && fb <= fc) {
            return Err("Internal error: bracketing condition violated.".to_string());
        }
    }

    Ok(RunStats {
        x_min: b,
        f_min: fb,
        iterations: opt.max_iter,
        evals_f: fe.evals_f,
        evals_df: fe.evals_df,
        accepted,
        rejected,
        fallback,
        final_a: a,
        final_b: b,
        final_c: c,
        final_width: (c - a).abs(),
        termination: TerminationReason::MaxIter,
    })
}

fn print_stats(label: &str, s: RunStats) {
    println!("{label}");
    println!("  x_min        = {:.16e}", s.x_min);
    println!("  f(x_min)     = {:.16e}", s.f_min);
    println!("  iterations   = {}", s.iterations);
    println!("  evals f      = {}", s.evals_f);
    println!("  evals f'     = {}", s.evals_df);
    println!("  accepted     = {}", s.accepted);
    println!("  rejected     = {}", s.rejected);
    println!("  fallback     = {}", s.fallback);
    println!(
        "  final [a,b,c]= [{:.16e}, {:.16e}, {:.16e}]",
        s.final_a, s.final_b, s.final_c
    );
    println!("  final width  = {:.16e}", s.final_width);
    println!("  termination  = {:?}", s.termination);
    println!();
}

fn main() {
    let opt = CompareOptions {
        interval_tol: 1e-12,
        df_tol: 1e-12,
        max_iter: 400,
        interior_margin_frac: 0.1,
        denom_rel_tol: 1e-12,
        tiny: 1e-14,
        require_both: true,
    };

    // Example A: convex quadratic. Both methods should behave well.
    let f1 = |x: f64| (x - 2.0) * (x - 2.0) + 1.0;
    let df1 = |x: f64| 2.0 * (x - 2.0);

    let (a1, b1, c1) = (0.0_f64, 2.2_f64, 4.0_f64);

    let s1_secant = run_search(f1, df1, a1, b1, c1, opt, ProposalKind::SecantOnDerivative)
        .expect("Secant run failed on quadratic");
    let s1_cubic = run_search(f1, df1, a1, b1, c1, opt, ProposalKind::CubicHermite)
        .expect("Cubic run failed on quadratic");

    print_stats("Example A: convex quadratic, secant on f'(x)", s1_secant);
    print_stats("Example A: convex quadratic, cubic Hermite proposal", s1_cubic);

    // Example B: smooth unimodal plus oscillation. This highlights safeguard behavior.
    let f2 = |x: f64| (x - 0.3).powi(2) + 0.05 * (5.0 * x).sin();
    let df2 = |x: f64| 2.0 * (x - 0.3) + 0.25 * (5.0 * x).cos();

    let (a2, b2, c2) = (0.0_f64, 0.5_f64, 2.0_f64);

    let s2_secant = run_search(f2, df2, a2, b2, c2, opt, ProposalKind::SecantOnDerivative)
        .expect("Secant run failed on oscillatory case");
    let s2_cubic = run_search(f2, df2, a2, b2, c2, opt, ProposalKind::CubicHermite)
        .expect("Cubic run failed on oscillatory case");

    print_stats("Example B: oscillatory unimodal, secant on f'(x)", s2_secant);
    print_stats("Example B: oscillatory unimodal, cubic Hermite proposal", s2_cubic);

    // Example C: derivative noise sensitivity demonstration.
    // We intentionally contaminate the derivative with a small perturbation term to illustrate
    // that higher-order interpolation can be more fragile when f'(x) is not perfectly reliable.
    let noise = 1e-6_f64;
    let df2_noisy = move |x: f64| df2(x) + noise * (1000.0 * x).sin();

    let s3_secant_noisy =
        run_search(f2, df2_noisy, a2, b2, c2, opt, ProposalKind::SecantOnDerivative)
            .expect("Secant run failed on noisy-derivative case");
    let s3_cubic_noisy =
        run_search(f2, df2_noisy, a2, b2, c2, opt, ProposalKind::CubicHermite)
            .expect("Cubic run failed on noisy-derivative case");

    print_stats("Example C: oscillatory unimodal with noisy f'(x), secant proposal", s3_secant_noisy);
    print_stats("Example C: oscillatory unimodal with noisy f'(x), cubic Hermite proposal", s3_cubic_noisy);
}
```

Program 10.5.4 demonstrates that higher-order interpolation does not automatically translate into superior performance in practical optimization. Although cubic Hermite models can, in principle, provide more accurate local approximations, their sensitivity to derivative noise and round-off error often limits their effectiveness. In contrast, the secant method relies on a simpler linear model of the derivative, which proves to be more robust under the same safeguards.

The numerical experiments reinforce a recurring theme in numerical optimization: moderate-order models combined with strong acceptance criteria and conservative fallbacks tend to outperform more sophisticated schemes in real computational environments. By embedding both approaches within the same bracketing framework, the program makes clear that reliability depends not only on the order of the interpolation, but also on how aggressively local information is trusted. This insight motivates the continued use of safeguarded secant steps in modern one-dimensional optimization algorithms.

## 10.5.5. Relation to Modern Line Search Methods

The safeguarded secant strategy described above can be viewed as a one-dimensional analogue of modern line search methods used in multidimensional optimization. In both settings, derivative information is employed to guide the search toward a minimizer, but it is never trusted unconditionally. Instead, each proposed step is subjected to acceptance criteria designed to ensure stability, progress, and consistency with the underlying optimization model.

In multidimensional algorithms, line search methods operate along a chosen search direction and seek an appropriate step length that reduces the objective function while maintaining favorable curvature properties. Conditions such as the Armijo and Wolfe criteria formalize this requirement by enforcing sufficient decrease in the objective and preventing steps that are either too short to be effective or too long to remain reliable. Importantly, these conditions are evaluated using both function values and directional derivatives, mirroring the way bracketing and derivative signs are combined in the one-dimensional setting.

The safeguarded secant method plays an analogous role in one dimension. The secant step corresponds to an aggressive, model-based proposal informed by derivative information, while the bracketing constraint and fallback strategies ensure that the algorithm does not lose the minimum or become unstable. Just as line search methods may revert to backtracking or interval reduction when curvature conditions are violated, the one-dimensional algorithm falls back to bisection or golden-section steps when the secant estimate is unreliable.

Recent surveys emphasize that line search methods remain central to modern optimization practice, including large-scale problems in machine learning and data-driven modeling, where step-size selection has a decisive impact on both efficiency and stability (Omesa et al., 2025). In such contexts, exact minimization along a search direction is rarely attempted, as it is computationally expensive and often unnecessary. Instead, inexact but safeguarded searches are used to achieve reliable convergence at acceptable cost.

Seen from this perspective, the one-dimensional secant-based approach outlined in this section embodies the core philosophy of modern line search methods in its simplest form. It demonstrates how derivative information can be leveraged to accelerate convergence without sacrificing robustness, and it provides a conceptual bridge between classical one-dimensional optimization and the line search techniques that underpin many contemporary multidimensional algorithms.

## 10.5.6. Section Summary

The availability of first-derivative information significantly enhances the efficiency of one-dimensional minimization by providing direct guidance toward the minimizer. When used in a safeguarded manner, derivative information allows the algorithm to propose informed trial points that reduce uncertainty more rapidly than interval subdivision alone, often resulting in substantially fewer function evaluations than purely derivative-free methods.

The safeguarded secant line search achieves superlinear convergence in the neighborhood of the minimum while preserving the essential bracketing guarantee enforced by function values. By accepting derivative-based steps only when they remain well inside the current bracket and reverting to conservative updates when necessary, the method combines the acceleration of root-finding techniques with the reliability of bracketing-based search.

This balance between speed and robustness makes derivative-based one-dimensional search particularly effective in practical settings. It remains stable even when derivatives are noisy, approximate, or imperfectly scaled, and it naturally aligns with the design principles of modern line search methods used in higher-dimensional optimization. As a result, safeguarded derivative-based search provides a natural and reliable extension of the methods developed earlier in this chapter.

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/dUi7wFTbfdcN3UR8AImd.7","tags":[]}

# 10.6. Downhill Simplex Method in Multidimensions

In many practical optimization problems, the objective function depends on several variables and derivative information is unavailable, unreliable, or prohibitively expensive to compute. This situation arises frequently when the objective involves experimental measurements, stochastic simulations, legacy code, or complex physical models whose internal structure is inaccessible. In such settings, methods that rely on gradients or Hessians are either impractical or unstable, making derivative-free multidimensional optimization essential.

Among derivative-free methods, the downhill simplex method, also known as the Nelder–Mead method, occupies a prominent position due to its conceptual simplicity and broad practical applicability. Rather than attempting to approximate derivatives, the method explores the objective function directly by comparing function values at a small set of points arranged in a geometric configuration called a simplex. The algorithm then adapts this simplex iteratively, reshaping and translating it so that it drifts toward regions of lower objective value.

Unlike line-search-based or trust-region methods, Nelder–Mead does not explicitly construct local models of the objective function using gradients or curvature. Instead, it relies on simple geometric transformations driven by relative function values. This makes the method easy to implement and relatively insensitive to noise or mild nonsmoothness in the objective. Although it lacks strong global convergence guarantees in higher dimensions and may stagnate on certain pathological problems, it has proven effective in practice for low- to moderate-dimensional optimization, particularly when robustness and ease of use are more important than asymptotic optimality.

## 10.6.1. The Simplex in (n) Dimensions

The central object manipulated by the downhill simplex method is the simplex itself. In an $n$-dimensional space, a simplex is the simplest possible polytope that spans the space locally, consisting of $n+1$ affinely independent vertices. This generalizes familiar geometric objects: in one dimension, a simplex consists of two points forming an interval; in two dimensions, it is a triangle; and in three dimensions, it is a tetrahedron. In higher dimensions, the simplex provides a compact set of sample points that captures local geometric information about the objective function.

Let the simplex be defined by $n+1$ vertices:

$$x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{n+1} \in \mathbb{R}^n \tag{10.6.1}$$

At each iteration, the objective function is evaluated at all vertices, and the vertices are ordered according to their function values,

$$f(x_1) \le f(x_2) \le \cdots \le f(x_{n+1}) \tag{10.6.2}$$

Here, $x_1$ represents the current best point, corresponding to the lowest observed function value, while $x_{n+1}$ represents the worst point. This ordering provides the basis for deciding how the simplex should be modified in the next step.

To guide this modification, the algorithm computes the centroid of the simplex excluding the worst point,

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \tag{10.6.3}$$

The centroid $\bar{x}$ can be interpreted as an estimate of the center of the promising region identified by the current simplex. It serves as a reference point for constructing new trial points intended to replace the worst vertex. By reflecting, expanding, contracting, or shrinking the simplex relative to this centroid, the algorithm systematically explores the local landscape of the objective function while discarding unproductive regions.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.6.1 on the geometric structure of an $n$-dimensional simplex and its ordered evaluation according to Equations (10.6.1)–(10.6.3), Program 10.6.1 provides a concrete implementation of the downhill simplex (Nelder–Mead) method for multidimensional, derivative-free optimization. In many practical settings where gradient information is unavailable or unreliable, optimization must proceed using only function evaluations and simple geometric reasoning. This program translates the conceptual simplex transformations introduced in this section into an explicit algorithmic form, showing how reflection, expansion, contraction, and shrinkage steps are orchestrated to guide the simplex toward regions of lower objective value. The implementation emphasizes clarity and correspondence with the mathematical formulation, making it suitable both as a practical optimization tool and as a pedagogical reference for understanding how the Nelder–Mead method operates in finite-precision environments.

At the core of the implementation is the explicit representation of the simplex as a collection of $n+1$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$, corresponding directly to the vertices $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{n+1}$ defined in Equation (10.6.1). Each vertex is stored as a vector of floating-point values, and a parallel array stores the corresponding objective function values. At every iteration, the simplex is reordered according to Equation (10.6.2), ensuring that the best and worst vertices are always identified consistently. This ordering step is fundamental, as all subsequent geometric transformations depend on the relative ranking of function values rather than their absolute magnitudes.

The centroid computation follows Equation (10.6.3) exactly, forming the average of the best $n$ vertices while excluding the worst one. This centroid represents the algorithm’s current estimate of the most promising region in parameter space and serves as the anchor point for all trial points generated during the iteration. The functions implementing vector addition, subtraction, scaling, and norm evaluation provide the basic linear algebra operations required to express these geometric constructions transparently and without reliance on external libraries.

The main Nelder–Mead iteration logic is implemented in the `nelder_mead` function. After sorting the simplex and computing the centroid, the algorithm first attempts a reflection step, which proposes a new point by mirroring the worst vertex across the centroid. If the reflected point improves the objective value relative to the existing simplex, it may be accepted directly or further extended through an expansion step when the improvement is particularly strong. If reflection fails to produce sufficient improvement, the algorithm instead attempts contraction, either toward the centroid or away from it, depending on the relative quality of the reflected point. When neither reflection nor contraction yields progress, a shrink step is applied, systematically pulling all vertices toward the current best point. These operations collectively implement the geometric update rules described qualitatively in Section 10.6 and ensure that unproductive regions of the search space are progressively discarded.

Termination is governed by two complementary criteria. The first monitors the spread of objective values across the simplex, providing a measure of how similar the function evaluations have become. The second monitors the geometric size of the simplex through the maximum edge length relative to the best vertex. When both measures fall below prescribed tolerances, the algorithm concludes that further progress is unlikely within the limits of finite precision. This dual stopping strategy reflects the practical balance between numerical accuracy and computational efficiency emphasized throughout this chapter.

The `main` function serves as a demonstration driver for the algorithm. It defines a standard test objective, the Rosenbrock function, initializes an initial guess and simplex scale, and then invokes the Nelder–Mead routine with user-controlled tolerances and iteration limits. The reported output allows direct inspection of convergence behavior, iteration count, and final accuracy, illustrating the effectiveness of the method on a smooth but nontrivial optimization problem.

```rust
// Program 10.6.1: Downhill simplex (Nelder–Mead) method in multidimensions
//
// This program implements the Nelder–Mead method as described in Section 10.6.
// A simplex in n dimensions consists of n+1 vertices x_i in R^n (Eq. 10.6.1).
// At each iteration we order vertices by objective value (Eq. 10.6.2),
// compute the centroid of the best n vertices excluding the worst (Eq. 10.6.3),
// then generate trial points by reflection, expansion, contraction, or shrink.
//
// The implementation is fully self-contained and uses only the Rust standard library.
// It includes a demonstrator on the 2D Rosenbrock function.

use std::cmp::Ordering;

#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
struct NMOptions {
    // Standard Nelder–Mead coefficients:
    // reflection (alpha), expansion (gamma), contraction (rho), shrink (sigma).
    alpha: f64,
    gamma: f64,
    rho: f64,
    sigma: f64,

    // Termination thresholds.
    // Stop when the spread of function values is small and the simplex is small.
    f_tol: f64,
    x_tol: f64,

    // Safety.
    max_iters: usize,
}

impl Default for NMOptions {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            alpha: 1.0,
            gamma: 2.0,
            rho: 0.5,
            sigma: 0.5,
            f_tol: 1e-10,
            x_tol: 1e-10,
            max_iters: 20_000,
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
struct NMResult {
    x_best: Vec<f64>,
    f_best: f64,
    iters: usize,
    converged: bool,
}

fn add(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x + y).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn scale(a: &[f64], s: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

fn l2_norm(a: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let ss: f64 = a.iter().map(|v| v * v).sum();
    ss.sqrt()
}

fn centroid_excluding_last(simplex: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<f64> {
    // Assumes simplex is sorted so that the last vertex is the worst.
    let n = simplex[0].len();
    let m = simplex.len() - 1; // exclude worst
    let mut c = vec![0.0; n];
    for i in 0..m {
        for j in 0..n {
            c[j] += simplex[i][j];
        }
    }
    for j in 0..n {
        c[j] /= m as f64; // equals 1/n * sum_{i=1}^n x_i in Eq. (10.6.3)
    }
    c
}

fn max_edge_length(simplex: &[Vec<f64>]) -> f64 {
    // A simple measure of simplex size: maximum distance from best vertex.
    let best = &simplex[0];
    simplex
        .iter()
        .map(|x| l2_norm(&sub(x, best)))
        .fold(0.0, f64::max)
}

fn f_spread(values: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    // Standard deviation of objective values across simplex.
    let m = values.len() as f64;
    let mean = values.iter().sum::<f64>() / m;
    let var = values.iter().map(|v| (v - mean) * (v - mean)).sum::<f64>() / m;
    var.sqrt()
}

fn sort_simplex_by_value(simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>], values: &mut [f64]) {
    // Sort simplex vertices and values together by increasing objective value.
    let mut idx: Vec<usize> = (0..simplex.len()).collect();
    idx.sort_by(|&i, &j| {
        values[i]
            .partial_cmp(&values[j])
            .unwrap_or(Ordering::Equal)
    });

    let new_simplex: Vec<Vec<f64>> = idx.iter().map(|&i| simplex[i].clone()).collect();
    let new_values: Vec<f64> = idx.iter().map(|&i| values[i]).collect();

    simplex.clone_from_slice(&new_simplex);
    values.clone_from_slice(&new_values);
}

fn nelder_mead<F>(
    f: F,
    x0: &[f64],
    step: f64,
    opts: &NMOptions,
) -> NMResult
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let n = x0.len();
    assert!(n >= 1, "Dimension must be at least 1.");
    assert!(step > 0.0, "Initial step must be positive.");

    // Build an initial simplex around x0:
    // vertex 0 is x0, and each subsequent vertex perturbs one coordinate.
    let mut simplex: Vec<Vec<f64>> = Vec::with_capacity(n + 1);
    simplex.push(x0.to_vec());
    for i in 0..n {
        let mut xi = x0.to_vec();
        xi[i] += step;
        simplex.push(xi);
    }

    let mut values: Vec<f64> = simplex.iter().map(|x| f(x)).collect();

    for iter in 0..=opts.max_iters {
        // Order vertices by objective values (Eq. 10.6.2).
        sort_simplex_by_value(&mut simplex, &mut values);

        let f_best = values[0];
        let f_worst = values[n];

        // Termination checks: objective spread and simplex size.
        let spread = f_spread(&values);
        let size = max_edge_length(&simplex);
        if spread <= opts.f_tol && size <= opts.x_tol {
            return NMResult {
                x_best: simplex[0].clone(),
                f_best,
                iters: iter,
                converged: true,
            };
        }

        // Centroid excluding worst vertex (Eq. 10.6.3).
        let x_bar = centroid_excluding_last(&simplex);

        // Reflection: x_r = x_bar + alpha * (x_bar - x_worst)
        let x_worst_vec = &simplex[n];
        let x_bar_minus_worst = sub(&x_bar, x_worst_vec);
        let x_r = add(&x_bar, &scale(&x_bar_minus_worst, opts.alpha));
        let f_r = f(&x_r);

        let f_second_worst = values[n - 1];

        if f_best <= f_r && f_r < f_second_worst {
            // Accept reflection.
            simplex[n] = x_r;
            values[n] = f_r;
            continue;
        }

        if f_r < f_best {
            // Expansion: x_e = x_bar + gamma * (x_r - x_bar)
            let x_r_minus_bar = sub(&x_r, &x_bar);
            let x_e = add(&x_bar, &scale(&x_r_minus_bar, opts.gamma));
            let f_e = f(&x_e);

            if f_e < f_r {
                simplex[n] = x_e;
                values[n] = f_e;
            } else {
                simplex[n] = x_r;
                values[n] = f_r;
            }
            continue;
        }

        // Contraction cases.
        if f_r < f_worst {
            // Outside contraction: x_oc = x_bar + rho * (x_r - x_bar)
            let x_r_minus_bar = sub(&x_r, &x_bar);
            let x_oc = add(&x_bar, &scale(&x_r_minus_bar, opts.rho));
            let f_oc = f(&x_oc);

            if f_oc <= f_r {
                simplex[n] = x_oc;
                values[n] = f_oc;
                continue;
            }
        } else {
            // Inside contraction: x_ic = x_bar - rho * (x_bar - x_worst)
            let x_ic = sub(&x_bar, &scale(&x_bar_minus_worst, opts.rho));
            let f_ic = f(&x_ic);

            if f_ic < f_worst {
                simplex[n] = x_ic;
                values[n] = f_ic;
                continue;
            }
        }

        // Shrink: x_i = x_best + sigma * (x_i - x_best) for i = 1..n
        let x_best_vec = simplex[0].clone();
        for i in 1..=n {
            let delta = sub(&simplex[i], &x_best_vec);
            simplex[i] = add(&x_best_vec, &scale(&delta, opts.sigma));
            values[i] = f(&simplex[i]);
        }
    }

    // If we fall through, return the best found so far.
    sort_simplex_by_value(&mut simplex, &mut values);
    NMResult {
        x_best: simplex[0].clone(),
        f_best: values[0],
        iters: opts.max_iters,
        converged: false,
    }
}

// Demonstration objective: Rosenbrock function in 2D (generalized to nD).
// f(x) = sum_{i=1}^{n-1} [100 (x_{i+1} - x_i^2)^2 + (1 - x_i)^2]
fn rosenbrock(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    if x.len() < 2 {
        return 0.0;
    }
    let mut s = 0.0;
    for i in 0..(x.len() - 1) {
        let a = x[i + 1] - x[i] * x[i];
        let b = 1.0 - x[i];
        s += 100.0 * a * a + b * b;
    }
    s
}

fn main() {
    // Starting point and initial simplex scale.
    let x0 = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let step = 0.5;

    let opts = NMOptions {
        f_tol: 1e-12,
        x_tol: 1e-10,
        max_iters: 50_000,
        ..NMOptions::default()
    };

    let result = nelder_mead(rosenbrock, &x0, step, &opts);

    println!("Nelder–Mead result");
    println!("  converged: {}", result.converged);
    println!("  iters:     {}", result.iters);
    println!("  x_best:    {:?}", result.x_best);
    println!("  f_best:    {:.16e}", result.f_best);

    // For Rosenbrock, the minimizer is at x = (1, 1, ..., 1) with f = 0.
}
```

Program 10.6.1 demonstrates how the downhill simplex method translates geometric intuition into a practical algorithm for multidimensional optimization without derivatives. By operating solely on function values and simplex transformations, the method provides a robust alternative when gradient-based approaches are infeasible or unreliable. The explicit correspondence between the code structure and Equations (10.6.1)–(10.6.3) reinforces the conceptual foundations of the algorithm and clarifies how each geometric operation contributes to progress toward a minimizer.

The example illustrates both the strengths and limitations of Nelder–Mead. On smooth, low-dimensional problems such as the Rosenbrock function, the method converges reliably and with minimal implementation complexity. At the same time, the reliance on heuristic stopping criteria and geometric updates highlights why the method may stagnate or lose efficiency in higher dimensions or on poorly scaled problems. These characteristics motivate the use of restart strategies, hybrid methods, or alternative derivative-free approaches in more demanding applications.

The modular structure of the implementation makes it straightforward to adapt the framework to other objective functions, termination rules, or simplex initialization strategies. This flexibility provides a natural foundation for exploring enhancements such as adaptive parameter tuning, noise handling, or integration with global search techniques, which are increasingly important in modern optimization practice.

## 10.6.2. Reflection Step

The first and most basic operation in the Nelder–Mead method is reflection. Reflection is designed to move the simplex away from regions associated with poor objective values and toward regions that appear more promising, without changing the overall scale of the simplex. Intuitively, the worst vertex is “flipped” across the centroid of the remaining vertices, producing a trial point on the opposite side of the simplex.

Formally, the reflected point is obtained by reflecting the worst vertex $x_{n+1}$ through the centroid $\bar{x}$,

$$x_r = \bar{x} + \alpha (\bar{x} - x_{n+1}), \tag{10.6.4}$$

where $\alpha > 0$ is the reflection coefficient, typically chosen as $\alpha = 1$. With this choice, the reflected point lies at the same distance from the centroid as the worst vertex, but in the opposite direction. Geometrically, this corresponds to a mirror image of the worst point across the face of the simplex defined by the remaining vertices.

The objective function is then evaluated at the reflected point $x_r$. The role of reflection is exploratory: it tests whether simply moving away from the worst region leads to improvement. If the reflected point is better than the second-worst vertex but not better than the best vertex, that is, if:

$$f(x_1) \le f(x_r) < f(x_n) \tag{10.6.5}$$

then the reflection is deemed successful but not exceptional. In this case, the reflected point replaces the worst vertex, and the iteration is complete. The simplex is thus translated in a downhill direction while retaining roughly the same size and shape.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.6.2 on the role of reflection as the fundamental exploratory mechanism in the downhill simplex method, Program 10.6.2 provides a focused implementation of the reflection step in isolation. Reflection constitutes the first response of the algorithm to poor objective values, attempting to improve the simplex by geometrically inverting the worst vertex across the centroid of the remaining vertices. This program translates the geometric construction defined by Equations (10.6.3) and (10.6.4) into executable form, showing how a reflected trial point is generated, evaluated, and conditionally accepted according to the criterion in Equation (10.6.5). By separating reflection from the other Nelder–Mead operations, the implementation clarifies its role as a conservative yet essential step that reorients the simplex without altering its overall scale.

At the core of the implementation is an explicit representation of the simplex as a collection of $n+1$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$, together with a corresponding list of objective function values. The simplex is first ordered according to Equation (10.6.2), ensuring that the best, second-worst, and worst vertices are consistently identified. This ordering is crucial, as the reflection step operates specifically on the worst vertex $x_{n+1}$ while using information from the remaining vertices to guide the search.

The centroid computation follows Equation (10.6.3) exactly, forming the arithmetic mean of the best $n$ vertices while excluding the worst one. This centroid $\bar{x}$ represents the geometric center of the currently promising region of the simplex and serves as the reference point for constructing the reflected trial point. The supporting vector operations for addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication are implemented explicitly to mirror the algebraic structure of the reflection formula and to keep the correspondence between code and mathematics transparent.

The reflected point is constructed according to Equation (10.6.4) by moving from the centroid in the direction opposite to the worst vertex, scaled by the reflection coefficient $\alpha$. With the standard choice $\alpha = 1$, the reflected point lies at the same distance from the centroid as the discarded vertex, but on the opposite side of the simplex. The objective function is then evaluated at this reflected point to assess whether the simple geometric inversion leads to improvement.

Acceptance of the reflection is governed by the condition in Equation (10.6.5). If the reflected point improves upon the worst vertex but does not outperform the current best vertex, it is considered a successful yet moderate improvement. In this case, the reflected point replaces the worst vertex, and the simplex is updated accordingly. If the condition is not satisfied, the reflection is rejected, signaling that more aggressive or conservative steps such as expansion or contraction must be considered in subsequent stages of the algorithm.

The `main` function serves as a minimal demonstration of this logic using a two-dimensional test objective. It constructs an initial simplex, performs a single reflection step, and prints the resulting simplex after reordering. This controlled setup allows the effect of reflection alone to be examined without interference from the other Nelder–Mead transformations.

```rust
// Program 10.6.2: Nelder–Mead Reflection Step
//
// This program isolates the reflection operation described in Section 10.6.2.
// Given an n-dimensional simplex with n+1 vertices (Eq. 10.6.1) ordered by
// objective value (Eq. 10.6.2), it computes the centroid of the best n vertices
// (Eq. 10.6.3) and forms the reflected point
//
//     x_r = x̄ + α (x̄ - x_{n+1})    (Eq. 10.6.4)
//
// It then applies the acceptance condition
//
//     f(x_1) ≤ f(x_r) < f(x_n)      (Eq. 10.6.5)
//
// If the condition holds, x_r replaces the worst vertex and the reflection step
// completes one Nelder–Mead iteration.

use std::cmp::Ordering;

fn add(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x + y).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn scale(a: &[f64], s: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

fn centroid_excluding_worst(sorted_simplex: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<f64> {
    // Computes x̄ = (1/n) * sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i (Eq. 10.6.3),
    // assuming sorted_simplex is ordered so the worst point is last.
    let n = sorted_simplex[0].len();
    let m = sorted_simplex.len() - 1; // exclude worst
    let mut c = vec![0.0; n];
    for i in 0..m {
        for j in 0..n {
            c[j] += sorted_simplex[i][j];
        }
    }
    for j in 0..n {
        c[j] /= m as f64;
    }
    c
}

fn sort_by_value<F>(simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>], values: &mut [f64], f: &F)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    // Evaluate if not already consistent, then sort vertices and values together.
    for (i, x) in simplex.iter().enumerate() {
        values[i] = f(x);
    }

    let mut idx: Vec<usize> = (0..simplex.len()).collect();
    idx.sort_by(|&i, &j| values[i].partial_cmp(&values[j]).unwrap_or(Ordering::Equal));

    let new_simplex: Vec<Vec<f64>> = idx.iter().map(|&i| simplex[i].clone()).collect();
    let new_values: Vec<f64> = idx.iter().map(|&i| values[i]).collect();

    simplex.clone_from_slice(&new_simplex);
    values.clone_from_slice(&new_values);
}

fn reflect_point(x_bar: &[f64], x_worst: &[f64], alpha: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x_r = x̄ + α (x̄ - x_{n+1})  (Eq. 10.6.4)
    let direction = sub(x_bar, x_worst);
    add(x_bar, &scale(&direction, alpha))
}

#[derive(Debug)]
enum ReflectionOutcome {
    // Reflection accepted and worst vertex replaced.
    Accepted,
    // Reflection rejected under Eq. (10.6.5); caller would proceed to expansion or contraction.
    Rejected,
}

fn reflection_step<F>(
    simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>],
    alpha: f64,
    f: &F,
) -> (ReflectionOutcome, Vec<f64>)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let n = simplex[0].len();
    assert_eq!(simplex.len(), n + 1, "Simplex must contain n+1 vertices.");

    // Values will be recomputed and then kept in sorted order.
    let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex.len()];
    sort_by_value(simplex, &mut values, f);

    // Identify best, second-worst, and worst.
    // After sorting: 0 is best (x_1), n-1 is second-worst (x_n), n is worst (x_{n+1}).
    let f_best = values[0];      // f(x_1)
    let f_second_worst = values[n - 1]; // f(x_n)
    let x_worst = simplex[n].clone();

    // Compute centroid excluding worst (Eq. 10.6.3).
    let x_bar = centroid_excluding_worst(simplex);

    // Form reflected point (Eq. 10.6.4).
    let x_r = reflect_point(&x_bar, &x_worst, alpha);
    let f_r = f(&x_r);

    // Apply acceptance condition (Eq. 10.6.5):
    //     f(x_1) ≤ f(x_r) < f(x_n)
    if f_best <= f_r && f_r < f_second_worst {
        simplex[n] = x_r.clone(); // replace worst
        (ReflectionOutcome::Accepted, x_r)
    } else {
        (ReflectionOutcome::Rejected, x_r)
    }
}

// Demonstration objective: Rosenbrock in 2D.
fn rosenbrock(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let a = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
    let b = 1.0 - x[0];
    100.0 * a * a + b * b
}

fn main() {
    // A 2D simplex has 3 vertices. These are intentionally not optimal.
    // The reflection step will attempt to improve the worst vertex by reflecting it about the centroid.
    let mut simplex = vec![
        vec![-1.2, 1.0],  // candidate vertex
        vec![-1.0, 0.8],  // candidate vertex
        vec![0.0, 0.0],   // candidate vertex (often not good for Rosenbrock)
    ];

    let alpha = 1.0; // typical choice in Eq. (10.6.4)

    println!("Initial simplex:");
    for (i, x) in simplex.iter().enumerate() {
        println!("  v{} = {:?}, f = {:.6e}", i, x, rosenbrock(x));
    }

    let (outcome, x_r) = reflection_step(&mut simplex, alpha, &rosenbrock);

    // Recompute values and sort again so output matches Eq. (10.6.2).
    let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex.len()];
    sort_by_value(&mut simplex, &mut values, &rosenbrock);

    println!("\nReflected point x_r (Eq. 10.6.4): {:?}, f = {:.6e}", x_r, rosenbrock(&x_r));
    println!("Reflection outcome under Eq. 10.6.5: {:?}", outcome);

    println!("\nSimplex after reflection step:");
    for (i, x) in simplex.iter().enumerate() {
        println!("  v{} = {:?}, f = {:.6e}", i, x, rosenbrock(x));
    }

    if let ReflectionOutcome::Rejected = outcome {
        println!("\nNote: When reflection is rejected, Nelder–Mead proceeds to expansion or contraction.");
    }
}
```

Program 10.6.2 illustrates how the reflection step implements the most basic corrective action in the downhill simplex method. By reflecting the worst vertex across the centroid, the algorithm tests whether a simple geometric reversal is sufficient to move the simplex toward lower objective values. This operation preserves the overall scale of the simplex while redirecting its orientation, making reflection a conservative yet effective exploratory step.

The example demonstrates that reflection can yield meaningful improvement without invoking more aggressive strategies. At the same time, the explicit acceptance criterion highlights the algorithm’s built-in safeguards against overcommitting to trial points that may be either insufficiently good or excessively optimistic. This balance between exploration and restraint underlies the robustness of the Nelder–Mead method in low-dimensional, derivative-free optimization.

By isolating reflection as a standalone procedure, this program prepares the ground for understanding the subsequent expansion and contraction steps. Together, these transformations form a hierarchy of responses that allow the simplex to adapt dynamically to the local structure of the objective function.

## 10.6.3. Expansion Step

If the reflected point yields an improvement even over the current best point,

$$f(x_r) < f(x_1) \tag{10.6.6}$$

this indicates that the direction of reflection points strongly downhill and that additional progress may be possible by moving further in the same direction. In response, the algorithm attempts an expansion step, which allows the simplex to extend outward and accelerate movement toward a region of lower objective value.

The expansion point is defined as:

$$x_e = \bar{x} + \gamma (x_r - \bar{x}) \tag{10.6.7}$$

where $\gamma > 1$ is the expansion coefficient, commonly chosen as $\gamma = 2$. This construction places $x_e$ beyond the reflected point, along the ray emanating from the centroid through $x_r$. The expansion step therefore increases the scale of the simplex in a promising direction, enabling faster progress when the objective function exhibits a clear downhill trend.

The function value at the expansion point is then evaluated. If,

$$f(x_e) < f(x_r) \tag{10.6.8}$$

the expanded point represents a genuine improvement and replaces the worst vertex. Otherwise, the reflected point $x_r$ is retained instead. In this way, the expansion step is opportunistic but cautious: it exploits favorable directions without committing to overly aggressive moves unless they are clearly beneficial.

Together, the reflection and expansion steps provide the primary mechanism by which the Nelder–Mead method explores the objective function. Reflection enables steady progress away from poor regions, while expansion allows rapid advancement when the landscape strongly favors a particular direction.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.6.3 on the role of expansion as an acceleration mechanism in the downhill simplex method, Program 10.6.3 provides a concrete implementation of the expansion step that follows a successful reflection. While reflection alone is sufficient to steer the simplex away from unfavorable regions, exceptionally strong improvement indicates that the objective function decreases rapidly along the reflected direction. In such cases, expansion allows the algorithm to exploit this favorable geometry by extending the simplex outward beyond the reflected point. This program translates the mathematical conditions expressed in Equations (10.6.6)–(10.6.8) into executable logic, demonstrating how the algorithm distinguishes between ordinary improvement and situations where a more aggressive step is justified. The implementation emphasizes clarity and traceability between the mathematical formulation and the resulting algorithmic decisions.

At the core of the implementation is an explicit representation of the simplex as a collection of $n+1$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$, together with an ordering of these vertices according to their objective function values as prescribed by Equation (10.6.2). This ordering ensures that the best vertex $x_1$ and the worst vertex $x_{n+1}$ are unambiguously identified at each step, which is essential for deciding whether expansion should be attempted.

The centroid computation follows Equation (10.6.3) exactly, forming the arithmetic mean of the best $n$ vertices while excluding the worst one. This centroid $\bar{x}$ serves as the geometric reference point for both reflection and expansion. Supporting vector operations for addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication are implemented explicitly to mirror the algebraic structure of the formulas and to maintain a transparent correspondence between code and theory.

The expansion logic is encapsulated in a function that first performs reflection using Equation (10.6.4) and evaluates the resulting reflected point $x_r$. The trigger condition for expansion, given by Equation (10.6.6), is then tested by comparing $f(x_r)$ against the current best value $f(x_1)$. Only when this condition is satisfied does the algorithm proceed to compute the expansion point $x_e$ using Equation (10.6.7). This design reflects the opportunistic nature of expansion: it is attempted only when reflection produces an improvement that surpasses the best point already known.

Once the expansion point is computed, its objective value is evaluated and compared to that of the reflected point according to Equation (10.6.8). If the expansion yields further improvement, the expanded point replaces the worst vertex in the simplex. Otherwise, the algorithm retains the reflected point instead. This conditional acceptance ensures that expansion accelerates convergence only when it is demonstrably beneficial and avoids destabilizing the simplex through unnecessarily large steps.

The `main` function serves as a controlled demonstration of this logic. A simple test objective is chosen to produce a clear downhill direction, ensuring that the expansion conditions are satisfied and that the acceptance of the expanded point can be observed directly. After the expansion decision is made, the simplex is reordered for reporting so that the printed output remains consistent with the ordering assumed throughout Section 10.6.

```rust
// Program 10.6.3: Nelder–Mead Expansion Step (textbook version)
//
// This program demonstrates the expansion mechanism described in Section 10.6.3.
// Given an n-dimensional simplex with vertices x_1, ..., x_{n+1} (Eq. 10.6.1)
// ordered by objective value (Eq. 10.6.2), it computes the centroid excluding
// the worst vertex (Eq. 10.6.3), forms the reflected point x_r (Eq. 10.6.4),
// and tests whether reflection is exceptionally good:
//
//     f(x_r) < f(x_1)  (Eq. 10.6.6)
//
// If Eq. (10.6.6) holds, the algorithm constructs the expansion point x_e
// (Eq. 10.6.7) and accepts it if:
//
//     f(x_e) < f(x_r)  (Eq. 10.6.8)
//
// Otherwise, it retains x_r. The updated simplex is then re-sorted so that the
// reporting order matches Eq. (10.6.2).
//
// To make the expansion logic visible in a deterministic way, the demonstration
// uses a shifted quadratic objective with a minimizer far from the initial
// simplex. This produces a clear downhill direction where expansion is often
// beneficial, so Eq. (10.6.6) and Eq. (10.6.8) can be observed directly.

use std::cmp::Ordering;

fn add(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x + y).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn scale(a: &[f64], s: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

fn centroid_excluding_worst(sorted_simplex: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x̄ = (1/n) * sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i  (Eq. 10.6.3),
    // assuming the simplex is sorted so that x_{n+1} is last.
    let n = sorted_simplex[0].len();
    let m = sorted_simplex.len() - 1; // exclude worst
    let mut c = vec![0.0; n];
    for i in 0..m {
        for j in 0..n {
            c[j] += sorted_simplex[i][j];
        }
    }
    for j in 0..n {
        c[j] /= m as f64;
    }
    c
}

fn sort_simplex_by_value<F>(simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>], values: &mut [f64], f: &F)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    for (i, x) in simplex.iter().enumerate() {
        values[i] = f(x);
    }

    let mut idx: Vec<usize> = (0..simplex.len()).collect();
    idx.sort_by(|&i, &j| values[i].partial_cmp(&values[j]).unwrap_or(Ordering::Equal));

    let new_simplex: Vec<Vec<f64>> = idx.iter().map(|&i| simplex[i].clone()).collect();
    let new_values: Vec<f64> = idx.iter().map(|&i| values[i]).collect();

    simplex.clone_from_slice(&new_simplex);
    values.clone_from_slice(&new_values);
}

fn reflect_point(x_bar: &[f64], x_worst: &[f64], alpha: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x_r = x̄ + α (x̄ - x_{n+1})  (Eq. 10.6.4)
    let dir = sub(x_bar, x_worst);
    add(x_bar, &scale(&dir, alpha))
}

fn expand_point(x_bar: &[f64], x_r: &[f64], gamma: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x_e = x̄ + γ (x_r - x̄)  (Eq. 10.6.7)
    let dir = sub(x_r, x_bar);
    add(x_bar, &scale(&dir, gamma))
}

#[derive(Debug)]
enum ExpansionDecision {
    // Eq. (10.6.6) failed, so expansion is not attempted.
    NoExpansionAttempted,
    // Eq. (10.6.8) held, so x_e replaces the worst vertex.
    ExpansionAccepted,
    // Eq. (10.6.8) failed, so x_r replaces the worst vertex.
    ReflectionRetained,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
struct StepReport {
    x_bar: Vec<f64>,
    x_r: Vec<f64>,
    f_r: f64,
    x_e: Option<Vec<f64>>,
    f_e: Option<f64>,
    decision: ExpansionDecision,
}

fn reflection_and_expansion_step<F>(
    simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>],
    alpha: f64,
    gamma: f64,
    f: &F,
) -> StepReport
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let n = simplex[0].len();
    assert_eq!(simplex.len(), n + 1, "Simplex must contain n+1 vertices.");
    assert!(alpha > 0.0, "alpha must be positive.");
    assert!(gamma > 1.0, "gamma must exceed 1.");

    let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex.len()];
    sort_simplex_by_value(simplex, &mut values, f);

    // After sorting, x_1 is best and x_{n+1} is worst.
    let f_best = values[0];
    let x_worst = simplex[n].clone();

    let x_bar = centroid_excluding_worst(simplex);

    // Reflection (Eq. 10.6.4)
    let x_r = reflect_point(&x_bar, &x_worst, alpha);
    let f_r = f(&x_r);

    // Trigger for expansion (Eq. 10.6.6)
    if !(f_r < f_best) {
        return StepReport {
            x_bar,
            x_r,
            f_r,
            x_e: None,
            f_e: None,
            decision: ExpansionDecision::NoExpansionAttempted,
        };
    }

    // Expansion (Eq. 10.6.7)
    let x_e = expand_point(&x_bar, &x_r, gamma);
    let f_e = f(&x_e);

    // Acceptance (Eq. 10.6.8)
    if f_e < f_r {
        simplex[n] = x_e.clone();
        StepReport {
            x_bar,
            x_r,
            f_r,
            x_e: Some(x_e),
            f_e: Some(f_e),
            decision: ExpansionDecision::ExpansionAccepted,
        }
    } else {
        simplex[n] = x_r.clone();
        StepReport {
            x_bar,
            x_r,
            f_r,
            x_e: Some(x_e),
            f_e: Some(f_e),
            decision: ExpansionDecision::ReflectionRetained,
        }
    }
}

// Demonstration objective: shifted quadratic bowl.
// f(x) = ||x - m||^2 with minimizer at m.
fn shifted_quadratic(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let m = [-10.0, -10.0];
    let dx0 = x[0] - m[0];
    let dx1 = x[1] - m[1];
    dx0 * dx0 + dx1 * dx1
}

fn print_simplex(title: &str, simplex: &[Vec<f64>], f: &dyn Fn(&[f64]) -> f64) {
    println!("{title}");
    for (i, x) in simplex.iter().enumerate() {
        println!("  v{} = [{:.6}, {:.6}], f = {:.6e}", i, x[0], x[1], f(x));
    }
}

fn main() {
    // A 2D simplex has three vertices. These points are chosen so that reflection
    // produces a strong improvement over the current best, making Eq. (10.6.6)
    // true and allowing the expansion test Eq. (10.6.8) to be observed.
    let mut simplex = vec![
        vec![-1.0, 0.0],  // relatively poor (far from (-10, -10))
        vec![0.0, -1.0],  // relatively poor
        vec![3.0, 3.0],   // very poor, expected to be worst
    ];

    let alpha = 1.0; // reflection coefficient in Eq. (10.6.4)
    let gamma = 2.0; // expansion coefficient in Eq. (10.6.7)

    print_simplex("Initial simplex:", &simplex, &shifted_quadratic);

    let report = reflection_and_expansion_step(&mut simplex, alpha, gamma, &shifted_quadratic);

    // Re-sort for reporting so that the printed order matches Eq. (10.6.2).
    let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex.len()];
    sort_simplex_by_value(&mut simplex, &mut values, &shifted_quadratic);

    println!("\nCentroid x̄ (Eq. 10.6.3): [{:.6}, {:.6}]", report.x_bar[0], report.x_bar[1]);
    println!(
        "Reflected point x_r (Eq. 10.6.4): [{:.6}, {:.6}], f = {:.6e}",
        report.x_r[0], report.x_r[1], report.f_r
    );

    match (&report.x_e, report.f_e) {
        (Some(xe), Some(fe)) => {
            println!(
                "Expansion point x_e (Eq. 10.6.7): [{:.6}, {:.6}], f = {:.6e}",
                xe[0], xe[1], fe
            );
        }
        _ => {
            println!("Expansion point x_e (Eq. 10.6.7): not computed (Eq. 10.6.6 not satisfied).");
        }
    }

    println!("Decision: {:?}\n", report.decision);

    print_simplex("Simplex after reflection/expansion step:", &simplex, &shifted_quadratic);

    println!("\nInterpretation:");
```
