```rust
    println!("  Eq. (10.6.6) controls whether expansion is attempted at all.");
    println!("  Eq. (10.6.8) determines whether the expanded point is accepted over the reflected point.");
}
```

Program 10.6.3 illustrates how the expansion step enhances the exploratory capability of the downhill simplex method when reflection reveals a strongly favorable direction. By extending the simplex beyond the reflected point, expansion allows rapid progress toward regions of lower objective value while preserving the method’s derivative-free character. The explicit trigger and acceptance conditions encoded in Equations (10.6.6) and (10.6.8) ensure that this acceleration is applied selectively rather than indiscriminately.

The example highlights the complementary roles of reflection and expansion within the Nelder–Mead framework. Reflection provides steady, conservative progress away from poor regions, while expansion exploits clear downhill trends to accelerate convergence. Together, these steps form the primary mechanism by which the simplex adapts its position and scale in response to the local structure of the objective function.

The modular structure of the implementation makes it straightforward to integrate expansion with the contraction and shrinkage steps discussed in subsequent sections. This progression completes the set of geometric transformations that define the downhill simplex method and prepares the ground for analyzing its convergence behavior and practical limitations.

## 10.6.4. Contraction Step

When reflection fails to produce sufficient improvement, the algorithm interprets this outcome as evidence that the simplex may be too large or poorly positioned relative to the local structure of the objective function. In such cases, a contraction step is used to reduce the simplex scale while still probing for a better point than the current worst vertex. Contraction is therefore a corrective operation, designed to temper overly aggressive exploration without abandoning the current search region entirely.

Formally, a contraction step is attempted when

$$f(x_r) \ge f(x_n) \tag{10.6.9}$$

indicating that the reflected point is no better than the second-worst vertex. Two distinct forms of contraction are employed, depending on how poor the reflected point is relative to the worst vertex.

*Outside contraction* is used when the reflected point is worse than the second-worst vertex but still better than the worst vertex,

$$f(x_n) \le f(x_r) < f(x_{n+1}) \tag{10.6.10}$$

In this case, the direction of reflection is not entirely unpromising, but the full reflection step appears too large. The algorithm therefore constructs a contracted point partway between the centroid and the reflected point,

$$x_c = \bar{x} + \beta (x_r - \bar{x}) \tag{10.6.11}$$

where $0 < \beta < 1$, typically $\beta = \tfrac{1}{2}$. Geometrically, this reduces the step length in the reflection direction, allowing the algorithm to probe the same general direction more cautiously.

*Inside contraction* is used when the reflected point is even worse than the worst vertex,

$$f(x_r) \ge f(x_{n+1}) \tag{10.6.12}$$

This situation suggests that moving away from the simplex in the reflection direction is actively harmful. The algorithm then contracts inward, toward the centroid, by constructing

$$x_c = \bar{x} - \beta (\bar{x} - x_{n+1}) \tag{10.6.13}$$

which lies between the centroid and the worst vertex. This step reduces the simplex size and pulls the search back toward the region defined by the better vertices.

In both cases, the contraction step is accepted only if it produces a meaningful improvement. Specifically, the contracted point replaces the worst vertex if:

$$f(x_c) < \min\{f(x_r), f(x_{n+1})\} \tag{10.6.14}$$

If this condition is not satisfied, the contraction is deemed unsuccessful, and a more drastic corrective action is required.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.6.4 on the limitations of reflection and expansion when the simplex becomes poorly scaled or misaligned with the local structure of the objective function, Program 10.6.4 provides a concrete implementation of the contraction step in the downhill simplex method. Contraction serves as a corrective mechanism that reduces the effective size of the simplex while preserving its orientation relative to promising regions of the search space. When reflection fails to yield sufficient improvement, contraction allows the algorithm to probe more cautiously, either by shortening the reflection step or by pulling the simplex inward toward its centroid. This program translates the case distinctions and acceptance criteria defined in Equations (10.6.9)–(10.6.14) into executable logic, clarifying how the Nelder–Mead method responds when aggressive exploratory moves prove ineffective.

At the core of the implementation is an explicit representation of the simplex as $n+1$ vertices in $\mathbb{R}^n$, ordered according to their objective function values as prescribed by Equation (10.6.2). This ordering identifies the second-worst vertex $x_n$ and the worst vertex $x_{n+1}$, which are central to determining whether contraction should be attempted. As in the preceding steps, the centroid $\bar{x}$ is computed from the best $n$ vertices according to Equation (10.6.3), providing the geometric reference point for subsequent transformations.

The contraction logic is activated only when the reflected point fails to improve upon the second-worst vertex, as specified by Equation (10.6.9). This condition signals that the simplex may be too large or that the reflection direction is not well aligned with the local descent structure. The program explicitly evaluates this condition before proceeding, ensuring that contraction is used only when reflection has been exhausted as a viable option.

Once contraction is triggered, the implementation distinguishes between outside and inside contraction based on the relative quality of the reflected point. When the reflected point is worse than the second-worst vertex but still better than the worst vertex, the program applies outside contraction as defined in Equations (10.6.10) and (10.6.11). This case reduces the step length along the reflection direction, probing the same general direction more conservatively. When the reflected point is even worse than the worst vertex, inside contraction is selected according to Equations (10.6.12) and (10.6.13), contracting the simplex inward toward the centroid and away from the unproductive region.

In both cases, the contracted point $x_c$ is evaluated and accepted only if it satisfies the improvement criterion in Equation (10.6.14). If this condition holds, the contracted point replaces the worst vertex in the simplex. Otherwise, the contraction is deemed unsuccessful, indicating that even cautious probing fails to improve the current configuration and that a more drastic correction, such as shrinkage, is required. The program records both the contraction type and the acceptance decision explicitly, making the algorithm’s control flow transparent.

The `main` function provides a concrete demonstration of this logic using a representative test objective. The initial simplex is chosen so that reflection performs poorly, thereby reliably triggering contraction. The printed output reports the centroid, reflected point, contracted point, and final decision, allowing the reader to trace the algorithm’s behavior step by step and to verify the correspondence between the numerical results and Equations (10.6.9)–(10.6.14).

```rust
// Program 10.6.4: Nelder–Mead Contraction Step (outside and inside contraction)
//
// This program isolates the contraction logic described in Section 10.6.4.
// Starting from a simplex with n+1 vertices x_1, ..., x_{n+1} (Eq. 10.6.1)
// ordered by objective value (Eq. 10.6.2), it computes the centroid excluding
// the worst vertex (Eq. 10.6.3), forms the reflected point x_r (Eq. 10.6.4),
// and then, when reflection fails to be sufficiently good, attempts contraction.
//
// Contraction is triggered when
//
//     f(x_r) >= f(x_n)                      (Eq. 10.6.9)
//
// and splits into two cases:
//
// Outside contraction when
//
//     f(x_n) <= f(x_r) < f(x_{n+1})         (Eq. 10.6.10)
//
// with contracted point
//
//     x_c = x̄ + β (x_r - x̄)                (Eq. 10.6.11)
//
// Inside contraction when
//
//     f(x_r) >= f(x_{n+1})                  (Eq. 10.6.12)
//
// with contracted point
//
//     x_c = x̄ - β (x̄ - x_{n+1})            (Eq. 10.6.13)
//
// In both cases, contraction is accepted only if
//
//     f(x_c) < min{ f(x_r), f(x_{n+1}) }    (Eq. 10.6.14)
//
// Otherwise the step is reported as unsuccessful, and Nelder–Mead would proceed
// to the shrink operation (Section 10.6.5).

use std::cmp::Ordering;

fn add(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x + y).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn scale(a: &[f64], s: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

fn centroid_excluding_worst(sorted_simplex: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x̄ = (1/n) * sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i  (Eq. 10.6.3),
    // assuming the simplex is sorted so that x_{n+1} is last.
    let n = sorted_simplex[0].len();
    let m = sorted_simplex.len() - 1; // exclude worst
    let mut c = vec![0.0; n];
    for i in 0..m {
        for j in 0..n {
            c[j] += sorted_simplex[i][j];
        }
    }
    for j in 0..n {
        c[j] /= m as f64;
    }
    c
}

fn sort_simplex_by_value<F>(simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>], values: &mut [f64], f: &F)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    for (i, x) in simplex.iter().enumerate() {
        values[i] = f(x);
    }

    let mut idx: Vec<usize> = (0..simplex.len()).collect();
    idx.sort_by(|&i, &j| values[i].partial_cmp(&values[j]).unwrap_or(Ordering::Equal));

    let new_simplex: Vec<Vec<f64>> = idx.iter().map(|&i| simplex[i].clone()).collect();
    let new_values: Vec<f64> = idx.iter().map(|&i| values[i]).collect();

    simplex.clone_from_slice(&new_simplex);
    values.clone_from_slice(&new_values);
}

fn reflect_point(x_bar: &[f64], x_worst: &[f64], alpha: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x_r = x̄ + α (x̄ - x_{n+1})  (Eq. 10.6.4)
    let dir = sub(x_bar, x_worst);
    add(x_bar, &scale(&dir, alpha))
}

fn outside_contraction(x_bar: &[f64], x_r: &[f64], beta: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x_c = x̄ + β (x_r - x̄)  (Eq. 10.6.11)
    let dir = sub(x_r, x_bar);
    add(x_bar, &scale(&dir, beta))
}

fn inside_contraction(x_bar: &[f64], x_worst: &[f64], beta: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x_c = x̄ - β (x̄ - x_{n+1})  (Eq. 10.6.13)
    let dir = sub(x_bar, x_worst); // (x̄ - x_{n+1})
    sub(x_bar, &scale(&dir, beta))
}

#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone)]
enum ContractionCase {
    Outside, // Eq. (10.6.10) -> Eq. (10.6.11)
    Inside,  // Eq. (10.6.12) -> Eq. (10.6.13)
}

#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone)]
enum ContractionDecision {
    NotTriggered,                  // Eq. (10.6.9) not satisfied
    Accepted(ContractionCase),
    Unsuccessful(ContractionCase), // Eq. (10.6.14) failed
}

#[derive(Debug)]
struct StepReport {
    x_bar: Vec<f64>,
    x_r: Vec<f64>,
    f_r: f64,
    x_c: Option<Vec<f64>>,
    f_c: Option<f64>,
    case: Option<ContractionCase>,
    decision: ContractionDecision,
}

fn contraction_step<F>(
    simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>],
    alpha: f64,
    beta: f64,
    f: &F,
) -> StepReport
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let n = simplex[0].len();
    assert_eq!(simplex.len(), n + 1, "Simplex must contain n+1 vertices.");
    assert!(alpha > 0.0, "alpha must be positive.");
    assert!(beta > 0.0 && beta < 1.0, "beta must satisfy 0 < beta < 1.");

    let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex.len()];
    sort_simplex_by_value(simplex, &mut values, f);

    // After sorting: x_1 is best, x_n is second-worst, x_{n+1} is worst.
    let f_second_worst = values[n - 1]; // f(x_n)
    let f_worst = values[n];            // f(x_{n+1})
    let x_worst = simplex[n].clone();   // x_{n+1}

    let x_bar = centroid_excluding_worst(simplex);

    // Reflection (Eq. 10.6.4)
    let x_r = reflect_point(&x_bar, &x_worst, alpha);
    let f_r = f(&x_r);

    // Trigger for contraction (Eq. 10.6.9)
    if !(f_r >= f_second_worst) {
        return StepReport {
            x_bar,
            x_r,
            f_r,
            x_c: None,
            f_c: None,
            case: None,
            decision: ContractionDecision::NotTriggered,
        };
    }

    // Select outside vs inside contraction (Eqs. 10.6.10 and 10.6.12)
    let (case, x_c) = if f_second_worst <= f_r && f_r < f_worst {
        (ContractionCase::Outside, outside_contraction(&x_bar, &x_r, beta))
    } else {
        // This corresponds to f(x_r) >= f(x_{n+1}) (Eq. 10.6.12).
        (ContractionCase::Inside, inside_contraction(&x_bar, &x_worst, beta))
    };

    let f_c = f(&x_c);

    // Acceptance condition (Eq. 10.6.14)
    let threshold = f_r.min(f_worst);
    if f_c < threshold {
        simplex[n] = x_c.clone(); // replace worst vertex
        StepReport {
            x_bar,
            x_r,
            f_r,
            x_c: Some(x_c),
            f_c: Some(f_c),
            case: Some(case),
            decision: ContractionDecision::Accepted(case),
        }
    } else {
        StepReport {
            x_bar,
            x_r,
            f_r,
            x_c: Some(x_c),
            f_c: Some(f_c),
            case: Some(case),
            decision: ContractionDecision::Unsuccessful(case),
        }
    }
}

// Demonstration objective: Rosenbrock in 2D.
fn rosenbrock(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let a = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
    let b = 1.0 - x[0];
    100.0 * a * a + b * b
}

fn print_simplex(title: &str, simplex: &[Vec<f64>], f: &dyn Fn(&[f64]) -> f64) {
    println!("{title}");
    for (i, x) in simplex.iter().enumerate() {
        println!("  v{} = [{:.6}, {:.6}], f = {:.6e}", i, x[0], x[1], f(x));
    }
}

fn main() {
    // A 2D simplex has three vertices. These points are chosen so that reflection
    // tends to be poor, often triggering Eq. (10.6.9) and then demonstrating contraction.
    //
    // You can experiment by changing the simplex to observe outside vs inside contraction.
    let mut simplex = vec![
        vec![0.0, 0.0],    // relatively good
        vec![0.2, 0.2],    // moderately good
        vec![-1.2, 1.0],   // poor (often worst)
    ];

    let alpha = 1.0; // reflection coefficient
    let beta = 0.5;  // typical contraction coefficient

    print_simplex("Initial simplex:", &simplex, &rosenbrock);

    let report = contraction_step(&mut simplex, alpha, beta, &rosenbrock);

    // Re-sort for reporting so that output follows Eq. (10.6.2).
    let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex.len()];
    sort_simplex_by_value(&mut simplex, &mut values, &rosenbrock);

    println!("\nCentroid x̄ (Eq. 10.6.3): [{:.6}, {:.6}]", report.x_bar[0], report.x_bar[1]);
    println!(
        "Reflected point x_r (Eq. 10.6.4): [{:.6}, {:.6}], f = {:.6e}",
        report.x_r[0], report.x_r[1], report.f_r
    );

    match (&report.x_c, report.f_c) {
        (Some(xc), Some(fc)) => {
            if let Some(case) = report.case {
                match case {
                    ContractionCase::Outside => {
                        println!(
                            "Outside contraction x_c (Eq. 10.6.11): [{:.6}, {:.6}], f = {:.6e}",
                            xc[0], xc[1], fc
                        );
                    }
                    ContractionCase::Inside => {
                        println!(
                            "Inside contraction x_c (Eq. 10.6.13): [{:.6}, {:.6}], f = {:.6e}",
                            xc[0], xc[1], fc
                        );
                    }
                }
            } else {
                println!("Contracted point x_c: [{:.6}, {:.6}], f = {:.6e}", xc[0], xc[1], fc);
            }
        }
        _ => {
            println!("Contracted point x_c: not computed (Eq. 10.6.9 not satisfied).");
        }
    }

    print!("Decision: ");
    match report.decision {
        ContractionDecision::NotTriggered => {
            println!("NotTriggered");
        }
        ContractionDecision::Accepted(k) => {
            println!("Accepted({:?})", k);
        }
        ContractionDecision::Unsuccessful(k) => {
             println!("Unsuccessful({:?})", k);
        }
    }
    println!();


    print_simplex("Simplex after contraction step (if accepted):", &simplex, &rosenbrock);

    println!("\nInterpretation:");
    println!("  Eq. (10.6.9) determines whether contraction is attempted.");
    println!("  Eq. (10.6.10) selects outside contraction; Eq. (10.6.12) selects inside contraction.");
    println!("  Eq. (10.6.14) governs acceptance of the contracted point.");
}
```

Program 10.6.4 demonstrates how contraction stabilizes the downhill simplex method when reflection and expansion fail to produce adequate improvement. By selectively reducing the simplex scale, contraction prevents uncontrolled wandering and helps the algorithm adapt to regions where the objective function exhibits narrow valleys or poor local conditioning. The explicit distinction between outside and inside contraction highlights how the method responds differently depending on whether the reflection direction remains partially promising or is entirely detrimental.

This example underscores the adaptive nature of the Nelder–Mead method. Rather than relying on a single update rule, the algorithm employs a hierarchy of geometric responses, with contraction serving as an intermediate corrective step between reflection and shrinkage. The acceptance criterion in Equation (10.6.14) ensures that even these corrective moves are justified by measurable improvement, reinforcing the method’s balance between robustness and efficiency.

The modular structure of the implementation allows contraction to be integrated seamlessly with the reflection, expansion, and shrink steps presented in adjacent sections. Together, these components complete the set of transformations that define the downhill simplex method and provide a foundation for understanding its practical behavior and limitations.

## 10.6.5. Shrink Step

If reflection, expansion, and contraction all fail to yield an acceptable new point, the algorithm concludes that the current simplex is poorly positioned or has become distorted relative to the local structure of the objective function. In this situation, the simplex is shrunk uniformly toward the best vertex, effectively resetting the local search region.

The shrink operation replaces all vertices except the best one according to:

$$x_i \leftarrow x_1 + \delta (x_i - x_1), \qquad i = 2, \ldots, n+1 \tag{10.6.15}$$

where $0 < \delta < 1$, commonly chosen as $\delta = \tfrac{1}{2}$. This transformation preserves the relative shape of the simplex while significantly reducing its size and centering it around the best-known point.

The shrink step is the most conservative and most expensive operation in the Nelder–Mead method, as it requires reevaluating the objective function at multiple points. Nevertheless, it plays a crucial stabilizing role. By collapsing the simplex around the best vertex, the algorithm can recover from stagnation, escape regions where local geometric assumptions have failed, and reestablish a well-scaled simplex for further exploration.

Together, contraction and shrink steps ensure that the Nelder–Mead method remains robust even when the objective function is irregular, noisy, or poorly scaled. They provide essential mechanisms for controlling simplex size and geometry, complementing the exploratory behavior of reflection and expansion.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.6.5 on the limitations of reflection, expansion, and contraction in poorly scaled or stagnant configurations, Program 10.6.5 provides a concrete implementation of the shrink step in the downhill simplex method. When all other simplex transformations fail to produce an acceptable improvement, the algorithm resorts to shrinkage as a conservative reset mechanism. By uniformly contracting the simplex toward the current best vertex, the shrink step reduces the search region while preserving the simplex geometry relative to that vertex. This program implements the transformation defined in Equation (10.6.15) and illustrates how shrinkage stabilizes the algorithm when local geometric assumptions break down, preparing the simplex for renewed exploration in subsequent iterations.

At the core of the implementation is an explicit representation of the simplex as $n+1$ vertices in $\mathbb{R}^n$, together with a consistent ordering by objective function value as required by Equation (10.6.2). Before shrinkage is applied, the simplex is sorted so that the best vertex $x_1$ is unambiguously identified. This ordering is essential because the shrink transformation defined in Equation (10.6.15) is expressed relative to the best vertex at the moment the operation is invoked.

The shrink step itself applies a uniform affine transformation to all vertices except $x_1$. For each remaining vertex $x_i$, the displacement from the best vertex is scaled by the factor (\\delta), with $0 < \delta < 1$, and added back to $x_1$. This operation preserves the shape of the simplex while reducing its size, effectively collapsing the search region toward the most promising point identified so far. The best vertex remains unchanged, ensuring that the algorithm does not discard its current best estimate.

Because shrinkage modifies multiple vertices simultaneously, it is the most computationally expensive simplex operation per iteration. The implementation therefore applies shrinkage only after the algorithm has exhausted reflection, expansion, and contraction. The code records the shrink factor $\delta$ and the reference vertex $x_1$ used in Equation (10.6.15), making the transformation explicit and traceable.

The `main` function demonstrates the shrink step on a deliberately ill-scaled simplex so that the effect of the transformation is easy to observe. To avoid ambiguity in interpretation, the program prints the post-shrink simplex in two complementary views. The first preserves the index of the reference vertex $x_1$, making the application of Equation (10.6.15) visually transparent. The second view reorders the simplex by objective value according to Equation (10.6.2), illustrating that shrinkage may produce a new best vertex even though the transformation is defined relative to the pre-shrink best point.

```rust
// Program 10.6.5: Nelder–Mead Shrink Step
//
// This program isolates the shrink operation described in Section 10.6.5.
// When reflection, expansion, and contraction fail, Nelder–Mead shrinks the
// entire simplex uniformly toward the best vertex x_1. The shrink rule is:
//
//     x_i <- x_1 + δ (x_i - x_1),    i = 2, ..., n+1      (Eq. 10.6.15)
//
// where 0 < δ < 1 (typically δ = 1/2). The best vertex remains unchanged.
// Since shrink replaces multiple vertices, it requires re-evaluating the
// objective function at all updated vertices, making it the most expensive
// transformation per iteration, but also an important stabilizer.
//
// Textbook note: after shrinking, it is possible that a different vertex becomes
// the best one (for example, shrink may move a vertex closer to the minimizer).
// For this reason, this program prints the post-shrink simplex in TWO ways:
//
//  1) "x_1-fixed view": keeps the vertex x_1 used in Eq. (10.6.15) at index 0,
//     making the shrink formula visually transparent.
//
//  2) "sorted-by-value view": re-sorts the vertices by objective value to match
//     Eq. (10.6.2), which is the ordering used at the beginning of the next
//     Nelder–Mead iteration.

use std::cmp::Ordering;

fn add(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x + y).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn scale(a: &[f64], s: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

fn sort_simplex_by_value<F>(simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>], values: &mut [f64], f: &F)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    for (i, x) in simplex.iter().enumerate() {
        values[i] = f(x);
    }

    let mut idx: Vec<usize> = (0..simplex.len()).collect();
    idx.sort_by(|&i, &j| values[i].partial_cmp(&values[j]).unwrap_or(Ordering::Equal));

    let new_simplex: Vec<Vec<f64>> = idx.iter().map(|&i| simplex[i].clone()).collect();
    let new_values: Vec<f64> = idx.iter().map(|&i| values[i]).collect();

    simplex.clone_from_slice(&new_simplex);
    values.clone_from_slice(&new_values);
}

#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone)]
enum ShrinkDecision {
    Performed,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
struct ShrinkReport {
    delta: f64,
    x_best: Vec<f64>, // the x_1 used in Eq. (10.6.15)
    decision: ShrinkDecision,
}

fn shrink_step<F>(simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>], delta: f64, f: &F) -> ShrinkReport
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let n = simplex[0].len();
    assert_eq!(simplex.len(), n + 1, "Simplex must contain n+1 vertices.");
    assert!(delta > 0.0 && delta < 1.0, "delta must satisfy 0 < delta < 1.");

    // Ensure x_1 is the best vertex at the time shrink is applied (Eq. 10.6.2).
    let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex.len()];
    sort_simplex_by_value(simplex, &mut values, f);

    let x_best = simplex[0].clone(); // x_1

    // Apply Eq. (10.6.15) for i = 2, ..., n+1 (indices 1..=n).
    for i in 1..=n {
        let diff = sub(&simplex[i], &x_best);             // (x_i - x_1)
        simplex[i] = add(&x_best, &scale(&diff, delta));  // x_1 + δ (x_i - x_1)
    }

    ShrinkReport {
        delta,
        x_best,
        decision: ShrinkDecision::Performed,
    }
}

// Demonstration objective: Rosenbrock in 2D.
fn rosenbrock(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let a = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
    let b = 1.0 - x[0];
    100.0 * a * a + b * b
}

fn print_simplex(title: &str, simplex: &[Vec<f64>], f: &dyn Fn(&[f64]) -> f64) {
    println!("{title}");
    for (i, x) in simplex.iter().enumerate() {
        println!("  v{} = [{:.6}, {:.6}], f = {:.6e}", i, x[0], x[1], f(x));
    }
}

fn main() {
    // A 2D simplex has three vertices. These points are intentionally spread out
    // to make the effect of shrink (Eq. 10.6.15) easy to see.
    let mut simplex = vec![
        vec![0.0, 0.0],   // relatively good
        vec![2.0, 2.0],   // poor
        vec![-1.2, 1.0],  // classic Rosenbrock start
    ];

    let delta = 0.5; // typical shrink factor in Eq. (10.6.15)

    print_simplex("Initial simplex (unordered):", &simplex, &rosenbrock);

    let report = shrink_step(&mut simplex, delta, &rosenbrock);

    println!(
        "\nBest vertex x_1 at time of shrink (Eq. 10.6.2): [{:.6}, {:.6}]",
        report.x_best[0], report.x_best[1]
    );
    println!("Shrink factor δ (Eq. 10.6.15): {:.6}", report.delta);
    println!("Decision: {:?}\n", report.decision);

    // View 1: keep x_1 fixed at v0 so Eq. (10.6.15) is visually explicit.
    print_simplex(
        "Simplex after shrink step (Eq. 10.6.15), x_1-fixed view:",
        &simplex,
        &rosenbrock,
    );

    // View 2: re-sort for the next iteration order (Eq. 10.6.2).
    let mut simplex_sorted = simplex.clone();
    let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex_sorted.len()];
    sort_simplex_by_value(&mut simplex_sorted, &mut values, &rosenbrock);

    print_simplex(
        "\nSame simplex re-ordered by objective value after shrink (Eq. 10.6.2):",
        &simplex_sorted,
        &rosenbrock,
    );

    println!("\nInterpretation:");
    println!("  Eq. (10.6.15) leaves x_1 unchanged and contracts all other vertices toward x_1.");
    println!("  After shrinking, a different vertex may become the best (as shown by the sorted view).");
    println!("  In full Nelder–Mead, shrink is used only after reflection, expansion, and contraction fail.");
}
```

Program 10.6.5 demonstrates how the shrink step restores stability to the downhill simplex method when local exploratory moves fail. By collapsing the simplex toward the best vertex, shrinkage reduces sensitivity to poor scaling, noise, or misleading local geometry and reestablishes a compact search region from which further progress can be made.

The example highlights an important practical subtlety: although the shrink transformation leaves the reference vertex $x_1$ unchanged, it may nonetheless produce a different vertex with a lower objective value after reevaluation. This behavior underscores why shrinkage is both conservative and corrective, serving as a reset rather than a simple refinement.

Together with reflection, expansion, and contraction, the shrink step completes the set of geometric transformations that define the Nelder–Mead method. These mechanisms allow the algorithm to adapt its simplex dynamically, balancing exploration and stabilization in derivative-free multidimensional optimization problems.

## 10.6.6. Convergence Behavior and Limitations

The Nelder–Mead method often performs well in practice for low-dimensional optimization problems, particularly when the objective function is noisy, discontinuous, or only piecewise smooth. Because it relies solely on comparisons of function values rather than derivatives, it can make steady progress even when gradient-based methods become unstable or misleading. This robustness explains its continued popularity in experimental optimization, parameter tuning, and simulation-based problems.

At the same time, it is important to recognize the method’s inherent limitations. Nelder–Mead does not possess a general convergence guarantee to a local minimum, even for smooth objective functions. In certain situations, the simplex may degenerate, flatten, or cycle without making meaningful progress. The method can also stagnate near non-optimal points, particularly on functions with narrow valleys, strong anisotropy, or poor scaling across variables. In such cases, the simplex transformations may fail to align effectively with the geometry of the objective landscape.

These issues become more pronounced as the dimensionality increases. In higher dimensions, the simplex contains relatively few points compared to the volume of the search space, limiting its ability to capture local structure. Recent theoretical and empirical analyses have clarified conditions under which the Nelder–Mead method converges, as well as scenarios in which it is likely to fail or converge extremely slowly. As a result, modern implementations often augment the basic algorithm with restart strategies, adaptive parameter choices, or hybridization with derivative-based methods to improve reliability.

Hybrid global–local frameworks, in which Nelder–Mead is embedded as a local search component within a broader optimization strategy, have proven particularly effective. By allowing the simplex method to operate where it is strongest, robust local exploration without derivatives, while relying on other techniques for global guidance or fine-scale convergence, such hybrids can achieve improved robustness and efficiency in challenging optimization tasks (Majhi and Mishra, 2025).

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.6.6 on the convergence behavior and limitations of the Nelder–Mead method, the following program provides a concrete implementation of simplex evolution with explicit convergence diagnostics and termination criteria. While the Nelder–Mead algorithm is often effective in practice, its behavior near convergence or stagnation can be subtle and difficult to interpret without quantitative measures. This program augments the classical reflection, expansion, contraction, and shrink steps with diagnostic quantities that track both objective value agreement and geometric collapse of the simplex. By monitoring these indicators throughout the iteration, the implementation makes explicit how apparent convergence, stagnation, and noise-dominated behavior arise in practical optimization problems. Two representative test functions are evaluated to illustrate successful convergence on a smooth objective and the more ambiguous termination behavior encountered for nonsmooth or noisy landscapes.

At the core of the implementation is a structured representation of the Nelder–Mead simplex, consisting of $n+1$ vertices in $\mathbb{R}^n$ together with their associated objective values. At each iteration, the simplex is explicitly sorted according to the ordering defined in Equation (10.6.2), ensuring that the best, second-worst, and worst vertices are consistently identified. This ordering is essential for correctly applying the reflection, expansion, contraction, and shrink transformations defined earlier in Section 10.6.

The algorithm evaluates convergence using two complementary diagnostics. The first is the function value spread, defined as the difference between the largest and smallest objective values among the simplex vertices. This quantity measures agreement in function values and reflects whether further improvement is likely under the current simplex geometry. The second diagnostic is the simplex diameter, which measures the maximum pairwise distance between vertices and provides a geometric indicator of simplex collapse. Together, these quantities operationalize the termination criteria discussed in Section 10.6.6 and distinguish between genuine contraction of the search region and mere stagnation in objective space.

Termination is triggered when both diagnostics fall below prescribed tolerances, corresponding to simultaneous agreement in objective values and collapse of the simplex geometry. The reason for termination is recorded explicitly and reported at the end of the run, making clear whether the algorithm halted due to tolerance satisfaction, iteration limits, or stagnation detection. This explicit reporting is particularly important in the absence of general convergence guarantees, as emphasized in Section 10.6.6.

To improve robustness, the implementation includes a restart mechanism that reinitializes the simplex around the current best point when progress stalls. This strategy reflects common practice in modern Nelder–Mead implementations and addresses scenarios in which the simplex becomes poorly positioned, degenerate, or aligned unfavorably with the objective landscape. Restart parameters, including the maximum number of restarts and restart scale, are configurable and reported alongside the main algorithm parameters.

The main function demonstrates the behavior of the algorithm on two contrasting objectives. The Rosenbrock function serves as a smooth, anisotropic benchmark with a narrow curved valley, illustrating successful convergence through progressive simplex contraction. In contrast, a noisy absolute-value bowl highlights the limitations of the method in nonsmooth and noise-dominated settings. In this case, the algorithm terminates due to simplex collapse and value agreement, even though the final objective value reflects the noise floor rather than a true smooth minimum. These examples concretely illustrate the interpretive cautions discussed in Section 10.6.6.

```rust
// Program 10.6.6 (Revised): Convergence Diagnostics, Stagnation Detection, and Restarts for Nelder–Mead
//
// This program supports the discussion in Section 10.6.6 by instrumenting Nelder–Mead with
// diagnostics that expose both progress and failure modes.
//
// The algorithm reports, at each iteration, (i) the spread f(x_{n+1}) - f(x_1) among simplex
// vertices and (ii) the simplex diameter. These are practical indicators of (apparent) local
// convergence versus stagnation or degeneration.
//
// Important: Nelder–Mead does not provide a general certificate of convergence to a local minimum.
// For this reason, the solver reports an explicit termination reason rather than claiming that a
// minimizer has been proved.
//
// The simplex updates correspond to:
// reflection (Eq. 10.6.4), expansion (Eq. 10.6.7), contraction (Eqs. 10.6.11, 10.6.13),
// and shrink (Eq. 10.6.15).

use std::cmp::Ordering;

// -------------------------- Basic vector utilities --------------------------

fn add(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x + y).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn scale(a: &[f64], s: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

fn norm2(a: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    a.iter().map(|x| x * x).sum::<f64>().sqrt()
}

fn simplex_diameter(simplex: &[Vec<f64>]) -> f64 {
    let mut dmax = 0.0;
    for i in 0..simplex.len() {
        for j in (i + 1)..simplex.len() {
            let dij = norm2(&sub(&simplex[i], &simplex[j]));
            if dij > dmax {
                dmax = dij;
            }
        }
    }
    dmax
}

fn centroid_excluding_worst(sorted_simplex: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x̄ = (1/n) * sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i (Eq. 10.6.3),
    // assuming the simplex is sorted so that x_{n+1} is last.
    let n = sorted_simplex[0].len();
    let m = sorted_simplex.len() - 1;
    let mut c = vec![0.0; n];
    for i in 0..m {
        for j in 0..n {
            c[j] += sorted_simplex[i][j];
        }
    }
    for j in 0..n {
        c[j] /= m as f64;
    }
    c
}

fn sort_simplex_by_value<F>(simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>], values: &mut [f64], f: &F)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    for (i, x) in simplex.iter().enumerate() {
        values[i] = f(x);
    }

    let mut idx: Vec<usize> = (0..simplex.len()).collect();
    idx.sort_by(|&i, &j| values[i].partial_cmp(&values[j]).unwrap_or(Ordering::Equal));

    let new_simplex: Vec<Vec<f64>> = idx.iter().map(|&i| simplex[i].clone()).collect();
    let new_values: Vec<f64> = idx.iter().map(|&i| values[i]).collect();

    simplex.clone_from_slice(&new_simplex);
    values.clone_from_slice(&new_values);
}

// -------------------------- Nelder–Mead parameters --------------------------

#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone)]
struct NelderMeadParams {
    alpha: f64, // reflection coefficient (Eq. 10.6.4)
    gamma: f64, // expansion coefficient (Eq. 10.6.7)
    beta: f64,  // contraction coefficient (Eqs. 10.6.11, 10.6.13)
    delta: f64, // shrink coefficient (Eq. 10.6.15)
}

impl Default for NelderMeadParams {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            alpha: 1.0,
            gamma: 2.0,
            beta: 0.5,
            delta: 0.5,
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone)]
struct NelderMeadStop {
    max_iters: usize,
    tol_f: f64,         // objective spread tolerance
    tol_x: f64,         // diameter tolerance
    stall_iters: usize, // iterations without meaningful best improvement
    stall_eps: f64,     // minimum best-improvement per iteration to count as progress
}

#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone)]
struct RestartPolicy {
    enabled: bool,
    max_restarts: usize,
    step: f64, // coordinate simplex step size
}

// -------------------------- Termination reporting --------------------------

#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone)]
enum TerminationReason {
    TolSatisfied { spread: f64, diam: f64 },
    MaxItersReached,
}

impl TerminationReason {
    fn summary(&self) -> String {
        match *self {
            TerminationReason::TolSatisfied { spread, diam } => {
                // Reading spread and diam here removes the dead_code warning and improves clarity.
                format!(
                    "Termination: tolerances satisfied (spread={:.3e}, diameter={:.3e}).",
                    spread, diam
                )
            }
            TerminationReason::MaxItersReached => {
                "Termination: maximum iteration limit reached.".to_string()
            }
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug)]
struct NelderMeadResult {
    terminated: bool,
    iters: usize,
    restarts: usize,
    x_best: Vec<f64>,
    f_best: f64,
    reason: TerminationReason,
}

// -------------------------- Nelder–Mead core steps --------------------------

fn reflect_point(x_bar: &[f64], x_worst: &[f64], alpha: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x_r = x̄ + α (x̄ - x_{n+1}) (Eq. 10.6.4)
    let dir = sub(x_bar, x_worst);
    add(x_bar, &scale(&dir, alpha))
}

fn expand_point(x_bar: &[f64], x_r: &[f64], gamma: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x_e = x̄ + γ (x_r - x̄) (Eq. 10.6.7)
    let dir = sub(x_r, x_bar);
    add(x_bar, &scale(&dir, gamma))
}

fn outside_contraction(x_bar: &[f64], x_r: &[f64], beta: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x_c = x̄ + β (x_r - x̄) (Eq. 10.6.11)
    let dir = sub(x_r, x_bar);
    add(x_bar, &scale(&dir, beta))
}

fn inside_contraction(x_bar: &[f64], x_worst: &[f64], beta: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    // x_c = x̄ - β (x̄ - x_{n+1}) (Eq. 10.6.13)
    let dir = sub(x_bar, x_worst);
    sub(x_bar, &scale(&dir, beta))
}

fn shrink_simplex(simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>], delta: f64) {
    // x_i <- x_1 + δ (x_i - x_1), i=2..n+1 (Eq. 10.6.15)
    let n = simplex[0].len();
    let x_best = simplex[0].clone();
    for i in 1..=n {
        let diff = sub(&simplex[i], &x_best);
        simplex[i] = add(&x_best, &scale(&diff, delta));
    }
}

fn build_simplex_around(x0: &[f64], step: f64) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = x0.len();
    let mut simplex = Vec::with_capacity(n + 1);
    simplex.push(x0.to_vec());
    for i in 0..n {
        let mut xi = x0.to_vec();
        xi[i] += step;
        simplex.push(xi);
    }
    simplex
}

// One Nelder–Mead iteration. Returns true if shrink was applied.
fn nelder_mead_iteration<F>(simplex: &mut [Vec<f64>], params: NelderMeadParams, f: &F) -> bool
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let n = simplex[0].len();
    let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex.len()];
    sort_simplex_by_value(simplex, &mut values, f);

    let x_best = simplex[0].clone();
    let f_best = values[0];

    let x_second_worst = simplex[n - 1].clone();
    let f_second_worst = values[n - 1];

    let x_worst = simplex[n].clone();
    let f_worst = values[n];

    let x_bar = centroid_excluding_worst(simplex);

    // Reflection (Eq. 10.6.4)
    let x_r = reflect_point(&x_bar, &x_worst, params.alpha);
    let f_r = f(&x_r);

    // Expansion condition (Eq. 10.6.6) and decision (Eq. 10.6.8)
    if f_r < f_best {
        let x_e = expand_point(&x_bar, &x_r, params.gamma);
        let f_e = f(&x_e);
        simplex[n] = if f_e < f_r { x_e } else { x_r };
        return false;
    }

    // Accept reflection if Eq. (10.6.5)
    if f_best <= f_r && f_r < f_second_worst {
        simplex[n] = x_r;
        return false;
    }

    // Contraction trigger (Eq. 10.6.9)
    if f_r >= f_second_worst {
        // Outside contraction if Eq. (10.6.10), otherwise inside contraction via Eq. (10.6.12)
        let (x_c, f_c) = if f_second_worst <= f_r && f_r < f_worst {
            let xc = outside_contraction(&x_bar, &x_r, params.beta);
            let fc = f(&xc);
            (xc, fc)
        } else {
            let xc = inside_contraction(&x_bar, &x_worst, params.beta);
            let fc = f(&xc);
            (xc, fc)
        };

        // Accept contraction if Eq. (10.6.14); else shrink (Eq. 10.6.15)
        if f_c < f_r.min(f_worst) {
            simplex[n] = x_c;
            return false;
        }

        // Ensure best is at index 0 for shrink.
        simplex[0] = x_best;
        simplex[n - 1] = x_second_worst;
        simplex[n] = x_worst;
        shrink_simplex(simplex, params.delta);
        return true;
    }

    // Conservative fallback: shrink to restore a usable simplex geometry.
    simplex[0] = x_best;
    simplex[n - 1] = x_second_worst;
    simplex[n] = x_worst;
    shrink_simplex(simplex, params.delta);
    true
}

// -------------------------- Solver with diagnostics and restarts --------------------------

fn nelder_mead_solve<F>(
    x0: &[f64],
    params: NelderMeadParams,
    stop: NelderMeadStop,
    restart: RestartPolicy,
    f: &F,
    label: &str,
) -> NelderMeadResult
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let mut simplex = build_simplex_around(x0, restart.step);
    let n = x0.len();

    let mut best_prev = f(x0);
    let mut stall_count = 0usize;
    let mut restarts = 0usize;

    println!("\n=== Nelder–Mead run: {label} ===");
    println!("n = {n}, max_iters = {}", stop.max_iters);
    println!(
        "params: alpha={:.3}, gamma={:.3}, beta={:.3}, delta={:.3}",
        params.alpha, params.gamma, params.beta, params.delta
    );
    println!(
        "stopping: tol_f={:.3e}, tol_x={:.3e}, stall_iters={}, stall_eps={:.3e}",
        stop.tol_f, stop.tol_x, stop.stall_iters, stop.stall_eps
    );
    if restart.enabled {
        println!(
            "restart: enabled, max_restarts={}, step={:.3e}",
            restart.max_restarts, restart.step
        );
    } else {
        println!("restart: disabled");
    }

    for k in 0..stop.max_iters {
        // Sort for diagnostics (Eq. 10.6.2)
        let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex.len()];
        sort_simplex_by_value(&mut simplex, &mut values, f);

        let f_best = values[0];
        let f_worst = values[n];
        let spread = f_worst - f_best;
        let diam = simplex_diameter(&simplex);

        println!(
            "iter {:4}: f_best={:.6e}, f_worst={:.6e}, spread={:.3e}, diam={:.3e}",
            k, f_best, f_worst, spread, diam
        );

        // Termination: simplex collapse and value agreement.
        if spread <= stop.tol_f && diam <= stop.tol_x {
            let reason = TerminationReason::TolSatisfied { spread, diam };
            return NelderMeadResult {
                terminated: true,
                iters: k,
                restarts,
                x_best: simplex[0].clone(),
                f_best,
                reason,
            };
        }

        // Stagnation detection based on best improvement magnitude.
        let improvement = best_prev - f_best;
        if improvement.abs() < stop.stall_eps {
            stall_count += 1;
        } else {
            stall_count = 0;
            best_prev = f_best;
        }

        // Restart if stalled and policy allows.
        if restart.enabled && stall_count >= stop.stall_iters && restarts < restart.max_restarts {
            restarts += 1;
            stall_count = 0;
            let x_best = simplex[0].clone();
            println!(
                "  restart {}: stalled for {} iters, rebuilding simplex around x_best={:?}",
                restarts, stop.stall_iters, x_best
            );
            simplex = build_simplex_around(&x_best, restart.step);
            best_prev = f(&x_best);
            continue;
        }

        // Perform one iteration step.
        let did_shrink = nelder_mead_iteration(&mut simplex, params, f);
        if did_shrink {
            println!("  note: shrink applied (Eq. 10.6.15)");
        }
    }

    // Final report after max_iters.
    let mut values = vec![0.0; simplex.len()];
    sort_simplex_by_value(&mut simplex, &mut values, f);

    NelderMeadResult {
        terminated: false,
        iters: stop.max_iters,
        restarts,
        x_best: simplex[0].clone(),
        f_best: values[0],
        reason: TerminationReason::MaxItersReached,
    }
}

// -------------------------- Demonstration objectives --------------------------

fn rosenbrock(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let a = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
    let b = 1.0 - x[0];
    100.0 * a * a + b * b
}

fn noisy_abs_bowl(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let base = (x[0] - 1.0).abs() + (x[1] + 2.0).abs();
    let noise = 0.05 * (8.0 * x[0]).sin() * (6.0 * x[1]).cos();
    base + noise
}

// -------------------------- Main --------------------------

fn main() {
    let params = NelderMeadParams::default();

    let stop = NelderMeadStop {
        max_iters: 200,
        tol_f: 1e-10,
        tol_x: 1e-8,
        stall_iters: 20,
        stall_eps: 1e-12,
    };

    let restart = RestartPolicy {
        enabled: true,
        max_restarts: 3,
        step: 0.25,
    };

    // Case 1: Rosenbrock
    let x0_rosen = [-1.2, 1.0];
    let res1 = nelder_mead_solve(&x0_rosen, params, stop, restart, &rosenbrock, "Rosenbrock");

    println!("\nResult: Rosenbrock");
    println!("  terminated: {}", res1.terminated);
    println!("  iters:      {}", res1.iters);
    println!("  restarts:   {}", res1.restarts);
    println!("  x_best:     {:?}", res1.x_best);
    println!("  f_best:     {:.6e}", res1.f_best);
    println!("  {}", res1.reason.summary());

    // Case 2: Noisy nonsmooth objective
    let x0_noisy = [3.0, 3.0];
    let res2 = nelder_mead_solve(&x0_noisy, params, stop, restart, &noisy_abs_bowl, "Noisy abs bowl");

    println!("\nResult: Noisy abs bowl");
    println!("  terminated: {}", res2.terminated);
    println!("  iters:      {}", res2.iters);
    println!("  restarts:   {}", res2.restarts);
    println!("  x_best:     {:?}", res2.x_best);
    println!("  f_best:     {:.6e}", res2.f_best);
    println!("  {}", res2.reason.summary());

    println!("\nInterpretation:");
    println!("  The spread and diameter diagnostics help distinguish apparent convergence from stagnation.");
    println!("  A small spread and diameter indicate simplex collapse and agreement of vertex values,");
    println!("  but they do not provide a general guarantee of convergence to a local minimizer.");
    println!("  Restarts can recover progress when the simplex becomes poorly positioned or degenerates.");
}
```

This program demonstrates how convergence diagnostics can be integrated directly into a Nelder–Mead implementation to make termination behavior explicit and interpretable. By tracking both function value spread and simplex diameter, the algorithm exposes the distinction between geometric collapse, objective agreement, and genuine minimization. This distinction is central to understanding the practical strengths and limitations of the method.

The two test problems illustrate contrasting outcomes. For the Rosenbrock function, decreasing spread and diameter coincide with convergence to the known minimizer, reflecting successful alignment between simplex geometry and objective structure. For the noisy absolute-value bowl, termination occurs at a nonzero objective level, illustrating how simplex collapse can occur even when noise or nonsmoothness prevents further meaningful improvement. These results reinforce the caution that tolerance-based termination does not constitute a general convergence guarantee.

The modular structure of the implementation allows for straightforward extensions, including adaptive parameter selection, alternative restart strategies, or hybridization with gradient-based refinement methods. Such enhancements reflect current best practice and align with the hybrid global–local frameworks discussed at the end of Section 10.6.6.

## 10.6.7. Practical Use and Recommendations

Despite its theoretical shortcomings, the downhill simplex method remains a valuable and widely used tool in numerical optimization. Its simplicity, minimal assumptions, and ease of implementation make it especially attractive when derivative information is unavailable or unreliable, or when the objective function is affected by noise that would undermine gradient-based updates. In exploratory phases of optimization, Nelder–Mead often provides useful progress with very little setup or tuning.

For problems of moderate to high dimension, typically greater than about ten variables, or when high-precision solutions are required, derivative-based or quasi-Newton methods are generally more effective. These methods exploit curvature information to achieve faster and more reliable convergence once the objective function behaves smoothly. Nonetheless, they often require good initial guesses and may perform poorly when started far from a minimizer.

In practical workflows, Nelder–Mead is therefore frequently used as an initial exploratory or refinement method. It can identify promising regions of the search space or provide a reasonable starting point, after which a more efficient local optimizer is applied. This complementary role aligns naturally with modern hybrid optimization strategies and reflects a broader principle in numerical optimization: simple, robust methods often play a crucial supporting role even when more sophisticated algorithms are available.

## 10.6.8. Section Remarks

The downhill simplex method, also known as the Nelder–Mead algorithm, offers a flexible and intuitive framework for multidimensional derivative-free optimization. By iteratively transforming a simplex through reflection, expansion, contraction, and shrink operations, the method navigates the parameter space using only function evaluations, adapting dynamically to the local structure of the objective function without requiring gradient or curvature information.

Although the method lacks strong theoretical convergence guarantees and may struggle on ill-scaled or high-dimensional problems, its practical robustness and ease of implementation have ensured its continued widespread use. Nelder–Mead is particularly effective for low- to moderate-dimensional problems involving noisy, discontinuous, or black-box objective functions. In modern optimization workflows, it is often employed as an exploratory or local search component within hybrid strategies, complementing more powerful derivative-based methods. This enduring relevance reflects a recurring theme in numerical optimization: simple geometric ideas, when carefully safeguarded, can yield reliable and useful results in complex practical settings.

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/ShEg3FnO0CBmjvMOkiKM.6","tags":[]}

# 10.7. Line Methods in Multidimensions

In multidimensional optimization, a fundamental strategy is to reduce the problem temporarily to one dimension by restricting the search to a line. Let $f(\mathbf{x})$ be a real-valued function of $N$ variables, and let the current iterate be a point $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^N$. Given a search direction $\mathbf{n} \in \mathbb{R}^N$, a one-dimensional function is defined along the line through $\mathbf{P}$ in direction $\mathbf{n}$ by:

$$\phi(\lambda) = f(\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n}) \tag{10.7.1}$$

where $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ is a scalar step parameter. Minimizing $\phi(\lambda)$ with respect to $\lambda$ yields the minimum of the original function $f$ restricted to that line. This operation is referred to as *line minimization* or *line search*.

Once the minimizing value $\lambda^*$ has been determined, the point is updated according to:

$$\mathbf{P} \leftarrow \mathbf{P} + \lambda^* \mathbf{n} \tag{10.7.2}$$

By repeating this procedure along a sequence of directions, a full $N$-dimensional minimization problem can be addressed through a succession of one-dimensional subproblems. Many multidimensional optimization algorithms adopt this framework; they differ primarily in how each new search direction $\mathbf{n}$ is chosen after a completed line search.

## 10.7.1. Motivation and Role of Line Searches

Line methods are attractive because they exploit the robustness and maturity of one-dimensional optimization techniques such as bracketing procedures and derivative-free minimization algorithms. In a multidimensional setting, the complexity of the optimization problem arises primarily from the interaction between variables and from the geometry of the objective function in high-dimensional space. By restricting attention temporarily to motion along a single direction $\mathbf{n}$, line methods reduce this complexity to a scalar problem in the step-length parameter $\lambda$. This reduction allows well-understood one-dimensional algorithms to be used as reliable subroutines within more complex multidimensional schemes.

A key conceptual advantage of line searches is the clean separation they provide between two fundamentally different tasks: choosing a promising search direction and determining how far to move along that direction. Direction selection encodes information about the local geometry of the objective function, whether derived from gradients, conjugacy conditions, or heuristic updates. Step-length determination, by contrast, is concerned with numerical stability and sufficient progress. By decoupling these roles, line methods allow sophisticated direction-generation strategies to be combined with robust and reusable step-length control mechanisms.

In modern optimization practice, line searches play a critical stabilizing role, particularly in iterative algorithms where naive step choices can lead to divergence, oscillation, or excessively slow convergence. Rather than insisting on an exact minimizer of the one-dimensional function $\phi(\lambda)$, most contemporary algorithms employ inexact line searches that enforce sufficient decrease of the objective function while preventing steps that are either too small to be effective or too large to be stable. This controlled relaxation significantly reduces computational cost, since exact line minimization is often unnecessary and expensive, especially when each function or gradient evaluation is costly.

Beyond efficiency, inexact line searches are central to the global convergence properties of many multidimensional algorithms. They act as a safeguard that ensures consistent progress toward a minimum, even when the local model used to generate the search direction is only approximate. As a result, line searches serve not merely as auxiliary routines, but as integral components that link local descent behavior to overall algorithmic reliability. Recent surveys classify line search methods according to criteria such as exact versus inexact minimization and monotone versus non-monotone decrease, emphasizing that the choice of line search can have a decisive impact on both efficiency and robustness across a wide range of optimization problems (Omesa et al., 2025).

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.7 on the role of line searches in multidimensional optimization, Program 10.7.1 provides a concrete implementation of line minimization based on the reduction defined in Equation (10.7.1). In multidimensional algorithms, the choice of a search direction and the determination of an appropriate step length play fundamentally different roles, and line methods isolate the latter by temporarily restricting the optimization problem to a single scalar parameter. This program implements a robust, derivative-free line search that minimizes the one-dimensional function $\phi(\lambda)$ along a prescribed direction and updates the iterate according to Equation (10.7.2). By combining bracketing of a minimum with Brent’s method for one-dimensional minimization, the implementation illustrates how mature scalar optimization techniques can be embedded as reliable subroutines within more complex multidimensional schemes.

At the core of the implementation is the construction of the one-dimensional function $\phi(\lambda)$ defined in Equation (10.7.1), which evaluates the original objective function along a line passing through the current iterate $\mathbf{P}$ in the direction $\mathbf{n}$. The function `line_minimize` encapsulates this reduction by normalizing the supplied direction vector and defining a closure that maps a scalar step length $\lambda$ to the corresponding point in $\mathbb{R}^N$. This design allows the multidimensional objective to be treated transparently as a scalar function, enabling the direct reuse of one-dimensional minimization algorithms without modification.

The line search proceeds in two stages. The first stage is handled by the `bracket_minimum` function, which attempts to locate an interval $(a,b,c)$ such that $\phi(b)$ is smaller than both $\phi(a)$ and $\phi(c)$. Starting from an initial trial step, the algorithm expands the interval geometrically until an increase in the function value is detected or a prescribed expansion limit is reached. This bracketing phase is essential for ensuring that the subsequent minimization step operates on a well-defined unimodal region of the line-restricted function.

Once a bracket has been established, the program applies Brent’s method through the `brent_minimize` function. This algorithm combines parabolic interpolation with safeguarded golden-section steps, allowing rapid convergence when the function is smooth while retaining robustness when curvature information is unreliable. No derivatives of $\phi(\lambda)$ are required, making the method suitable for objectives that are noisy, expensive to evaluate, or unavailable in analytic form. The termination criterion is controlled by a relative tolerance on the step length, ensuring that the minimizer is located with sufficient accuracy for use in a larger multidimensional iteration.

After the minimizing value $\lambda^*$ has been computed, the update prescribed by Equation (10.7.2) is applied explicitly. The new point is formed by advancing the current iterate along the normalized direction vector by the computed step length. The function returns both the updated point and the corresponding function value, allowing the calling algorithm to assess progress and decide on subsequent search directions.

The `main` function demonstrates the complete line minimization procedure using the two-dimensional Rosenbrock function as a representative test case. An initial point and a fixed search direction are supplied, and the program reports the optimal step length along that direction together with the resulting decrease in the objective function. Although the direction used in this example is chosen heuristically, the same line search routine can be integrated unchanged into more sophisticated methods such as Powell’s algorithm, conjugate-gradient schemes, or quasi-Newton methods, where the direction is generated dynamically based on local geometry.

```rust
// Program 10.7.1: Line Methods in Multidimensions (warning-free, derivative-free line minimization)
//
// This program implements the reduction described by (10.7.1) by defining
//     phi(lambda) = f(P + lambda * n)
// and then minimizing phi with respect to the scalar parameter lambda. Once lambda* is found,
// the multidimensional point update follows (10.7.2):
//     P <- P + lambda* n
//
// The line minimization is performed in two stages:
//   (1) Bracket a 1D minimum of phi(lambda) by expanding an interval until the decrease stops.
//   (2) Apply Brent's method on the bracket to obtain an accurate minimizer without derivatives.
//
// The code is intended as a reusable line-search subroutine for multidimensional optimization
// schemes that differ mainly in how the search direction n is chosen.

fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(a.len(), b.len());
    let mut s = 0.0;
    for i in 0..a.len() {
        s += a[i] * b[i];
    }
    s
}

fn norm2(a: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(a, a).sqrt()
}

fn add_scaled(p: &[f64], n: &[f64], lambda: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    assert_eq!(p.len(), n.len());
    let mut out = vec![0.0; p.len()];
    for i in 0..p.len() {
        out[i] = p[i] + lambda * n[i];
    }
    out
}

/// Bracket a minimum of a (reasonably) unimodal function.
/// Returns (a, b, c) with a < b < c and phi(b) <= phi(a), phi(b) <= phi(c).
fn bracket_minimum<F>(phi: &F, step0: f64, grow: f64, max_expand: usize) -> (f64, f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let mut a: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut b: f64 = if step0 == 0.0 { 1e-3 } else { step0 };

    let fa: f64 = phi(a);
    let mut fb: f64 = phi(b);

    // Ensure we move in a decreasing direction; otherwise reverse.
    if fb > fa {
        b = -b;
        fb = phi(b);

        // If neither direction decreases, return a small symmetric bracket around 0.
        if fb > fa {
            let left = -b.abs();
            let right = b.abs();
            return (left, 0.0, right);
        }
    }

    // Expand until we see an increase (or max expansions reached).
    let mut step: f64 = b - a;
    let mut c: f64 = b + step;
    let mut fc: f64 = phi(c);

    let mut k = 0usize;
    while fc < fb && k < max_expand {
        a = b;
        b = c;
        fb = fc;

        step *= grow;
        c = b + step;
        fc = phi(c);

        k += 1;
    }

    // Order as (x1 < x2 < x3).
    let (x1, x2, x3) = if a < c { (a, b, c) } else { (c, b, a) };

    // If degenerate, widen slightly.
    if !(x1 < x2 && x2 < x3) {
        let eps = 1e-6;
        return (x1 - eps, x2, x3 + eps);
    }

    (x1, x2, x3)
}

/// Brent's method for 1D minimization on a bracket (a < b < c) where f(b) < f(a), f(c).
/// Returns x* approximating the minimizer in [a, c].
fn brent_minimize<F>(f: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64, tol: f64, max_iter: usize) -> f64
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let mut lo: f64 = a.min(c);
    let mut hi: f64 = a.max(c);

    let mut x: f64 = b;
    let mut w: f64 = x;
    let mut v: f64 = x;

    let mut fx: f64 = f(x);
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;

    // (3 - sqrt(5))/2
    let golden: f64 = 0.3819660112501051;

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        let m: f64 = 0.5 * (lo + hi);
        let tol1: f64 = tol * x.abs() + 1e-12;
        let tol2: f64 = 2.0 * tol1;

        // Termination condition.
        if (x - m).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (hi - lo)) {
            return x;
        }

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            // Parabolic interpolation step.
            let r: f64 = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q0: f64 = (x - v) * (fx - fw);

            let mut p: f64 = (x - v) * q0 - (x - w) * r;
            let mut q: f64 = 2.0 * (q0 - r);

            if q > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            }
            q = q.abs();

            let etemp: f64 = e;
            e = d;

            // Accept only if the parabolic step stays inside the bracket and is not too large.
            if p.abs() < 0.5 * q * etemp && p > q * (lo - x) && p < q * (hi - x) {
                d = p / q;
                let u: f64 = x + d;

                // If too close to an endpoint, enforce a minimum step.
                if (u - lo) < tol2 || (hi - u) < tol2 {
                    d = if x < m { tol1 } else { -tol1 };
                }
            } else {
                // Fall back to golden-section step.
                e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
                d = golden * e;
            }
        } else {
            // Golden-section step.
            e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
            d = golden * e;
        }

        // Candidate point u, ensuring at least tol1 movement.
        let u: f64 = if d.abs() >= tol1 {
            x + d
        } else {
            x + d.signum() * tol1
        };

        let fu: f64 = f(u);

        // Update the bracket and the triple (v, w, x).
        if fu <= fx {
            if u < x {
                hi = x;
            } else {
                lo = x;
            }

            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            if u < x {
                lo = u;
            } else {
                hi = u;
            }

            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() < 1e-15 {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() < 1e-15 || (v - w).abs() < 1e-15 {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    // If max_iter is reached, return the best current estimate.
    x
}

/// Perform a derivative-free line minimization along direction n from point P.
/// Returns (lambda_star, new_point, f(new_point)).
fn line_minimize<F>(
    f: &F,
    p: &[f64],
    n: &[f64],
    step0: f64,
    tol: f64,
    max_expand: usize,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> (f64, Vec<f64>, f64)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    assert_eq!(p.len(), n.len());

    // Normalize n so lambda measures a step length in x-space.
    let nn: f64 = norm2(n);
    assert!(nn > 0.0, "Search direction must be nonzero.");

    let mut dir: Vec<f64> = vec![0.0; n.len()];
    for i in 0..n.len() {
        dir[i] = n[i] / nn;
    }

    // Define phi(lambda) = f(P + lambda * dir) as in (10.7.1).
    let phi = |lambda: f64| -> f64 {
        let x = add_scaled(p, &dir, lambda);
        f(&x)
    };

    // Bracket and then minimize.
    let (a, b, c) = bracket_minimum(&phi, step0, 1.618033988749895, max_expand);
    let lambda_star: f64 = brent_minimize(&phi, a, b, c, tol, max_iter);

    // Apply the update (10.7.2), using the normalized direction.
    let p_new: Vec<f64> = add_scaled(p, &dir, lambda_star);
    let f_new: f64 = f(&p_new);

    (lambda_star, p_new, f_new)
}

// Example objective function for demonstration: 2D Rosenbrock function.
fn rosenbrock(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(x.len(), 2);
    let a: f64 = 1.0;
    let b: f64 = 100.0;
    let t1: f64 = a - x[0];
    let t2: f64 = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
    t1 * t1 + b * t2 * t2
}

fn main() {
    // Current iterate P in R^N.
    let p0: Vec<f64> = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let f0: f64 = rosenbrock(&p0);

    // A sample search direction n in R^N.
    // In practice, n is generated by the surrounding multidimensional method
    // (steepest descent, conjugate directions, quasi-Newton, etc.).
    let n: Vec<f64> = vec![1.0, 1.0];

    // Perform the line minimization and update.
    let (lambda_star, p1, f1) = line_minimize(
        &rosenbrock,
        &p0,
        &n,
        0.05,   // initial trial step along the line
        1e-10,  // 1D tolerance for Brent's method
        60,     // maximum bracket expansions
        200,    // maximum Brent iterations
    );

    println!("Initial P  = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", p0, f0);
    println!("Direction n = {:?} (normalized internally)", n);
    println!("lambda*     = {:.12e}", lambda_star);
    println!("Updated P   = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", p1, f1);
}
```

Program 10.7.1 demonstrates how multidimensional optimization can be decomposed into a sequence of reliable one-dimensional subproblems through the use of line searches. By reducing the objective function to the scalar form defined in Equation (10.7.1) and applying a robust derivative-free minimization strategy, the program illustrates the practical mechanics underlying many widely used optimization algorithms.

The example highlights the stabilizing role of line searches in controlling step length independently of direction selection. Even when the chosen direction is only approximate or heuristic, an effective line minimization can produce substantial reduction in the objective function while avoiding overly aggressive or ineffective steps. This separation of responsibilities is central to the robustness of modern multidimensional optimization methods.

The modular structure of the implementation allows the line search routine to be reused without modification across a wide range of algorithms and objective functions. In later sections, this framework can be extended to incorporate inexact line searches based on sufficient decrease conditions, non-monotone strategies, or curvature-based acceptance tests, all of which trade exact minimization for improved efficiency and global convergence behavior in large-scale or expensive optimization problems.

## 10.7.2. The Line-Minimization Abstraction (`linmin`)

Within this text, line minimization is encapsulated using the symbolic name `linmin`. It represents a conceptual algorithmic abstraction that isolates a common operation shared by a wide class of multidimensional optimization methods: minimizing the objective function $f$ along a prescribed line in parameter space and updating the current iterate accordingly. By treating this operation as a single abstract step, the essential structure of many algorithms can be expressed without committing to particular implementation details.

Formally, `linmin` denotes the process of computing:

$$\lambda^* = \arg\min_\lambda f(\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n}), \tag{10.7.3}$$

followed by the point update given in (10.7.2). The internal mechanism used to obtain $\lambda^*$ is deliberately left unspecified. Depending on the broader algorithmic context, it may involve exact or inexact minimization, derivative-free or gradient-based procedures, or simple safeguarded heuristics. What matters at this level of description is not how the minimizer is found, but that the operation consistently produces a step length that yields sufficient progress along the chosen direction.

The abstraction emphasizes that `linmin` is responsible only for step-length determination and point updating, not for selecting the search direction itself. Direction selection reflects higher-level algorithmic logic, such as exploiting gradient information, building conjugate directions, or adapting to local curvature. By separating these roles, the abstraction allows direction-generation strategies to evolve independently of the mechanisms used to control step size and numerical stability.

At a high level, the abstraction consists of the following conceptual components:

*Input*: the current point $\mathbf{P}$, a search direction $\mathbf{n}$, and the objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$.

*Operation*: minimize the one-dimensional function $f(\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n})$ with respect to the scalar parameter $\lambda$.

*Update*: replace the current point by $\mathbf{P} \leftarrow \mathbf{P} + \lambda^* \mathbf{n}$, with the option to rescale, replace, or otherwise modify the direction vector depending on the surrounding algorithm.

*Output*: the updated point $\mathbf{P}$, and in some methods an updated direction or auxiliary information derived from the line search.

By encapsulating this sequence of actions in a single abstract operation, multidimensional optimization algorithms can be described at a higher conceptual level, focusing on strategy rather than mechanics. This abstraction makes it easier to compare different algorithms, to reason about their convergence behavior, and to substitute different one-dimensional minimization techniques without altering the surrounding multidimensional framework.

### Rust Implementation

Following the introduction of the line-minimization abstraction in Section 10.7.2, Program 10.7.2 provides a concrete realization of the symbolic operation `linmin` as a reusable computational component. In multidimensional optimization algorithms, line minimization represents a recurring subtask in which the objective function is minimized along a prescribed search direction, as formalized in Equation (10.7.3), and the current iterate is updated according to Equation (10.7.2). This program encapsulates that operation behind a clean abstraction, allowing higher-level algorithms to invoke line minimization without reference to the specific numerical technique used to compute the step length. By separating the conceptual role of `linmin` from its implementation, the program illustrates how complex optimization strategies can be expressed clearly in terms of modular algorithmic building blocks.

At the core of the implementation is the `LinMin` trait, which defines a general interface for the line-minimization abstraction. This trait specifies a single operation, `linmin`, which accepts the current point $\mathbf{P}$, a search direction $\mathbf{n}$, and the objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$, and returns the result of minimizing the one-dimensional function defined in Equation (10.7.3). By requiring only this minimal interface, the abstraction allows the surrounding multidimensional algorithm to remain agnostic about how the step length $\lambda^*$ is determined.

A concrete realization of this abstraction is provided by the `BrentLinMin` struct, which implements the `LinMin` trait using a derivative-free strategy based on bracketing and Brent’s method. Internally, the algorithm constructs the scalar function $f(\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n})$ and applies a two-stage procedure: first locating an interval that brackets a minimum, and then refining the minimizer using safeguarded parabolic interpolation. This approach yields an accurate approximation of $\lambda^*$ while avoiding the need for gradient information, making it suitable for objective functions that are noisy, expensive to evaluate, or unavailable in analytic form.

The abstraction also makes explicit a design choice that is often implicit in algorithm descriptions: whether the search direction $\mathbf{n}$ should be normalized before the line search. In this implementation, normalization is enabled by default so that the parameter $\lambda$ represents an actual step length in parameter space. This choice simplifies the interpretation of the line search and ensures that the reported step norm coincides with $\lambda^*$. At the same time, the option to disable normalization preserves fidelity to the formal statement of Equation (10.7.3) when required by a particular algorithmic context.

The `LinMinResult` structure collects the essential outputs of the abstraction. In addition to the minimizing step length $\lambda^*$ and the updated point $\mathbf{P}$, it reports the objective value at the new iterate and the Euclidean norm of the displacement. This information can be used by higher-level methods to assess progress, adapt future search directions, or enforce additional acceptance criteria, without entangling those decisions with the mechanics of the line search itself.

The `main` function demonstrates the use of the linmin abstraction on the Rosenbrock function in two dimensions. A fixed search direction is supplied to represent the output of a hypothetical higher-level algorithm, and the line minimization is performed by invoking the `linmin` method. The example confirms that the abstraction correctly computes the step length specified by Equation (10.7.3) and applies the update prescribed by Equation (10.7.2), producing a substantial reduction in the objective function along the chosen direction.

```rust
// Program 10.7.2: The Line-Minimization Abstraction (linmin)
//
// This program implements the conceptual abstraction linmin introduced in Section 10.7.2.
// The purpose of linmin is to encapsulate the common operation appearing throughout
// multidimensional optimization methods: given a current point P and a search direction n,
// minimize the one-dimensional function f(P + lambda n) as in (10.7.3), then update the
// iterate according to (10.7.2).
//
// The internal mechanism for determining lambda* is intentionally hidden behind a small
// interface so that different line-search strategies (exact, inexact, derivative-free,
// gradient-based) can be substituted without changing the surrounding multidimensional
// algorithm. Here, we provide a concrete derivative-free implementation based on
// bracketing a minimum followed by Brent minimization, and we expose it via a LinMin
// trait that defines the linmin abstraction.

/// Euclidean dot product.
fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(a.len(), b.len());
    let mut s = 0.0;
    for i in 0..a.len() {
        s += a[i] * b[i];
    }
    s
}

/// Euclidean norm.
fn norm2(a: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(a, a).sqrt()
}

/// Compute P + lambda * n.
fn add_scaled(p: &[f64], n: &[f64], lambda: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    assert_eq!(p.len(), n.len());
    let mut out = vec![0.0; p.len()];
    for i in 0..p.len() {
        out[i] = p[i] + lambda * n[i];
    }
    out
}

/// Bracket a minimum of a 1D function phi.
/// Returns (a, b, c) with a < b < c and phi(b) <= phi(a), phi(b) <= phi(c).
fn bracket_minimum<F>(phi: &F, step0: f64, grow: f64, max_expand: usize) -> (f64, f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let mut a: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut b: f64 = if step0 == 0.0 { 1e-3 } else { step0 };

    let fa: f64 = phi(a);
    let mut fb: f64 = phi(b);
```
