```rust
    // Ensure movement is initially downhill; otherwise reverse direction.
    if fb > fa {
        b = -b;
        fb = phi(b);

        // If neither direction is downhill, return a small symmetric bracket around 0.
        if fb > fa {
            let left = -b.abs();
            let right = b.abs();
            return (left, 0.0, right);
        }
    }

    // Expand geometrically until phi stops decreasing.
    let mut step: f64 = b - a;
    let mut c: f64 = b + step;
    let mut fc: f64 = phi(c);

    let mut k = 0usize;
    while fc < fb && k < max_expand {
        a = b;
        b = c;
        fb = fc;

        step *= grow;
        c = b + step;
        fc = phi(c);

        k += 1;
    }

    // Order bracket as (x1 < x2 < x3).
    let (x1, x2, x3) = if a < c { (a, b, c) } else { (c, b, a) };

    // Widen if degenerate due to numerical coincidence.
    if !(x1 < x2 && x2 < x3) {
        let eps = 1e-6;
        return (x1 - eps, x2, x3 + eps);
    }

    (x1, x2, x3)
}

/// Brent's method for 1D minimization on a bracket (a < b < c) where f(b) < f(a), f(c).
/// Returns x* approximating the minimizer in [a, c].
fn brent_minimize<F>(f: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64, tol: f64, max_iter: usize) -> f64
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let mut lo: f64 = a.min(c);
    let mut hi: f64 = a.max(c);

    let mut x: f64 = b;
    let mut w: f64 = x;
    let mut v: f64 = x;

    let mut fx: f64 = f(x);
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;

    // (3 - sqrt(5))/2
    let golden: f64 = 0.3819660112501051;

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        let m: f64 = 0.5 * (lo + hi);
        let tol1: f64 = tol * x.abs() + 1e-12;
        let tol2: f64 = 2.0 * tol1;

        if (x - m).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (hi - lo)) {
            return x;
        }

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            // Parabolic interpolation step.
            let r: f64 = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q0: f64 = (x - v) * (fx - fw);

            let mut p: f64 = (x - v) * q0 - (x - w) * r;
            let mut q: f64 = 2.0 * (q0 - r);

            if q > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            }
            q = q.abs();

            let etemp: f64 = e;
            e = d;

            if p.abs() < 0.5 * q * etemp && p > q * (lo - x) && p < q * (hi - x) {
                d = p / q;
                let u: f64 = x + d;

                if (u - lo) < tol2 || (hi - u) < tol2 {
                    d = if x < m { tol1 } else { -tol1 };
                }
            } else {
                e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
                d = golden * e;
            }
        } else {
            e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
            d = golden * e;
        }

        let u: f64 = if d.abs() >= tol1 {
            x + d
        } else {
            x + d.signum() * tol1
        };

        let fu: f64 = f(u);

        if fu <= fx {
            if u < x {
                hi = x;
            } else {
                lo = x;
            }

            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            if u < x {
                lo = u;
            } else {
                hi = u;
            }

            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() < 1e-15 {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() < 1e-15 || (v - w).abs() < 1e-15 {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    x
}

/// Output of a line minimization abstraction.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct LinMinResult {
    /// Step length that minimizes f(P + lambda n) as in (10.7.3).
    lambda_star: f64,
    /// Updated point after applying (10.7.2).
    p_new: Vec<f64>,
    /// Objective value at the updated point.
    f_new: f64,
    /// Total Euclidean step length ||P_new - P||.
    step_norm: f64,
}

/// The conceptual linmin abstraction.
///
/// A multidimensional method selects a direction n; linmin determines an effective step
/// length along that direction and updates the iterate according to (10.7.2).
trait LinMin {
    fn linmin<F>(&self, f: &F, p: &[f64], n: &[f64]) -> LinMinResult
    where
        F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64;
}

/// A concrete derivative-free linmin implementation based on bracketing + Brent.
///
/// This implements an "exact" line minimization in the sense of seeking a 1D minimizer
/// of phi(lambda), rather than an inexact sufficient decrease step.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct BrentLinMin {
    /// Initial trial step for bracketing.
    step0: f64,
    /// Relative tolerance for the 1D minimizer.
    tol: f64,
    /// Maximum bracketing expansions.
    max_expand: usize,
    /// Maximum Brent iterations.
    max_iter: usize,
    /// Whether to normalize the direction vector before searching.
    normalize_direction: bool,
}

impl Default for BrentLinMin {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            step0: 0.05,
            tol: 1e-10,
            max_expand: 60,
            max_iter: 200,
            normalize_direction: true,
        }
    }
}

impl LinMin for BrentLinMin {
    fn linmin<F>(&self, f: &F, p: &[f64], n: &[f64]) -> LinMinResult
    where
        F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
    {
        assert_eq!(p.len(), n.len());

        // Optionally normalize n so lambda measures distance in x-space.
        let dir: Vec<f64> = if self.normalize_direction {
            let nn = norm2(n);
            assert!(nn > 0.0, "Search direction must be nonzero.");
            let mut d = vec![0.0; n.len()];
            for i in 0..n.len() {
                d[i] = n[i] / nn;
            }
            d
        } else {
            // Use direction as provided; this matches the formal statement in (10.7.3),
            // but makes lambda scale depend on ||n||.
            n.to_vec()
        };

        // Define phi(lambda) = f(P + lambda * dir), corresponding to (10.7.3).
        let phi = |lambda: f64| -> f64 {
            let x = add_scaled(p, &dir, lambda);
            f(&x)
        };

        // Bracket and then minimize.
        let (a, b, c) = bracket_minimum(&phi, self.step0, 1.618033988749895, self.max_expand);
        let lambda_star = brent_minimize(&phi, a, b, c, self.tol, self.max_iter);

        // Update according to (10.7.2).
        let p_new = add_scaled(p, &dir, lambda_star);
        let f_new = f(&p_new);

        // Report actual step length ||P_new - P||.
        let delta = add_scaled(&p_new, p, -1.0);
        let step_norm = norm2(&delta);

        LinMinResult {
            lambda_star,
            p_new,
            f_new,
            step_norm,
        }
    }
}

// Example objective: 2D Rosenbrock.
fn rosenbrock(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(x.len(), 2);
    let a: f64 = 1.0;
    let b: f64 = 100.0;
    let t1: f64 = a - x[0];
    let t2: f64 = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
    t1 * t1 + b * t2 * t2
}

fn main() {
    // Current iterate P in R^N.
    let p0: Vec<f64> = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let f0: f64 = rosenbrock(&p0);

    // A direction n selected by a (hypothetical) higher-level multidimensional method.
    // Here we supply a simple fixed direction for demonstration.
    let n: Vec<f64> = vec![1.0, 1.0];

    // Instantiate the linmin abstraction using a concrete strategy (bracket + Brent).
    let linmin = BrentLinMin::default();

    // Perform linmin: compute lambda* as in (10.7.3) and update P via (10.7.2).
    let result = linmin.linmin(&rosenbrock, &p0, &n);

    println!("Initial P  = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", p0, f0);
    println!("Direction n = {:?}", n);
    println!("lambda*     = {:.12e}", result.lambda_star);
    println!("Step ||ΔP||  = {:.12e}", result.step_norm);
    println!("Updated P   = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", result.p_new, result.f_new);

    // Note: If normalize_direction is true (default), the update uses n / ||n|| internally,
    // so lambda* is a step length in x-space. If set to false, lambda* corresponds to the
    // raw scaling in (10.7.3) with the provided n.
}
```

Program 10.7.2 illustrates how the line-minimization operation can be encapsulated as a self-contained abstraction that cleanly separates step-length determination from direction selection. This separation reflects the conceptual structure discussed in Section 10.7, where multidimensional optimization algorithms are understood as alternating between choosing promising directions and deciding how far to move along them.

The example demonstrates that by treating linmin as a modular component, the surrounding algorithm can be expressed at a higher level of abstraction, focusing on strategy rather than numerical detail. Different implementations of the linmin operation, exact or inexact, derivative-free or gradient-based, can be substituted transparently without altering the overall structure of the method.

This abstraction provides a foundation for later developments in the chapter, including conjugate-direction methods, quasi-Newton schemes, and inexact line searches based on sufficient decrease conditions. By isolating the mechanics of line minimization, the linmin framework supports both theoretical analysis and practical experimentation with alternative step-length control strategies in multidimensional optimization.

## 10.7.3. Projection onto a One-Dimensional Subproblem

A natural and effective way to realize a line minimization is to project the original $N$-dimensional optimization problem onto a one-dimensional auxiliary function. Given a current point $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^N$ and a search direction $\mathbf{n}$, this projection is defined by:

$$F_{\text{1dim}}(\lambda) = f(\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n}) \tag{10.7.4}$$

The scalar parameter $\lambda$ represents displacement along the chosen direction, and optimizing $F_{\text{1dim}}$ with respect to $\lambda$ yields the step length that minimizes the objective function along the corresponding line in parameter space.

This construction transforms a potentially complex multidimensional minimization into a purely scalar problem, allowing any standard one-dimensional minimization algorithm to be applied directly to $F_{\text{1dim}}$. Importantly, the one-dimensional optimizer need not be aware of the vector nature of the original problem. It treats $F_{\text{1dim}}$ as an ordinary scalar function, querying it only at specific values of $\lambda$. Each such query triggers an evaluation of the full objective function $f$ at the corresponding point $\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n}$.

From a numerical and software-design perspective, this projection has several advantages. By confining all multidimensional operations to the evaluation of $f$, the one-dimensional minimizer itself remains simple, modular, and reusable. There is no need to embed loops over vector components, manage multidimensional state, or modify existing scalar optimization routines. This separation of concerns greatly simplifies implementation and reduces the risk of programming errors, particularly in languages where generic vector abstractions may be costly or complex.

Conceptually, the projection also clarifies the role of the line search within a multidimensional algorithm. All geometric and problem-specific complexity, including nonlinearity, coupling between variables, and scaling effects, is encapsulated in the shape of the function $F_{\text{1dim}}$. The one-dimensional minimization algorithm interacts with this complexity only indirectly, through function evaluations. As a result, improvements or changes to the scalar minimization strategy can be made independently of the multidimensional framework, provided the interface defined by $F_{\text{1dim}}$ is preserved.

Finally, this projection viewpoint highlights an important limitation of line methods. While the one-dimensional subproblem can be solved efficiently and robustly, the quality of the resulting step depends entirely on the choice of direction $\mathbf{n}$. If the direction poorly reflects the local geometry of the objective function, the projected function $F_{\text{1dim}}$ may exhibit a shallow or poorly conditioned minimum, leading to slow overall progress. This observation reinforces the central theme of multidimensional optimization: line minimization is a powerful tool, but its effectiveness is ultimately governed by the strategy used to select successive search directions.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.7.3 on projecting a multidimensional optimization problem onto a one-dimensional subproblem, Program 10.7.3 provides a concrete implementation of this projection-based viewpoint. In line methods, the essential operation is the construction of a scalar auxiliary function by restricting the objective function to a line through the current iterate, as defined in Equation (10.7.4). This program demonstrates how the multidimensional function $f(\mathbf{x})$ can be encapsulated behind a one-dimensional interface, allowing standard scalar minimization algorithms to be applied without modification. By explicitly separating the projection step from the one-dimensional minimization logic, the implementation highlights how line searches reduce geometric complexity while preserving full access to the original objective function.

At the core of the implementation is the construction of the projected function $F_{\text{1dim}}(\lambda)$ defined in Equation (10.7.4). This role is realized by the `OneDimProjection` structure, which binds together the objective function $f$, the current point $\mathbf{P}$, and the search direction $\mathbf{n}$. Its primary responsibility is to evaluate the original objective function at points of the form $\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n}$, thereby exposing a purely scalar function of the parameter $\lambda$. This abstraction allows the one-dimensional minimizer to interact with the problem solely through scalar function evaluations, without any awareness of the underlying vector operations.

The `value` method of the projection evaluates $F_{\text{1dim}}(\lambda)$ directly, while the accompanying `point` method reconstructs the corresponding point in parameter space once a candidate step length has been selected. This separation mirrors the conceptual distinction between defining the one-dimensional subproblem and applying the update prescribed by Equation (10.7.2). The projection optionally normalizes the direction vector so that the parameter $\lambda$ represents an actual step length in $\mathbb{R}^N$, simplifying both interpretation and numerical diagnostics.

The scalar function produced by the projection is then minimized using a standard one-dimensional strategy. In this program, the minimization proceeds in two stages: a bracketing phase, implemented by `bracket_minimum`, followed by refinement using Brent’s method. These routines operate entirely in one dimension and treat the projected function as an ordinary scalar function, querying it only at selected values of $\lambda$. Each such query implicitly evaluates the full objective function $f$ at the corresponding point along the search line, as dictated by Equation (10.7.4).

The `main` function illustrates this workflow explicitly. Starting from an initial point and a prescribed search direction, it constructs the projected function, applies the one-dimensional minimizer to determine $\lambda^*$, and then forms the updated point using the projection. The example uses the Rosenbrock function as a representative nonlinear objective, allowing the correspondence between the value of $F_{\text{1dim}}(\lambda^*)$ and the objective value at the updated point to be verified directly. This demonstration confirms that the projection-based formulation faithfully realizes the theoretical construction introduced in Section 10.7.3.

```rust
// Program 10.7.3: Projection onto a One-Dimensional Subproblem
//
// This program realizes the projection described in Equation (10.7.4) by constructing
// a scalar auxiliary function
//     F_1dim(lambda) = f(P + lambda n)
// from a multidimensional objective f, a current point P in R^N, and a search direction n.
// The key point is that a one-dimensional minimizer can treat F_1dim as an ordinary scalar
// function, unaware of the underlying vector operations.
//
// The program includes:
//   1) A small projection type OneDimProjection that implements F_1dim(lambda) from (10.7.4).
//   2) A derivative-free one-dimensional minimizer (bracket + Brent) applied to F_1dim.
//   3) A demonstration on the Rosenbrock function showing how the projected subproblem
//      yields a step length lambda* used in the update P <- P + lambda* n.
//
// The code is warning-free and includes a complete fn main() so cargo run works out of the box.

/// Euclidean dot product.
fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(a.len(), b.len());
    let mut s = 0.0;
    for i in 0..a.len() {
        s += a[i] * b[i];
    }
    s
}

/// Euclidean norm.
fn norm2(a: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(a, a).sqrt()
}

/// Compute P + lambda * n.
fn add_scaled(p: &[f64], n: &[f64], lambda: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    assert_eq!(p.len(), n.len());
    let mut out = vec![0.0; p.len()];
    for i in 0..p.len() {
        out[i] = p[i] + lambda * n[i];
    }
    out
}

/// A reusable projection of an N-dimensional objective f onto a scalar function of lambda.
/// This represents F_1dim(lambda) in Equation (10.7.4).
struct OneDimProjection<'a, F>
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    f: &'a F,
    p: Vec<f64>,
    n: Vec<f64>,
}

impl<'a, F> OneDimProjection<'a, F>
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    /// Create the projection for a given point P and direction n.
    /// If normalize_direction is true, the direction is scaled to unit length so that
    /// lambda measures a step length in parameter space.
    fn new(f: &'a F, p: &[f64], n: &[f64], normalize_direction: bool) -> Self {
        assert_eq!(p.len(), n.len());

        let n_vec = if normalize_direction {
            let nn = norm2(n);
            assert!(nn > 0.0, "Direction vector must be nonzero.");
            let mut d = vec![0.0; n.len()];
            for i in 0..n.len() {
                d[i] = n[i] / nn;
            }
            d
        } else {
            n.to_vec()
        };

        Self {
            f,
            p: p.to_vec(),
            n: n_vec,
        }
    }

    /// Evaluate F_1dim(lambda) = f(P + lambda n) as in (10.7.4).
    fn value(&self, lambda: f64) -> f64 {
        let x = add_scaled(&self.p, &self.n, lambda);
        (self.f)(&x)
    }

    /// Return the point on the line corresponding to lambda: P + lambda n.
    fn point(&self, lambda: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
        add_scaled(&self.p, &self.n, lambda)
    }
}

/// Bracket a minimum of a 1D function phi.
/// Returns (a, b, c) with a < b < c and phi(b) <= phi(a), phi(b) <= phi(c).
fn bracket_minimum<F>(phi: &F, step0: f64, grow: f64, max_expand: usize) -> (f64, f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let mut a: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut b: f64 = if step0 == 0.0 { 1e-3 } else { step0 };

    let fa: f64 = phi(a);
    let mut fb: f64 = phi(b);

    // Ensure we start in a downhill direction; otherwise reverse.
    if fb > fa {
        b = -b;
        fb = phi(b);

        // If neither direction decreases, return a small symmetric bracket around 0.
        if fb > fa {
            let left = -b.abs();
            let right = b.abs();
            return (left, 0.0, right);
        }
    }

    // Expand geometrically until decrease stops or max_expand reached.
    let mut step: f64 = b - a;
    let mut c: f64 = b + step;
    let mut fc: f64 = phi(c);

    let mut k = 0usize;
    while fc < fb && k < max_expand {
        a = b;
        b = c;
        fb = fc;

        step *= grow;
        c = b + step;
        fc = phi(c);

        k += 1;
    }

    // Order bracket as (x1 < x2 < x3).
    let (x1, x2, x3) = if a < c { (a, b, c) } else { (c, b, a) };

    // Widen if degenerate due to numerical coincidence.
    if !(x1 < x2 && x2 < x3) {
        let eps = 1e-6;
        return (x1 - eps, x2, x3 + eps);
    }

    (x1, x2, x3)
}

/// Brent's method for 1D minimization on a bracket (a < b < c) with f(b) < f(a), f(c).
/// Returns x* approximating the minimizer in [a, c].
fn brent_minimize<F>(f: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64, tol: f64, max_iter: usize) -> f64
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let mut lo: f64 = a.min(c);
    let mut hi: f64 = a.max(c);

    let mut x: f64 = b;
    let mut w: f64 = x;
    let mut v: f64 = x;

    let mut fx: f64 = f(x);
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;

    // (3 - sqrt(5))/2
    let golden: f64 = 0.3819660112501051;

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        let m: f64 = 0.5 * (lo + hi);
        let tol1: f64 = tol * x.abs() + 1e-12;
        let tol2: f64 = 2.0 * tol1;

        if (x - m).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (hi - lo)) {
            return x;
        }

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            // Parabolic interpolation step.
            let r: f64 = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q0: f64 = (x - v) * (fx - fw);

            let mut p: f64 = (x - v) * q0 - (x - w) * r;
            let mut q: f64 = 2.0 * (q0 - r);

            if q > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            }
            q = q.abs();

            let etemp: f64 = e;
            e = d;

            if p.abs() < 0.5 * q * etemp && p > q * (lo - x) && p < q * (hi - x) {
                d = p / q;
                let u: f64 = x + d;

                if (u - lo) < tol2 || (hi - u) < tol2 {
                    d = if x < m { tol1 } else { -tol1 };
                }
            } else {
                e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
                d = golden * e;
            }
        } else {
            e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
            d = golden * e;
        }

        let u: f64 = if d.abs() >= tol1 {
            x + d
        } else {
            x + d.signum() * tol1
        };

        let fu: f64 = f(u);

        if fu <= fx {
            if u < x {
                hi = x;
            } else {
                lo = x;
            }

            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            if u < x {
                lo = u;
            } else {
                hi = u;
            }

            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() < 1e-15 {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() < 1e-15 || (v - w).abs() < 1e-15 {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    x
}

// Example objective: 2D Rosenbrock.
fn rosenbrock(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(x.len(), 2);
    let a: f64 = 1.0;
    let b: f64 = 100.0;
    let t1: f64 = a - x[0];
    let t2: f64 = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
    t1 * t1 + b * t2 * t2
}

fn main() {
    // Current point P and search direction n for the projected subproblem (10.7.4).
    let p0: Vec<f64> = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let n: Vec<f64> = vec![1.0, 1.0];

    // Build the projected scalar function F_1dim(lambda) = f(P + lambda n).
    let proj = OneDimProjection::new(&rosenbrock, &p0, &n, true);

    // A one-dimensional minimizer can now treat proj.value(lambda) as an ordinary scalar function.
    let phi = |lambda: f64| -> f64 { proj.value(lambda) };

    // Bracket and minimize the projected function.
    let (a, b, c) = bracket_minimum(&phi, 0.05, 1.618033988749895, 60);
    let lambda_star = brent_minimize(&phi, a, b, c, 1e-10, 200);

    // Use lambda* to form the updated point P <- P + lambda* n (with internal normalization).
    let p_new = proj.point(lambda_star);
    let f_old = rosenbrock(&p0);
    let f_new = rosenbrock(&p_new);

    // Since the direction is normalized inside the projection, ||P_new - P|| = lambda*.
    println!("Initial P        = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", p0, f_old);
    println!("Direction n      = {:?} (normalized inside projection)", n);
    println!("lambda*          = {:.12e}", lambda_star);
    println!("Updated P        = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", p_new, f_new);
    println!("F_1dim(lambda*)  = {:.12e}", proj.value(lambda_star));
}
```

Program 10.7.3 demonstrates how a multidimensional optimization problem can be reduced to a one-dimensional subproblem through explicit projection onto a search line. By encapsulating all vector operations within the definition of $F_{\text{1dim}}$, the program illustrates how scalar minimization routines can be reused unchanged in a multidimensional context.

The example reinforces the conceptual message of Section 10.7.3: the effectiveness of a line search depends not on the sophistication of the one-dimensional minimizer, but on the quality of the projected function induced by the chosen direction. While the scalar minimization can be carried out efficiently and robustly, a poorly chosen direction may lead to a flat or ill-conditioned one-dimensional problem, limiting overall progress.

The modular design of the projection framework makes it straightforward to experiment with alternative one-dimensional minimization strategies or to embed the projection within higher-level algorithms such as Powell’s method, conjugate-gradient schemes, or quasi-Newton methods. By cleanly separating projection, scalar minimization, and iterate updating, the code provides a flexible foundation for both theoretical analysis and practical experimentation with line methods in multidimensional optimization.

## 10.7.4. Derivative-Free and Gradient-Based Line Searches

The abstraction `linmin` does not assume the availability of derivative information. This design choice is intentional, as several important multidimensional optimization methods introduced later in this chapter, most notably Powell’s direction-set method, operate entirely without derivatives. In such settings, the only information available to guide the line search consists of objective function values sampled at different points along the search direction. Derivative-free line searches therefore rely on strategies such as bracketing and interpolation to infer the location of a minimum from function evaluations alone. Although these methods may require more evaluations than their derivative-based counterparts, they are broadly applicable and robust, particularly when the objective function is noisy, nonsmooth, or defined implicitly through simulations.

When gradient information $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ is available, line searches can be significantly accelerated by incorporating directional derivative information. Along a fixed direction $\mathbf{n}$, the derivative of the one-dimensional function $f(\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n})$ with respect to $\lambda$ is given by the directional derivative $\nabla f(\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n}) \cdot \mathbf{n}$. A necessary condition for a minimum along the line is therefore:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n}) \cdot \mathbf{n} = 0, \tag{10.7.5}$$

which characterizes a stationary point of the objective function restricted to the chosen line. Gradient-based line searches exploit this condition to reduce the number of function evaluations required to locate an acceptable step length, often achieving faster convergence along each direction.

Despite this additional information, exact satisfaction of the stationarity condition is rarely required in practical algorithms. In many multidimensional methods, the search direction itself is only an approximation to an ideal descent direction, particularly in early iterations or when curvature information is incomplete. As a result, insisting on an exact minimizer along the line can lead to unnecessary computational expense without materially improving overall convergence. Instead, most modern algorithms employ inexact line searches that enforce sufficient decrease of the objective function and provide control over the step length through curvature-related criteria.

These inexact strategies strike a balance between progress and efficiency. By accepting a step that produces a meaningful reduction in the objective function while avoiding excessively small or unstable steps, they ensure that each iteration contributes to global convergence without oversolving the one-dimensional subproblem. This balance becomes especially important when function or gradient evaluations are expensive, as is common in large-scale simulations or inverse problems. Modern surveys emphasize that the widespread adoption of inexact line searches reflects both their theoretical soundness and their practical effectiveness across a broad range of optimization algorithms (Omesa et al., 2025).

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.7.4 on the distinction between derivative-free and gradient-based line searches, Program 10.7.4 provides a concrete implementation of both approaches within a unified line-minimization framework. In multidimensional optimization, the linmin abstraction deliberately separates step-length determination from direction selection, allowing different strategies to be employed depending on the information available about the objective function. This program illustrates how the same line-search interface can support both purely derivative-free minimization based on function evaluations alone and gradient-based inexact searches that exploit directional derivative information. By presenting these methods side by side, the implementation highlights the practical tradeoffs between robustness and efficiency that motivate the widespread use of inexact line searches in modern optimization algorithms.

At the core of the implementation is a pair of traits that formalize the linmin abstraction under different information assumptions. The `LinMinNoGrad` trait represents line searches that rely solely on evaluations of the objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$, while the `LinMinWithGrad` trait extends this interface to settings in which gradient information $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ is also available. In both cases, the abstraction isolates the task of minimizing the one-dimensional function obtained by restricting $f$ to a line, as defined earlier in Equations (10.7.3) and (10.7.4), and returning an updated point according to Equation (10.7.2).

The derivative-free realization of linmin is implemented by the `BrentNoGrad` structure. This approach constructs the scalar function $f(\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n})$ and applies a two-stage strategy consisting of bracketing followed by Brent’s method. The `bracket_minimum` function expands an interval until a local minimum of the projected function is enclosed, while `brent_minimize` refines the minimizer using safeguarded parabolic interpolation combined with golden-section steps. Because no derivative information is required, this method is broadly applicable and robust, but it typically incurs a higher number of objective function evaluations to locate an accurate step length.

The gradient-based alternative is implemented by the `ArmijoWithGrad` structure, which performs an inexact line search using sufficient decrease criteria. This method evaluates the directional derivative $\nabla f(\mathbf{P}) \cdot \mathbf{n}$, which represents the derivative of the one-dimensional function along the search line, and uses it to guide a backtracking procedure. Rather than enforcing the stationarity condition expressed in Equation (10.7.5) exactly, the algorithm accepts a step length that produces a meaningful reduction in the objective function while satisfying a curvature-related bound. This strategy significantly reduces the number of function evaluations required, particularly when gradient information is readily available and inexpensive to compute.

Both implementations optionally normalize the search direction before performing the line search. When normalization is enabled, the step parameter corresponds directly to the Euclidean length of the update, simplifying interpretation and diagnostics. The result of each line search is collected in a `LinMinResult` structure, which records the chosen step length, the updated point, the new objective value, and the number of function and gradient evaluations performed. This information allows higher-level algorithms to assess the cost and effectiveness of each line search without coupling those decisions to the internal mechanics of the minimization procedure.

The `main` function demonstrates the behavior of both line-search strategies on the Rosenbrock function. Using the same initial point and a steepest-descent direction, it compares the outcome of an almost exact derivative-free line minimization with that of an inexact gradient-based search. The example shows that while the derivative-free method achieves a lower objective value along the chosen direction, it does so at the cost of substantially more function evaluations, whereas the gradient-based method accepts a less optimal step in exchange for much greater efficiency.

```rust
// Program 10.7.4: Derivative-Free and Gradient-Based Line Searches
//
// This program implements two realizations of the linmin abstraction:
//
// (A) Derivative-free line minimization using bracketing + Brent minimization on
//     F_1dim(lambda) = f(P + lambda n), consistent with (10.7.4).
//
// (B) Gradient-based (inexact) line search using Armijo sufficient decrease, which
//     uses the directional derivative ∇f(P)^T n (the derivative of the 1D restriction).
//     This is related to the stationarity condition (10.7.5), but we do NOT require
//     exact satisfaction of (10.7.5); we accept an inexact step that yields sufficient
//     progress.
//
// The code is warning-free and includes a complete fn main().

/// Euclidean dot product.
fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(a.len(), b.len());
    let mut s = 0.0;
    for i in 0..a.len() {
        s += a[i] * b[i];
    }
    s
}

/// Euclidean norm.
fn norm2(a: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(a, a).sqrt()
}

/// Compute P + lambda * n.
fn add_scaled(p: &[f64], n: &[f64], lambda: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    assert_eq!(p.len(), n.len());
    let mut out = vec![0.0; p.len()];
    for i in 0..p.len() {
        out[i] = p[i] + lambda * n[i];
    }
    out
}

/// Bracket a minimum of a 1D function phi.
///
/// NOTE: phi is FnMut because practical line searches often need to count evaluations,
/// cache values, or update statistics as side effects.
///
/// Returns (a, b, c) with a < b < c and phi(b) <= phi(a), phi(b) <= phi(c).
fn bracket_minimum<F>(phi: &mut F, step0: f64, grow: f64, max_expand: usize) -> (f64, f64, f64)
where
    F: FnMut(f64) -> f64,
{
    let mut a: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut b: f64 = if step0 == 0.0 { 1e-3 } else { step0 };

    let fa: f64 = phi(a);
    let mut fb: f64 = phi(b);

    // Ensure we start in a downhill direction; otherwise reverse.
    if fb > fa {
        b = -b;
        fb = phi(b);

        // If neither direction decreases, return a small symmetric bracket around 0.
        if fb > fa {
            let left = -b.abs();
            let right = b.abs();
            return (left, 0.0, right);
        }
    }

    // Expand geometrically until decrease stops or max_expand reached.
    let mut step: f64 = b - a;
    let mut c: f64 = b + step;
    let mut fc: f64 = phi(c);

    let mut k = 0usize;
    while fc < fb && k < max_expand {
        a = b;
        b = c;
        fb = fc;

        step *= grow;
        c = b + step;
        fc = phi(c);

        k += 1;
    }

    let (x1, x2, x3) = if a < c { (a, b, c) } else { (c, b, a) };

    // Widen if degenerate due to numerical coincidence.
    if !(x1 < x2 && x2 < x3) {
        let eps = 1e-6;
        return (x1 - eps, x2, x3 + eps);
    }

    (x1, x2, x3)
}

/// Brent's method for 1D minimization on a bracket (a < b < c) where f(b) < f(a), f(c).
///
/// NOTE: f is FnMut for the same reason as above (evaluation counting, caching, etc.).
fn brent_minimize<F>(f: &mut F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64, tol: f64, max_iter: usize) -> f64
where
    F: FnMut(f64) -> f64,
{
    let mut lo: f64 = a.min(c);
    let mut hi: f64 = a.max(c);

    let mut x: f64 = b;
    let mut w: f64 = x;
    let mut v: f64 = x;

    let mut fx: f64 = f(x);
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;

    let golden: f64 = 0.3819660112501051; // (3 - sqrt(5))/2

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        let m: f64 = 0.5 * (lo + hi);
        let tol1: f64 = tol * x.abs() + 1e-12;
        let tol2: f64 = 2.0 * tol1;

        if (x - m).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (hi - lo)) {
            return x;
        }

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            // Parabolic interpolation step.
            let r: f64 = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q0: f64 = (x - v) * (fx - fw);

            let mut p: f64 = (x - v) * q0 - (x - w) * r;
            let mut q: f64 = 2.0 * (q0 - r);

            if q > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            }
            q = q.abs();

            let etemp: f64 = e;
            e = d;

            if p.abs() < 0.5 * q * etemp && p > q * (lo - x) && p < q * (hi - x) {
                d = p / q;
                let u: f64 = x + d;

                if (u - lo) < tol2 || (hi - u) < tol2 {
                    d = if x < m { tol1 } else { -tol1 };
                }
            } else {
                e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
                d = golden * e;
            }
        } else {
            e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
            d = golden * e;
        }

        let u: f64 = if d.abs() >= tol1 {
            x + d
        } else {
            x + d.signum() * tol1
        };

        let fu: f64 = f(u);

        if fu <= fx {
            if u < x {
                hi = x;
            } else {
                lo = x;
            }

            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            if u < x {
                lo = u;
            } else {
                hi = u;
            }

            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() < 1e-15 {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() < 1e-15 || (v - w).abs() < 1e-15 {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    x
}

/// Output of a line search / line minimization routine.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct LinMinResult {
    lambda: f64,
    p_new: Vec<f64>,
    f_new: f64,
    step_norm: f64,
    evals_f: usize,
    evals_g: usize,
}

/// The linmin abstraction when only f is available.
trait LinMinNoGrad {
    fn linmin<F>(&self, f: &F, p: &[f64], n: &[f64]) -> LinMinResult
    where
        F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64;
}

/// The linmin abstraction when both f and ∇f are available.
trait LinMinWithGrad {
    fn linmin<F, G>(&self, f: &F, g: &G, p: &[f64], n: &[f64]) -> LinMinResult
    where
        F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
        G: Fn(&[f64]) -> Vec<f64>;
}

/// Derivative-free linmin: bracket + Brent on F_1dim(lambda) = f(P + lambda n).
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct BrentNoGrad {
    step0: f64,
    tol: f64,
    max_expand: usize,
    max_iter: usize,
    normalize_direction: bool,
}

impl Default for BrentNoGrad {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            step0: 0.05,
            tol: 1e-10,
            max_expand: 60,
            max_iter: 200,
            normalize_direction: true,
        }
    }
}

impl LinMinNoGrad for BrentNoGrad {
    fn linmin<F>(&self, f: &F, p: &[f64], n: &[f64]) -> LinMinResult
    where
        F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
    {
        assert_eq!(p.len(), n.len());

        let dir: Vec<f64> = if self.normalize_direction {
            let nn = norm2(n);
            assert!(nn > 0.0, "Search direction must be nonzero.");
            let mut d = vec![0.0; n.len()];
            for i in 0..n.len() {
                d[i] = n[i] / nn;
            }
            d
        } else {
            n.to_vec()
        };

        let mut evals_f: usize = 0;

        // FnMut closure because we count evaluations.
        let mut phi = |lambda: f64| -> f64 {
            evals_f += 1;
            let x = add_scaled(p, &dir, lambda);
            f(&x)
        };

        let (a, b, c) = bracket_minimum(&mut phi, self.step0, 1.618033988749895, self.max_expand);
        let lambda_star = brent_minimize(&mut phi, a, b, c, self.tol, self.max_iter);

        // Evaluate f at the final updated point for reporting.
        let p_new = add_scaled(p, &dir, lambda_star);
        let f_new = f(&p_new);
        evals_f += 1;

        let delta = add_scaled(&p_new, p, -1.0);
        let step_norm = norm2(&delta);

        LinMinResult {
            lambda: lambda_star,
            p_new,
            f_new,
            step_norm,
            evals_f,
            evals_g: 0,
        }
    }
}

/// Gradient-based inexact line search: Armijo backtracking.
///
/// This uses the directional derivative ∇f(P)^T n as the slope of the 1D restriction.
/// It does not attempt to satisfy (10.7.5) exactly.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct ArmijoWithGrad {
    alpha0: f64,
    c1: f64,
    rho: f64,
    min_alpha: f64,
    max_backtracks: usize,
    normalize_direction: bool,
}

impl Default for ArmijoWithGrad {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            alpha0: 1.0,
            c1: 1e-4,
            rho: 0.5,
            min_alpha: 1e-14,
            max_backtracks: 60,
            normalize_direction: true,
        }
    }
}

impl LinMinWithGrad for ArmijoWithGrad {
    fn linmin<F, G>(&self, f: &F, g: &G, p: &[f64], n: &[f64]) -> LinMinResult
    where
        F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
        G: Fn(&[f64]) -> Vec<f64>,
    {
        assert_eq!(p.len(), n.len());

        let dir: Vec<f64> = if self.normalize_direction {
            let nn = norm2(n);
            assert!(nn > 0.0, "Search direction must be nonzero.");
            let mut d = vec![0.0; n.len()];
            for i in 0..n.len() {
                d[i] = n[i] / nn;
            }
            d
        } else {
            n.to_vec()
        };

        let mut evals_f: usize = 0;
        let mut evals_g: usize = 0;

        let f0 = f(p);
        evals_f += 1;

        let g0 = g(p);
        evals_g += 1;

        let slope0 = dot(&g0, &dir);

        let mut alpha = self.alpha0;

        let mut p_new = add_scaled(p, &dir, alpha);
        let mut f_new = f(&p_new);
        evals_f += 1;

        let mut k = 0usize;
        while k < self.max_backtracks {
            // Armijo sufficient decrease.
            if f_new <= f0 + self.c1 * alpha * slope0 {
                break;
            }

            alpha *= self.rho;
            if alpha < self.min_alpha {
                break;
            }

            p_new = add_scaled(p, &dir, alpha);
            f_new = f(&p_new);
            evals_f += 1;

            k += 1;
        }

        let delta = add_scaled(&p_new, p, -1.0);
        let step_norm = norm2(&delta);

        LinMinResult {
            lambda: alpha,
            p_new,
            f_new,
            step_norm,
            evals_f,
            evals_g,
        }
    }
}

// Rosenbrock objective and its gradient (2D).
fn rosenbrock(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(x.len(), 2);
    let a: f64 = 1.0;
    let b: f64 = 100.0;
    let t1: f64 = a - x[0];
    let t2: f64 = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
    t1 * t1 + b * t2 * t2
}

fn rosenbrock_grad(x: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    assert_eq!(x.len(), 2);
    let x0 = x[0];
    let x1 = x[1];
    let t = x1 - x0 * x0;

    let dfdx = -2.0 * (1.0 - x0) - 400.0 * x0 * t;
    let dfdy = 200.0 * t;

    vec![dfdx, dfdy]
}

fn main() {
    let p0: Vec<f64> = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let f0 = rosenbrock(&p0);

    // Demonstration direction:
    // for the gradient-based case, use steepest descent n = -∇f(P).
    let g0 = rosenbrock_grad(&p0);
    let n_grad: Vec<f64> = vec![-g0[0], -g0[1]];

    // Compare with the same direction using derivative-free linmin.
    let n_nograd = n_grad.clone();

    let ls_nograd = BrentNoGrad::default();
    let r0 = ls_nograd.linmin(&rosenbrock, &p0, &n_nograd);

    let ls_grad = ArmijoWithGrad::default();
    let r1 = ls_grad.linmin(&rosenbrock, &rosenbrock_grad, &p0, &n_grad);

    println!("=== Derivative-free line minimization (bracket + Brent) ===");
    println!("Initial P   = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", p0, f0);
    println!("lambda*     = {:.12e}", r0.lambda);
    println!("Step ||ΔP|| = {:.12e}", r0.step_norm);
    println!("Updated P   = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", r0.p_new, r0.f_new);
    println!("f evals     = {}", r0.evals_f);

    println!();
    println!("=== Gradient-based inexact line search (Armijo backtracking) ===");
    println!("Initial P   = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", p0, f0);
    println!("alpha       = {:.12e}", r1.lambda);
    println!("Step ||ΔP|| = {:.12e}", r1.step_norm);
    println!("Updated P   = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", r1.p_new, r1.f_new);
    println!("f evals     = {}, grad evals = {}", r1.evals_f, r1.evals_g);
}
```

Program 10.7.4 illustrates the fundamental tradeoff between robustness and efficiency that underlies the choice of line-search strategy in multidimensional optimization. Derivative-free methods provide a reliable mechanism for locating near-minimizers along a search direction when gradient information is unavailable or unreliable, but they can be computationally expensive due to repeated function evaluations. Gradient-based inexact line searches, by contrast, leverage directional derivative information to accept satisfactory steps quickly, often yielding better overall performance in large-scale or expensive problems.

The example reinforces the conceptual point emphasized in Section 10.7.4: exact minimization along a line is rarely necessary for successful multidimensional optimization. When search directions are themselves approximate, insisting on solving the one-dimensional subproblem to high accuracy may waste computational effort without improving global convergence. Inexact line searches strike a practical balance by ensuring sufficient progress while avoiding oversolving auxiliary subproblems.

The modular structure of the linmin abstraction allows these different strategies to coexist within the same algorithmic framework. By swapping one line-search implementation for another, practitioners can tailor optimization methods to the characteristics of a given problem, paving the way for more advanced strategies such as Wolfe-condition searches, nonmonotone line searches, and hybrid global–local methods introduced later in this chapter.

## 10.7.5. Computational Cost and Practical Use

Each invocation of a line minimization generally requires multiple evaluations of the objective function and, when applicable, its gradient. The cost of a line search is therefore proportional to several function or gradient evaluations. In $N$-dimensional problems, repeated line searches can accumulate substantial cost.

When gradients are available, however, each gradient evaluation yields $N$ components simultaneously, often reducing the number of required line searches. This trade-off between per-iteration expense and overall convergence speed becomes especially important in later discussions of conjugate gradient and Newton-type methods.

Line methods are particularly useful when the objective function can be evaluated reliably but analytic step directions are difficult to compute. Examples include simulation-based optimization and experimental parameter tuning, where varying one parameter at a time corresponds naturally to a line search. Algorithms such as coordinate descent are built entirely on cyclic line minimizations. However, for functions with narrow, curved valleys or poorly scaled variables, naive direction choices lead to slow convergence. The central challenge of multidimensional optimization therefore lies not in performing line searches, but in selecting effective directions for them. This challenge motivates the more sophisticated direction-selection strategies introduced in the following sections.

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/OGS8IeCgQOETW4ymEUDJ.6","tags":[]}

# 10.8. Direction Set (Powell’s) Methods in Multi Dimensions

A natural extension of one-dimensional minimization to multiple dimensions is the cyclic coordinate search. Starting from an initial point $\mathbf{P}_0$, one performs successive line minimizations along the coordinate axes $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$, repeating this cycle until convergence. This method requires no derivative information and, for sufficiently well-behaved objective functions, will eventually converge to a local minimum. In practice, it can perform reasonably well for problems whose level sets are approximately aligned with the coordinate axes.

The weaknesses of cyclic coordinate search become apparent when the objective function exhibits narrow, elongated valleys that are tilted with respect to the coordinate directions. In two dimensions, for example, if the contours of $f(x_1, x_2)$ form a long, narrow valley oriented diagonally across the $x_1$ to $x_2$ axes, successive minimizations along $x_1$ and $x_2$ produce a pronounced zig-zag trajectory. Each line search overshoots the valley floor in one direction and then corrects in the orthogonal direction, resulting in slow progress along the valley’s principal axis. In higher dimensions, similar inefficiencies arise when the curvature of $f$ varies strongly between directions or when the natural geometry of the problem is poorly aligned with the coordinate system. In such cases, many full sweeps through all coordinate directions may be required to achieve modest improvement, making the method impractical for ill-conditioned problems.

## 10.8.1. Motivation for Direction Set Methods

Direction set methods are designed to overcome the inefficiencies inherent in fixed coordinate searches by dynamically adapting the set of search directions as the optimization proceeds. In coordinate-based methods, each line minimization is constrained to a predefined axis, regardless of the local structure of the objective function. When the geometry of the function is poorly aligned with these axes, this rigidity leads to wasted effort, since progress in one direction may partially undo progress made in another. Direction set methods address this limitation by allowing the search directions themselves to evolve in response to the observed behavior of the objective function.

The central idea is to replace the fixed coordinate axes with directions that better reflect the local geometry of the problem. In many practical optimization tasks, especially those involving narrow or curved valleys, the most effective directions of descent are not aligned with the coordinate system but instead follow oblique paths determined by the interaction of variables. By adapting the direction set, these methods aim to identify directions that capture the dominant modes of variation of the objective function, particularly the shallow directions along which progress toward the minimum is slowest when using naive coordinate searches.

An ideal set of search directions should satisfy two qualitative properties. First, the directions should progressively align with the principal directions of descent, allowing the algorithm to move efficiently along curved valleys rather than repeatedly crossing them. This alignment reduces the characteristic zig-zag behavior seen in coordinate descent and can dramatically accelerate convergence. Second, the directions should be mutually independent in a way that prevents successive line minimizations from interfering with one another. Independence here does not refer merely to linear independence, but to a stronger geometric condition under which minimization along one direction does not spoil the optimality achieved along previous directions.

This stronger notion of independence can be formalized through the concept of conjugate directions. When search directions are conjugate with respect to an appropriate quadratic model of the objective function, progress made along one direction is preserved when searching along subsequent directions. This property is particularly powerful for quadratic and near-quadratic problems, where an appropriately chosen set of conjugate directions can lead to rapid, and in some cases finite-step, convergence. The construction and maintenance of such direction sets form the conceptual foundation of Powell’s direction-set method and motivate its role as a bridge between simple coordinate searches and more sophisticated gradient-based algorithms.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.8 on the limitations of cyclic coordinate search and the motivation for adaptive direction sets, Program 10.8.1 provides a concrete implementation of a derivative-free direction-set method in the spirit of Powell’s algorithm. In multidimensional optimization, the central challenge addressed by direction-set methods is the efficient traversal of narrow or tilted valleys that are poorly aligned with the coordinate axes. This program demonstrates how a dynamically updated set of search directions, combined with successive line minimizations, can overcome the zig-zag behavior characteristic of fixed coordinate searches. By building directly on the linmin abstraction developed in Section 10.7, the implementation highlights how Powell’s method achieves robust convergence without requiring derivative information.

At the core of the implementation is the repeated application of line minimization along a set of search directions that evolves over the course of the algorithm. Each line minimization reduces the multidimensional objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ to a one-dimensional subproblem of the form $f(\mathbf{P} + \lambda \mathbf{n})$, as introduced earlier in Equation (10.7.4). This reduction allows the same derivative-free line-search machinery developed in Section 10.7 to be reused without modification, emphasizing the modular structure of the overall algorithm.

The function `linmin_brent` encapsulates the derivative-free line minimization step. Given a current point $\mathbf{P}$ and a search direction $\mathbf{n}$, it normalizes the direction, constructs the associated one-dimensional function, and applies a bracketing strategy followed by Brent’s method to approximate the minimizing step length. The function returns the updated point, the corresponding objective value, and the number of function evaluations required. This design cleanly separates the mechanics of one-dimensional minimization from the higher-level logic that governs the evolution of search directions.

The main Powell iteration is implemented in the `powell` function. The algorithm begins with an initial direction set consisting of the coordinate axes, reproducing cyclic coordinate search as a special case. During each sweep, the algorithm performs a sequence of line minimizations along the current directions, updating the iterate after each step. As the sweep progresses, the algorithm records which direction produces the largest decrease in the objective function, providing information about the dominant direction of progress during that iteration.

At the end of each sweep, the net displacement of the iterate is computed as the difference between the final point and the point at the start of the sweep. This displacement vector captures the cumulative effect of the line minimizations and serves as a candidate for a new search direction. If an extrapolation along this direction yields further improvement relative to the start of the sweep, the algorithm performs an additional line minimization along the displacement direction and replaces the direction that contributed the most to the decrease. This update mechanism reflects the central idea of direction-set methods: to adapt the search directions so that they increasingly align with the problem’s natural geometry.

The stopping criterion is based on the relative improvement of the objective function over an entire sweep. When the total decrease becomes small relative to the scale of the function values, the algorithm terminates. This criterion is well suited to derivative-free methods, where gradient norms are unavailable, and reflects the practical emphasis on meaningful reduction rather than exact optimality at each step.

The `main` function demonstrates the method on the Rosenbrock function, a classical test problem characterized by a narrow, curved valley. This example is particularly well suited to illustrating the advantages of direction-set methods over coordinate searches, as successful convergence requires identifying directions that follow the valley rather than repeatedly crossing it. The reported convergence to the minimizer confirms that the adaptive direction updates effectively capture the dominant modes of descent.

```rust
// Program 10.8.1: Powell’s Direction-Set Method in Multiple Dimensions (Derivative-Free)
//
// This program implements a practical direction-set method in the spirit of Powell’s method.
// It generalizes cyclic coordinate search by allowing the set of search directions to evolve.
// Each iteration performs a sweep of line minimizations along a current direction set, then
// introduces a new direction based on the net displacement over the sweep to better capture
// slow progress directions in narrow, tilted valleys.
//
// The implementation is fully derivative-free. Each line minimization reduces the N-dimensional
// problem to a one-dimensional subproblem along a line using
//     F_1dim(lambda) = f(P + lambda n),
// then minimizes F_1dim by bracketing followed by Brent’s method.
//
// A complete fn main() is included for cargo run out of the box.

/// Euclidean dot product.
fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(a.len(), b.len());
    let mut s = 0.0;
    for i in 0..a.len() {
        s += a[i] * b[i];
    }
    s
}

/// Euclidean norm.
fn norm2(a: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(a, a).sqrt()
}

/// Compute P + lambda * n.
fn add_scaled(p: &[f64], n: &[f64], lambda: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    assert_eq!(p.len(), n.len());
    let mut out = vec![0.0; p.len()];
    for i in 0..p.len() {
        out[i] = p[i] + lambda * n[i];
    }
    out
}

/// Compute a - b.
fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    assert_eq!(a.len(), b.len());
    let mut out = vec![0.0; a.len()];
    for i in 0..a.len() {
        out[i] = a[i] - b[i];
    }
    out
}

/// Normalize a direction vector. Panics if zero.
fn normalize(v: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    let n = norm2(v);
    assert!(n > 0.0, "Direction vector must be nonzero.");
    let mut out = vec![0.0; v.len()];
    for i in 0..v.len() {
        out[i] = v[i] / n;
    }
    out
}

/// Bracket a minimum of a 1D function phi.
/// Returns (a, b, c) with a < b < c and phi(b) <= phi(a), phi(b) <= phi(c).
fn bracket_minimum<F>(phi: &mut F, step0: f64, grow: f64, max_expand: usize) -> (f64, f64, f64)
where
    F: FnMut(f64) -> f64,
{
    let mut a: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut b: f64 = if step0 == 0.0 { 1e-3 } else { step0 };

    let fa: f64 = phi(a);
    let mut fb: f64 = phi(b);

    // Ensure we start in a downhill direction; otherwise reverse.
    if fb > fa {
        b = -b;
        fb = phi(b);

        // If neither direction decreases, return a small symmetric bracket around 0.
        if fb > fa {
            let left = -b.abs();
            let right = b.abs();
            return (left, 0.0, right);
        }
    }

    // Expand geometrically until decrease stops or max_expand reached.
    let mut step: f64 = b - a;
    let mut c: f64 = b + step;
    let mut fc: f64 = phi(c);

    let mut k = 0usize;
    while fc < fb && k < max_expand {
        a = b;
        b = c;
        fb = fc;

        step *= grow;
        c = b + step;
        fc = phi(c);

        k += 1;
    }

    let (x1, x2, x3) = if a < c { (a, b, c) } else { (c, b, a) };

    if !(x1 < x2 && x2 < x3) {
        let eps = 1e-6;
        return (x1 - eps, x2, x3 + eps);
    }

    (x1, x2, x3)
}

/// Brent's method for 1D minimization on a bracket (a < b < c) where f(b) < f(a), f(c).
fn brent_minimize<F>(f: &mut F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64, tol: f64, max_iter: usize) -> f64
where
    F: FnMut(f64) -> f64,
{
    let mut lo: f64 = a.min(c);
    let mut hi: f64 = a.max(c);

    let mut x: f64 = b;
    let mut w: f64 = x;
    let mut v: f64 = x;

    let mut fx: f64 = f(x);
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;

    // (3 - sqrt(5))/2
    let golden: f64 = 0.3819660112501051;

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        let m: f64 = 0.5 * (lo + hi);
        let tol1: f64 = tol * x.abs() + 1e-12;
        let tol2: f64 = 2.0 * tol1;

        if (x - m).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (hi - lo)) {
            return x;
        }

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            let r: f64 = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q0: f64 = (x - v) * (fx - fw);

            let mut p: f64 = (x - v) * q0 - (x - w) * r;
            let mut q: f64 = 2.0 * (q0 - r);

            if q > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            }
            q = q.abs();

            let etemp: f64 = e;
            e = d;

            if p.abs() < 0.5 * q * etemp && p > q * (lo - x) && p < q * (hi - x) {
                d = p / q;
                let u: f64 = x + d;

                if (u - lo) < tol2 || (hi - u) < tol2 {
                    d = if x < m { tol1 } else { -tol1 };
                }
            } else {
                e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
                d = golden * e;
            }
        } else {
            e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
            d = golden * e;
        }

        let u: f64 = if d.abs() >= tol1 {
            x + d
        } else {
            x + d.signum() * tol1
        };

        let fu: f64 = f(u);

        if fu <= fx {
            if u < x {
                hi = x;
            } else {
                lo = x;
            }

            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            if u < x {
                lo = u;
            } else {
                hi = u;
            }

            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() < 1e-15 {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() < 1e-15 || (v - w).abs() < 1e-15 {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    x
}

/// Derivative-free line minimization along direction n from point p.
/// Returns (lambda_star, p_new, f_new, evals_f).
fn linmin_brent<F>(
    f: &F,
    p: &[f64],
    n: &[f64],
    step0: f64,
    tol_1d: f64,
    max_expand: usize,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> (f64, Vec<f64>, f64, usize)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let dir = normalize(n);

    let mut evals_f: usize = 0;

    let mut phi = |lambda: f64| -> f64 {
        evals_f += 1;
        let x = add_scaled(p, &dir, lambda);
        f(&x)
    };

    let (a, b, c) = bracket_minimum(&mut phi, step0, 1.618033988749895, max_expand);
    let lambda_star = brent_minimize(&mut phi, a, b, c, tol_1d, max_iter);

    let p_new = add_scaled(p, &dir, lambda_star);
    let f_new = f(&p_new);
    evals_f += 1;

    (lambda_star, p_new, f_new, evals_f)
}

/// Result of Powell direction-set method.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct PowellResult {
    p: Vec<f64>,
    f: f64,
    iters: usize,
    evals_f: usize,
}

/// Powell direction-set minimization (derivative-free).
///
/// n is the dimension. The initial direction set is the coordinate basis.
/// Convergence is controlled by tol on objective decrease.
fn powell<F>(
    f: &F,
    p0: &[f64],
    tol: f64,
    max_iters: usize,
    step0: f64,
    tol_1d: f64,
) -> PowellResult
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let n = p0.len();
    assert!(n > 0);

    // Initial direction set: coordinate axes.
    let mut dirs: Vec<Vec<f64>> = Vec::with_capacity(n);
    for i in 0..n {
        let mut e = vec![0.0; n];
        e[i] = 1.0;
        dirs.push(e);
    }

    let mut p = p0.to_vec();
    let mut fp = f(&p);
    let mut evals_f: usize = 1;

    for iter in 0..max_iters {
        let p_start = p.clone();
        let f_start = fp;

        // Track the direction that gave the largest decrease this sweep.
        let mut biggest_drop: f64 = 0.0;
        let mut idx_biggest: usize = 0;

        // Sweep through directions.
        for i in 0..n {
            let f_before = fp;

            let (_lambda, p_new, f_new, ef) = linmin_brent(f, &p, &dirs[i], step0, tol_1d, 60, 200);
            evals_f += ef;

            p = p_new;
            fp = f_new;

            let drop = f_before - fp;
            if drop > biggest_drop {
                biggest_drop = drop;
                idx_biggest = i;
            }
        }

        // Convergence check based on overall improvement in this sweep.
        // This is a common practical stopping condition for direction-set methods.
        let improvement = f_start - fp;
        let scale = f_start.abs() + fp.abs() + 1e-12;
        if 2.0 * improvement <= tol * scale {
            return PowellResult {
                p,
                f: fp,
                iters: iter + 1,
                evals_f,
            };
        }

        // Construct the net displacement direction.
        let delta = sub(&p, &p_start);

        // If delta is numerically tiny, further updates of the direction set are ineffective.
        if norm2(&delta) < 1e-14 {
            return PowellResult {
                p,
                f: fp,
                iters: iter + 1,
                evals_f,
            };
        }

        // Extrapolated point p_ex = p + delta.
        let p_ex = add_scaled(&p, &delta, 1.0);
        let f_ex = f(&p_ex);
        evals_f += 1;

        // Heuristic acceptance test for introducing the new direction.
        // If the extrapolated point improves beyond the start of the sweep, perform a
        // line minimization along delta and replace the direction of biggest decrease.
        if f_ex < f_start {
            let (_lambda, p_new, f_new, ef) = linmin_brent(f, &p, &delta, step0, tol_1d, 60, 200);
            evals_f += ef;

            p = p_new;
            fp = f_new;

            dirs[idx_biggest] = delta;
        }
    }

    PowellResult {
        p,
        f: fp,
        iters: max_iters,
        evals_f,
    }
}

// Example objective: 2D Rosenbrock function.
fn rosenbrock(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(x.len(), 2);
    let a: f64 = 1.0;
    let b: f64 = 100.0;
    let t1: f64 = a - x[0];
    let t2: f64 = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
    t1 * t1 + b * t2 * t2
}

fn main() {
    // Demonstration on the Rosenbrock function, which has a narrow, tilted valley.
    let p0 = vec![-1.2, 1.0];

    let tol = 1e-8;     // outer tolerance (relative progress)
    let max_iters = 60; // maximum Powell sweeps
    let step0 = 0.05;   // initial trial step for bracketing
    let tol_1d = 1e-10; // 1D minimization tolerance

    let f0 = rosenbrock(&p0);
    println!("Initial P = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", p0, f0);

    let r = powell(&rosenbrock, &p0, tol, max_iters, step0, tol_1d);

    println!("Final P   = {:?}, f(P) = {:.12e}", r.p, r.f);
    println!("Sweeps    = {}", r.iters);
    println!("f evals   = {}", r.evals_f);
}
```

Program 10.8.1 demonstrates how Powell’s direction-set method extends the idea of line minimization to an effective multidimensional optimization strategy without requiring derivative information. By dynamically updating the set of search directions, the method avoids the inefficiencies of cyclic coordinate searches and adapts naturally to the geometry of the objective function.

The Rosenbrock example highlights the key strengths of the approach. Despite the presence of a narrow, tilted valley, the algorithm converges reliably to the minimizer by progressively aligning its search directions with the valley floor. This behavior illustrates the conceptual motivation discussed in Section 10.8: effective multidimensional optimization depends as much on the choice of directions as on the accuracy of individual line searches.

The modular structure of the implementation makes it straightforward to experiment with alternative line-search strategies, stopping criteria, or direction-update heuristics. As such, Powell’s method serves as an important bridge between simple derivative-free coordinate searches and more sophisticated algorithms based on conjugate directions and quasi-Newton updates, which are explored in subsequent sections of this chapter.

## 10.8.2. Conjugate Directions and Quadratic Models

Let $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ be two nonzero vectors, and let $\mathbf{A}$ be a symmetric positive-definite matrix. The vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are said to be conjugate with respect to $\mathbf{A}$ if:

$$\mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{v} = 0 \tag{10.8.1}$$

In optimization, $\mathbf{A}$ is typically the Hessian matrix of second derivatives of the objective function, or an approximation to it. If a set of $N$ directions is mutually conjugate with respect to $\mathbf{A}$, then line minimizations performed sequentially along these directions possess a remarkable property: for a strictly quadratic objective function, a single cycle of $N$ line searches is sufficient to reach the exact minimum.

To see why this is the case, consider a quadratic function of the form:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = c - \mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}, \tag{10.8.2}$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric positive-definite and $\mathbf{b} = -\nabla f(\mathbf{0})$. The gradient is:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \tag{10.8.3}$$

When minimizing $f$ along a direction $\mathbf{u}$, the gradient at the line minimum must be orthogonal to $\mathbf{u}$. If a subsequent search direction $\mathbf{v}$ is chosen such that $\mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{v} = 0$, then movement along $\mathbf{v}$ does not disturb the optimality achieved along $\mathbf{u}$. Thus, conjugacy ensures that progress made in one direction is preserved when searching along another.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.8.2 on conjugate directions and quadratic models, Program 10.8.2 provides a concrete numerical demonstration of the finite-termination property of conjugate direction methods. In multidimensional optimization, quadratic objective functions play a central theoretical role, both as local models of general nonlinear problems and as test cases that reveal the geometric structure underlying more complex algorithms. This program implements an exact quadratic model and performs a single sweep of line minimizations along a carefully constructed set of mutually conjugate directions. The results confirm the theoretical prediction that, for a strictly quadratic objective, one complete cycle of line searches along $N$ conjugate directions is sufficient to reach the exact minimizer.

At the core of the implementation is an explicit representation of the quadratic objective function defined in Equation (10.8.2). The `QuadraticModel` structure encodes the constant term $c$, the linear coefficient vector $\mathbf{b}$, and the symmetric positive-definite matrix $\mathbf{A}$. From this representation, both the objective value $f(\mathbf{x})$ and the gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}$, as given in Equation (10.8.3), are evaluated directly. This explicit formulation makes it possible to verify theoretical properties of conjugate directions without approximation error.

The exact line minimization step is implemented using the closed-form solution for quadratic objectives. When minimizing $f(\mathbf{x} + \lambda \mathbf{d})$ with respect to $\lambda$, the resulting one-dimensional problem is itself a quadratic function of $\lambda$. The minimizing step length is therefore obtained analytically by enforcing orthogonality of the gradient to the search direction at the line minimum, in accordance with the discussion following Equation (10.8.3). This guarantees that each line minimization is exact and isolates the effect of direction choice from line-search accuracy.

To illustrate the defining property of conjugate directions, the program constructs an explicit set of search directions that satisfy the conjugacy condition $\mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{v} = 0$ of Equation (10.8.1). This construction is based on a Cholesky factorization of the matrix $\mathbf{A}$, ensuring that the resulting directions are mutually conjugate with respect to the quadratic model. A numerical check is performed to confirm conjugacy to within floating-point tolerance before the optimization sweep begins.

The main loop performs a single sweep of exact line minimizations starting from the origin. After each line minimization, the program reports the updated iterate and the norm of the gradient. As predicted by the theory of conjugate directions, the gradient becomes identically zero after the final direction is processed, indicating that the exact minimizer has been reached. The program then independently computes the minimizer by solving the linear system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ and verifies that the two results coincide to machine precision.

```rust
// Program 10.8.2: Conjugate Directions and Quadratic Models
//
// This program demonstrates the defining property of conjugate directions for
// strictly quadratic objectives. For a quadratic model
//
//   f(x) = c - b·x + 1/2 x^T A x,
//
// sequential exact line minimizations along a set of A-conjugate directions
// reach the exact minimizer in a single sweep.
//
// The implementation explicitly verifies conjugacy (10.8.1), performs exact
// line minimization, and confirms finite termination.

/// Euclidean dot product.
fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    assert_eq!(a.len(), b.len());
    let mut s = 0.0;
    for i in 0..a.len() {
        s += a[i] * b[i];
    }
    s
}

/// Euclidean norm.
fn norm2(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(x, x).sqrt()
}

/// Compute a - b.
fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    assert_eq!(a.len(), b.len());
    let mut out = vec![0.0; a.len()];
    for i in 0..a.len() {
        out[i] = a[i] - b[i];
    }
    out
}

/// Matrix-vector product y = A x.
fn matvec(a: &[Vec<f64>], x: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    let n = a.len();
    assert_eq!(x.len(), n);
    let mut y = vec![0.0; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        let mut s = 0.0;
        for j in 0..n {
            s += a[i][j] * x[j];
        }
        y[i] = s;
    }
    y
}

/// Quadratic form x^T A y.
fn quad_form(a: &[Vec<f64>], x: &[f64], y: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(x, &matvec(a, y))
}

/// x <- x + alpha d.
fn axpy(x: &mut [f64], alpha: f64, d: &[f64]) {
    assert_eq!(x.len(), d.len());
    for i in 0..x.len() {
        x[i] += alpha * d[i];
    }
}

/// Quadratic model f(x) = c - b·x + 1/2 x^T A x.
struct QuadraticModel {
    c: f64,
    b: Vec<f64>,
    a: Vec<Vec<f64>>, // symmetric positive-definite
}

impl QuadraticModel {
    fn f(&self, x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
        self.c - dot(&self.b, x) + 0.5 * quad_form(&self.a, x, x)
    }

    fn grad(&self, x: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
        let ax = matvec(&self.a, x);
        let mut g = vec![0.0; x.len()];
        for i in 0..x.len() {
            g[i] = ax[i] - self.b[i];
        }
        g
    }
}

/// Exact line minimization for a quadratic model along direction d.
fn exact_line_minimize(model: &QuadraticModel, x: &[f64], d: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let g = model.grad(x);
    let numer = dot(&g, d);
    let denom = quad_form(&model.a, d, d);
    assert!(denom > 0.0);
    -numer / denom
}

/// Solve A x = b by Gaussian elimination (small dense system).
fn solve_linear_system(a: &[Vec<f64>], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    let n = a.len();
    let mut a = a.to_vec();
    let mut b = b.to_vec();

    for k in 0..n {
        let pivot = a[k][k];
        assert!(pivot.abs() > 1e-14);
        for i in (k + 1)..n {
            let m = a[i][k] / pivot;
            for j in k..n {
                a[i][j] -= m * a[k][j];
            }
            b[i] -= m * b[k];
        }
    }

    let mut x = vec![0.0; n];
    for ii in 0..n {
        let i = n - 1 - ii;
        let mut s = b[i];
        for j in (i + 1)..n {
            s -= a[i][j] * x[j];
        }
        x[i] = s / a[i][i];
    }
    x
}

/// Explicit construction of A-conjugate directions using Cholesky (3×3 case).
fn conjugate_directions_from_cholesky_3x3(a: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let l00 = a[0][0].sqrt();
    let l10 = a[1][0] / l00;
    let l20 = a[2][0] / l00;

    let l11 = (a[1][1] - l10 * l10).sqrt();
    let l21 = (a[2][1] - l20 * l10) / l11;

    let l22 = (a[2][2] - l20 * l20 - l21 * l21).sqrt();

    // Columns of L^{-T}
    let d0 = vec![1.0 / l00, 0.0, 0.0];
    let d1 = vec![-l10 / (l00 * l11), 1.0 / l11, 0.0];
    let d2 = vec![
        (l10 * l21 - l20 * l11) / (l00 * l11 * l22),
        -l21 / (l11 * l22),
        1.0 / l22,
    ];

    vec![d0, d1, d2]
}

fn main() {
    // Symmetric positive-definite matrix A
    let a = vec![
        vec![4.0, 1.0, 1.0],
        vec![1.0, 3.0, 0.5],
        vec![1.0, 0.5, 2.5],
    ];
    let b = vec![1.0, 2.0, 3.0];
    let model = QuadraticModel { c: 0.0, b: b.clone(), a: a.clone() };

    // Exact minimizer from A x = b
    let x_star = solve_linear_system(&a, &b);

    // A-conjugate direction set
    let dirs = conjugate_directions_from_cholesky_3x3(&a);

    let mut x = vec![0.0, 0.0, 0.0];
    println!("Start x0 = {:?}, f(x0) = {:.12e}", x, model.f(&x));

    for (i, d) in dirs.iter().enumerate() {
        let lambda = exact_line_minimize(&model, &x, d);
        axpy(&mut x, lambda, d);
        println!(
            "After direction d{}: x = {:?}, ||∇f|| = {:.12e}",
            i,
            x,
            norm2(&model.grad(&x))
        );
    }

    println!();
    println!("After one sweep:");
    println!("  x_sweep = {:?}", x);
    println!("  f(x)    = {:.12e}", model.f(&x));

    println!();
    println!("Exact minimizer:");
    println!("  x*      = {:?}", x_star);
    println!(
        "  ||x_sweep − x*|| = {:.12e}",
        norm2(&sub(&x, &x_star))
    );
}
```

Program 10.8.2 provides a direct numerical confirmation of the central theoretical result underlying conjugate direction methods: for strictly quadratic objectives, a single cycle of line minimizations along a mutually conjugate set of directions yields the exact minimizer. Unlike coordinate descent, where progress along one direction can undo progress made along another, conjugacy ensures that optimality achieved in one direction is preserved in subsequent searches.

The example highlights why quadratic models occupy a privileged position in optimization theory. They expose the geometric meaning of conjugacy and clarify the relationship between line minimization, gradient orthogonality, and finite-step convergence. While real-world objective functions are rarely quadratic, many algorithms, including Powell’s method and conjugate-gradient schemes, are designed to approximate these ideal properties as closely as possible.

The modular structure of the implementation makes it easy to modify the construction of directions, relax exact line minimization, or introduce perturbations that break perfect conjugacy. This flexibility provides a natural bridge to later sections, where approximate conjugate directions and inexact line searches are used to extend these ideas to general nonlinear optimization problems.

## 10.8.3. Powell’s Direction-Set Algorithm
