Powell’s direction-set method is a classical derivative-free algorithm that generates approximately conjugate search directions without using derivative information. The method is designed to retain the simplicity and robustness of line search techniques while overcoming the inefficiencies of fixed coordinate descent. Rather than relying on gradient or Hessian evaluations, it infers information about the local geometry of the objective function indirectly through the outcomes of successive line minimizations.

The algorithm begins with an initial set of $N$ linearly independent directions, most commonly chosen as the coordinate unit vectors $\{\mathbf{e}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{e}_N\}$. Starting from an initial point $\mathbf{P}_0$, the method proceeds in cycles, each consisting of $N$ line minimizations, one along each direction in the current set. At this stage, the procedure closely resembles cyclic coordinate search, but its distinctive features emerge in the way the direction set is updated after each cycle.

During a single cycle, line minimizations are performed sequentially along each direction in the current set. If $\mathbf{P}_i$ denotes the point before the $i$-th line search, the update rule is:

$$\mathbf{P}_{i+1} = \mathbf{P}_i + \lambda_i^* \mathbf{u}_i, \quad i = 0,1,\ldots,N-1, \tag{10.8.4}$$

where $\lambda_i^*$ minimizes the one-dimensional function $f(\mathbf{P}_i + \lambda \mathbf{u}_i)$. Each line search locally optimizes the objective function along a single direction while holding all other directions fixed. After completing all $N$ line searches in the cycle, the algorithm has moved from the starting point $\mathbf{P}_0$ to the endpoint $\mathbf{P}_N$, accumulating information about how the function responds to motion in different directions.

The key innovation of Powell’s method lies in how the direction set is updated at the end of each cycle. The net displacement over the cycle is computed as:

$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{P}_N - \mathbf{P}_0 \tag{10.8.5}$$

This vector summarizes the combined effect of all line searches and often provides a good approximation to the dominant direction of progress over the cycle. Geometrically, $\mathbf{v}$ tends to align with the long axis of a narrow or curved valley, since it reflects the average direction in which the objective function decreased most effectively across all directions in the set.

Powell’s original strategy replaces one of the existing search directions with the newly formed vector $\mathbf{v}$, thereby allowing the direction set to adapt to the observed geometry of the objective function. Through repeated cycles of line minimization and direction updating, the method progressively builds a set of directions that are better aligned with the local structure of the problem. For a strictly quadratic objective function, this process leads to the generation of mutually conjugate directions over successive iterations. As a result, the algorithm reaches the exact minimum in a finite number of cycles, illustrating how directional adaptation can eliminate the zig-zag inefficiencies of coordinate-based methods even in the absence of derivative information.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.8.3 on derivative-free optimization and the construction of adaptive search directions, Program 10.8.3 provides a concrete implementation of Powell’s direction-set algorithm. In multidimensional minimization problems where gradient or Hessian information is unavailable or unreliable, line-search-based methods offer a robust alternative, but naive coordinate descent often suffers from slow convergence due to zig-zagging. Powell’s method addresses this limitation by dynamically updating the set of search directions based on the observed progress of successive line minimizations. The program implements the full cyclic structure of the algorithm, combining sequential one-dimensional minimizations with direction-set adaptation to approximate conjugate directions and achieve efficient convergence using only function evaluations.

At the core of the implementation is the representation of search directions as explicit vectors in $\mathbb{R}^N$, together with a general interface for evaluating the objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$. The algorithm begins with an initial set of linearly independent directions, chosen here as the coordinate unit vectors $\{\mathbf{e}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{e}_N\}$, and an initial point $\mathbf{P}_0$. Each cycle of the method performs a sequence of one-dimensional minimizations along these directions, updating the current iterate according to the rule given in Equation (10.8.4). This stepwise structure mirrors cyclic coordinate search but serves primarily as a mechanism for extracting geometric information from the objective function rather than as a fixed descent strategy.

The one-dimensional minimizations required by Equation (10.8.4) are carried out using a derivative-free line search based on bracketing followed by Brent’s method. The bracketing method expands an initial interval until a local minimum of the restricted function $f(\mathbf{P}_i + \lambda \mathbf{u}_i)$ is enclosed, while Brent’s algorithm refines this bracket to locate the minimizing step length $\lambda_i^*$ with high numerical reliability. This combination provides a robust line search that does not rely on smoothness assumptions beyond unimodality along the search direction, making it well suited to the derivative-free context of Powell’s method.

After completing all $N$ line minimizations in a cycle, the program computes the net displacement vector $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{P}_N - \mathbf{P}_0$ as defined in Equation (10.8.5). This vector summarizes the cumulative effect of the cycle and captures the dominant direction of descent inferred from the combined line searches. In the implementation, the direction that produced the largest reduction in the objective function during the cycle is replaced by a normalized version of $\mathbf{v}$, thereby updating the direction set in accordance with Powell’s original strategy. An additional line minimization along this new direction is then performed to immediately exploit the accumulated directional information.

The stopping criteria in the program reflect the practical considerations discussed in Section 10.8.3. Convergence is assessed using both the relative reduction in the objective function over a cycle and the magnitude of the net displacement vector. This dual criterion ensures that the algorithm terminates when further cycles would yield negligible improvement, while avoiding premature termination in early stages when directional adaptation is still taking place. For strictly quadratic objective functions, these mechanisms allow the method to generate mutually conjugate directions over successive cycles and to reach the exact minimizer in a finite number of iterations.

```rust
// Program 10.8.3: Powell’s Direction-Set Algorithm
//
// --------------------------- Problem statement ---------------------------
// Given a continuously evaluable objective function f: R^N -> R (derivatives unavailable),
// determine an approximate minimizer P* using only function values.
//
// Powell’s direction-set method proceeds in cycles starting from an initial point P0 and an
// initial set of N linearly independent directions {u_0,...,u_{N-1}} (typically the coordinate
// unit vectors). In each cycle it performs N one-dimensional minimizations sequentially:
//
//   P_{i+1} = P_i + λ_i^* u_i,   i = 0,1,...,N-1,                                   (10.8.4)
//
// where λ_i^* minimizes the one-dimensional function f(P_i + λ u_i). After completing the N
// line searches, the net displacement over the cycle is
//
//   v = P_N - P_0.                                                                 (10.8.5)
//
// Powell’s strategy replaces one of the existing directions by v, allowing the direction set
// to adapt to the observed geometry of f. For strictly quadratic objectives, the method
// generates mutually conjugate directions over cycles and reaches the exact minimizer in a
// finite number of cycles (in exact arithmetic and exact line minimization).
//
// This implementation uses a robust derivative-free line search: bracketing + Brent’s method.
// It demonstrates the method on the 2D Rosenbrock function.
//
// Build and run:
//   cargo run --release
//
use std::f64;

type VecN = Vec<f64>;

fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn norm2(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(x, x).sqrt()
}

fn add_scaled(x: &[f64], alpha: f64, u: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    x.iter().zip(u.iter()).map(|(xi, ui)| xi + alpha * ui).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn scale(u: &[f64], s: f64) -> VecN {
    u.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

fn unit_vectors(n: usize) -> Vec<VecN> {
    let mut dirs = Vec::with_capacity(n);
    for i in 0..n {
        let mut e = vec![0.0; n];
        e[i] = 1.0;
        dirs.push(e);
    }
    dirs
}

/* --------------------------- 1D minimization --------------------------- */

// Bracket a local minimum for a unimodal function φ by expanding from (ax,bx).
// Returns (ax,bx,cx) with φ(bx) < φ(ax) and φ(bx) < φ(cx).
fn bracket_minimum<F>(phi: &F, mut ax: f64, mut bx: f64) -> (f64, f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let gold = 1.618033988749895_f64;

    let mut fa = phi(ax);
    let mut fb = phi(bx);

    // Ensure downhill from ax to bx.
    if fb > fa {
        std::mem::swap(&mut ax, &mut bx);
        std::mem::swap(&mut fa, &mut fb);
    }

    let mut cx = bx + gold * (bx - ax);
    let mut fc = phi(cx);

    // Expand until bx is a strict local minimum among ax,bx,cx.
    let mut iter = 0usize;
    while !(fb < fa && fb < fc) && iter < 120 {
        ax = bx;
        fa = fb;
        bx = cx;
        fb = fc;
        cx = bx + gold * (bx - ax);
        fc = phi(cx);
        iter += 1;
    }

    (ax, bx, cx)
}

// Brent’s method on the bracket (a,b,c). Returns (xmin, φ(xmin)).
//
// This version is written to be warning-free and explicitly typed as f64 throughout.
fn brent_minimize<F>(
    phi: &F,
    a: f64,
    b: f64,
    c: f64,
    tol: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let (mut lo, mut hi) = if a < c { (a, c) } else { (c, a) };

    let mut x: f64 = b;
    let mut w: f64 = b;
    let mut v: f64 = b;

    let mut fx: f64 = phi(x);
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    let golden: f64 = 0.3819660112501051; // 1 - 1/φ
    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        let m = 0.5 * (lo + hi);
        let tol1 = tol * x.abs() + 1e-12;
        let tol2 = 2.0 * tol1;

        // Termination criterion.
        if (x - m).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (hi - lo)) {
            return (x, fx);
        }

        // Decide whether to attempt a parabolic step.
        let mut use_parabolic = false;
        let mut p_step: f64 = 0.0;
        let mut q_step: f64 = 1.0;

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            let r = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q = (x - v) * (fx - fw);
            let mut p = (x - v) * q - (x - w) * r;
            let mut qq = 2.0 * (q - r);

            if qq > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            } else {
                qq = -qq;
            }

            // Accept parabolic step only if it stays within bounds and is not too large.
            let etemp = e;
            if p.abs() < 0.5 * qq * etemp.abs() && p > qq * (lo - x) && p < qq * (hi - x) {
                use_parabolic = true;
                p_step = p;
                q_step = qq;
            }
        }

        // Update movement scales (Brent bookkeeping).
        if use_parabolic {
            e = d;
            d = p_step / q_step;
        } else {
            e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
            d = golden * e;
        }

        // Enforce a minimal step size.
        let u = if d.abs() >= tol1 {
            x + d
        } else {
            x + d.signum() * tol1
        };

        let fu = phi(u);

        // Update bracket and the three best points (x,w,v).
        if fu <= fx {
            if u < x {
                hi = x;
            } else {
                lo = x;
            }
            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            if u < x {
                lo = u;
            } else {
                hi = u;
            }
            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() < 1e-30 {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() < 1e-30 || (v - w).abs() < 1e-30 {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    (x, fx)
}

// Minimizes f(P + λ u) over λ by bracketing + Brent.
// Returns (λ*, f(P + λ* u)).
fn line_minimize<F>(f: &F, p: &[f64], u: &[f64], tol: f64) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let phi = |lambda: f64| -> f64 { f(&add_scaled(p, lambda, u)) };
    let (a, b, c) = bracket_minimum(&phi, 0.0, 1.0);
    brent_minimize(&phi, a, b, c, tol, 250)
}

/* --------------------------- Powell method --------------------------- */

fn powell_direction_set<F>(
    f: &F,
    p0: &[f64],
    tol: f64,
    max_cycles: usize,
) -> (VecN, f64, usize)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let n = p0.len();
    let mut dirs = unit_vectors(n);

    let mut p = p0.to_vec();
    let mut fp = f(&p);

    for cycle in 0..max_cycles {
        let p_start = p.clone();
        let f_start = fp;

        // Track which direction produced the largest reduction during this cycle.
        let mut biggest_drop = 0.0;
        let mut idx_biggest = 0usize;

        // Perform N sequential line minimizations (10.8.4).
        for i in 0..n {
            let f_before = fp;

            let (lambda_star, _) = line_minimize(f, &p, &dirs[i], tol);
            p = add_scaled(&p, lambda_star, &dirs[i]);
            fp = f(&p);

            let drop = f_before - fp;
            if drop > biggest_drop {
                biggest_drop = drop;
                idx_biggest = i;
            }
        }

        // Net displacement over the cycle v = P_N - P_0 (10.8.5).
        let v = sub(&p, &p_start);
        let vnorm = norm2(&v);

        // Stopping criteria: small net motion and small relative improvement.
        let rel_impr = (f_start - fp).abs() / (f_start.abs() + 1.0);
        if rel_impr <= tol && vnorm <= tol * (norm2(&p_start) + 1.0) {
            return (p, fp, cycle + 1);
        }

        // Replace one direction by v (normalized to control scaling).
        if vnorm > 1e-14 {
            dirs[idx_biggest] = scale(&v, 1.0 / vnorm);

            // Optional but standard in practice: immediately exploit the new direction.
            let (lambda_star, _) = line_minimize(f, &p, &dirs[idx_biggest], tol);
            p = add_scaled(&p, lambda_star, &dirs[idx_biggest]);
            fp = f(&p);
        }

        // If the cycle barely changes f, stop.
        let rel_impr2 = (f_start - fp).abs() / (f_start.abs() + 1.0);
        if rel_impr2 <= tol {
            return (p, fp, cycle + 1);
        }
    }

    (p, fp, max_cycles)
}

fn main() {
    // Demonstration objective: Rosenbrock function in 2D.
    // Global minimizer: (1,1) with f=0.
    let f = |x: &[f64]| -> f64 {
        let a = 1.0 - x[0];
        let b = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
        a * a + 100.0 * b * b
    };

    let p0 = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let tol = 1e-8;
    let max_cycles = 250;

    println!("Program 10.8.3: Powell’s Direction-Set Algorithm");
    println!("Initial P0 = {:?}, f(P0) = {:.15e}", p0, f(&p0));

    let (p_star, f_star, cycles) = powell_direction_set(&f, &p0, tol, max_cycles);

    println!("Finished after {} cycles", cycles);
    println!("P* = {:?}", p_star);
    println!("f(P*) = {:.15e}", f_star);
    println!("Distance to (1,1) = {:.3e}", ((p_star[0]-1.0).powi(2) + (p_star[1]-1.0).powi(2)).sqrt());

}
```

Program 10.8.3 illustrates how Powell’s direction-set method transforms a sequence of simple line minimizations into an effective multidimensional optimization strategy through adaptive direction updates. By replacing one direction per cycle with the net displacement vector, the algorithm progressively aligns its search directions with the local geometry of the objective function, mitigating the inefficiencies of fixed coordinate descent.

The numerical example demonstrates that, even without derivative information, Powell’s method can achieve high accuracy on challenging nonlinear problems such as the Rosenbrock function. While the method lacks the global convergence guarantees of gradient-based algorithms, its robustness and simplicity make it a valuable tool when derivatives are unavailable, noisy, or expensive to compute. Moreover, for strictly quadratic objectives, the finite-cycle convergence behavior predicted by the theory is recovered in practice, reinforcing the conceptual link between Powell’s method and classical conjugate-direction techniques.

The modular structure of the implementation allows the line-search strategy, stopping criteria, or direction-update policy to be modified independently, providing a flexible foundation for exploring hybrid methods that combine derivative-free global search with locally convergent techniques. As such, Powell’s direction-set algorithm remains an important bridge between elementary line search methods and more sophisticated multidimensional optimization frameworks.

## 10.8.4. Practical Issues and Algorithmic Refinements

Despite its elegant theoretical properties for quadratic functions, Powell’s original algorithm can encounter difficulties in practice. In finite-precision arithmetic and for non-quadratic objectives, the direction set may lose linear independence if the newly introduced direction $\mathbf{v}$ lies nearly within the span of the existing directions. When this occurs, subsequent line searches may become confined to a lower-dimensional subspace, leading to premature or false convergence.

Several refinements have been proposed to mitigate this issue. One simple remedy is periodic reinitialization, in which the direction set is occasionally reset to the coordinate axes. Although this sacrifices some accumulated geometric information, it restores linear independence and prevents long-term degeneration. More sophisticated approaches involve modifying the rule for discarding directions or introducing checks that determine whether the new direction $\mathbf{v}$ provides sufficient additional progress to justify its inclusion.

A widely used modern variant of Powell’s method adopts a heuristic safeguard in which the direction discarded is chosen based on the magnitude of the decrease achieved during the cycle, rather than being fixed in advance. This modification has been found to improve robustness in practice and is implemented in several contemporary optimization libraries. Recent work on Powell-type solvers and their software realizations highlights the continued relevance of these derivative-free direction-set methods in modern computational environments (Ragonneau and Zhang, 2024).

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.8.4 on the numerical weaknesses of Powell’s original direction-set method, Program 10.8.4 provides a refined implementation that incorporates practical safeguards designed to improve robustness in finite-precision arithmetic. While Powell’s method exhibits elegant finite-cycle convergence on strictly quadratic objectives, real-world objective functions are rarely quadratic, and the direction set can gradually lose effective linear independence as newly introduced displacement directions become nearly redundant. This program extends the baseline implementation of Powell’s algorithm by introducing progress-based direction acceptance and periodic direction reinitialization, thereby reducing the likelihood of stagnation and premature convergence. The resulting solver retains the derivative-free structure of sequential line minimization while improving its stability and reliability for nonlinear objectives.

At the foundation of the implementation is a collection of low-level vector utilities for representing points and directions in $\mathbb{R}^N$. The functions `dot`, `norm2`, `add_scaled`, and `sub` provide the basic linear algebra operations required to implement the iterative update rule in Equation (10.8.4). The direction set is initialized by the function `unit_vectors`, which constructs the coordinate basis ${\mathbf{e}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{e}_N}$, ensuring that the initial directions are linearly independent. Since line minimization performance is sensitive to scaling, the helper function `normalize_in_place` is applied to directions to ensure that the direction vectors remain well-conditioned as the algorithm progresses.

The one-dimensional minimizations required by Equation (10.8.4) are performed using a derivative-free line search composed of two stages. The function `bracket_minimum` expands an initial interval until it encloses a local minimizer of the restricted function $\phi(\lambda)=f(\mathbf{P}+\lambda \mathbf{u})$. This bracketing step ensures that the subsequent minimization is performed on a valid interval where a minimum is guaranteed to exist. Once a bracket is found, the function `brent_minimize` applies Brent’s method to refine the minimizer efficiently. Brent’s method combines parabolic interpolation with golden-section fallback, providing both rapid convergence when the objective is smooth and reliable progress when the function is irregular or poorly approximated by a quadratic model. The wrapper function `line_minimize` combines these two steps into a single reusable routine that returns both the minimizing step length $\lambda^*$ and the corresponding objective value.

The refined Powell solver itself is implemented in the function `powell_refined`, which preserves the cyclic structure of Powell’s direction-set method. Within each cycle, the algorithm performs $N$ line minimizations sequentially, updating the current iterate using Equation (10.8.4). During this process, the program records the direction that produces the largest decrease in the objective value, which is later used in the direction replacement strategy. After completing the cycle, the algorithm computes the net displacement vector $\mathbf{v}$ as defined in Equation (10.8.5), which summarizes the cumulative effect of all line minimizations.

The key refinement described in Section 10.8.4 is implemented through a progress-based acceptance rule for incorporating the new direction $\mathbf{v}$. Rather than inserting $\mathbf{v}$ unconditionally, the program first performs an additional line minimization along $\mathbf{v}$ and measures the improvement obtained. Only if this additional improvement is sufficiently significant relative to the total progress of the cycle is $\mathbf{v}$ accepted and used to replace the direction that produced the largest decrease. This safeguard reduces the risk of injecting nearly dependent directions into the set, which would otherwise cause the method to lose effective dimensionality and stagnate. In addition, the implementation includes periodic reinitialization of the direction set to the coordinate axes, restoring linear independence and preventing long-term degeneration of the direction basis.

The `main` function provides a concrete demonstration of the refined solver on the Rosenbrock function, a standard nonlinear test problem with a narrow curved valley that often causes difficulty for coordinate-based search methods. The program begins from the classical starting point $(-1.2,1.0)$, prints the initial objective value, and then executes the refined Powell algorithm until convergence or a cycle limit is reached. The output reports the final minimizer approximation, the final objective value, and the Euclidean distance to the known minimizer $(1,1)$. This demonstration confirms that the refinement strategy preserves the derivative-free character of Powell’s method while significantly improving its robustness on nonlinear objectives.

```rust
// Program 10.8.4: Practical Issues and Algorithmic Refinements for Powell’s Direction-Set Method
//
// This refined implementation focuses on practical safeguards discussed in Section 10.8.4:
//
// (i)  Discard rule based on the largest decrease during the cycle (robust heuristic).
// (ii) Progress-based acceptance of the new direction v = P_N - P_0, avoiding near-dependence
//      tests that are unreliable when the direction set is not orthonormal.
// (iii) Periodic reinitialization to coordinate axes to restore linear independence.
//
// Build and run:
//   cargo run --release
//
use std::f64;

type VecN = Vec<f64>;

fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn norm2(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(x, x).sqrt()
}

fn add_scaled(x: &[f64], alpha: f64, u: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    x.iter().zip(u.iter()).map(|(xi, ui)| xi + alpha * ui).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn unit_vectors(n: usize) -> Vec<VecN> {
    let mut dirs = Vec::with_capacity(n);
    for i in 0..n {
        let mut e = vec![0.0; n];
        e[i] = 1.0;
        dirs.push(e);
    }
    dirs
}

fn normalize_in_place(u: &mut [f64]) -> bool {
    let nrm = norm2(u);
    if nrm <= 1e-14 {
        return false;
    }
    for ui in u.iter_mut() {
        *ui /= nrm;
    }
    true
}

/* --------------------------- 1D minimization --------------------------- */

fn bracket_minimum<F>(phi: &F, mut ax: f64, mut bx: f64) -> (f64, f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let gold = 1.618033988749895_f64;

    let mut fa = phi(ax);
    let mut fb = phi(bx);

    if fb > fa {
        std::mem::swap(&mut ax, &mut bx);
        std::mem::swap(&mut fa, &mut fb);
    }

    let mut cx = bx + gold * (bx - ax);
    let mut fc = phi(cx);

    let mut iter = 0usize;
    while !(fb < fa && fb < fc) && iter < 120 {
        ax = bx;
        fa = fb;
        bx = cx;
        fb = fc;
        cx = bx + gold * (bx - ax);
        fc = phi(cx);
        iter += 1;
    }

    (ax, bx, cx)
}

fn brent_minimize<F>(phi: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64, tol: f64, max_iter: usize) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let (mut lo, mut hi) = if a < c { (a, c) } else { (c, a) };

    let mut x: f64 = b;
    let mut w: f64 = b;
    let mut v: f64 = b;

    let mut fx: f64 = phi(x);
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    let golden: f64 = 0.3819660112501051;
    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        let m = 0.5 * (lo + hi);
        let tol1 = tol * x.abs() + 1e-12;
        let tol2 = 2.0 * tol1;

        if (x - m).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (hi - lo)) {
            return (x, fx);
        }

        let mut use_parabolic = false;
        let mut p_step: f64 = 0.0;
        let mut q_step: f64 = 1.0;

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            let r = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q = (x - v) * (fx - fw);
            let mut p = (x - v) * q - (x - w) * r;
            let mut qq = 2.0 * (q - r);

            if qq > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            } else {
                qq = -qq;
            }

            let etemp = e;
            if p.abs() < 0.5 * qq * etemp.abs() && p > qq * (lo - x) && p < qq * (hi - x) {
                use_parabolic = true;
                p_step = p;
                q_step = qq;
            }
        }

        if use_parabolic {
            e = d;
            d = p_step / q_step;
        } else {
            e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
            d = golden * e;
        }

        let u = if d.abs() >= tol1 { x + d } else { x + d.signum() * tol1 };
        let fu = phi(u);

        if fu <= fx {
            if u < x {
                hi = x;
            } else {
                lo = x;
            }
            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            if u < x {
                lo = u;
            } else {
                hi = u;
            }
            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() < 1e-30 {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() < 1e-30 || (v - w).abs() < 1e-30 {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    (x, fx)
}

fn line_minimize<F>(f: &F, p: &[f64], u: &[f64], tol: f64) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let phi = |lambda: f64| -> f64 { f(&add_scaled(p, lambda, u)) };
    let (a, b, c) = bracket_minimum(&phi, 0.0, 1.0);
    brent_minimize(&phi, a, b, c, tol, 250)
}

/* --------------------------- Refined Powell method --------------------------- */

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
struct PowellRefinements {
    reinit_period: usize,     // reset to coordinate axes every this many cycles (0 disables)
    accept_gain_frac: f64,    // require extra v-search gain >= this fraction of the cycle gain
}

fn powell_refined<F>(
    f: &F,
    p0: &[f64],
    tol: f64,
    max_cycles: usize,
    opts: PowellRefinements,
) -> (VecN, f64, usize)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
{
    let n = p0.len();
    let mut dirs = unit_vectors(n);
    for d in dirs.iter_mut() {
        let _ = normalize_in_place(d);
    }

    let mut p = p0.to_vec();
    let mut fp = f(&p);

    for cycle in 0..max_cycles {
        if opts.reinit_period > 0 && cycle > 0 && cycle % opts.reinit_period == 0 {
            dirs = unit_vectors(n);
            for d in dirs.iter_mut() {
                let _ = normalize_in_place(d);
            }
        }

        let p_start = p.clone();
        let f_start = fp;

        let mut biggest_drop = 0.0;
        let mut idx_biggest = 0usize;

        // N sequential line minimizations (10.8.4).
        for i in 0..n {
            let f_before = fp;
            let (lambda_star, _) = line_minimize(f, &p, &dirs[i], tol);
            p = add_scaled(&p, lambda_star, &dirs[i]);
            fp = f(&p);

            let drop = f_before - fp;
            if drop > biggest_drop {
                biggest_drop = drop;
                idx_biggest = i;
            }
        }

        // Net displacement (10.8.5).
        let mut v = sub(&p, &p_start);
        if !normalize_in_place(&mut v) {
            return (p, fp, cycle + 1);
        }

        // Extra line minimization along v (practical refinement).
        let f_before_v = fp;
        let (lambda_v, _) = line_minimize(f, &p, &v, tol);
        p = add_scaled(&p, lambda_v, &v);
        fp = f(&p);
        let gain_v = f_before_v - fp;

        // Accept v into the direction set only if it provides meaningful additional progress.
        // This avoids injecting nearly dependent directions that do not help numerically.
        let cycle_gain = f_start - f_before_v;
        if gain_v > opts.accept_gain_frac * cycle_gain.max(0.0) {
            dirs[idx_biggest] = v;
        }

        // Convergence check.
        let rel_impr = (f_start - fp).abs() / (f_start.abs() + 1.0);
        let step = norm2(&sub(&p, &p_start));
        if rel_impr <= tol && step <= tol * (norm2(&p_start) + 1.0) {
            return (p, fp, cycle + 1);
        }
    }

    (p, fp, max_cycles)
}

fn main() {
    let f = |x: &[f64]| -> f64 {
        let a = 1.0 - x[0];
        let b = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
        a * a + 100.0 * b * b
    };

    let p0 = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let tol = 1e-8;

    let opts = PowellRefinements {
        reinit_period: 25,
        accept_gain_frac: 1e-3,
    };

    let max_cycles = 250;

    println!("Program 10.8.4: Refined Powell direction-set method");
    println!("Initial P0 = {:?}, f(P0) = {:.15e}", p0, f(&p0));

    let (p_star, f_star, cycles) = powell_refined(&f, &p0, tol, max_cycles, opts);

    println!("Finished after {} cycles", cycles);
    println!("P* = {:?}", p_star);
    println!("f(P*) = {:.15e}", f_star);
    println!(
        "Distance to (1,1) = {:.3e}",
        ((p_star[0] - 1.0).powi(2) + (p_star[1] - 1.0).powi(2)).sqrt()
    );
}
```

Program 10.8.4 demonstrates how Powell’s direction-set method can be strengthened through practical algorithmic refinements without altering its fundamental derivative-free structure. By combining sequential line minimizations with progress-based direction updating, the implementation addresses one of the most significant numerical failure modes of the original method: the gradual loss of linear independence in the direction set. The periodic reinitialization mechanism further improves stability by preventing the search from collapsing into a lower-dimensional subspace.

The Rosenbrock example illustrates the effectiveness of these safeguards in a setting where simple coordinate descent often converges slowly due to zig-zagging along a curved valley. The refined direction acceptance rule ensures that the net displacement direction (\\mathbf{v}) is only introduced when it contributes genuine additional progress, reducing the risk of stagnation and false convergence. These practical enhancements reflect the modern viewpoint that derivative-free solvers must incorporate numerical checks and heuristics to remain reliable in finite precision.

The modular design of the code makes it straightforward to extend this framework to other refinements, such as adaptive reinitialization schedules, alternative discard rules, or more sophisticated line search procedures. In this way, the program provides a foundation for exploring modern Powell-type solvers and their role in contemporary derivative-free optimization software, consistent with the continuing relevance of these methods emphasized in recent work (Ragonneau and Zhang, 2024).

## 10.8.5. Practical Performance and Applications

Powell’s direction-set method is particularly attractive in situations where derivative information is unavailable, unreliable, or prohibitively expensive to compute. It is well suited to moderate-dimensional problems and has been successfully applied in a range of engineering and inverse problems. For example, recent studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of Powell’s method in estimating optical properties of semitransparent media by fitting simulation outputs to experimental data, achieving accurate parameter recovery despite the absence of explicit gradients and the presence of measurement noise (Chang et al., 2023).

Beyond standalone use, Powell’s method is frequently employed as a local optimization component within hybrid global–local frameworks. In such settings, a global search algorithm identifies promising regions of the parameter space, and Powell’s method is then applied to refine candidate solutions. This combination leverages the rapid local convergence of direction-set methods once the search is within the basin of attraction of a single minimum.

## 10.8.6. Summary and Transition

Powell’s direction-set method illustrates how careful selection and adaptation of search directions can dramatically improve the efficiency of derivative-free optimization. By approximating conjugate directions through successive line searches, the method overcomes the zig-zag inefficiencies of naive coordinate descent and provides rapid convergence for problems with curved valleys. While it lacks the strong global guarantees associated with gradient-based methods, its simplicity, robustness, and derivative-free nature ensure its continued practical relevance.

In the next section, attention shifts to methods that explicitly exploit gradient information. Many of the ideas introduced here, particularly the use of line searches and conjugacy concepts, reappear in the development of conjugate gradient algorithms, where derivative information is used to achieve even faster convergence.

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/rGMPJlIckTOIyVPt5dsA.6","tags":[]}

# 10.9. Conjugate Gradient Methods in Multidimensions

We now turn to multidimensional optimization algorithms that explicitly exploit gradient information. When the gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ is available, it provides substantially richer information about the local behavior of the objective function than function values alone. This additional information can dramatically reduce the number of iterations required to locate a minimum.

A simple information-based argument illustrates this advantage. A general quadratic function of $N$ variables has on the order of $N^2$ independent parameters in its Hessian matrix, in addition to $N$ parameters in its gradient. A derivative-free method, which extracts at most one scalar piece of information per function evaluation, may therefore require on the order of $N^2$ function evaluations to determine the minimum. By contrast, a single gradient evaluation yields $N$ scalar components simultaneously, suggesting that a well-designed gradient-based algorithm might converge in $O(N)$ iterations.

Conjugate gradient methods achieve this ideal efficiency on quadratic problems. For a strictly quadratic objective, they converge to the exact minimum in at most $N$ iterations, in sharp contrast to derivative-free direction-set methods. Although gradient evaluations are typically more expensive than function evaluations, they often cost far less than $N$ times a function evaluation due to shared computations or algorithmic differentiation. As a result, gradient-based methods frequently outperform derivative-free methods in practice when gradients are available.

## 10.9.1. Steepest Descent and Its Limitations

The simplest gradient-based method for minimizing a function $f(\mathbf{x})$ in $\mathbb{R}^N$ is the method of steepest descent. Starting from an initial point $\mathbf{P}_0$, the algorithm proceeds iteratively as follows:

1. Compute the gradient:

$$\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{P}_k) \tag{10.9.1}$$

2. Choose the search direction

$$\mathbf{h}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k \tag{10.9.2}$$

3. Perform a line minimization to determine $\alpha_k$ minimizing $f(\mathbf{P}_k + \alpha \mathbf{h}_k)$.
4. Update the iterate:

$$\mathbf{P}_{k+1} = \mathbf{P}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{h}_k \tag{10.9.3}$$

Although steepest descent is guaranteed to converge under mild conditions, its practical performance is often poor, particularly for ill-conditioned problems. After each exact line minimization, the new gradient is orthogonal to the previous search direction. Consequently, the path taken by the algorithm consists of successive right-angle turns. In narrow, elongated valleys, this behavior produces a zig-zag trajectory that advances only slowly along the valley floor.

For quadratic functions, the rate of convergence of steepest descent is governed by the condition number of the Hessian matrix. When the ratio of the largest to smallest curvature is large, the method may require an excessive number of iterations to achieve acceptable accuracy. This inefficiency motivates the development of methods that retain useful information from previous steps rather than discarding it entirely at each iteration.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.9.1 on steepest descent and the limitations imposed by zig-zagging trajectories in narrow valleys, Program 10.9.1 provides a practical implementation of gradient-based minimization using the method of steepest descent with line minimization. Although steepest descent is conceptually straightforward and requires only gradient evaluations, its performance can deteriorate severely on ill-conditioned problems, where successive search directions become nearly orthogonal and progress along the valley floor becomes slow. This program implements the iteration rules given in Equations (10.9.1)–(10.9.3) and demonstrates how a robust one-dimensional minimization procedure can be combined with gradient information to produce a complete multidimensional optimization solver. The implementation serves as a baseline against which more advanced conjugate gradient methods can be compared.

At the foundation of the implementation is a set of low-level vector operations used to represent points and directions in $\mathbb{R}^N$. The helper functions `dot` and `norm2` provide the Euclidean inner product and the corresponding 2-norm, allowing the algorithm to measure gradient magnitudes and step sizes. The function `scale` constructs the scaled direction vector needed to form the steepest descent direction $\mathbf{h}_k = -\mathbf{g}k$ as required by Equation (10.9.2). The update of the iterate according to Equation (10.9.3) is performed by the function `add_scaled`, which computes $\mathbf{P}_{k+1} = \mathbf{P}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{h}_k$ in a single pass. These utility routines isolate the linear algebra required by the method and keep the main iteration logic clear and directly aligned with the mathematical formulation.

The one-dimensional minimization step required between Equations (10.9.2) and (10.9.3) is implemented through a bracketing phase followed by Brent’s method. The function `bracket_minimum` expands an initial interval until it encloses a local minimizer of the restricted function $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{P}_k + \alpha \mathbf{h}_k)$. This ensures that the subsequent minimization is performed on an interval where the existence of a minimum is numerically well supported. Once a bracket is obtained, the function `brent_minimize` applies Brent’s algorithm to refine the minimizer. Brent’s method combines parabolic interpolation with golden-section fallback, making it effective on smooth objective functions while remaining robust when the function is irregular or poorly approximated by a quadratic model. The wrapper routine `line_minimize` provides a clean interface to this process by returning both the minimizing step length $\alpha_k$ and the corresponding function value.

The steepest descent iteration itself is implemented in the function `steepest_descent`. At each iteration, the gradient is evaluated as $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{P}_k)$, corresponding to Equation (10.9.1). The descent direction is then formed as $\mathbf{h}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ as in Equation (10.9.2), and a line minimization is performed to determine the optimal step size $\alpha_k$. The iterate is updated according to Equation (10.9.3), and the process repeats until either the gradient norm becomes sufficiently small or a maximum iteration count is reached. The stopping condition based on $|\mathbf{g}_k|_2$ reflects the fundamental optimality condition for unconstrained minimization, namely that a stationary point satisfies $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$.

The `main` function demonstrates the algorithm on the Rosenbrock function, a standard nonlinear benchmark known for its narrow curved valley and ill-conditioned geometry. The program defines both the objective function and its gradient explicitly, illustrating how steepest descent relies on derivative information to construct search directions. Starting from the classical initial point $(-1.2,1.0)$, the program runs the solver, prints the final iterate, and reports the final objective value and gradient norm. The output provides direct evidence of the limitations described in Section 10.9.1, since steepest descent tends to make slow progress on Rosenbrock even when combined with an accurate line minimization procedure.

```rust
// Program 10.9.1: Steepest Descent in Multidimensions with Line Minimization
//
// --------------------------- Problem statement ---------------------------
// Minimize a differentiable function f: R^N -> R when its gradient ∇f(x) is available.
// Starting from an initial point P0, steepest descent constructs the gradient
//
//   g_k = ∇f(P_k),                                                                  (10.9.1)
//
// chooses the search direction
//
//   h_k = -g_k,                                                                     (10.9.2)
//
// performs a one-dimensional minimization to determine α_k that minimizes
// f(P_k + α h_k), and updates
//
//   P_{k+1} = P_k + α_k h_k.                                                        (10.9.3)
//
// This program implements steepest descent with a robust derivative-free line search
// (bracketing + Brent) applied to the one-dimensional function φ(α)=f(P_k+α h_k).
//
// Build and run:
//   cargo run --release
//
use std::f64;

type VecN = Vec<f64>;

fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn norm2(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(x, x).sqrt()
}

fn add_scaled(x: &[f64], alpha: f64, u: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    x.iter().zip(u.iter()).map(|(xi, ui)| xi + alpha * ui).collect()
}

fn scale(u: &[f64], s: f64) -> VecN {
    u.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

/* --------------------------- 1D minimization (Brent) --------------------------- */

fn bracket_minimum<F>(phi: &F, mut ax: f64, mut bx: f64) -> (f64, f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let gold = 1.618033988749895_f64;

    let mut fa = phi(ax);
    let mut fb = phi(bx);

    // Ensure downhill from ax to bx.
    if fb > fa {
        std::mem::swap(&mut ax, &mut bx);
        std::mem::swap(&mut fa, &mut fb);
    }

    let mut cx = bx + gold * (bx - ax);
    let mut fc = phi(cx);

    let mut iter = 0usize;
    while !(fb < fa && fb < fc) && iter < 120 {
        ax = bx;
        fa = fb;
        bx = cx;
        fb = fc;
        cx = bx + gold * (bx - ax);
        fc = phi(cx);
        iter += 1;
    }

    (ax, bx, cx)
}

fn brent_minimize<F>(phi: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64, tol: f64, max_iter: usize) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let (mut lo, mut hi) = if a < c { (a, c) } else { (c, a) };

    let mut x: f64 = b;
    let mut w: f64 = b;
    let mut v: f64 = b;

    let mut fx: f64 = phi(x);
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    let golden: f64 = 0.3819660112501051; // 1 - 1/φ
    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        let m = 0.5 * (lo + hi);
        let tol1 = tol * x.abs() + 1e-12;
        let tol2 = 2.0 * tol1;

        if (x - m).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (hi - lo)) {
            return (x, fx);
        }

        let mut use_parabolic = false;
        let mut p_step: f64 = 0.0;
        let mut q_step: f64 = 1.0;

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            let r = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q = (x - v) * (fx - fw);
            let mut p = (x - v) * q - (x - w) * r;
            let mut qq = 2.0 * (q - r);

            if qq > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            } else {
                qq = -qq;
            }

            let etemp = e;
            if p.abs() < 0.5 * qq * etemp.abs() && p > qq * (lo - x) && p < qq * (hi - x) {
                use_parabolic = true;
                p_step = p;
                q_step = qq;
            }
        }

        if use_parabolic {
            e = d;
            d = p_step / q_step;
        } else {
            e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
            d = golden * e;
        }

        let u = if d.abs() >= tol1 { x + d } else { x + d.signum() * tol1 };
        let fu = phi(u);

        if fu <= fx {
            if u < x {
                hi = x;
            } else {
                lo = x;
            }
            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            if u < x {
                lo = u;
            } else {
                hi = u;
            }
            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() < 1e-30 {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() < 1e-30 || (v - w).abs() < 1e-30 {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    (x, fx)
}

fn line_minimize<F>(phi: &F, tol: f64) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let (a, b, c) = bracket_minimum(phi, 0.0, 1.0);
    brent_minimize(phi, a, b, c, tol, 250)
}

/* --------------------------- Steepest descent --------------------------- */

fn steepest_descent<F, G>(
    f: &F,
    grad: &G,
    p0: &[f64],
    tol: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> (VecN, f64, usize)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
    G: Fn(&[f64]) -> VecN,
{
    let mut p = p0.to_vec();
    let mut fp = f(&p);

    for k in 0..max_iter {
        // g_k = ∇f(P_k)   (10.9.1)
        let gk = grad(&p);
        let gnorm = norm2(&gk);

        // Stop when the gradient is small.
        if gnorm <= tol * (norm2(&p) + 1.0) {
            return (p, fp, k);
        }

        // h_k = -g_k      (10.9.2)
        let hk = scale(&gk, -1.0);

        // Line minimization for α_k along h_k.
        let phi = |alpha: f64| -> f64 {
            let x = add_scaled(&p, alpha, &hk);
            f(&x)
        };
        let (alpha_k, _) = line_minimize(&phi, tol);

        // P_{k+1} = P_k + α_k h_k   (10.9.3)
        p = add_scaled(&p, alpha_k, &hk);
        fp = f(&p);
    }

    (p, fp, max_iter)
}

fn main() {
    // Demonstration objective: Rosenbrock function in 2D.
    // Its minimizer is (1,1) with f=0, but steepest descent typically zig-zags in the valley.
    let f = |x: &[f64]| -> f64 {
        let a = 1.0 - x[0];
        let b = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
        a * a + 100.0 * b * b
    };

    // Gradient of Rosenbrock.
    let grad = |x: &[f64]| -> VecN {
        let x0 = x[0];
        let x1 = x[1];
        vec![
            -2.0 * (1.0 - x0) - 400.0 * x0 * (x1 - x0 * x0),
            200.0 * (x1 - x0 * x0),
        ]
    };

    let p0 = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let tol = 1e-8;
    let max_iter = 2000;

    println!("Program 10.9.1: Steepest descent with line minimization");
    println!("Initial P0 = {:?}, f(P0) = {:.15e}", p0, f(&p0));

    let (p_star, f_star, iters) = steepest_descent(&f, &grad, &p0, tol, max_iter);

    println!("Finished after {} iterations", iters);
    println!("P* = {:?}", p_star);
    println!("f(P*) = {:.15e}", f_star);
    println!(
        "||∇f(P*)||_2 = {:.15e}",
        norm2(&grad(&p_star))
    );
    println!(
        "Distance to (1,1) = {:.3e}",
        ((p_star[0] - 1.0).powi(2) + (p_star[1] - 1.0).powi(2)).sqrt()
    );
}
```

Program 10.9.1 demonstrates the basic mechanics of gradient-based minimization in multiple dimensions using the method of steepest descent. By combining gradient evaluation with a robust line minimization strategy, the program implements the iteration rules in Equations (10.9.1)–(10.9.3) in a form suitable for practical numerical experimentation. The implementation highlights the fundamental advantage of gradient-based methods over derivative-free approaches, since each gradient evaluation provides (N) pieces of directional information simultaneously.

At the same time, the Rosenbrock example illustrates the principal weakness of steepest descent emphasized in Section 10.9.1. Because the method repeatedly chooses the direction of maximal local descent, it tends to oscillate across narrow valleys and makes slow progress along the valley floor. This zig-zagging behavior becomes increasingly severe as the condition number of the underlying curvature grows, leading to very slow convergence even when line minimization is performed accurately.

The program therefore serves as a baseline reference for the more sophisticated methods introduced in the remainder of Section 10.9. In particular, conjugate gradient methods modify the direction update rule to retain information from previous iterations, thereby reducing the inefficiency caused by orthogonal successive gradients. The modular design of the code, with the line search implemented independently from the multidimensional iteration, also provides a natural framework for substituting conjugate-direction updates while reusing the same reliable one-dimensional minimization procedure.

## 10.9.2. From Linear to Nonlinear Conjugate Gradient Methods

In Chapter 2, the conjugate gradient method was introduced as an efficient algorithm for solving symmetric positive-definite linear systems $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, which is equivalent to minimizing the quadratic function:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{x} \tag{10.9.4}$$

The linear conjugate gradient method constructs a sequence of mutually conjugate search directions and converges to the exact solution in at most $N$ iterations.

Nonlinear conjugate gradient methods extend this idea to general, non-quadratic objective functions. The key insight is to construct each new search direction as a combination of the current gradient and the previous search direction, thereby preserving useful information accumulated during earlier iterations.

The generic structure of a nonlinear conjugate gradient algorithm is as follows. Let $\mathbf{P}_k$ denote the current iterate and $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{P}_k)$ the corresponding gradient.

1. Initialize

$$\mathbf{h}_0 = -\mathbf{g}_0 \tag{10.9.5}$$

2. For $k = 0,1,2,\ldots$
   - Perform a line search to determine $\alpha_k$ minimizing $f(\mathbf{P}_k + \alpha \mathbf{h}_k)$
   - Update the iterate

$$\mathbf{P}_{k+1} = \mathbf{P}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{h}_k \tag{10.9.6}$$

3. Compute the new gradient $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}$.
4. Update the search direction

$$\mathbf{h}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_k \mathbf{h}_k \tag{10.9.7}$$

The scalar parameter $\beta_k$ determines how much of the previous direction is retained and is central to the behavior of the algorithm.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.9.2 on the extension of linear conjugate gradient ideas to nonlinear optimization, Program 10.9.2 provides a practical implementation of nonlinear conjugate gradient minimization in multiple dimensions. While steepest descent repeatedly discards past directional information and therefore exhibits slow zig-zag convergence in ill-conditioned valleys, conjugate gradient methods construct each new search direction by combining the current gradient with a scaled contribution from the previous direction. This preserves useful information accumulated during earlier iterations and can dramatically accelerate convergence, particularly on problems that locally resemble quadratic objectives. The program implements the update structure given in Equations (10.9.5)–(10.9.7) and demonstrates how different choices of the parameter $\beta_k$ affect performance on a representative nonlinear test function.

At the foundation of the implementation is a small collection of vector operations for representing points and directions in $\mathbb{R}^N$. The helper functions `dot` and `norm2` provide the Euclidean inner product and the corresponding 2-norm, which are essential for computing gradient magnitudes and forming the scalar coefficients used in the conjugate gradient updates. The functions `add_scaled`, `sub`, and `scale` implement the elementary vector operations needed to form trial points of the form $\mathbf{P}_k + \alpha \mathbf{h}_k$ and to update the search direction. In addition, the helper routine `axpy` implements the standard linear combination $a\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{y}$, which is used directly when forming the new search direction in Equation (10.9.7). These low-level routines keep the iteration logic close to the mathematical formulation and make the algorithm easy to generalize to higher dimensions.

The one-dimensional minimization step required between Equations (10.9.5) and (10.9.6) is implemented using a robust derivative-free line search based on bracketing followed by Brent’s method. The function `bracket_minimum` expands an initial interval until it encloses a local minimizer of the restricted function $\phi(\alpha)=f(\mathbf{P}_k+\alpha\mathbf{h}_k)$. This ensures that the minimization is performed on a valid interval containing a minimum. Once a bracket is obtained, the function `brent_minimize` refines the minimizer using Brent’s algorithm, which combines parabolic interpolation with golden-section fallback. This hybrid approach is particularly effective in optimization contexts because it can converge rapidly when the objective behaves smoothly along the search direction, while still guaranteeing progress when the function is irregular or poorly approximated by a quadratic model. The wrapper function `line_minimize` packages these steps into a reusable procedure that returns both the minimizing step length $\alpha_k$ and the corresponding objective value.

The nonlinear conjugate gradient iteration is implemented in the function `nonlinear_cg`, which follows exactly the generic structure described in Section 10.9.2. The initial direction is set to $\mathbf{h}_0=-\mathbf{g}_0$ as stated in Equation (10.9.5), where $\mathbf{g}_0=\nabla f(\mathbf{P}0)$*.* At each iteration, a line minimization is performed to determine $\alpha_k$, and the iterate is updated according to Equation (10.9.6). The new gradient $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}$ is then computed, and the next search direction is constructed using Equation (10.9.7). The parameter $\beta_k$ is computed using one of two update rules, implemented by the functions `beta_fr` and `beta_pr_plus`.

The Fletcher–Reeves rule, implemented in `beta_fr`, computes $\beta_k$ as the ratio of the squared gradient norms, which is a direct nonlinear extension of the linear conjugate gradient formula and preserves the interpretation of conjugacy for quadratic objectives. The Polak–Ribière update, implemented in `beta_pr_plus`, incorporates the gradient difference $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}-\mathbf{g}_k$, allowing the method to respond more effectively to nonlinear curvature changes. The nonnegativity safeguard included in this implementation enforces $\beta_k \ge 0$, which prevents the direction update from producing erratic ascent-like behavior and provides an automatic restart mechanism when conjugacy becomes unreliable. A further restart safeguard is applied in the main loop: if the computed direction ceases to be a descent direction, the algorithm resets $\mathbf{h}_{k+1}$ to $-\mathbf{g}_{k+1}$, ensuring that each iteration continues to produce a locally decreasing search direction.

The `main` function demonstrates the method on the Rosenbrock function, a classical nonlinear benchmark characterized by a narrow curved valley and strong anisotropy. The program defines both the objective function and its gradient explicitly, then applies the nonlinear conjugate gradient solver twice: once using Fletcher–Reeves and once using Polak–Ribière+. The printed output reports the number of iterations required to reach the stopping tolerance, the final iterate, the final objective value, and the gradient norm. The comparison illustrates how the choice of $\beta_k$ strongly influences practical convergence behavior, even though both variants follow the same fundamental structure given in Equations (10.9.5)–(10.9.7).

```rust
// Program 10.9.2: Nonlinear Conjugate Gradient Methods (Fletcher–Reeves and Polak–Ribière)
//
// --------------------------- Problem statement ---------------------------
// Minimize a differentiable function f: R^N -> R when its gradient ∇f(x) is available.
// Nonlinear conjugate gradient methods generalize the linear CG method for SPD systems by
// constructing search directions that combine the new gradient with the previous direction.
//
// The iteration follows the structure of Equations (10.9.5)–(10.9.7):
//
//   h_0 = -g_0,                                                                     (10.9.5)
//   P_{k+1} = P_k + α_k h_k,                                                        (10.9.6)
//   h_{k+1} = -g_{k+1} + β_k h_k.                                                   (10.9.7)
//
// Each step uses a line search to determine α_k minimizing f(P_k + α h_k). The choice of β_k
// determines how much of the previous direction is retained. This program implements two
// standard choices:
//
//   Fletcher–Reeves:   β_k = (g_{k+1}·g_{k+1}) / (g_k·g_k)
//   Polak–Ribière+:    β_k = max(0, (g_{k+1}·(g_{k+1}-g_k)) / (g_k·g_k))
//
// Both variants are widely used in practice; PR+ includes a nonnegativity safeguard that
// improves robustness on non-quadratic objectives.
//
// Build and run:
//   cargo run --release
//
use std::f64;

type VecN = Vec<f64>;

fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn norm2(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(x, x).sqrt()
}

fn add_scaled(x: &[f64], alpha: f64, u: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    x.iter().zip(u.iter()).map(|(xi, ui)| xi + alpha * ui).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn scale(u: &[f64], s: f64) -> VecN {
    u.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

fn axpy(a: f64, x: &[f64], y: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    // returns a*x + y
    x.iter().zip(y.iter()).map(|(xi, yi)| a * xi + yi).collect()
}

/* --------------------------- 1D minimization (Brent) --------------------------- */

fn bracket_minimum<F>(phi: &F, mut ax: f64, mut bx: f64) -> (f64, f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let gold = 1.618033988749895_f64;

    let mut fa = phi(ax);
    let mut fb = phi(bx);

    if fb > fa {
        std::mem::swap(&mut ax, &mut bx);
        std::mem::swap(&mut fa, &mut fb);
    }

    let mut cx = bx + gold * (bx - ax);
    let mut fc = phi(cx);

    let mut iter = 0usize;
    while !(fb < fa && fb < fc) && iter < 120 {
        ax = bx;
        fa = fb;
        bx = cx;
        fb = fc;
        cx = bx + gold * (bx - ax);
        fc = phi(cx);
        iter += 1;
    }

    (ax, bx, cx)
}

fn brent_minimize<F>(phi: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64, tol: f64, max_iter: usize) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let (mut lo, mut hi) = if a < c { (a, c) } else { (c, a) };

    let mut x: f64 = b;
    let mut w: f64 = b;
    let mut v: f64 = b;

    let mut fx: f64 = phi(x);
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    let golden: f64 = 0.3819660112501051;
    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        let m = 0.5 * (lo + hi);
        let tol1 = tol * x.abs() + 1e-12;
        let tol2 = 2.0 * tol1;

        if (x - m).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (hi - lo)) {
            return (x, fx);
        }

        let mut use_parabolic = false;
        let mut p_step: f64 = 0.0;
        let mut q_step: f64 = 1.0;

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            let r = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q = (x - v) * (fx - fw);
            let mut p = (x - v) * q - (x - w) * r;
            let mut qq = 2.0 * (q - r);

            if qq > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            } else {
                qq = -qq;
            }

            let etemp = e;
            if p.abs() < 0.5 * qq * etemp.abs() && p > qq * (lo - x) && p < qq * (hi - x) {
                use_parabolic = true;
                p_step = p;
                q_step = qq;
            }
        }

        if use_parabolic {
            e = d;
            d = p_step / q_step;
        } else {
            e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
            d = golden * e;
        }

        let u = if d.abs() >= tol1 { x + d } else { x + d.signum() * tol1 };
        let fu = phi(u);

        if fu <= fx {
            if u < x {
                hi = x;
            } else {
                lo = x;
            }
            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            if u < x {
                lo = u;
            } else {
                hi = u;
            }
            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() < 1e-30 {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() < 1e-30 || (v - w).abs() < 1e-30 {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    (x, fx)
}

fn line_minimize<F>(phi: &F, tol: f64) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let (a, b, c) = bracket_minimum(phi, 0.0, 1.0);
    brent_minimize(phi, a, b, c, tol, 250)
}

/* --------------------------- Nonlinear conjugate gradient --------------------------- */

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
enum BetaRule {
    FletcherReeves,
    PolakRibierePlus,
}

fn beta_fr(g_next: &[f64], g: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let denom = dot(g, g);
    if denom <= 1e-30 {
        0.0
    } else {
        dot(g_next, g_next) / denom
    }
}

fn beta_pr_plus(g_next: &[f64], g: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let denom = dot(g, g);
    if denom <= 1e-30 {
        return 0.0;
    }
    let y = sub(g_next, g); // y = g_{k+1} - g_k
    let raw = dot(g_next, &y) / denom;
    if raw.is_finite() && raw > 0.0 { raw } else { 0.0 }
}

fn nonlinear_cg<F, G>(
    f: &F,
    grad: &G,
    p0: &[f64],
    tol: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
    rule: BetaRule,
) -> (VecN, f64, usize)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
    G: Fn(&[f64]) -> VecN,
{
    let mut p = p0.to_vec();
    let mut fp = f(&p);

    // g0 = ∇f(P0)
    let mut g = grad(&p);

    // h0 = -g0  (10.9.5)
    let mut h = scale(&g, -1.0);

    for k in 0..max_iter {
        let gnorm = norm2(&g);
        if gnorm <= tol * (norm2(&p) + 1.0) {
            return (p, fp, k);
        }

        // Line search: minimize φ(α) = f(P_k + α h_k)
        let phi = |alpha: f64| -> f64 {
            let x = add_scaled(&p, alpha, &h);
            f(&x)
        };
        let (alpha_k, _) = line_minimize(&phi, tol);

        // P_{k+1} = P_k + α_k h_k  (10.9.6)
        p = add_scaled(&p, alpha_k, &h);
        fp = f(&p);

        // g_{k+1} = ∇f(P_{k+1})
        let g_next = grad(&p);

        // β_k rule
        let beta_k = match rule {
            BetaRule::FletcherReeves => beta_fr(&g_next, &g),
            BetaRule::PolakRibierePlus => beta_pr_plus(&g_next, &g),
        };

        // h_{k+1} = -g_{k+1} + β_k h_k  (10.9.7)
        let minus_g_next = scale(&g_next, -1.0);
        h = axpy(beta_k, &h, &minus_g_next);

        // A common practical restart: if h ceases to be a descent direction, reset to -g.
        if dot(&h, &g_next) >= 0.0 {
            h = scale(&g_next, -1.0);
        }

        g = g_next;
    }

    (p, fp, max_iter)
}

fn main() {
    // Demonstration objective: Rosenbrock function in 2D.
    let f = |x: &[f64]| -> f64 {
        let a = 1.0 - x[0];
        let b = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
        a * a + 100.0 * b * b
    };

    // Gradient of Rosenbrock.
    let grad = |x: &[f64]| -> VecN {
        let x0 = x[0];
        let x1 = x[1];
        vec![
            -2.0 * (1.0 - x0) - 400.0 * x0 * (x1 - x0 * x0),
            200.0 * (x1 - x0 * x0),
        ]
    };

    let p0 = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let tol = 1e-8;
    let max_iter = 500;

    println!("Program 10.9.2: Nonlinear conjugate gradient (FR / PR+)");
    println!("Initial P0 = {:?}, f(P0) = {:.15e}", p0, f(&p0));

    let (p_fr, f_fr, it_fr) = nonlinear_cg(&f, &grad, &p0, tol, max_iter, BetaRule::FletcherReeves);
    println!("\nFletcher–Reeves:");
    println!("  iterations = {}", it_fr);
    println!("  P* = {:?}", p_fr);
    println!("  f(P*) = {:.15e}", f_fr);
    println!("  ||∇f(P*)||_2 = {:.15e}", norm2(&grad(&p_fr)));

    let (p_pr, f_pr, it_pr) =
        nonlinear_cg(&f, &grad, &p0, tol, max_iter, BetaRule::PolakRibierePlus);
    println!("\nPolak–Ribière+:");
    println!("  iterations = {}", it_pr);
    println!("  P* = {:?}", p_pr);
    println!("  f(P*) = {:.15e}", f_pr);
    println!("  ||∇f(P*)||_2 = {:.15e}", norm2(&grad(&p_pr)));

    println!(
        "\nDistance to (1,1) for PR+ = {:.3e}",
        ((p_pr[0] - 1.0).powi(2) + (p_pr[1] - 1.0).powi(2)).sqrt()
    );
}
```

**Program 10.9.2** demonstrates how the central idea of conjugacy from linear conjugate gradient solvers can be extended to nonlinear optimization through a simple modification of the search direction update. By constructing each new direction as a combination of the negative gradient and the previous direction, as expressed in Equation (10.9.7), the method retains information accumulated during earlier iterations and avoids the repeated orthogonal turning behavior characteristic of steepest descent. This provides a substantial improvement in efficiency on problems with ill-conditioned curvature, where steepest descent converges slowly even when accurate line minimization is performed.

The Rosenbrock example illustrates that nonlinear conjugate gradient methods can achieve rapid convergence using only gradient evaluations and one-dimensional minimizations. In particular, the Polak–Ribière+ variant demonstrates improved robustness by incorporating curvature information through the gradient difference term and by applying a restart safeguard when conjugacy deteriorates. These practical enhancements allow the method to behave like steepest descent when necessary while preserving the accelerated convergence of conjugate directions whenever the objective is locally close to quadratic.

The modular structure of the implementation makes it straightforward to experiment with alternative line search strategies, restart rules, or $\beta_k$ formulas, and provides a natural bridge to more advanced quasi-Newton methods. As such, the program forms a practical foundation for understanding why conjugate gradient methods represent one of the most effective gradient-based optimization approaches for large-scale unconstrained minimization problems.

## 10.9.3. Choices of the Conjugacy Parameter

Several formulas have been proposed for computing $\beta_k$. Two classical choices are the Fletcher–Reeves and Polak–Ribiere formulas.

The Fletcher–Reeves parameter is defined as:

$$
\beta_k^{\mathrm{FR}} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{g}_{k+1}}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k} \tag{10.9.8}
$$

The Polak–Ribiere parameter is given by:

$$
\beta_k^{\mathrm{PR}} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \bigl(\mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k\bigr)}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k} \tag{10.9.9}
$$

For strictly quadratic functions with exact line searches, these two formulas coincide. For general nonlinear problems, however, their behavior can differ significantly. The Polak–Ribiere update often exhibits superior practical performance because it adapts more flexibly to changes in the gradient. In many implementations, if $\beta_k^{PR}$ becomes negative, it is reset to zero, causing the algorithm to revert temporarily to a steepest descent step. This safeguard improves robustness on strongly non-quadratic problems.

Numerous alternative formulas exist, including the Hestenes–Stiefel, Dai–Yuan, and conjugate descent variants. Ongoing research continues to explore hybrid and modified schemes that combine favorable theoretical properties with improved practical performance, particularly in conjunction with inexact line searches (Hemici et al., 2024; Yahaya et al., 2025).

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.9.3 on the role of the conjugacy parameter $\beta_k$ in nonlinear conjugate gradient methods, Program 10.9.3 provides a practical implementation that compares several classical update formulas within a single unified framework. While the nonlinear conjugate gradient iteration retains the structure of Equations (10.9.5)–(10.9.7), its behavior is strongly influenced by the choice of $\beta_k$, which determines how much information from the previous search direction is preserved. This program implements the Fletcher–Reeves and Polak–Ribière formulas defined in Equations (10.9.8) and (10.9.9), along with additional variants such as Hestenes–Stiefel, Dai–Yuan, and conjugate descent. By testing these formulas on both a strictly quadratic objective and a nonlinear benchmark, the program illustrates the theoretical equivalence of the classical parameters in the quadratic case and their markedly different practical performance in nonlinear optimization.

At the foundation of the implementation is a small set of vector operations for representing points and search directions in $\mathbb{R}^N$. The functions `dot` and `norm2` compute the Euclidean inner product and the corresponding 2-norm, which are required for evaluating gradient magnitudes and constructing the scalar coefficients used in the conjugacy updates. The functions `add_scaled`, `sub`, and `scale` implement the elementary linear algebra needed to form trial points of the form $\mathbf{P}_k + \alpha \mathbf{h}_k$ and to compute gradient differences such as $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}-\mathbf{g}_k$. The helper routine `axpy` performs the standard linear combination $a\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{y}$, which is used directly to form the next direction vector according to Equation (10.9.7). These routines isolate the vector arithmetic and allow the main algorithm to be written in a form that closely mirrors the mathematical description of nonlinear conjugate gradient methods.

The line minimization step required between Equations (10.9.5) and (10.9.6) is implemented using a derivative-free bracketing strategy followed by Brent’s method. The function `bracket_minimum` expands an interval until it encloses a local minimizer of the one-dimensional function $\phi(\alpha)=f(\mathbf{P}_k+\alpha\mathbf{h}_k)$. Once a valid bracket is obtained, the function `brent_minimize` refines the minimizer using Brent’s algorithm, which combines parabolic interpolation with golden-section fallback. This hybrid strategy is particularly effective in optimization settings because it can converge rapidly when the restricted objective is smooth, while still guaranteeing progress when the function is irregular. The wrapper function `line_minimize` combines bracketing and Brent refinement into a reusable procedure that returns both the minimizing step length $\alpha_k$ and the associated objective value.

The central feature of the program is the implementation of multiple formulas for the conjugacy parameter $\beta_k$. These are represented by the enumeration `BetaRule`, which includes the Fletcher–Reeves, Polak–Ribière, Polak–Ribière+ safeguard, Hestenes–Stiefel, Dai–Yuan, and conjugate descent variants. The function `beta_value` computes the corresponding $\beta_k$ given the current and previous gradients and the current search direction. In particular, the Fletcher–Reeves parameter is computed exactly as in Equation (10.9.8), using the ratio of squared gradient norms. The Polak–Ribière parameter is computed as in Equation (10.9.9), using the gradient difference term $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}-\mathbf{g}_k$. The safeguarded Polak–Ribière+ rule resets $\beta_k$ to zero when the raw Polak–Ribière value becomes negative, forcing a temporary return to steepest descent behavior and improving robustness on strongly nonlinear objectives.

The nonlinear conjugate gradient iteration is implemented in the function `nonlinear_cg`, which follows the generic structure described in Section 10.9.2. Starting from an initial point $\mathbf{P}_0$, the method forms the initial direction $\mathbf{h}_0=-\mathbf{g}_0$ in accordance with Equation (10.9.5). At each iteration, the program performs a line minimization to compute $\alpha_k$, then updates the iterate using Equation (10.9.6). The new gradient is evaluated, and the direction update rule in Equation (10.9.7) is applied using the selected $\beta_k$ formula. To ensure numerical stability, the implementation includes a descent safeguard: if the computed direction fails to satisfy a descent condition, the algorithm restarts by setting $\beta_k=0$, thereby reverting to a steepest descent step. This restart mechanism reflects the practical observation that conjugacy is easily degraded by finite-precision effects and inexact line searches.

The `main` function demonstrates the theoretical and practical behavior of the $\beta_k$ formulas using two test problems. The first test is a strictly quadratic objective of the form given in Equation (10.9.4), with a small symmetric positive-definite matrix. For this case, the output logs the $\beta_k$ sequence for both Fletcher–Reeves and Polak–Ribière, illustrating that they coincide numerically when line searches are sufficiently accurate. The second test applies the same nonlinear conjugate gradient framework to the Rosenbrock function, a classical nonlinear benchmark with a narrow curved valley. The program runs the solver with each $\beta_k$ rule and reports the iteration count, final objective value, gradient norm, and distance to the known minimizer. This comparison demonstrates that while the formulas coincide for quadratic objectives, their practical performance on nonlinear problems can differ substantially, validating the discussion in Section 10.9.3.

```rust
// Program 10.9.3: Choices of the Conjugacy Parameter in Nonlinear Conjugate Gradient
//
// --------------------------- Problem statement ---------------------------
// Implement nonlinear conjugate gradient minimization with multiple choices of the conjugacy
// parameter β_k, focusing on the Fletcher–Reeves and Polak–Ribière formulas:
//
//   β_k^FR = (g_{k+1}^T g_{k+1}) / (g_k^T g_k),                                     (10.9.8)
//   β_k^PR = (g_{k+1}^T (g_{k+1} - g_k)) / (g_k^T g_k).                             (10.9.9)
//
// For strictly quadratic objectives with exact line searches, β_k^FR and β_k^PR coincide.
// For nonlinear objectives, their behavior can differ substantially. A common safeguard in
// practice resets β_k^PR to zero when it becomes negative, temporarily reverting to a
// steepest descent step.
//
// This program provides:
//
// 1) A quadratic SPD test where FR and PR agree (numerically) under an accurate line search.
// 2) A nonlinear test (Rosenbrock) illustrating different β_k behavior and the PR+ safeguard.
// 3) Additional β_k options (Hestenes–Stiefel, Dai–Yuan, Conjugate Descent) as examples of
//    alternative formulas used in practice.
//
// Build and run:
//   cargo run --release
//
use std::f64;

type VecN = Vec<f64>;

fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn norm2(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(x, x).sqrt()
}

fn add_scaled(x: &[f64], alpha: f64, u: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    x.iter().zip(u.iter()).map(|(xi, ui)| xi + alpha * ui).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn scale(u: &[f64], s: f64) -> VecN {
    u.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

fn axpy(a: f64, x: &[f64], y: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    // returns a*x + y
    x.iter().zip(y.iter()).map(|(xi, yi)| a * xi + yi).collect()
}

/* --------------------------- 1D minimization (Brent) --------------------------- */

fn bracket_minimum<F>(phi: &F, mut ax: f64, mut bx: f64) -> (f64, f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let gold = 1.618033988749895_f64;

    let mut fa = phi(ax);
    let mut fb = phi(bx);

    if fb > fa {
        std::mem::swap(&mut ax, &mut bx);
        std::mem::swap(&mut fa, &mut fb);
    }

    let mut cx = bx + gold * (bx - ax);
    let mut fc = phi(cx);

    let mut iter = 0usize;
    while !(fb < fa && fb < fc) && iter < 120 {
        ax = bx;
        fa = fb;
        bx = cx;
        fb = fc;
        cx = bx + gold * (bx - ax);
        fc = phi(cx);
        iter += 1;
    }

    (ax, bx, cx)
}

fn brent_minimize<F>(phi: &F, a: f64, b: f64, c: f64, tol: f64, max_iter: usize) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let (mut lo, mut hi) = if a < c { (a, c) } else { (c, a) };

    let mut x: f64 = b;
    let mut w: f64 = b;
    let mut v: f64 = b;

    let mut fx: f64 = phi(x);
    let mut fw: f64 = fx;
    let mut fv: f64 = fx;

    let golden: f64 = 0.3819660112501051;
    let mut d: f64 = 0.0;
```
