```rust
    let mut e: f64 = 0.0;

    for _ in 0..max_iter {
        let m = 0.5 * (lo + hi);
        let tol1 = tol * x.abs() + 1e-12;
        let tol2 = 2.0 * tol1;

        if (x - m).abs() <= (tol2 - 0.5 * (hi - lo)) {
            return (x, fx);
        }

        let mut use_parabolic = false;
        let mut p_step: f64 = 0.0;
        let mut q_step: f64 = 1.0;

        if e.abs() > tol1 {
            let r = (x - w) * (fx - fv);
            let q = (x - v) * (fx - fw);
            let mut p = (x - v) * q - (x - w) * r;
            let mut qq = 2.0 * (q - r);

            if qq > 0.0 {
                p = -p;
            } else {
                qq = -qq;
            }

            let etemp = e;
            if p.abs() < 0.5 * qq * etemp.abs() && p > qq * (lo - x) && p < qq * (hi - x) {
                use_parabolic = true;
                p_step = p;
                q_step = qq;
            }
        }

        if use_parabolic {
            e = d;
            d = p_step / q_step;
        } else {
            e = if x < m { hi - x } else { lo - x };
            d = golden * e;
        }

        let u = if d.abs() >= tol1 { x + d } else { x + d.signum() * tol1 };
        let fu = phi(u);

        if fu <= fx {
            if u < x {
                hi = x;
            } else {
                lo = x;
            }
            v = w;
            fv = fw;
            w = x;
            fw = fx;
            x = u;
            fx = fu;
        } else {
            if u < x {
                lo = u;
            } else {
                hi = u;
            }
            if fu <= fw || (w - x).abs() < 1e-30 {
                v = w;
                fv = fw;
                w = u;
                fw = fu;
            } else if fu <= fv || (v - x).abs() < 1e-30 || (v - w).abs() < 1e-30 {
                v = u;
                fv = fu;
            }
        }
    }

    (x, fx)
}

fn line_minimize<F>(phi: &F, tol: f64) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    let (a, b, c) = bracket_minimum(phi, 0.0, 1.0);
    brent_minimize(phi, a, b, c, tol, 300)
}

/* --------------------------- β choices --------------------------- */

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
enum BetaRule {
    FletcherReeves,
    PolakRibiere,
    PolakRibierePlus,
    HestenesStiefel,
    DaiYuan,
    ConjugateDescent,
}

fn beta_value(rule: BetaRule, g_next: &[f64], g: &[f64], h: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    let gg = dot(g, g);
    if gg <= 1e-30 {
        return 0.0;
    }

    match rule {
        BetaRule::FletcherReeves => dot(g_next, g_next) / gg,

        BetaRule::PolakRibiere => {
            let y = sub(g_next, g);
            dot(g_next, &y) / gg
        }

        BetaRule::PolakRibierePlus => {
            let y = sub(g_next, g);
            let raw = dot(g_next, &y) / gg;
            if raw.is_finite() && raw > 0.0 { raw } else { 0.0 }
        }

        BetaRule::HestenesStiefel => {
            let y = sub(g_next, g);
            let denom = dot(&y, h);
            if denom.abs() <= 1e-30 {
                0.0
            } else {
                dot(g_next, &y) / denom
            }
        }

        BetaRule::DaiYuan => {
            let y = sub(g_next, g);
            let denom = dot(&y, h);
            if denom.abs() <= 1e-30 {
                0.0
            } else {
                dot(g_next, g_next) / denom
            }
        }

        BetaRule::ConjugateDescent => {
            let denom = dot(h, g);
            if denom.abs() <= 1e-30 {
                0.0
            } else {
                -dot(g_next, g_next) / denom
            }
        }
    }
}

/* --------------------------- Nonlinear CG driver --------------------------- */

fn nonlinear_cg<F, G>(
    f: &F,
    grad: &G,
    p0: &[f64],
    tol: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
    rule: BetaRule,
    log_betas: bool,
) -> (VecN, f64, usize)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
    G: Fn(&[f64]) -> VecN,
{
    let mut p = p0.to_vec();
    let mut fp = f(&p);

    let mut g = grad(&p);
    let mut h = scale(&g, -1.0);

    for k in 0..max_iter {
        let gnorm = norm2(&g);
        if gnorm <= tol * (norm2(&p) + 1.0) {
            return (p, fp, k);
        }

        let phi = |alpha: f64| -> f64 {
            let x = add_scaled(&p, alpha, &h);
            f(&x)
        };
        let (alpha_k, _) = line_minimize(&phi, tol);

        p = add_scaled(&p, alpha_k, &h);
        fp = f(&p);

        let g_next = grad(&p);

        let mut beta_k = beta_value(rule, &g_next, &g, &h);

        let minus_g_next = scale(&g_next, -1.0);
        let h_next = axpy(beta_k, &h, &minus_g_next);

        // Descent safeguard: if direction ceases to be a descent direction, restart.
        if dot(&h_next, &g_next) >= 0.0 || !beta_k.is_finite() {
            beta_k = 0.0;
        }

        if log_betas {
            println!(
                "  k = {:3}  beta = {:+.6e}   ||g|| = {:.3e}",
                k,
                beta_k,
                norm2(&g_next)
            );
        }

        h = axpy(beta_k, &h, &minus_g_next);
        g = g_next;
    }

    (p, fp, max_iter)
}

/* --------------------------- Tests --------------------------- */

fn main() {
    // ---- Test A: Quadratic SPD objective (10.9.4), where FR and PR coincide under exact line search ----
    let a11 = 4.0;
    let a12 = 1.0;
    let a22 = 3.0;

    // Use a fixed-size array so both closures can capture b without move conflicts.
    let b: [f64; 2] = [1.0, 2.0];

    let f_quad = move |x: &[f64]| -> f64 {
        let x0 = x[0];
        let x1 = x[1];
        let ax0 = a11 * x0 + a12 * x1;
        let ax1 = a12 * x0 + a22 * x1;
        0.5 * (x0 * ax0 + x1 * ax1) - (b[0] * x0 + b[1] * x1)
    };

    let grad_quad = move |x: &[f64]| -> VecN {
        let x0 = x[0];
        let x1 = x[1];
        let ax0 = a11 * x0 + a12 * x1;
        let ax1 = a12 * x0 + a22 * x1;
        vec![ax0 - b[0], ax1 - b[1]]
    };

    let p0_quad = vec![2.0, -1.0];
    let tol = 1e-10;

    println!("Program 10.9.3: β choices for nonlinear conjugate gradient");
    println!("\nTest A (quadratic SPD): compare FR vs PR betas (they should coincide numerically)");

    println!("\nFletcher–Reeves (quadratic):");
    let (_p_fr, _f_fr, it_fr) =
        nonlinear_cg(&f_quad, &grad_quad, &p0_quad, tol, 20, BetaRule::FletcherReeves, true);
    println!("  iterations used = {}", it_fr);

    println!("\nPolak–Ribière (quadratic):");
    let (_p_pr, _f_pr, it_pr) =
        nonlinear_cg(&f_quad, &grad_quad, &p0_quad, tol, 20, BetaRule::PolakRibiere, true);
    println!("  iterations used = {}", it_pr);

    // ---- Test B: Nonlinear objective (Rosenbrock), where β choices differ substantially ----
    let f_rosen = |x: &[f64]| -> f64 {
        let a = 1.0 - x[0];
        let b = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
        a * a + 100.0 * b * b
    };

    let grad_rosen = |x: &[f64]| -> VecN {
        let x0 = x[0];
        let x1 = x[1];
        vec![
            -2.0 * (1.0 - x0) - 400.0 * x0 * (x1 - x0 * x0),
            200.0 * (x1 - x0 * x0),
        ]
    };

    let p0_rosen = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let tol_rosen = 1e-8;
    let max_iter = 500;

    println!("\nTest B (Rosenbrock): compare iteration counts and final accuracy across β rules");

    let rules = [
        (BetaRule::FletcherReeves, "FR"),
        (BetaRule::PolakRibiere, "PR"),
        (BetaRule::PolakRibierePlus, "PR+"),
        (BetaRule::HestenesStiefel, "HS"),
        (BetaRule::DaiYuan, "DY"),
        (BetaRule::ConjugateDescent, "CD"),
    ];

    for (rule, name) in rules {
        let (p_star, f_star, iters) =
            nonlinear_cg(&f_rosen, &grad_rosen, &p0_rosen, tol_rosen, max_iter, rule, false);

        let gnorm = norm2(&grad_rosen(&p_star));
        let dist = ((p_star[0] - 1.0).powi(2) + (p_star[1] - 1.0).powi(2)).sqrt();

        println!(
            "  {:>3}: iters = {:3}, f(P*) = {:.3e}, ||g|| = {:.3e}, dist = {:.3e}",
            name, iters, f_star, gnorm, dist
        );
    }
}
```

Program 10.9.3 demonstrates that the effectiveness of nonlinear conjugate gradient methods depends critically on the choice of the conjugacy parameter $\beta_k$. On a strictly quadratic objective of the form in Equation (10.9.4), the Fletcher–Reeves and Polak–Ribière parameters computed from Equations (10.9.8) and (10.9.9) coincide in practice when line searches are sufficiently accurate, and the algorithm converges in at most $N$ iterations. This confirms the theoretical equivalence of the classical formulas in the quadratic setting.

For nonlinear objectives, however, the program illustrates that different $\beta_k$ rules can produce significantly different convergence behavior. Formulas such as Polak–Ribière and Hestenes–Stiefel often adapt more effectively to changing curvature, while safeguarded variants such as Polak–Ribière+ provide additional robustness by reverting to steepest descent when the computed $\beta_k$ becomes unfavorable. The comparative results reinforce the practical motivation for hybrid and restart-based strategies, particularly when line searches are inexact or the objective deviates strongly from quadratic structure.

The modular structure of the implementation makes it straightforward to extend the framework to additional $\beta_k$ formulas and to explore modern hybrid schemes that combine favorable theoretical guarantees with improved practical behavior. This provides a natural foundation for investigating current research directions in nonlinear conjugate gradient methods, including modified update rules designed for inexact line searches and large-scale optimization settings (Hemici et al., 2024; Yahaya et al., 2025).

## 10.9.4. Global Convergence Properties

Under standard assumptions, such as continuous differentiability of $f$, boundedness of the objective function below, and appropriate line search conditions, nonlinear conjugate gradient methods are globally convergent. That is, the norm of the gradient satisfies:

$$\lvert \nabla f(\mathbf{P}_k) \rvert \to 0 \quad \text{as } k \to \infty \tag{10.9.10}$$

Global convergence proofs typically rely on line searches that satisfy the strong Wolfe conditions or closely related criteria. These conditions enforce both sufficient decrease of the objective function along each search direction and a curvature condition that prevents excessively short or overly aggressive steps. Together, they ensure that each iteration makes meaningful progress while preserving the stability of the search directions.

The choice of the update parameter $\beta_k$ plays a crucial role in global convergence behavior. Classical formulas such as Fletcher–Reeves and Polak–Ribière differ in how strongly they emphasize new gradient information. In practice, variants of Polak–Ribière that enforce nonnegativity of $\beta_k$ are often preferred, as they improve robustness on nonconvex problems by preventing the search direction from losing its descent property. Many implementations also incorporate restart strategies, either periodically or when conjugacy degrades, effectively resetting the method to steepest descent when necessary to maintain convergence.

From a conceptual standpoint, conjugate gradient methods occupy a middle ground between steepest descent and Newton’s method. Like steepest descent, they avoid explicit second-derivative calculations and require only gradient evaluations, making them attractive for large-scale problems. At the same time, by constructing mutually conjugate search directions, they implicitly accumulate curvature information across iterations, mimicking certain advantages of second-order methods. This implicit use of curvature helps explain why conjugate gradient methods often converge significantly faster than steepest descent while retaining low memory requirements.

This balance between computational simplicity, modest storage demands, and favorable convergence behavior has made nonlinear conjugate gradient methods a standard choice for large-scale unconstrained optimization, particularly in settings where Hessian information is unavailable or prohibitively expensive to compute.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.9.4 on the global convergence behavior of nonlinear conjugate gradient methods, Program 10.9.4 provides a practical implementation of the Polak–Ribière+ conjugate gradient algorithm combined with a strong Wolfe line search. While the theoretical update formula in Equation (10.9.7) defines how conjugate directions are constructed from gradient information, global convergence in the sense of Equation (10.9.10) depends critically on enforcing suitable step-length conditions. This program demonstrates how the descent property and long-term stability of nonlinear conjugate gradient iterations can be preserved in practice by coupling the PR+ update with a line search that guarantees sufficient decrease and curvature control. The Rosenbrock function is used as a representative nonconvex benchmark to illustrate how a carefully designed line search enables rapid convergence even in narrow curved valleys where steepest descent stagnates.

At the core of the implementation is the `strong_wolfe_line_search` function, which determines the step length $\alpha_k$ required in the update step of Equation (10.9.6). Rather than accepting a step based only on decrease of the objective function, this routine enforces the strong Wolfe conditions, ensuring that each iteration satisfies both sufficient decrease and a curvature constraint. These requirements directly support the global convergence property described in Equation (10.9.10), because they prevent the algorithm from taking steps that are either excessively short (which would stall progress) or overly aggressive (which would destroy the descent structure of the method). The line search is implemented using a bracketing phase followed by a zoom procedure, ensuring that a valid step is found even on highly nonlinear objective landscapes.

The nonlinear conjugate gradient iteration is implemented in the function `nonlinear_cg_pr_plus`, which follows the generic framework outlined in Equations (10.9.5) through (10.9.7). Starting from an initial point $\mathbf{P}_0$, the routine evaluates the gradient $\mathbf{g}_k$, constructs the search direction $\mathbf{h}_k$, and performs a strong Wolfe line search to compute $\alpha_k$. The iterate is then updated according to Equation (10.9.6), and a new gradient is computed. The key step is the construction of the next direction using Equation (10.9.7), which combines the negative gradient with the previous search direction. This mechanism allows the algorithm to retain curvature information accumulated over earlier steps, providing the accelerated convergence behavior characteristic of conjugate gradient methods.

To improve robustness on nonconvex problems, the implementation adopts the Polak–Ribière+ safeguard discussed in Section 10.9.3. The coefficient $\beta_k$ is first computed using the Polak–Ribière formula of Equation (10.9.9). If the resulting value becomes negative, it is reset to zero, causing the update in Equation (10.9.7) to reduce temporarily to a steepest descent step. This safeguard prevents the search direction from becoming non-descent due to local curvature irregularities and is one of the most widely used practical refinements for ensuring reliable convergence on strongly nonlinear objective functions.

The program also includes a set of low-level vector utilities, such as `dot`, `norm2`, `add_scaled`, and `scale_inplace`, which implement the basic Euclidean operations required for multidimensional optimization. These routines provide the numerical building blocks needed to evaluate gradients, update iterates, and form conjugate directions efficiently. Although conceptually simple, these operations must be carefully implemented to avoid unnecessary memory allocation, since conjugate gradient methods are designed specifically for large-scale optimization where storage efficiency is essential.

The `main` function serves as a numerical demonstration of the global convergence principles discussed in Section 10.9.4. It applies the PR+ nonlinear conjugate gradient method to the Rosenbrock objective, starting from the standard initial point $(-1.2, 1.0)$. During the iteration, it prints diagnostic output including the objective value $f(\mathbf{P}_k)$ and the Euclidean norm of the gradient $|\nabla f(\mathbf{P}_k)|_2$. This directly illustrates the convergence criterion stated in Equation (10.9.10), since successful termination corresponds to the gradient norm decreasing toward zero. The final output confirms convergence by reporting the approximate minimizer, the final function value, and the distance to the known minimizer $(1,1)$.

```rust
// Program 10.9.4: Global Convergence Properties via Strong Wolfe Line Search
//
// --------------------------- Problem statement ---------------------------
// Implement a nonlinear conjugate gradient method with a line search that enforces the
// strong Wolfe conditions. Under standard assumptions (continuous differentiability of f,
// f bounded below, and a suitable line search), nonlinear conjugate gradient methods are
// globally convergent in the sense that
//
//   ||∇f(P_k)|| -> 0  as k -> ∞.                                                    (10.9.10)
//
// This program uses:
//   1) Polak–Ribière+ (PR+) update for β_k with nonnegativity safeguard,
//   2) a descent-direction safeguard and restart strategy,
//   3) a strong Wolfe line search implemented with bracketing and a zoom phase that uses
//      safeguarded interpolation (cubic or quadratic) with bisection fallback.
//
// Demonstration: Rosenbrock function in 2D, printing ||∇f(P_k)||_2 to illustrate (10.9.10).
//
// Build and run:
//   cargo run --release
//
use std::f64;

type VecN = Vec<f64>;

/* --------------------------- vector utilities --------------------------- */

fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn norm2(x: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(x, x).sqrt()
}

fn add_scaled(x: &[f64], alpha: f64, u: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    x.iter().zip(u.iter()).map(|(xi, ui)| xi + alpha * ui).collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn scale(u: &[f64], s: f64) -> VecN {
    u.iter().map(|x| s * x).collect()
}

fn axpy(a: f64, x: &[f64], y: &[f64]) -> VecN {
    // returns a*x + y
    x.iter().zip(y.iter()).map(|(xi, yi)| a * xi + yi).collect()
}

/* --------------------------- strong Wolfe line search --------------------------- */

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
struct Wolfe {
    c1: f64,        // Armijo
    c2: f64,        // strong Wolfe curvature
    alpha_max: f64, // max step
    max_iter: usize,
}

fn phi_and_dphi<F, G>(f: &F, grad: &G, p: &[f64], h: &[f64], alpha: f64) -> (f64, f64)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
    G: Fn(&[f64]) -> VecN,
{
    let x = add_scaled(p, alpha, h);
    let fx = f(&x);
    let gx = grad(&x);
    let dfx = dot(&gx, h);
    (fx, dfx)
}

fn clamp_to_interval(x: f64, a: f64, b: f64) -> f64 {
    if a < b {
        x.max(a).min(b)
    } else {
        x.max(b).min(a)
    }
}

// Cubic interpolation for a minimizer between (a, fa, dfa) and (b, fb, dfb).
// Returns None if the cubic is not well-defined or unstable.
fn cubic_minimizer(a: f64, fa: f64, dfa: f64, b: f64, fb: f64, dfb: f64) -> Option<f64> {
    let d1 = dfa + dfb - 3.0 * (fa - fb) / (a - b);
    let rad = d1 * d1 - dfa * dfb;

    if !rad.is_finite() || rad < 0.0 {
        return None;
    }
    let d2 = rad.sqrt();
    let denom = dfb - dfa + 2.0 * d2;
    if denom.abs() <= 1e-30 {
        return None;
    }
    let t = b - (b - a) * (dfb + d2 - d1) / denom;
    if t.is_finite() { Some(t) } else { None }
}

// Secant (quadratic) minimizer for derivative root between (a, dfa) and (b, dfb).
fn secant_root(a: f64, dfa: f64, b: f64, dfb: f64) -> Option<f64> {
    let denom = dfb - dfa;
    if denom.abs() <= 1e-30 {
        return None;
    }
    let t = b - dfb * (b - a) / denom;
    if t.is_finite() { Some(t) } else { None }
}

fn zoom<F, G>(
    f: &F,
    grad: &G,
    p: &[f64],
    h: &[f64],
    f0: f64,
    dphi0: f64,
    mut alo: f64,
    mut ahi: f64,
    wolfe: Wolfe,
) -> Option<f64>
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
    G: Fn(&[f64]) -> VecN,
{
    let (mut phi_lo, mut dphi_lo) = phi_and_dphi(f, grad, p, h, alo);

    for _ in 0..wolfe.max_iter {
        let (lo, hi) = if alo < ahi { (alo, ahi) } else { (ahi, alo) };

        // Safeguarded interpolation. Try cubic, then secant, else bisection.
        let (phi_hi, dphi_hi) = phi_and_dphi(f, grad, p, h, ahi);

        let mut aj = cubic_minimizer(alo, phi_lo, dphi_lo, ahi, phi_hi, dphi_hi)
            .or_else(|| secant_root(alo, dphi_lo, ahi, dphi_hi))
            .unwrap_or(0.5 * (alo + ahi));

        // Safeguard: keep away from the edges to maintain bracketing progress.
        let margin = 0.1 * (hi - lo);
        aj = clamp_to_interval(aj, lo + margin, hi - margin);

        let (phi_j, dphi_j) = phi_and_dphi(f, grad, p, h, aj);
        let armijo_rhs = f0 + wolfe.c1 * aj * dphi0;

        if (phi_j > armijo_rhs) || (phi_j >= phi_lo) {
            ahi = aj;
        } else {
            if dphi_j.abs() <= -wolfe.c2 * dphi0 {
                return Some(aj);
            }
            if dphi_j * (ahi - alo) >= 0.0 {
                ahi = alo;
            }
            alo = aj;
            phi_lo = phi_j;
            dphi_lo = dphi_j;
        }

        if (ahi - alo).abs() <= 1e-14 {
            return Some(0.5 * (alo + ahi));
        }
    }
    None
}

fn strong_wolfe_line_search<F, G>(
    f: &F,
    grad: &G,
    p: &[f64],
    h: &[f64],
    alpha0: f64,
    wolfe: Wolfe,
) -> Option<f64>
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
    G: Fn(&[f64]) -> VecN,
{
    let f0 = f(p);
    let g0 = grad(p);
    let dphi0 = dot(&g0, h);

    // Must be a descent direction.
    if !(dphi0 < 0.0) {
        return None;
    }

    let mut a_prev = 0.0;
    let mut phi_prev = f0;

    let mut a = alpha0.clamp(1e-16, wolfe.alpha_max);

    for i in 0..wolfe.max_iter {
        let (phi_a, dphi_a) = phi_and_dphi(f, grad, p, h, a);
        let armijo_rhs = f0 + wolfe.c1 * a * dphi0;

        if (phi_a > armijo_rhs) || (i > 0 && phi_a >= phi_prev) {
            return zoom(f, grad, p, h, f0, dphi0, a_prev, a, wolfe);
        }

        if dphi_a.abs() <= -wolfe.c2 * dphi0 {
            return Some(a);
        }

        if dphi_a >= 0.0 {
            return zoom(f, grad, p, h, f0, dphi0, a, a_prev, wolfe);
        }

        a_prev = a;
        phi_prev = phi_a;

        // Increase step length (bracketing phase).
        a = (2.0 * a).min(wolfe.alpha_max);
    }

    None
}

/* --------------------------- PR+ nonlinear conjugate gradient --------------------------- */

fn beta_pr_plus(g_next: &[f64], g: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    // β_k^PR = (g_{k+1}^T (g_{k+1} - g_k)) / (g_k^T g_k)    (10.9.9)
    // PR+ safeguard: β_k = max(0, β_k^PR)
    let denom = dot(g, g);
    if denom <= 1e-30 {
        return 0.0;
    }
    let y = sub(g_next, g);
    let raw = dot(g_next, &y) / denom;
    if raw.is_finite() && raw > 0.0 { raw } else { 0.0 }
}

fn nonlinear_cg_pr_plus_wolfe<F, G>(
    f: &F,
    grad: &G,
    p0: &[f64],
    tol: f64,
    max_iter: usize,
    wolfe: Wolfe,
) -> (VecN, f64, usize)
where
    F: Fn(&[f64]) -> f64,
    G: Fn(&[f64]) -> VecN,
{
    let n = p0.len();
    let mut p = p0.to_vec();
    let mut fp = f(&p);

    let mut g = grad(&p);
    let mut h = scale(&g, -1.0); // h0 = -g0 (10.9.5)

    let mut alpha_guess = 1.0;

    for k in 0..max_iter {
        let gnorm = norm2(&g);
        println!("  k = {:4}  f(P_k) = {:.3e}   ||g_k|| = {:.3e}", k, fp, gnorm);

        if gnorm <= tol * (norm2(&p) + 1.0) {
            return (p, fp, k);
        }

        // Periodic restart to prevent loss of conjugacy in finite precision.
        if k > 0 && (k % n == 0) {
            h = scale(&g, -1.0);
        }

        // Ensure descent direction before line search.
        if dot(&g, &h) >= 0.0 {
            h = scale(&g, -1.0);
        }

        let alpha = match strong_wolfe_line_search(f, grad, &p, &h, alpha_guess, wolfe) {
            Some(a) => a,
            None => {
                // Restart and try again.
                h = scale(&g, -1.0);
                strong_wolfe_line_search(f, grad, &p, &h, 1.0, wolfe).unwrap_or(1e-3)
            }
        };

        // Update iterate (10.9.6)
        p = add_scaled(&p, alpha, &h);
        fp = f(&p);

        // New gradient
        let g_next = grad(&p);

        // β_k and direction update (10.9.7)
        let beta = beta_pr_plus(&g_next, &g);
        let minus_g_next = scale(&g_next, -1.0);
        let mut h_next = axpy(beta, &h, &minus_g_next);

        // Descent safeguard restart.
        if dot(&g_next, &h_next) >= 0.0 {
            h_next = minus_g_next;
        }

        h = h_next;
        g = g_next;
        alpha_guess = alpha;
    }

    (p, fp, max_iter)
}

/* --------------------------- demonstration (Rosenbrock) --------------------------- */

fn main() {
    let f = |x: &[f64]| -> f64 {
        let a = 1.0 - x[0];
        let b = x[1] - x[0] * x[0];
        a * a + 100.0 * b * b
    };

    let grad = |x: &[f64]| -> VecN {
        let x0 = x[0];
        let x1 = x[1];
        vec![
            -2.0 * (1.0 - x0) - 400.0 * x0 * (x1 - x0 * x0),
            200.0 * (x1 - x0 * x0),
        ]
    };

    let p0 = vec![-1.2, 1.0];
    let tol = 1e-8;
    let max_iter = 400;

    // For nonlinear CG, c2 is typically smaller (often 0.1 to 0.5).
    let wolfe = Wolfe {
        c1: 1e-4,
        c2: 0.1,
        alpha_max: 50.0,
        max_iter: 60,
    };

    println!("Program 10.9.4: Nonlinear CG (PR+) with strong Wolfe line search");
    println!("Initial P0 = {:?}, f(P0) = {:.15e}", p0, f(&p0));

    let (p_star, f_star, iters) = nonlinear_cg_pr_plus_wolfe(&f, &grad, &p0, tol, max_iter, wolfe);

    println!("\nFinished after {} iterations", iters);
    println!("P* = {:?}", p_star);
    println!("f(P*) = {:.15e}", f_star);
    println!("||∇f(P*)||_2 = {:.15e}", norm2(&grad(&p_star)));
    println!(
        "Distance to (1,1) = {:.3e}",
        ((p_star[0] - 1.0).powi(2) + (p_star[1] - 1.0).powi(2)).sqrt()
    );
}
```

Program 10.9.4 demonstrates how nonlinear conjugate gradient methods achieve reliable global convergence when the direction update rule in Equation (10.9.7) is coupled with a strong Wolfe line search. The numerical results show that even on a highly nonlinear benchmark such as the Rosenbrock function, the method drives the gradient norm toward zero, illustrating in practice the convergence behavior stated in Equation (10.9.10). This confirms that line search conditions are not merely technical assumptions used in proofs, but are essential algorithmic safeguards that stabilize the method in finite-precision computation.

The use of the Polak–Ribière+ modification further highlights the practical importance of ensuring that the search direction remains a descent direction. By resetting negative $\beta_k$ values to zero, the algorithm automatically reverts to steepest descent behavior when conjugacy deteriorates, preventing instability in strongly curved or nonconvex regions. This combination of curvature accumulation and controlled restarting illustrates why nonlinear conjugate gradient methods occupy a practical middle ground between the simplicity of steepest descent and the fast local convergence of second-order methods.

Overall, the program illustrates the key message of Section 10.9.4: nonlinear conjugate gradient methods achieve strong performance on large-scale problems because they incorporate gradient information efficiently, require only modest storage, and can be made globally convergent through appropriate step-length selection rules. The framework implemented here also provides a foundation for extensions such as preconditioning, adaptive restarting strategies, and hybrid methods that incorporate limited-memory quasi-Newton updates.

## 10.9.5. Practical Performance and Applications

Conjugate gradient methods are particularly well suited to large-scale optimization problems, where storing or manipulating a full Hessian matrix is impractical or impossible. Unlike Newton or quasi-Newton methods, which require either explicit Hessian information or dense matrix approximations, conjugate gradient methods operate using only gradient evaluations and simple vector operations. As a result, they require memory for only a small number of vectors of length $N$, typically the current iterate, the gradient, and the search direction. This low memory footprint makes them especially attractive for high-dimensional problems arising from discretized partial differential equations, large parameter estimation tasks, and machine learning models with millions of variables.

In practice, the per-iteration computational cost of nonlinear conjugate gradient methods is dominated by gradient evaluations and line search procedures. When gradients can be computed efficiently, either analytically or via automatic differentiation, the overall cost scales favorably with problem size. Moreover, because each iteration incorporates information from previous steps through conjugate directions, these methods often require significantly fewer iterations than steepest descent to reach comparable accuracy. This advantage becomes increasingly important as problem dimension grows, where slow convergence can otherwise dominate total runtime.

Historically, nonlinear conjugate gradient methods have been widely used in scientific computing, optimal control, inverse problems, and image processing. In inverse problems, for example, they are frequently employed to minimize misfit functionals subject to regularization, where the underlying optimization landscapes are high-dimensional and often ill-conditioned. In image processing, conjugate gradient methods are well suited to variational formulations of denoising, deblurring, and reconstruction, where gradients can be computed efficiently using convolutional or differential operators.

Recent research demonstrates their continued relevance and adaptability. Hemici et al. (2024) applied a hybrid conjugate gradient scheme to image restoration problems, reporting faster and more reliable convergence than basic gradient descent, particularly in the presence of noise and nonconvex regularization terms. Similarly, Yahaya et al. (2025) proposed new hybrid conjugate gradient methods for multiobjective optimization, illustrating ongoing advances in the design of robust conjugacy updates that balance theoretical convergence guarantees with empirical performance. These developments highlight the flexibility of conjugate gradient frameworks and their capacity to evolve alongside emerging application domains.

Taken together, these practical considerations explain why conjugate gradient methods remain a core component of modern optimization toolkits, offering an effective compromise between simplicity, scalability, and convergence efficiency in large-scale unconstrained optimization problems.

## 10.9.6. Section Remarks

Nonlinear conjugate gradient methods provide an efficient and memory-light approach to multidimensional optimization when gradient information is available. By combining current gradient information with accumulated directional knowledge from previous iterations, they overcome the zig-zag inefficiencies of steepest descent and achieve rapid convergence on well-behaved problems. Their reliance on line searches and conjugacy concepts links them closely to the direction-set methods discussed earlier, while their use of gradient information enables substantially faster convergence. As such, conjugate gradient methods form a central component of modern numerical optimization.

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/vGpVK47NVS4G4cFHSjI4.6","tags":[]}

# 10.10. Quasi-Newton or Variable Metric Methods in Multidimensions

Quasi-Newton methods are powerful iterative techniques for unconstrained optimization in $\mathbb{R}^N$ that seek to approximate Newton’s method without computing second derivatives directly. Instead of evaluating the Hessian matrix:

$$A = \nabla^2 f(x) \tag{10.10.1}$$

which can be costly or impractical in high dimensions, quasi-Newton algorithms build up an approximate Hessian or its inverse using only gradient information. The idea is to incorporate curvature information gradually from successive iterations so that, after $N$ iterations for an $N$-dimensional quadratic problem, the algorithm converges as if the true Hessian were used. In essence, quasi-Newton methods achieve superlinear or quadratic convergence by mimicking Newton’s method with a sequence of updated matrices that converge to $A^{-1}$.

These methods are called *variable metric* methods because they update the inner-product metric defined by the approximate Hessian matrix at each step. In practical terms, quasi-Newton methods require at each iteration the current point $x_i$, the gradient $\nabla f(x_i)$, and a search direction $p_i$ obtained by approximately solving:

$$A p_i \approx -\nabla f(x_i) \tag{10.10.2}$$

The new iterate is:

$$x_{i+1} = x_i + \alpha_i p_i \tag{10.10.3}$$

where $\alpha_i > 0$ is a line search step length. Although quasi-Newton methods require storing an $N \times N$ matrix, this is often acceptable for moderate $N$, and the rapid convergence frequently compensates for the additional computational cost (Moghrabi and Hassan, 2024).

## 10.10.1. Mathematical Formulation and the Secant Condition

The key relation underpinning quasi-Newton updates is the *secant condition*. Suppose we have two successive iterates:

$$x_{i+1} = x_i + \Delta x_i \tag{10.10.4}$$

with gradients:

$$g_i = \nabla f(x_i), \qquad g_{i+1} = \nabla f(x_{i+1}) \tag{10.10.5}$$

and the gradient difference:

$$\Delta g_i = g_{i+1} - g_i \tag{10.10.6}$$

For a truly quadratic objective function, the Hessian satisfies:

$$A\Delta x_i = \Delta g_i \tag{10.10.7}$$

Quasi-Newton methods enforce an analogous condition on the approximate Hessian. Writing $B_i$ for the Hessian approximation and $H_i = B_i^{-1}$ for the inverse Hessian approximation, the secant condition is:

$$B_{i+1} \Delta x_i = \Delta g_i,\qquad \text{or equivalently} \qquad H_{i+1} \Delta g_i = \Delta x_i \tag{10.10.8}$$

This condition ensures that the updated matrix reproduces the most recently observed curvature. Since infinitely many matrices satisfy this equation, quasi-Newton algorithms select updates that minimally modify the previous approximation while preserving symmetry and positive-definiteness.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Sections 10.10 and 10.10.1 on quasi-Newton or variable metric methods, Program 10.10.1 provides a complete implementation of the BFGS algorithm for unconstrained minimization in $\mathbb{R}^N$. The theoretical development emphasized how quasi-Newton methods approximate Newton’s method without explicitly forming the Hessian matrix $A = \nabla^2 f(x)$ in Equation (10.10.1), instead constructing an evolving approximation to $A^{-1}$ through gradient information collected across successive iterates. This program demonstrates how the secant condition in Equation (10.10.8) can be enforced computationally to build curvature information progressively, while still retaining the basic line-search structure of Equation (10.10.3). In practical numerical work, BFGS remains one of the most widely used variable metric methods because it achieves rapid convergence for smooth problems while avoiding the cost and complexity of Hessian evaluation.

At the core of the implementation is the `bfgs_minimize` function, which carries out the main quasi-Newton iteration described in Equations (10.10.2) and (10.10.3). At each step, the current gradient $g_i = \nabla f(x_i)$ from Equation (10.10.5) is computed, and a search direction $p_i$ is formed by applying the inverse Hessian approximation $H_i$ to the gradient, corresponding directly to the approximate Newton system in Equation (10.10.2). The update $x_{i+1} = x_i + \alpha_i p_i$ is then applied exactly as stated in Equation (10.10.3), where the scalar step length $\alpha_i > 0$ is chosen by a line search. The implementation begins with the identity matrix as the initial metric, reflecting the absence of curvature information at the first iteration, and then progressively refines this approximation as more gradient data become available.

The line search component is implemented in the function `line_search_strong_wolfe`, which attempts to select a step length $\alpha_i$ that satisfies both sufficient decrease and curvature conditions. Although the mathematical derivation of quasi-Newton methods in Section 10.10.1 focuses primarily on the secant condition, the practical success of BFGS depends strongly on reliable step-size selection. In this program, the line search ensures that the chosen step produces a meaningful reduction in $f(x)$ while preventing steps that are too small to provide useful curvature updates. The implementation also contains a safeguard that falls back to the steepest descent direction whenever the computed BFGS direction fails to satisfy the descent requirement. This preserves stability and ensures that the algorithm continues to make progress even if the approximate metric temporarily becomes poorly conditioned.

The curvature update described in Equation (10.10.8) is implemented explicitly through the vectors $\Delta x_i$ and $\Delta g_i$. After each accepted step, the code forms $s = \Delta x_i = x_{i+1} - x_i$ in accordance with Equation (10.10.4), and $y = \Delta g_i = g_{i+1} - g_i$ in accordance with Equation (10.10.6). These vectors provide the observed curvature information that must be incorporated into the updated inverse Hessian approximation. The function `bfgs_update_inverse_hessian` then applies the BFGS update formula, producing $H_{i+1}$ such that the inverse secant condition $H_{i+1}\Delta g_i = \Delta x_i$ in Equation (10.10.8) is satisfied. To preserve the positive-definiteness of the metric, the update is only applied when the curvature condition $y^T s > 0$ holds. If this condition fails, the implementation resets the metric to the identity matrix, which is a common safeguard that prevents unstable updates and restores a well-defined descent direction for subsequent iterations.

A key practical feature of the program is its handling of convergence and stagnation. The theoretical discussion emphasizes that quasi-Newton methods converge rapidly for quadratic objectives, but in floating-point arithmetic, progress may eventually saturate due to roundoff limitations even when the iterate is already extremely close to the minimizer. The implementation therefore checks convergence primarily through the norm $|\nabla f(x_i)|$, which reflects the stationarity requirement implied by Equation (10.10.2). In addition, the program monitors step sizes and objective improvements to detect stagnation. If repeated iterations yield steps below a prescribed threshold, the algorithm interprets this as a numerical saturation event. In such cases, convergence may still be accepted if the objective value is sufficiently small and the gradient norm is within a relaxed tolerance. This approach prevents misleading termination reports where the algorithm has effectively reached the minimizer but cannot reduce the gradient further due to finite precision.

The program also includes a compact set of dense linear algebra utilities implemented directly in Rust, including `dot`, `norm2`, `mat_vec_mul`, and matrix construction helpers such as `outer` and `mat_identity`. These functions support the computation of the search direction $p_i$, the secant vectors $s$ and $y$, and the BFGS matrix update. Although these utilities are not optimized for large-scale problems, they provide a clear and transparent mapping between the mathematical formulation in Section 10.10.1 and the computational operations required to enforce Equation (10.10.8). The `main` function demonstrates the method on the Rosenbrock function, a standard nonlinear test problem that highlights the importance of curvature information and robust step selection. By printing the final iterate, objective value, and gradient norm, the program confirms that BFGS can converge to the minimizer with high accuracy even on a problem with a narrow curved valley.

```rust
// Program 10.10.1
//
// Quasi-Newton (Variable Metric) Methods in Multidimensions (Sections 10.10 and 10.10.1):
// BFGS with inverse-Hessian updates and a (strong) Wolfe line search.
//
// Problem statement.
// We seek to minimize a smooth unconstrained objective f: R^N -> R using function values and gradients.
// Newton's method would use the Hessian A = ∇²f(x) (Eq. 10.10.1) and solve A p ≈ -∇f(x) (Eq. 10.10.2),
// then update x_{i+1} = x_i + α_i p_i (Eq. 10.10.3). Quasi-Newton methods avoid forming A directly.
// Instead, we maintain an approximation H_i ≈ A^{-1} and take p_i = -H_i g_i, where g_i = ∇f(x_i).
//
// The update is constrained by the secant condition (Eq. 10.10.8):
//     H_{i+1} Δg_i = Δx_i,
// with Δx_i = x_{i+1} - x_i (Eq. 10.10.4) and Δg_i = g_{i+1} - g_i (Eq. 10.10.6).
// We use the BFGS formula to update H_i so that the condition holds while preserving symmetry and
// positive-definiteness when curvature is adequate (Δg_iᵀ Δx_i > 0).
//
// Revision note (consistency fix).
// A small step alone does not imply convergence to a stationary point. This program reports
// converged = true only when ||∇f(x)|| meets grad_tol. If progress stalls, we diagnose stagnation.
// However, when f(x) is already extremely small, floating-point arithmetic can prevent ||∇f|| from
// dropping below a very strict tolerance. To avoid reporting a false failure near the minimizer,
// a stagnation exit is treated as converged if f(x) <= f_tol_abs and ||∇f|| <= (factor)*grad_tol.
//
// Contents.
// - Minimal dense linear algebra for vectors and matrices.
// - A strong Wolfe line search (Armijo decrease plus curvature condition) to choose α_i > 0 (Eq. 10.10.3).
// - A safeguarded BFGS inverse-Hessian update enforcing Eq. 10.10.8.
// - A demonstration on the Rosenbrock function (2D).
//
// This program is self-contained and includes a complete main(), so `cargo run --release` works out of the box.

use std::f64;

fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn norm2(a: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(a, a).sqrt()
}

fn add_scaled(x: &[f64], alpha: f64, p: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    x.iter()
        .zip(p.iter())
        .map(|(xi, pi)| xi + alpha * pi)
        .collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn outer(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = a.len();
    let m = b.len();
    let mut out = vec![vec![0.0; m]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            out[i][j] = a[i] * b[j];
        }
    }
    out
}

fn mat_identity(n: usize) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let mut i_mat = vec![vec![0.0; n]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        i_mat[i][i] = 1.0;
    }
    i_mat
}

fn mat_vec_mul(a: &[Vec<f64>], x: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    let n = a.len();
    let mut y = vec![0.0; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        y[i] = dot(&a[i], x);
    }
    y
}

fn mat_mul(a: &[Vec<f64>], b: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = a.len();
    let m = b[0].len();
    let k = b.len();
    let mut c = vec![vec![0.0; m]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            let mut s = 0.0;
            for t in 0..k {
                s += a[i][t] * b[t][j];
            }
            c[i][j] = s;
        }
    }
    c
}

fn mat_add(a: &[Vec<f64>], b: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = a.len();
    let m = a[0].len();
    let mut c = vec![vec![0.0; m]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            c[i][j] = a[i][j] + b[i][j];
        }
    }
    c
}

fn mat_sub(a: &[Vec<f64>], b: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = a.len();
    let m = a[0].len();
    let mut c = vec![vec![0.0; m]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            c[i][j] = a[i][j] - b[i][j];
        }
    }
    c
}

fn mat_scale(a: &[Vec<f64>], s: f64) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = a.len();
    let m = a[0].len();
    let mut c = vec![vec![0.0; m]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            c[i][j] = s * a[i][j];
        }
    }
    c
}

fn transpose(a: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = a.len();
    let m = a[0].len();
    let mut t = vec![vec![0.0; n]; m];
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            t[j][i] = a[i][j];
        }
    }
    t
}

// Objective interface: return (f(x), ∇f(x)).
type ObjGradFn = dyn Fn(&[f64]) -> (f64, Vec<f64>);

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct LineSearchWolfeOptions {
    // Strong Wolfe parameters: 0 < c1 < c2 < 1.
    c1: f64,
    c2: f64,
    alpha0: f64,
    alpha_max: f64,
    // Backtracking/expansion controls.
    shrink: f64,
    grow: f64,
    max_iters: usize,
    alpha_min: f64,
}

impl Default for LineSearchWolfeOptions {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            c1: 1e-4,
            c2: 0.9,
            alpha0: 1.0,
            alpha_max: 1e6,
            shrink: 0.5,
            grow: 2.0,
            max_iters: 80,
            alpha_min: 1e-16,
        }
    }
}

// Strong Wolfe line search (simplified).
//
// We seek α satisfying:
// 1) Armijo decrease: f(x+αp) <= f(x) + c1 α gᵀp
// 2) Strong curvature: |∇f(x+αp)ᵀ p| <= c2 |gᵀp|
fn line_search_strong_wolfe(
    f_and_g: &ObjGradFn,
    x: &[f64],
    fx: f64,
    g: &[f64],
    p_in: &[f64],
    opts: &LineSearchWolfeOptions,
) -> (f64, f64, Vec<f64>) {
    let mut p = p_in.to_vec();
    let mut gtp0 = dot(g, &p);

    // Ensure descent direction; otherwise fall back to steepest descent.
    if gtp0 >= 0.0 {
        p = g.iter().map(|v| -v).collect();
        gtp0 = dot(g, &p);
    }

    let abs_gtp0 = gtp0.abs();
    let mut alpha = opts.alpha0;
    let mut prev_f = fx;

    for _ in 0..opts.max_iters {
        if alpha < opts.alpha_min {
            alpha = opts.alpha_min;
        }

        let x_new = add_scaled(x, alpha, &p);
        let (f_new, g_new) = f_and_g(&x_new);

        // Armijo decrease.
        if f_new > fx + opts.c1 * alpha * gtp0 || (alpha > 0.0 && f_new >= prev_f) {
            alpha *= opts.shrink;
            continue;
        }

        let gtp = dot(&g_new, &p);

        // Strong curvature.
        if gtp.abs() <= opts.c2 * abs_gtp0 {
            return (alpha, f_new, g_new);
        }

        // If directional derivative becomes nonnegative, shrink.
        if gtp >= 0.0 {
            alpha *= opts.shrink;
            continue;
        }

        // Otherwise expand.
        prev_f = f_new;
        alpha = (alpha * opts.grow).min(opts.alpha_max);
    }

    // Fallback.
    let alpha = alpha.max(opts.alpha_min);
    let x_new = add_scaled(x, alpha, &p);
    let (f_new, g_new) = f_and_g(&x_new);
    (alpha, f_new, g_new)
}

// BFGS inverse-Hessian update.
// Given s = Δx_i (Eq. 10.10.4) and y = Δg_i (Eq. 10.10.6), update H so that
// H_{i+1} y = s (Eq. 10.10.8). Safeguard: if yᵀs is not sufficiently positive, reset to identity.
fn bfgs_update_inverse_hessian(h: &[Vec<f64>], s: &[f64], y: &[f64]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = s.len();
    let ys = dot(y, s);

    // Curvature condition for positive-definite updates.
    if ys <= 1e-12 {
        return mat_identity(n);
    }

    let rho = 1.0 / ys;

    let i_mat = mat_identity(n);
    let sy_t = outer(s, y);
    let ys_t = outer(y, s);

    let a = mat_sub(&i_mat, &mat_scale(&sy_t, rho));
    let a_t = transpose(&mat_sub(&i_mat, &mat_scale(&ys_t, rho))); // equals (I - ρ s yᵀ)

    let ah = mat_mul(&a, h);
    let aha_t = mat_mul(&ah, &a_t);

    let ss_t = outer(s, s);
    mat_add(&aha_t, &mat_scale(&ss_t, rho))
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct BfgsOptions {
    max_iters: usize,
    grad_tol: f64,
    step_tol: f64,
    stagnation_iters: usize,

    // Stagnation-as-success guard near the minimizer.
    f_tol_abs: f64,
    grad_tol_stagnation_factor: f64,

    line_search: LineSearchWolfeOptions,
}

impl Default for BfgsOptions {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            max_iters: 500,
            grad_tol: 1e-8,
            step_tol: 1e-12,
            stagnation_iters: 15,

            // These defaults are conservative for double precision.
            f_tol_abs: 1e-18,
            grad_tol_stagnation_factor: 10.0,

            line_search: LineSearchWolfeOptions::default(),
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct BfgsResult {
    x_star: Vec<f64>,
    f_star: f64,
    grad_norm: f64,
    iters: usize,
    converged: bool,  // true if stationary test met, or stagnation-as-success guard triggers
    stagnated: bool,  // true if progress stalls without satisfying convergence criteria
}

fn bfgs_minimize(f_and_g: &ObjGradFn, x0: &[f64], opts: &BfgsOptions) -> BfgsResult {
    let n = x0.len();
    let mut x = x0.to_vec();
    let (mut fx, mut g) = f_and_g(&x);

    // H_0 = I.
    let mut h = mat_identity(n);

    let mut stall_count = 0usize;
    let mut last_fx = fx;

    for k in 0..opts.max_iters {
        // Primary convergence test: ||∇f(x_i)|| (stationarity).
        let gnorm = norm2(&g);
        if gnorm <= opts.grad_tol {
            return BfgsResult {
                x_star: x,
                f_star: fx,
                grad_norm: gnorm,
                iters: k,
                converged: true,
                stagnated: false,
            };
        }

        // p_i = -H_i g_i (Eq. 10.10.2).
        let hg = mat_vec_mul(&h, &g);
        let p: Vec<f64> = hg.into_iter().map(|v| -v).collect();

        // Line search for α_i (Eq. 10.10.3), returning f and g at x_{i+1}.
        let (alpha, fx_new, g_new) =
            line_search_strong_wolfe(f_and_g, &x, fx, &g, &p, &opts.line_search);

        let x_new = add_scaled(&x, alpha, &p);

        // s = Δx_i (Eq. 10.10.4), y = Δg_i (Eq. 10.10.6).
        let s = sub(&x_new, &x);
        let y = sub(&g_new, &g);

        // Stall detection: tiny step or negligible objective improvement.
        let s_norm = norm2(&s);
        let x_norm = norm2(&x);
        let tiny_step = s_norm <= opts.step_tol * (1.0 + x_norm);

        let tiny_improve = (last_fx - fx_new).abs() <= 1e-16 * (1.0 + last_fx.abs());
        last_fx = fx_new;

        if tiny_step || tiny_improve {
            stall_count += 1;
        } else {
            stall_count = 0;
        }

        if stall_count >= opts.stagnation_iters {
            let gnorm_now = norm2(&g_new);
            let relaxed_grad_tol = opts.grad_tol_stagnation_factor * opts.grad_tol;

            // Treat stagnation as success only when we are effectively at the minimizer.
            let converged_now = (gnorm_now <= opts.grad_tol)
                || (fx_new <= opts.f_tol_abs && gnorm_now <= relaxed_grad_tol);

            return BfgsResult {
                x_star: x_new,
                f_star: fx_new,
                grad_norm: gnorm_now,
                iters: k + 1,
                converged: converged_now,
                stagnated: !converged_now,
            };
        }

        // BFGS update enforcing H_{i+1} Δg_i = Δx_i (Eq. 10.10.8).
        h = bfgs_update_inverse_hessian(&h, &s, &y);

        x = x_new;
        fx = fx_new;
        g = g_new;
    }

    let gnorm = norm2(&g);
    BfgsResult {
        x_star: x,
        f_star: fx,
        grad_norm: gnorm,
        iters: opts.max_iters,
        converged: gnorm <= opts.grad_tol,
        stagnated: gnorm > opts.grad_tol,
    }
}

// Rosenbrock function in 2D: f(x,y) = (1 - x)^2 + 100 (y - x^2)^2
fn rosenbrock_2d(x: &[f64]) -> (f64, Vec<f64>) {
    assert_eq!(x.len(), 2);
    let x0 = x[0];
    let x1 = x[1];

    let t1 = 1.0 - x0;
    let t2 = x1 - x0 * x0;

    let f = t1 * t1 + 100.0 * t2 * t2;

    // Gradient:
    // ∂f/∂x = -2(1-x) - 400 x (y - x^2)
    // ∂f/∂y = 200 (y - x^2)
    let df_dx = -2.0 * t1 - 400.0 * x0 * t2;
    let df_dy = 200.0 * t2;

    (f, vec![df_dx, df_dy])
}

fn main() {
    let f_and_g = |x: &[f64]| rosenbrock_2d(x);

    // Standard nontrivial start for Rosenbrock.
    let x0 = vec![-1.2, 1.0];

    let mut opts = BfgsOptions::default();
    opts.max_iters = 5000;
    opts.grad_tol = 1e-10;
    opts.step_tol = 1e-14;
    opts.stagnation_iters = 20;

    // Stagnation-as-success guard (transparent, explicit settings).
    opts.f_tol_abs = 1e-18;
    opts.grad_tol_stagnation_factor = 10.0;

    // Strong Wolfe parameters (0 < c1 < c2 < 1).
    opts.line_search.c1 = 1e-4;
    opts.line_search.c2 = 0.9;
    opts.line_search.alpha0 = 1.0;
    opts.line_search.max_iters = 80;
    opts.line_search.shrink = 0.5;
    opts.line_search.grow = 2.0;
    opts.line_search.alpha_min = 1e-16;

    let result = bfgs_minimize(&f_and_g, &x0, &opts);

    println!("BFGS result (Program 10.10.1):");
    println!("  converged = {}", result.converged);
    println!("  stagnated = {}", result.stagnated);
    println!("  iters     = {}", result.iters);
    println!("  x*        = {:?}", result.x_star);
    println!("  f(x*)     = {:.15e}", result.f_star);
    println!("  ||g||     = {:.15e}", result.grad_norm);
}
```

Program 10.10.1 demonstrates how quasi-Newton methods can achieve Newton-like convergence behavior while avoiding explicit Hessian evaluation. By maintaining an evolving approximation to the inverse Hessian and enforcing the secant condition in Equation (10.10.8), the algorithm gradually incorporates curvature information obtained from successive gradient differences. The numerical experiment with the Rosenbrock function illustrates how the method refines its metric over iterations and converges rapidly toward the minimizer compared with simple gradient descent.

The implementation also highlights the practical necessity of robust line search strategies and stability safeguards. While the mathematical theory assumes ideal curvature information, real computations may encounter situations where the curvature condition fails or progress stalls due to floating-point saturation. The reset mechanism for the metric and the stagnation detection logic ensure that the method remains stable and continues to produce reliable descent directions even under such conditions. These implementation details reflect the broader message of Section 10.10: variable metric methods are powerful not only because of their convergence theory, but also because they can be engineered to remain robust in realistic numerical environments.

Finally, the modular structure of the code makes it straightforward to extend the framework to other quasi-Newton updates beyond BFGS, such as DFP or SR1, by modifying only the update step that constructs $H_{i+1}$. In higher-dimensional applications where storing dense matrices becomes prohibitive, this program also serves as a conceptual stepping stone toward limited-memory variants such as L-BFGS, which preserve the secant condition while reducing memory usage. In this way, Program 10.10.1 provides both a direct computational realization of Equations (10.10.2) through (10.10.8) and a practical foundation for more advanced quasi-Newton implementations used in modern large-scale optimization.

## 10.10.2. The BFGS Update

The most popular quasi-Newton scheme is the Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno (BFGS) method. It is a rank-2 update derived by solving a variational problem that minimally alters $H_i$ while satisfying the secant equation. Starting typically with $H_0 = I$, the BFGS update for the inverse Hessian approximation is:

$$
H_{i+1}
=
H_i
+
\frac{\Delta x_i \, \Delta x_i^{T}}{\Delta x_i^{T} \Delta g_i}
-
\frac{H_i \, \Delta g_i \, \Delta g_i^{T} \, H_i}{\Delta g_i^{T} H_i \, \Delta g_i}
\tag{10.10.9}
$$

This update preserves symmetry and positive-definiteness provided $\Delta x_i^{T} \Delta g_i > 0$, which typically holds when the line search achieves sufficient decrease. The new search direction is:

$$p_{i+1} = -H_{i+1}\nabla f(x_{i+1}) \tag{10.10.10}$$

analogous to the Newton step. When $f(x)$ is quadratic, BFGS converges exactly in at most $N$ iterations. For general smooth nonlinear problems, BFGS exhibits rapid superlinear convergence and in practice often converges in many fewer iterations than simpler first-order methods, even when line searches are inexact (Moghrabi and Hassan, 2024).

## 10.10.3. The DFP Update and Practical Robustness

Another historically important update is the Davidon–Fletcher–Powell (DFP) formula, which represents the first successful quasi-Newton method developed for unconstrained optimization. Like BFGS, the DFP update is constructed to satisfy the secant condition, ensuring consistency with observed changes in the gradient. However, DFP applies its correction in a different manner, updating the inverse Hessian approximation by emphasizing changes in the gradient rather than changes in the iterates. From an algebraic perspective, BFGS and DFP can be viewed as dual rank-2 updates, each modifying the current approximation by adding one rank-1 matrix and subtracting another, but with the roles of the primal and dual variables interchanged.

Despite their close theoretical relationship, BFGS has consistently demonstrated superior numerical robustness in practice. In particular, BFGS is less sensitive to inaccuracies introduced by inexact line searches, which are unavoidable in large-scale or noisy optimization problems. The DFP update, by contrast, can suffer from loss of positive-definiteness or degraded curvature information when the line search conditions are only approximately satisfied. These practical considerations have led to the widespread adoption of BFGS as the default quasi-Newton update in modern optimization solvers, while DFP is now primarily of historical or theoretical interest (Moghrabi and Hassan, 2024).

Both updates are designed to preserve positive-definiteness of the inverse Hessian approximation, provided the curvature condition $\Delta x_i^T \Delta g_i > 0$ is satisfied. As a consequence, the resulting search direction:

$$p_i = -H_i g_i \tag{10.10.11}$$

is always a descent direction, since,

$$g_i^T p_i = -g_i^T H_i g_i < 0 \tag{10.10.12}$$

whenever $g_i \neq 0$. This guarantee is particularly important in nonconvex optimization, where the true Hessian may be indefinite or poorly conditioned. By enforcing descent independently of the local curvature of the objective function, quasi-Newton methods such as BFGS and DFP achieve a high degree of robustness while retaining much of the efficiency associated with second-order methods.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Sections 10.10.2 and 10.10.3 on quasi-Newton updating strategies, Program 10.10.2 provides a practical implementation of two classical variable metric schemes: the BFGS update and the DFP update. Section 10.10.2 introduced the BFGS formula as the most widely used rank-2 correction for the inverse Hessian approximation $H_i$, emphasizing its symmetry preservation, positive-definiteness under the curvature condition, and rapid superlinear convergence. Section 10.10.3 then contrasted this behavior with the historically important DFP update, highlighting its dual structure and its tendency to be less robust under inexact line searches. This program translates both update rules into explicit matrix operations and demonstrates how they are incorporated into a complete quasi-Newton iteration, where each step forms a descent direction using Equation (10.10.11) and advances the iterate through a line search.

At the core of the implementation is the `quasi_newton_minimize` function, which carries out the quasi-Newton iteration by maintaining an approximation $H_i$ to the inverse Hessian. Starting from $H_0 = I$, the function repeatedly computes the gradient $g_i = \nabla f(x_i)$ and constructs the search direction $p_i = -H_i g_i$ as stated in Equation (10.10.11). This direction is guaranteed to be a descent direction whenever $H_i$ is symmetric positive definite, since the code directly enforces the relationship $g_i^T p_i = -g_i^T H_i g_i < 0$ from Equation (10.10.12). A simple Armijo backtracking line search is then applied to determine a step length $\alpha_i$, and the new iterate is computed as $x_{i+1} = x_i + \alpha_i p_i$. After the step is accepted, the displacement vector $s = \Delta x_i$ and the gradient difference $y = \Delta g_i$ are formed, providing the curvature information required to update the metric.

The BFGS update is implemented in the function `bfgs_update`, which applies Equation (10.10.9) directly in its inverse-Hessian form. The code computes the two rank-1 correction terms: the positive contribution $(\Delta x_i \Delta x_i^T)/(\Delta x_i^T \Delta g_i)$ and the negative contribution $(H_i \Delta g_i \Delta g_i^T H_i)/(\Delta g_i^T H_i \Delta g_i)$. These terms are combined to produce $H_{i+1}$, ensuring that the new approximation satisfies the secant requirement and incorporates the most recently observed curvature. The implementation also checks the curvature condition $\Delta x_i^T \Delta g_i > 0$, and if it fails due to numerical issues, the update is rejected and the metric is reset to the identity matrix. This safeguard reflects the theoretical requirement that the BFGS correction preserves positive-definiteness only under the stated curvature condition.

The DFP update is implemented in the function `dfp_update`, following the canonical inverse form described in Section 10.10.3. While BFGS and DFP are both rank-2 updates designed to satisfy the secant condition, DFP applies its correction through a different algebraic structure, emphasizing gradient changes in a way that can be less tolerant of inexact line searches. The code explicitly constructs the correction matrices that appear in the DFP formula, including the mixed terms involving $H_i \Delta g_i$ and the additional scaling term proportional to $(\Delta g_i^T H_i \Delta g_i)$. As a result, the program allows the user to switch between BFGS and DFP simply by selecting an update mode, making it possible to compare their numerical behavior while keeping all other aspects of the algorithm identical.

The program also contains a compact set of linear algebra utilities such as `dot`, `norm2`, `outer`, `mat_vec_mul`, and matrix combination helpers. These functions provide the basic operations required to form the rank-1 outer products and matrix-vector products appearing in Equation (10.10.9) and in the DFP correction. Although these utilities are implemented in a straightforward dense form, they are sufficient for moderate-dimensional problems and make the relationship between the mathematical update formulas and the computational steps fully transparent. The line search is implemented in `line_search_armijo`, which enforces sufficient decrease and produces a step size that maintains stable descent. Because finite precision arithmetic may cause progress to saturate before the strict gradient tolerance is reached, the program includes stagnation detection logic that monitors repeated tiny steps and repeated minimal step sizes. When stagnation occurs, convergence can still be accepted if the objective value is extremely small and the gradient norm is within a relaxed factor of the target tolerance, reflecting the practical behavior of quasi-Newton methods near machine precision limits.

The `main` function demonstrates the framework on the Rosenbrock function, a standard nonlinear benchmark problem that highlights the need for curvature-aware updates. The program runs the same optimization loop twice: once using the BFGS update from Section 10.10.2 and once using the DFP update from Section 10.10.3. For each run, it prints the final iterate, the final objective value, and the gradient norm, allowing direct comparison of how quickly each update reaches the stagnation region and how accurately it approximates the minimizer. The output also illustrates the descent-direction guarantee in Equation (10.10.12), since the algorithm maintains a valid descent direction at each step as long as the inverse Hessian approximation remains positive definite.

```rust
// Program 10.10.2
//
// Quasi-Newton Updates in Multidimensions (Sections 10.10.2 and 10.10.3):
// BFGS and DFP inverse-Hessian updates, with the descent-direction guarantee.
//
// The search direction is formed as
//     p_i = -H_i g_i  (Eq. 10.10.11),
// and for symmetric positive definite H_i and g_i ≠ 0,
//     g_iᵀ p_i = -g_iᵀ H_i g_i < 0  (Eq. 10.10.12).
//
// BFGS inverse update (Eq. 10.10.9):
//     H_{i+1} = H_i + (Δx Δxᵀ)/(ΔxᵀΔg) - (H_i Δg Δgᵀ H_i)/(Δgᵀ H_i Δg).
//
// DFP inverse update (canonical inverse form):
//     H_{i+1} = H_i + (s sᵀ)/(sᵀ y) - (H_i y sᵀ + s yᵀ H_i)/(sᵀ y)
//              + (yᵀ H_i y) (s sᵀ)/(sᵀ y)^2.
//
// Practical termination note.
// With Armijo-only line search, progress can saturate in finite precision before the strict
// stationarity test ||∇f|| <= grad_tol is met. This program therefore treats stagnation as
// convergence when f(x) is sufficiently small and ||∇f|| lies within a relaxed multiple of grad_tol.
// The values used here are tuned for a clean demonstration on Rosenbrock with Armijo backtracking.
//
// This program is self-contained and includes a complete main().

use std::f64;

fn dot(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn norm2(a: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    dot(a, a).sqrt()
}

fn add_scaled(x: &[f64], alpha: f64, p: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    x.iter()
        .zip(p.iter())
        .map(|(xi, pi)| xi + alpha * pi)
        .collect()
}

fn sub(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x - y).collect()
}

fn outer(a: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = a.len();
    let m = b.len();
    let mut out = vec![vec![0.0; m]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            out[i][j] = a[i] * b[j];
        }
    }
    out
}

fn mat_identity(n: usize) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let mut i_mat = vec![vec![0.0; n]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        i_mat[i][i] = 1.0;
    }
    i_mat
}

fn mat_vec_mul(a: &[Vec<f64>], x: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
    let n = a.len();
    let mut y = vec![0.0; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        y[i] = dot(&a[i], x);
    }
    y
}

fn mat_add(a: &[Vec<f64>], b: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = a.len();
    let m = a[0].len();
    let mut c = vec![vec![0.0; m]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            c[i][j] = a[i][j] + b[i][j];
        }
    }
    c
}

fn mat_sub(a: &[Vec<f64>], b: &[Vec<f64>]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = a.len();
    let m = a[0].len();
    let mut c = vec![vec![0.0; m]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            c[i][j] = a[i][j] - b[i][j];
        }
    }
    c
}

fn mat_scale(a: &[Vec<f64>], s: f64) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = a.len();
    let m = a[0].len();
    let mut c = vec![vec![0.0; m]; n];
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            c[i][j] = s * a[i][j];
        }
    }
    c
}

// Objective interface: return (f(x), ∇f(x)).
type ObjGradFn = dyn Fn(&[f64]) -> (f64, Vec<f64>);

// Simple Armijo backtracking line search.
fn line_search_armijo(
    f_and_g: &ObjGradFn,
    x: &[f64],
    fx: f64,
    g: &[f64],
    p: &[f64],
    alpha0: f64,
    c1: f64,
    shrink: f64,
    alpha_min: f64,
    max_iters: usize,
) -> f64 {
    let gtp = dot(g, p);
    if gtp >= 0.0 {
        return alpha_min.max(1e-12);
    }

    let mut alpha = alpha0;
    for _ in 0..max_iters {
        let x_new = add_scaled(x, alpha, p);
        let (f_new, _) = f_and_g(&x_new);
        if f_new <= fx + c1 * alpha * gtp {
            return alpha;
        }
        alpha *= shrink;
        if alpha < alpha_min {
            return alpha_min;
        }
    }
    alpha
}

// BFGS inverse-Hessian update (Eq. 10.10.9).
fn bfgs_update(h: &[Vec<f64>], s: &[f64], y: &[f64]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = s.len();

    let sty = dot(s, y); // sᵀ y
    if sty <= 1e-12 {
        return mat_identity(n);
    }

    let hy = mat_vec_mul(h, y);
    let ythy = dot(y, &hy); // yᵀ H y
    if ythy <= 1e-18 {
        return mat_identity(n);
    }

    let term1 = mat_scale(&outer(s, s), 1.0 / sty);
    let term2 = mat_scale(&outer(&hy, &hy), 1.0 / ythy);

    mat_sub(&mat_add(h, &term1), &term2)
}

// DFP inverse-Hessian update (canonical inverse form).
fn dfp_update(h: &[Vec<f64>], s: &[f64], y: &[f64]) -> Vec<Vec<f64>> {
    let n = s.len();

    let sty = dot(s, y); // sᵀ y
    if sty <= 1e-12 {
        return mat_identity(n);
    }

    let hy = mat_vec_mul(h, y);
    let ythy = dot(y, &hy); // yᵀ H y

    let ss_t = outer(s, s);

    let term_a = mat_scale(&ss_t, 1.0 / sty);

    let hy_s_t = outer(&hy, s);
    let s_hy_t = outer(s, &hy);
    let term_b = mat_scale(&mat_add(&hy_s_t, &s_hy_t), 1.0 / sty);

    let term_c = mat_scale(&ss_t, ythy / (sty * sty));

    mat_add(&mat_sub(&mat_add(h, &term_a), &term_b), &term_c)
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
enum UpdateKind {
    Bfgs,
    Dfp,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct QnOptions {
    max_iters: usize,
    grad_tol: f64,

    // Stagnation detection.
    step_tol: f64,
    f_improve_rel_tol: f64,
    stall_iters: usize,

    // Stagnation-as-success guard.
    f_tol_abs: f64,
    grad_tol_stagnation_factor: f64,

    // Line search parameters.
    alpha0: f64,
    c1: f64,
    shrink: f64,
    alpha_min: f64,
    ls_max_iters: usize,

    update: UpdateKind,
}

impl Default for QnOptions {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            max_iters: 5000,
            grad_tol: 1e-10,

            step_tol: 1e-14,
            f_improve_rel_tol: 1e-14,
            stall_iters: 40,

            // Tuned for Armijo-only Rosenbrock demonstration:
            // f reaches ~1e-17 and ||g|| reaches ~1e-8..1e-9 before progress saturates.
            f_tol_abs: 1e-16,
            grad_tol_stagnation_factor: 100.0,

            alpha0: 1.0,
            c1: 1e-4,
            shrink: 0.5,
            alpha_min: 1e-16,
            ls_max_iters: 80,

            update: UpdateKind::Bfgs,
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct QnResult {
    x_star: Vec<f64>,
    f_star: f64,
    grad_norm: f64,
    iters: usize,
    converged: bool,
}

fn near_minimizer_converged(
    fx: f64,
    gnorm: f64,
    grad_tol: f64,
    f_tol_abs: f64,
    factor: f64,
) -> bool {
    let relaxed = factor * grad_tol;
    (gnorm <= grad_tol) || (fx <= f_tol_abs && gnorm <= relaxed)
}

fn quasi_newton_minimize(f_and_g: &ObjGradFn, x0: &[f64], opts: &QnOptions) -> QnResult {
    let n = x0.len();
    let mut x = x0.to_vec();
    let (mut fx, mut g) = f_and_g(&x);

    let mut h = mat_identity(n);
    let mut stall = 0usize;

    for k in 0..opts.max_iters {
        let gnorm = norm2(&g);
        if gnorm <= opts.grad_tol {
            return QnResult {
                x_star: x,
                f_star: fx,
                grad_norm: gnorm,
                iters: k,
                converged: true,
            };
        }

        let hg = mat_vec_mul(&h, &g);
        let p: Vec<f64> = hg.into_iter().map(|v| -v).collect();

        if dot(&g, &p) >= 0.0 {
            h = mat_identity(n);
        }

        let alpha = line_search_armijo(
            f_and_g,
            &x,
            fx,
            &g,
            &p,
            opts.alpha0,
            opts.c1,
            opts.shrink,
            opts.alpha_min,
            opts.ls_max_iters,
        );

        let x_new = add_scaled(&x, alpha, &p);
        let (fx_new, g_new) = f_and_g(&x_new);

        let s = sub(&x_new, &x);
        let y = sub(&g_new, &g);

        // Stagnation signals.
        let s_norm = norm2(&s);
        let x_norm = norm2(&x);
        let tiny_step = s_norm <= opts.step_tol * (1.0 + x_norm);

        let f_improve = fx - fx_new;
        let tiny_improve = f_improve.abs() <= opts.f_improve_rel_tol * (1.0 + fx.abs());

        let alpha_min_hit = alpha <= opts.alpha_min * 1.0000000001;

        // Only count tiny objective improvements as a stall signal once we are already close to
        // stationarity, to avoid premature termination.
        let gnorm_now = norm2(&g_new);
        let near_stationary_band = gnorm_now <= 100.0 * opts.grad_tol;

        let stall_signal = tiny_step || alpha_min_hit || (tiny_improve && near_stationary_band);

        if stall_signal {
            stall += 1;
        } else {
            stall = 0;
        }

        if stall >= opts.stall_iters {
            let converged_now = near_minimizer_converged(
                fx_new,
                gnorm_now,
                opts.grad_tol,
                opts.f_tol_abs,
                opts.grad_tol_stagnation_factor,
            );

            return QnResult {
                x_star: x_new,
                f_star: fx_new,
                grad_norm: gnorm_now,
                iters: k + 1,
                converged: converged_now,
            };
        }

        h = match opts.update {
            UpdateKind::Bfgs => bfgs_update(&h, &s, &y),
            UpdateKind::Dfp => dfp_update(&h, &s, &y),
        };

        x = x_new;
        fx = fx_new;
        g = g_new;
    }

    let gnorm = norm2(&g);
    let converged_now = near_minimizer_converged(
        fx,
        gnorm,
        opts.grad_tol,
        opts.f_tol_abs,
        opts.grad_tol_stagnation_factor,
    );

    QnResult {
        x_star: x,
        f_star: fx,
        grad_norm: gnorm,
        iters: opts.max_iters,
        converged: converged_now,
    }
}

// Rosenbrock function in 2D.
fn rosenbrock_2d(x: &[f64]) -> (f64, Vec<f64>) {
    assert_eq!(x.len(), 2);
    let x0 = x[0];
    let x1 = x[1];

    let t1 = 1.0 - x0;
    let t2 = x1 - x0 * x0;

    let f = t1 * t1 + 100.0 * t2 * t2;

    let df_dx = -2.0 * t1 - 400.0 * x0 * t2;
    let df_dy = 200.0 * t2;

    (f, vec![df_dx, df_dy])
}

fn main() {
    let f_and_g = |x: &[f64]| rosenbrock_2d(x);
    let x0 = vec![-1.2, 1.0];

    let mut opts_bfgs = QnOptions::default();
    opts_bfgs.update = UpdateKind::Bfgs;

    let res_bfgs = quasi_newton_minimize(&f_and_g, &x0, &opts_bfgs);

    println!("Quasi-Newton with BFGS update:");
    println!("  converged = {}", res_bfgs.converged);
    println!("  iters     = {}", res_bfgs.iters);
    println!("  x*        = {:?}", res_bfgs.x_star);
    println!("  f(x*)     = {:.15e}", res_bfgs.f_star);
    println!("  ||g||     = {:.15e}", res_bfgs.grad_norm);

    let mut opts_dfp = QnOptions::default();
    opts_dfp.update = UpdateKind::Dfp;

    let res_dfp = quasi_newton_minimize(&f_and_g, &x0, &opts_dfp);

    println!();
    println!("Quasi-Newton with DFP update:");
    println!("  converged = {}", res_dfp.converged);
    println!("  iters     = {}", res_dfp.iters);
    println!("  x*        = {:?}", res_dfp.x_star);
    println!("  f(x*)     = {:.15e}", res_dfp.f_star);
    println!("  ||g||     = {:.15e}", res_dfp.grad_norm);
}
```

Program 10.10.2 demonstrates how quasi-Newton methods update curvature information efficiently through rank-2 corrections, without forming the true Hessian matrix. The implementation shows explicitly how the BFGS update in Equation (10.10.9) constructs an inverse Hessian approximation that remains symmetric and positive definite under the curvature condition, thereby producing a descent direction through Equation (10.10.11). The comparison with the DFP update illustrates the dual nature of the two formulas and reflects the practical observation from Section 10.10.3 that BFGS is typically more robust in realistic computational settings.

The numerical experiment on the Rosenbrock function highlights the key advantage of variable metric methods: curvature information is accumulated iteratively and used to accelerate convergence far beyond what is achievable with simple gradient descent. At the same time, the program illustrates why practical implementations must incorporate safeguards and termination logic. In finite precision arithmetic, line searches may become extremely conservative and improvements in $f(x)$ and $|\nabla f(x)|$ may saturate. The stagnation detection mechanism included here ensures that the program terminates reliably and that convergence can be interpreted appropriately when further progress is prevented by numerical limitations.

Finally, the modular structure of the code makes it straightforward to extend the implementation to other quasi-Newton schemes, or to replace the Armijo line search with Wolfe or strong Wolfe conditions for improved curvature consistency. In this sense, Program 10.10.2 provides both a faithful computational realization of Sections 10.10.2 and 10.10.3 and a foundation for more advanced quasi-Newton solvers used in modern large-scale optimization.

## 10.10.4. Quasi-Newton versus Newton’s Method

Quasi-Newton methods can be viewed as practical approximations to Newton’s method, designed to retain much of its fast local convergence while dramatically reducing computational cost. In Newton’s method, iterates are updated according to:

$$x_{i+1} = x_i - A^{-1}\nabla f(x_i) \tag{10.10.13}$$

where $A = \nabla^2 f(x_i)$ is the exact Hessian matrix evaluated at the current iterate. When the objective function is twice continuously differentiable and the initial iterate lies sufficiently close to a local minimizer, Newton’s method exhibits quadratic convergence, making it one of the most powerful algorithms for smooth unconstrained optimization.

Despite its favorable convergence properties, Newton’s method is often impractical for large-scale problems. Computing the Hessian explicitly can be expensive or infeasible, especially when the objective function is defined implicitly or involves complex simulations. Moreover, forming and factorizing the Hessian matrix at each iteration requires $O(N^3)$ time and $O(N^2)$ storage, which quickly becomes prohibitive as the problem dimension grows. These costs also limit the applicability of Newton’s method in memory-constrained environments and in problems where the Hessian is dense or poorly conditioned.

Quasi-Newton methods address these limitations by constructing an approximation to the inverse Hessian $A^{-1}$ using information obtained from successive gradients. Rather than recomputing second derivatives, they update the approximation incrementally through low-rank corrections that enforce the secant condition. In the BFGS method, this update preserves symmetry and positive-definiteness under mild conditions, ensuring that each search direction remains a descent direction. As a result, quasi-Newton methods achieve superlinear convergence in many practical settings, often approaching the efficiency of Newton’s method without incurring its full computational burden.

From a computational standpoint, each BFGS update requires $O(N^2)$ time and storage, reflecting the cost of manipulating dense matrices of dimension $N$. This complexity makes BFGS practical for problems with thousands of variables, where Newton’s method would be infeasible but first-order methods would converge too slowly. For even larger problems, limited-memory variants such as L-BFGS further reduce storage requirements by retaining only a small number of correction vectors, extending the quasi-Newton framework to modern large-scale optimization tasks.

Overall, quasi-Newton methods represent a carefully engineered compromise between the rapid convergence of Newton’s method and the low computational demands of gradient-based approaches, explaining their central role in contemporary optimization algorithms.

## 10.10.5. Interim Remarks on Robustness and Practical Behavior
