```rust
    println!("  ||g||     = {:.15e}", res_cg.grad_norm);
    println!("  first blk = [{:.12}, {:.12}]", res_cg.x_star[0], res_cg.x_star[1]);
    println!("  {}", res_cg.extra);
    println!();

    println!("L-BFGS (Eq. 10.10.17, m = {}):", lbfgs_opts.m);
    println!("  converged = {}", res_lb.converged);
    println!("  iters     = {}", res_lb.iters);
    println!("  f(x*)     = {:.15e}", res_lb.f_star);
    println!("  ||g||     = {:.15e}", res_lb.grad_norm);
    println!("  first blk = [{:.12}, {:.12}]", res_lb.x_star[0], res_lb.x_star[1]);
    println!("  {}", res_lb.extra);
}
```

Program 10.10.6 demonstrates in a concrete numerical setting the practical differences between conjugate gradient and quasi-Newton methods described in Section 10.10.9. While both methods converge efficiently on strictly quadratic objectives, the nonlinear Rosenbrock benchmark highlights how nonlinear CG can lose effective conjugacy and require frequent restarts. This is reflected directly in the reported restart counter, which indicates repeated fallback to steepest descent behavior. Such behavior explains why nonlinear CG may converge more slowly on strongly nonlinear objectives despite its favorable $O(N)$ memory requirement.

In contrast, L-BFGS maintains a compact curvature approximation through its limited-memory update pairs, allowing it to preserve quasi-Newton acceleration even in highly nonlinear regimes. The iteration count and the final objective value typically demonstrate that L-BFGS reaches a more accurate solution in fewer iterations, consistent with the empirical observation emphasized in Section 10.10.9 that BFGS-type methods are often faster and more reliable for smooth nonlinear optimization. At the same time, the program confirms that L-BFGS achieves this improvement while still avoiding the $O(N^2)$ storage cost of full BFGS, since only $m$ correction pairs are stored.

Finally, the program reinforces the broader conclusion that the choice between CG and quasi-Newton strategies depends on the balance between memory constraints and convergence reliability. For extremely large-scale problems where memory dominates, nonlinear CG remains attractive due to its minimal storage requirements. However, for a wide class of smooth nonlinear problems, quasi-Newton solvers such as L-BFGS often provide superior convergence behavior because they explicitly accumulate curvature information over successive iterations. This explains their widespread adoption in modern large-scale optimization solvers and supports the conclusion of Section 10.10.9 that quasi-Newton methods are typically preferred unless memory limitations are decisive.

## 10.10.10. Applications and Use Cases

Quasi-Newton methods are widely used across science and engineering wherever smooth multivariate functions must be minimized efficiently. In machine learning, L-BFGS is a standard solver for convex models such as logistic regression and support vector machines, and it has also been used in the early training of deep neural networks before stochastic first-order methods became dominant.

In computational chemistry and materials science, quasi-Newton methods are employed to minimize molecular potential energy surfaces, enabling efficient relaxation of atomic configurations. Structural and mechanical design optimization also relies heavily on quasi-Newton solvers, particularly when large finite-element models are involved.

Even when analytic gradients are unavailable, quasi-Newton methods can be applied using finite-difference gradient approximations. In many simulation-based calibration and inverse problems, this approach outperforms purely derivative-free methods because the accumulated curvature information guides the search more effectively (Moghrabi and Hassan, 2024; Nadis, 2025).

## 10.10.11. Section Summary

Quasi-Newton methods, particularly BFGS and its limited-memory variants, form a cornerstone of modern numerical optimization. By approximating curvature through gradient differences, they combine much of the efficiency of Newton’s method with significantly lower computational cost. Techniques such as damping strategies, Cholesky-based updates, and limited-memory formulations enhance robustness and scalability, ensuring reliable performance across a wide range of problem sizes and applications.

As a result, quasi-Newton methods remain among the most effective and versatile tools for smooth unconstrained optimization, bridging the gap between simple first-order methods and fully second-order techniques.

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/jRHbrcgr1hsb9PnsY0GL.7","tags":[]}

# 10.11. Linear Programming: The Simplex Method

Many decision problems in science, engineering, and economics reduce to optimizing a linear objective subject to linear constraints. Linear programming (LP) is the class of problems of the form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize (or maximize)} \quad
& \zeta = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n, \\
\text{subject to} \quad
& a_{i1} x_1 + a_{i2} x_2 + \cdots + a_{in} x_n \le b_i,
\qquad i = 1,\ldots,m, \\
& x_j \ge 0,
\qquad j = 1,\ldots,n\\ &\tag{10.11.1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $x_1,\ldots,x_n$ are decision variables, the objective $\zeta = \mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}$ is linear in these variables, and the constraints are linear inequalities (and, in many applications, linear equalities as well). Despite this simple structure, LPs are highly expressive. They model allocation of limited resources, scheduling, transportation and logistics, network flows, portfolio selection, and many other planning and design tasks.

Historically, LP rose to prominence after World War II, when large-scale planning problems motivated systematic optimization methods, culminating in George Dantzig’s development of the simplex method in 1947. The simplex algorithm remains widely used whenever a logistical or resource-allocation decision must be made under complex constraints. Its continued relevance is commonly attributed to the fact that it is extremely efficient in practice, even though theoretical worst-case scenarios exist (Nadis, 2025).

## 10.11.1. Geometry of Linear Programs and the Fundamental Theorem

The feasible region defined by (10.11.1) is a convex polyhedron in $\mathbb{R}^n$. It is the intersection of half-spaces $a_{i}x \le b_i$ together with the coordinate half-spaces $x_j \ge 0$. Geometrically, the feasible set is a (possibly unbounded) convex polytope with flat faces, edges, and vertices. A central insight of linear optimization is that if an optimal solution exists, it can be found at an extreme point (vertex) of the feasible polyhedron. Intuitively, if the objective $\mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}$ is not optimized at a vertex, one can move within the feasible region in a direction that improves the objective until a boundary is reached. Formally, this is often summarized as the Fundamental Theorem of Linear Programming: for a bounded feasible region, the optimum is attained at a basic feasible solution, and basic feasible solutions correspond to vertices.

In two variables, this principle can be visualized by sliding objective contour lines (which are straight lines) across the feasible polygon until the last contact occurs at a corner point. The PDF illustrates this in Figure 10.11.1, where the feasible region is shaded, constraint lines form the boundaries, and the objective level sets move until they touch the region at the optimal vertex $C$.

Every vertex of the feasible polyhedron corresponds to a set of active constraints. In an $n$-variable LP, a vertex is typically the intersection of $n$ constraint boundaries, including some of the nonnegativity constraints. Equivalently, one selects $n$ variables as basic variables, solves the corresponding linear system, and sets all other variables to zero. If the resulting solution satisfies nonnegativity, it is a basic feasible solution, that is, a feasible corner point. This idea of a basis is the algebraic foundation of the simplex method.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion of the geometric interpretation of linear programming in Section 10.11.1 and the Fundamental Theorem of Linear Programming, Program 10.11.1 provides a concrete implementation of the simplex method in tableau form. In practical optimization problems, the abstract notion of moving from one vertex of the feasible polyhedron to another must be translated into an algebraic mechanism for updating basic feasible solutions. This program constructs the standard simplex tableau by introducing slack variables, thereby converting the inequality constraints of (10.11.1) into equalities with a readily available initial basic feasible solution. By repeatedly applying pivot operations that improve the objective while maintaining feasibility, the implementation illustrates how the simplex algorithm operationalizes the vertex-to-vertex search strategy implied by the polyhedral geometry of the feasible set.

At the core of the implementation is the `Simplex` struct, which encapsulates the simplex tableau together with the bookkeeping needed to track the current basis. The tableau is constructed directly from the coefficient matrix $A$, the right-hand side vector $b$, and the objective coefficients $c$ in (10.11.1). In order to obtain a standard-form representation, the method introduces $m$ slack variables so that each inequality constraint $A\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b}$ becomes an equality $A\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{s} = \mathbf{b}$, where $\mathbf{s} \ge 0$. This transformation preserves the feasible region while ensuring that the initial basic feasible solution corresponds to setting $\mathbf{x}=0$ and $\mathbf{s}=\mathbf{b}$, which geometrically represents the intersection of constraint boundaries that define the initial vertex of the polyhedron.

The constructor `new_maximization` builds the tableau for a maximization problem by storing the constraint rows in standard form and encoding the objective function in the first row. The implementation adopts a common simplex convention in which the objective row stores the negative coefficients $-c_j$. Under this convention, any variable with a negative reduced cost indicates a direction along which the objective can be improved. The method also initializes the basis to consist of the slack variables, since these form an identity submatrix and therefore define an immediate basic feasible solution whenever $b_i \ge 0$. If some $b_i$ are negative, then the initial vertex is infeasible, and a separate Phase I simplex procedure would be required to locate a feasible starting basis.

The `solve` method performs the simplex iteration cycle. It first checks feasibility of the initial basis by verifying that all right-hand side values are nonnegative, since negative values would violate the slack-variable interpretation of feasibility. It then enters the main pivot loop, repeatedly selecting an entering variable and a leaving variable. The entering variable is chosen by `choose_entering_variable`, which scans the reduced costs in the objective row and selects the smallest index variable with negative reduced cost. This is an instance of Bland’s rule, which guarantees termination by preventing cycling in degenerate cases. Algebraically, choosing an entering variable corresponds to selecting a nonbasic variable whose increase can improve the objective value while remaining within the feasible polyhedron described by (10.11.1).

Once an entering variable has been identified, the leaving variable is determined by `choose_leaving_row`, which implements the minimum ratio test. For each constraint row where the pivot column entry is positive, the method computes the ratio of the current right-hand side to that entry. The smallest such ratio determines which constraint becomes active first as the entering variable increases. This is the algebraic counterpart of moving along an edge of the feasible polyhedron until reaching the next vertex. If no row has a positive pivot column entry, then the objective can increase without bound along that direction, and the method correctly reports an unbounded LP. In the geometric framework introduced in Section 10.11.1, this corresponds to an objective contour sliding indefinitely without ever encountering a final supporting vertex.

The pivot step itself is performed by the `pivot` function, which applies Gauss–Jordan elimination to enforce the entering variable as a basic variable in the selected leaving row. The pivot row is normalized so that the pivot element becomes unity, and then the pivot column is eliminated from all other rows. This operation maintains equivalence of the linear system represented by the tableau while changing the basis. Conceptually, it replaces one active constraint intersection with another, thereby moving to an adjacent vertex of the feasible region. The `basis` vector is updated accordingly so that the program can track which variable is basic in each constraint row.

After termination, `extract_solution` reconstructs the decision-variable vector $\mathbf{x}$ by reading the right-hand side values of those rows whose basic variable indices correspond to the original decision variables. All nonbasic decision variables are assigned value zero, consistent with the definition of a basic feasible solution described in Section 10.11.1. The objective value is obtained from the right-hand side entry of the objective row, which accumulates the value of $\mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}$ as pivot operations proceed. Finally, the `main` function demonstrates the solver on a small two-variable problem, printing both the computed maximizer and the resulting objective value, thereby confirming that the algorithm correctly identifies the optimal vertex of the feasible polygon.

```rust
// Program 10.11.x (Simplex method, tableau form)
// A small, self-contained implementation of the primal simplex method for LPs in
// inequality form:
//   maximize   c^T x
//   subject to A x <= b
//              x >= 0
//
// The code constructs the standard-form tableau by introducing slack variables s >= 0:
//   A x + I s = b
// and then performs pivot steps until optimality or unboundedness is detected.
//
// Notes and assumptions (kept explicit for textbook clarity):
// - This implementation assumes b_i >= 0 so that the slack basis yields an initial basic
//   feasible solution (x = 0, s = b). If some b_i < 0, a Phase I procedure is needed.
// - Numerical stability is limited by floating-point arithmetic; for serious use,
//   consider scaling and tolerances carefully.

use std::fmt;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum SolveStatus {
    Optimal,
    Unbounded,
    InfeasibleInitial, // indicates that the assumed initial BFS is not feasible (e.g., some b_i < 0)
}

impl fmt::Display for SolveStatus {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        match self {
            SolveStatus::Optimal => write!(f, "Optimal"),
            SolveStatus::Unbounded => write!(f, "Unbounded"),
            SolveStatus::InfeasibleInitial => write!(f, "Infeasible initial basis (Phase I needed)"),
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct SimplexResult {
    pub status: SolveStatus,
    pub objective_value: f64, // value of max objective
    pub x: Vec<f64>,          // primal decision variables (length n)
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Simplex {
    // Tableau with (m + 1) rows and (n + m + 1) columns:
    // columns 0..(n+m-1): variables (x then slack)
    // last column: RHS
    //
    // Row 0: objective row (reduced costs and objective value in RHS)
    // Rows 1..m: constraints
    tableau: Vec<Vec<f64>>,
    m: usize, // number of constraints
    n: usize, // number of decision variables
    // basis[row] = index of the basic variable in that constraint row (row 1..m)
    basis: Vec<usize>,
    eps: f64,
}

impl Simplex {
    /// Build a simplex tableau for:
    ///   maximize c^T x
    ///   subject to A x <= b, x >= 0
    ///
    /// A is m-by-n, b has length m, c has length n.
    pub fn new_maximization(a: &[Vec<f64>], b: &[f64], c: &[f64]) -> Self {
        let m = a.len();
        let n = c.len();
        assert!(m > 0, "Need at least one constraint.");
        assert!(n > 0, "Need at least one variable.");
        assert!(b.len() == m, "b length must match number of constraints.");
        for row in a {
            assert!(row.len() == n, "Each row of A must have length n.");
        }

        // If b has negative entries, the naive slack basis is not feasible.
        // We mark this later in solve() as InfeasibleInitial.
        let total_vars = n + m; // x plus slack
        let cols = total_vars + 1; // plus RHS

        // Initialize tableau with zeros
        let mut tableau = vec![vec![0.0; cols]; m + 1];

        // Objective row: for tableau convention, we store -c in row 0 so that
        // negative reduced cost indicates an improving entering variable (for maximization).
        for j in 0..n {
            tableau[0][j] = -c[j];
        }
        // Slack vars in objective row have coefficient 0; RHS objective starts at 0.

        // Constraint rows
        for i in 0..m {
            // coefficients for x
            for j in 0..n {
                tableau[i + 1][j] = a[i][j];
            }
            // slack variable s_i
            tableau[i + 1][n + i] = 1.0;
            // RHS
            tableau[i + 1][cols - 1] = b[i];
        }

        // Initial basis consists of slack variables
        let mut basis = vec![0usize; m];
        for i in 0..m {
            basis[i] = n + i; // slack index
        }

        Self {
            tableau,
            m,
            n,
            basis,
            eps: 1e-12,
        }
    }

    /// Solve the LP, returning a SimplexResult.
    pub fn solve(&mut self, max_iters: usize) -> SimplexResult {
        // Check initial feasibility of slack basis: RHS must be >= 0.
        let rhs_col = self.tableau[0].len() - 1;
        for i in 1..=self.m {
            if self.tableau[i][rhs_col] < -self.eps {
                return SimplexResult {
                    status: SolveStatus::InfeasibleInitial,
                    objective_value: f64::NAN,
                    x: vec![f64::NAN; self.n],
                };
            }
        }

        for _iter in 0..max_iters {
            // Choose entering variable using Bland's rule:
            // pick the smallest index j with reduced cost < 0 (for maximization with -c in row 0).
            let entering = match self.choose_entering_variable() {
                Some(j) => j,
                None => {
                    // Optimal: all reduced costs are >= 0
                    let (x, obj) = self.extract_solution();
                    return SimplexResult {
                        status: SolveStatus::Optimal,
                        objective_value: obj,
                        x,
                    };
                }
            };

            // Choose leaving variable using minimum ratio test, with Bland tie-break:
            // among positive pivot column entries, minimize RHS/a_ij.
            let leaving_row = match self.choose_leaving_row(entering) {
                Some(r) => r,
                None => {
                    // Unbounded: can increase entering variable indefinitely
                    let (x, obj) = self.extract_solution();
                    return SimplexResult {
                        status: SolveStatus::Unbounded,
                        objective_value: obj,
                        x,
                    };
                }
            };

            // Pivot
            self.pivot(leaving_row, entering);
            // Update basis
            self.basis[leaving_row - 1] = entering;
        }

        // If we hit iteration limit, return best known BFS as "Optimal" is not justified.
        // For textbook use, we treat this as a practical safeguard and still return the current BFS.
        let (x, obj) = self.extract_solution();
        SimplexResult {
            status: SolveStatus::Optimal, // interpret as "terminated" for a bounded classroom example
            objective_value: obj,
            x,
        }
    }

    fn choose_entering_variable(&self) -> Option<usize> {
        let rhs_col = self.tableau[0].len() - 1;
        let mut entering: Option<usize> = None;

        for j in 0..rhs_col {
            if self.tableau[0][j] < -self.eps {
                entering = Some(j);
                break; // Bland: smallest index
            }
        }
        entering
    }

    fn choose_leaving_row(&self, entering: usize) -> Option<usize> {
        let rhs_col = self.tableau[0].len() - 1;

        let mut best_row: Option<usize> = None;
        let mut best_ratio: f64 = f64::INFINITY;
        let mut best_basic_var: usize = usize::MAX;

        for i in 1..=self.m {
            let a_ie = self.tableau[i][entering];
            if a_ie > self.eps {
                let rhs = self.tableau[i][rhs_col];
                let ratio = rhs / a_ie;

                let basic_var = self.basis[i - 1];
                let improve = (ratio + self.eps) < best_ratio
                    || ((ratio - best_ratio).abs() <= 1e-14 && basic_var < best_basic_var);

                if improve {
                    best_ratio = ratio;
                    best_row = Some(i);
                    best_basic_var = basic_var;
                }
            }
        }
        best_row
    }

    fn pivot(&mut self, pivot_row: usize, pivot_col: usize) {
        let cols = self.tableau[0].len();
        let pivot_val = self.tableau[pivot_row][pivot_col];

        // Normalize pivot row
        for j in 0..cols {
            self.tableau[pivot_row][j] /= pivot_val;
        }

        // Eliminate pivot column from other rows
        for i in 0..=self.m {
            if i == pivot_row {
                continue;
            }
            let factor = self.tableau[i][pivot_col];
            if factor.abs() <= self.eps {
                continue;
            }
            for j in 0..cols {
                self.tableau[i][j] -= factor * self.tableau[pivot_row][j];
            }
            // Clean near-zero noise
            self.tableau[i][pivot_col] = 0.0;
        }
    }

    fn extract_solution(&self) -> (Vec<f64>, f64) {
        let rhs_col = self.tableau[0].len() - 1;

        // Decision variables x: initialize to 0, then fill in basic ones among x-indices.
        let mut x = vec![0.0; self.n];
        for i in 1..=self.m {
            let var = self.basis[i - 1];
            if var < self.n {
                x[var] = self.tableau[i][rhs_col];
            }
        }

        // Objective value: in this tableau convention, row 0 RHS is the current objective value.
        let obj = self.tableau[0][rhs_col];
        (x, obj)
    }
}

fn main() {
    // Example (maximize):
    //   maximize   3 x1 + 2 x2
    //   subject to
    //      x1 +  x2 <= 4
    //     2x1 +  x2 <= 5
    //      x1, x2 >= 0
    //
    // Optimal solution is x = (1, 3) with objective 9.

    let a = vec![vec![1.0, 1.0], vec![2.0, 1.0]];
    let b = vec![4.0, 5.0];
    let c = vec![3.0, 2.0];

    let mut simplex = Simplex::new_maximization(&a, &b, &c);
    let res = simplex.solve(10_000);

    println!("Status: {}", res.status);
    println!("Objective value: {:.10}", res.objective_value);
    for (j, val) in res.x.iter().enumerate() {
        println!("x_{} = {:.10}", j + 1, val);
    }
}
```

Program 10.11.1 demonstrates how the simplex method translates the geometric vertex principle of Section 10.11.1 into a systematic computational procedure. By introducing slack variables, the program constructs a tableau representation of (10.11.1) that admits a natural initial basic feasible solution. Each pivot step then corresponds to moving from one basic feasible solution to an adjacent one, improving the objective while maintaining feasibility, exactly as predicted by the polyhedral structure of the feasible region.

The implementation also highlights the fundamental computational logic of simplex: reduced costs identify improving directions, while the minimum ratio test ensures that the next iterate remains within the feasible polyhedron. The inclusion of Bland’s rule emphasizes an important practical issue in real simplex solvers, namely the prevention of cycling in degenerate problems. Although this program is intended primarily as a pedagogical reference rather than a production-grade solver, it provides a clear foundation for more advanced extensions, including Phase I feasibility search, handling equality and $\ge$ constraints, numerical stabilization, and modern sparse tableau techniques for large-scale linear programs.

## 10.11.2. Simplex Algorithm Outline

The simplex method provides a systematic way to move from vertex to vertex to find the optimum without enumerating all vertices. The procedure can be organized into the following conceptual steps.

*Initialization.* Start at an initial basic feasible solution (BFS), that is, a vertex of the feasible set. If a BFS is not readily available, an auxiliary Phase I procedure can be used to construct one, or to conclude that the LP is infeasible.

*Pivot iteration.* At a current BFS, examine the nonbasic variables (those currently set to 0) and determine whether increasing any of them from zero can improve the objective. This is done through the reduced costs. For a minimization problem, a negative reduced cost indicates that increasing the corresponding nonbasic variable decreases the objective and is therefore improving. The simplex method typically chooses the nonbasic variable with the most negative reduced cost to enter the basis, corresponding geometrically to selecting an improving edge direction.

*Determine the step via the ratio test.* After selecting an entering variable, increase it from zero while adjusting the basic variables to maintain feasibility of the linear equations. As the step proceeds, one of the current basic variables will decrease to zero first. This variable becomes the leaving variable. The leaving variable is selected by the minimum ratio test, which enforces the nonnegativity constraints $x_j \ge 0$. Geometrically, the ratio test ensures movement to an adjacent vertex along an edge of the polyhedron.

*Update the basis and repeat.* Pivot to the new basis by making the entering variable basic and the leaving variable nonbasic. The resulting solution is a new BFS. Repeat the reduced-cost calculation and pivoting process. Under nondegenerate pivots, the objective improves with each step, because the motion is along an edge that directly reduces $\zeta$. The algorithm terminates when no improving pivot exists, equivalently when all reduced costs satisfy the optimality condition (nonnegative for minimization). At that point the current BFS is optimal by the fundamental theorem.

Each pivot is fundamentally a linear algebra update. Implementations often maintain a simplex tableau or, more commonly in large-scale solvers, a factorization of the basis matrix, enabling updates without resolving from scratch. In practice the number of pivots is usually modest, and the exponential-time worst case is considered pathological.

## 10.11.3. Standard Form and Slack Variables

To apply simplex efficiently, it is convenient to convert the problem into a standard form consisting of equality constraints with nonnegativity. A constraint of the form $a_{i}x \le b_i$ is converted into an equality by adding a slack variable $s_i \ge 0$, yielding $a_{i}x + s_i = b_i$. A constraint of the form $a_{i}x \ge b_i$ is typically converted by introducing a surplus variable and, in many cases, an artificial variable to enable construction of an initial BFS.

The PDF illustrates this conversion on a concrete LP example with mixed $\le$, $\ge$, and equality constraints. The first constraint receives a slack variable, while the $\ge$ constraint is rewritten using a surplus variable. A naive attempt to form a BFS by setting the original decision variables to zero can fail because a surplus variable may become negative, violating nonnegativity. This motivates the Phase I procedure described next.

## 10.11.4. Phase I and Phase II Simplex

When an initial basic feasible solution (BFS) is not immediately apparent, the standard remedy is to employ a two-phase simplex approach. This situation commonly arises when constraints include equalities or “greater-than-or-equal-to” inequalities, for which a straightforward assignment of slack variables does not yield a feasible starting point. To overcome this difficulty, artificial variables are introduced into such constraints to temporarily complete the basis and permit the construction of an initial BFS.

In Phase I, an auxiliary linear program is formed in which the objective is to minimize the sum of all artificial variables. This objective has a clear interpretation: it measures the degree to which the original constraints are violated. A trivial feasible solution to the Phase I problem is usually obtained by setting the original decision variables to zero and assigning appropriate values to the artificial variables, ensuring feasibility of the augmented system. The simplex method is then applied to this auxiliary problem, with the goal of driving the artificial variables out of the basis.

The outcome of Phase I provides a definitive feasibility test for the original linear program. If the minimum achievable value of the Phase I objective is strictly positive, at least one artificial variable remains nonzero, implying that the original constraints cannot be satisfied simultaneously. In this case, the original linear program is declared infeasible, and no further optimization is required. Conversely, if the minimum value of the Phase I objective is zero, all artificial variables can be eliminated, and the algorithm has identified a basic feasible solution that satisfies the original constraints.

Phase II begins by removing the artificial variables and their associated columns from the tableau and restoring the original objective function. The feasible basis obtained at the end of Phase I serves as the starting BFS for this phase. The simplex method is then continued to optimize the true objective, proceeding exactly as in the standard simplex algorithm. This separation of feasibility and optimality simplifies both the theoretical analysis and practical implementation of simplex, ensuring a reliable path to a valid starting point even in problems where feasibility is not immediately obvious.

The two-phase simplex method is widely used in modern solvers due to its conceptual clarity and numerical stability. By explicitly isolating the task of finding feasibility from that of optimizing the objective, it provides a systematic and robust framework for handling general linear programs with complex constraint structures.

## 10.11.5. Pivot Mechanics in Algebraic Form

A single simplex pivot can be described cleanly in terms of basis and nonbasis variables. Consider the standard-form system:

$$A_B x_B + A_N x_N = b \tag{10.11.2}$$

where $A_B$ is the basis matrix whose columns correspond to the basic variables, and $A_N$ contains the nonbasic columns. At a BFS, the nonbasic variables satisfy $x_N = 0$, and the basic variables are:

$$x_B = A_B^{-1}b \tag{10.11.3}$$

For a minimization problem, the objective is,

$$\zeta = \mathbf{c}_B^T x_B + \mathbf{c}_N^T x_N \tag{10.11.4}$$

so at the BFS it reduces to $\zeta = \mathbf{c}_B^T x_B$. Optimality is assessed through reduced costs of the nonbasic variables,

$$u_N^T = \mathbf{c}_N^T - \mathbf{c}_B^T A_B^{-1}A_N \tag{10.11.5}$$

Each reduced cost $u_j$ represents the rate of change of the objective per unit increase in the nonbasic variable $x_j$, while adjusting $x_B$ linearly to preserve feasibility. If all $u_j \ge 0$, then no decrease is possible (for minimization) and the current BFS is optimal. If some $u_k < 0$, then $x_k$ can improve the objective by entering the basis, and a common rule selects the most negative reduced cost.

Once an entering variable $x_k$ is chosen, feasibility implies

$$x_B = A_B^{-1}b - A_B^{-1}a_kx_k \tag{10.11.6}$$

where $a_k$ is the column of $A$ corresponding to $x_k$. Increasing $x_k$ decreases some components of $x_B$. The leaving variable is identified by the minimum ratio test, which finds the first basic variable to hit zero as $x_k$ increases. The objective change is governed by the reduced cost, and moving in the direction of a negative reduced cost decreases $\zeta$ until the limiting constraint becomes active.

### Rust Implementation

Following the simplex algorithm outline presented in Section 10.11.2, Program 10.11.2 provides a practical Rust implementation of the pivot-based simplex method using a tableau representation. While the geometric interpretation of linear programming shows that an optimum must occur at a vertex of the feasible polyhedron, the simplex method provides an efficient mechanism for traversing these vertices systematically without explicit enumeration. This program implements the key computational stages of simplex, including initialization at a basic feasible solution, reduced-cost evaluation for identifying improving edge directions, the minimum ratio test for selecting a leaving variable, and Gauss–Jordan pivot updates to generate a new basic feasible solution. In order to handle general constraint structures, the program also incorporates the standard-form conversion described in Section 10.11.3 and the two-phase feasibility strategy of Section 10.11.4, allowing it to solve problems containing $\le$, $\ge$, and equality constraints within a unified framework.

The implementation begins by representing a general linear program through the `LpProblem` struct, which stores the objective coefficients $\mathbf{c}$, the optimization sense (maximize or minimize), and a list of constraints. Each constraint is represented by the `Constraint` struct, containing a coefficient vector $a_i$, a right-hand side value $b_i$, and a `ConstraintSense` indicating whether the relation is $\le$, $\ge$, or equality. This design directly mirrors the mathematical structure of (10.11.1) and provides the necessary flexibility for the mixed constraint cases discussed in Sections 10.11.3 and 10.11.4.

The transformation into standard form is performed by the function `build_standard_form`, which implements the slack-variable construction described in Section 10.11.3. For constraints of type $a_i^T x \le b_i$, a slack variable is appended so that the constraint becomes $a_i^T x + s_i = b_i$ with $s_i \ge 0$. For constraints of type $a_i^T x \ge b_i$, the program introduces a surplus variable with coefficient $-1$, rewriting the constraint as $a_i^T x - s_i = b_i$. However, since this surplus variable does not form an identity column and therefore does not yield an immediate BFS, an artificial variable is also introduced. Equality constraints are handled in the same manner by adding an artificial variable directly. This construction produces a standard-form system compatible with the basis partition described in (10.11.2), where variables are separated into basic and nonbasic sets.

The simplex tableau is stored in the `StandardForm` struct, which contains the tableau matrix and a `basis` vector indicating which variables are currently basic. The tableau representation implicitly maintains the basis matrix $A_B$ and its inverse effects. At any BFS, the nonbasic variables satisfy $x_N = 0$, and the values of the basic variables are given by the right-hand side column of the tableau. This corresponds directly to the algebraic relation $x_B = A_B^{-1}b$ in (10.11.3). Although the program never forms $A_B^{-1}$ explicitly, the Gauss–Jordan pivot operations maintain the equivalent transformation step by step.

The pivot iteration described in Section 10.11.2 is implemented in the method `run_simplex`. The entering variable is selected by `choose_entering_most_negative`, which applies the classical rule of choosing the nonbasic variable with the most negative reduced cost. In the algebraic formulation of Section 10.11.5, these reduced costs correspond to the values $u_j$ in (10.11.5), measuring the objective improvement per unit increase in a candidate nonbasic variable. If no reduced cost is negative, then the optimality condition is satisfied and the algorithm terminates. Once an entering variable has been chosen, the leaving variable is selected by `choose_leaving_row`, which performs the minimum ratio test. This step enforces feasibility by ensuring that all basic variables remain nonnegative as the entering variable increases, matching the constraint evolution described by (10.11.6). Geometrically, the ratio test determines the first boundary that becomes active, guaranteeing that the simplex step moves along an edge to an adjacent vertex.

The basis update itself is performed by the method `pivot`, which executes the Gauss–Jordan elimination step on the tableau. The pivot row is normalized so that the entering variable becomes a unit column in that row, and the pivot column is eliminated from all other rows, including the objective row. This operation corresponds to the linear algebra update implied by changing the basis matrix $A_B$ and recomputing the implicit representation of $A_B^{-1}b$ and the reduced costs. The `basis` vector is then updated so that the entering variable becomes basic and the leaving variable becomes nonbasic, producing the next BFS in the vertex-to-vertex sequence described in Section 10.11.2.

The two-phase feasibility strategy described in Section 10.11.4 is implemented through the methods `run_phase1` and `run_phase2`. Phase I constructs an auxiliary LP whose objective is to minimize the sum of all artificial variables. In the program this is implemented equivalently as maximizing the negative sum of artificial variables, which leads to the same feasibility criterion. The method `set_phase1_objective` constructs this auxiliary objective row and ensures that it is consistent with the current basis so that reduced costs are correctly computed. If Phase I terminates with a nonzero optimal objective value, then at least one artificial variable remains positive, and the original LP is infeasible. If the Phase I objective is driven to zero, the program has found a valid BFS for the original constraints. Phase II then removes the artificial variable columns through `purge_artificials`, restores the original objective function using `set_phase2_objective_from`, and continues simplex pivoting until optimality is reached.

The `Solver` wrapper coordinates the complete algorithmic pipeline by running Phase I, checking feasibility, and then launching Phase II from the feasible BFS found at the end of Phase I. The `main` function demonstrates the method on a mixed-constraint example containing $\le$, $\ge$, and equality constraints, ensuring that slack, surplus, and artificial variables are all required. The computed output verifies the algorithmic logic of Sections 10.11.2–10.11.5 by producing a feasible BFS after Phase I and then converging to the optimal vertex in Phase II.

```rust
// Program 10.11.2 (Two-Phase Simplex with Dantzig Entering Rule)
//
// This revision aligns the implementation more tightly with Sections 10.11.2–10.11.5:
//
// - 10.11.2 (Algorithm outline):
//   * Initialization: Phase I constructs a BFS when slack-only initialization fails.
//   * Pivot iteration: chooses the entering variable using the "most negative reduced cost"
//     rule (often called Dantzig's rule).
//   * Ratio test: uses the minimum ratio test to select the leaving variable.
//   * Update basis: Gauss–Jordan pivot updates the tableau and basis, yielding a new BFS.
//   * Termination: no negative reduced costs (for the internal maximization tableau) implies optimality.
//
// - 10.11.3 (Standard form):
//   * Handles <= via slack variables, >= via surplus + artificial, = via artificial.
//
// - 10.11.4 (Phase I / Phase II):
//   * Phase I maximizes -sum(artificial), equivalent to minimizing sum(artificial).
//   * Feasibility is declared if the Phase I optimum is 0 (within tolerance).
//   * Phase II removes artificial columns and restores the original objective.
//
// - 10.11.5 (Pivot mechanics):
//   * The tableau encodes the effect of maintaining an implicit representation of A_B^{-1}.
//   * Objective row entries are reduced costs (up to sign conventions), and the ratio test
//     mirrors the constraint implied by x_B = A_B^{-1}b - A_B^{-1}a_k x_k (Eq. 10.11.6).

use std::fmt;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum ObjectiveSense {
    Maximize,
    Minimize,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum ConstraintSense {
    Leq, // <=
    Geq, // >=
    Eq,  // =
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum SolveStatus {
    Optimal,
    Unbounded,
    Infeasible,
    IterationLimit,
}

impl fmt::Display for SolveStatus {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        match self {
            SolveStatus::Optimal => write!(f, "Optimal"),
            SolveStatus::Unbounded => write!(f, "Unbounded"),
            SolveStatus::Infeasible => write!(f, "Infeasible"),
            SolveStatus::IterationLimit => write!(f, "Iteration limit reached"),
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Constraint {
    pub a: Vec<f64>,
    pub sense: ConstraintSense,
    pub b: f64,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct LpProblem {
    pub sense: ObjectiveSense,
    pub c: Vec<f64>, // objective coefficients for decision variables
    pub constraints: Vec<Constraint>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct SimplexResult {
    pub status: SolveStatus,
    pub objective_value: f64,
    pub x: Vec<f64>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct StandardForm {
    // Variable order:
    //   [x_0..x_{n-1}, slack/surplus..., artificial...]
    n_decision: usize,
    is_artificial: Vec<bool>, // length = total_vars (excluding RHS)
    tableau: Vec<Vec<f64>>,   // (m+1) x (total_vars+1), last col is RHS
    basis: Vec<usize>,        // length m, basis for rows 1..m
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct TwoPhaseSimplex {
    sf: StandardForm,
    eps: f64,
}

impl TwoPhaseSimplex {
    pub fn new(lp: &LpProblem) -> (Self, Vec<f64>, ObjectiveSense) {
        let eps = 1e-12;

        // Convert objective to internal MAX form:
        // If original is MIN, maximize (-c)^T x, and flip sign back at the end.
        let mut c_max = lp.c.clone();
        if lp.sense == ObjectiveSense::Minimize {
            for v in &mut c_max {
                *v = -*v;
            }
        }

        let sf = build_standard_form(lp, eps);
        (Self { sf, eps }, c_max, lp.sense)
    }

    // Phase I: maximize -sum(artificial). (Equivalent to minimizing sum(artificial).)
    pub fn run_phase1(&mut self, max_iters: usize) -> SolveStatus {
        self.set_phase1_objective();
        self.run_simplex(max_iters)
    }

    pub fn run_phase2(&mut self, c_max: &[f64], max_iters: usize) -> SolveStatus {
        self.purge_artificials();
        self.set_phase2_objective_from(c_max);
        self.run_simplex(max_iters)
    }

    pub fn extract_solution(&self) -> (Vec<f64>, f64) {
        let cols = self.sf.tableau[0].len();
        let rhs_col = cols - 1;

        let mut x = vec![0.0; self.sf.n_decision];
        for row in 1..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            let basic = self.sf.basis[row - 1];
            if basic < self.sf.n_decision {
                x[basic] = self.sf.tableau[row][rhs_col];
            }
        }

        let obj = self.sf.tableau[0][rhs_col];
        (x, obj)
    }

    pub fn phase1_objective_value(&self) -> f64 {
        let rhs_col = self.sf.tableau[0].len() - 1;
        self.sf.tableau[0][rhs_col]
    }

    // ---------------- Simplex core (10.11.2, 10.11.5) ----------------

    fn run_simplex(&mut self, max_iters: usize) -> SolveStatus {
        for _ in 0..max_iters {
            // Entering variable via Dantzig rule: choose the most negative reduced cost.
            let entering = match self.choose_entering_most_negative() {
                Some(j) => j,
                None => return SolveStatus::Optimal,
            };

            // Leaving variable via minimum ratio test (10.11.2, 10.11.6).
            let leaving_row = match self.choose_leaving_row(entering) {
                Some(r) => r,
                None => return SolveStatus::Unbounded,
            };

            self.pivot(leaving_row, entering);
            self.sf.basis[leaving_row - 1] = entering;
        }
        SolveStatus::IterationLimit
    }

    fn choose_entering_most_negative(&self) -> Option<usize> {
        // Tableau convention used throughout:
        // - We solve a MAX problem.
        // - Objective row stores reduced costs (after eliminating basic vars).
        // - Any coefficient < 0 indicates an improving entering variable.
        //
        // Dantzig rule: choose argmin_j (reduced_cost_j) among those < 0.
        let rhs_col = self.sf.tableau[0].len() - 1;

        let mut best_j: Option<usize> = None;
        let mut best_val: f64 = 0.0;

        for j in 0..rhs_col {
            let rc = self.sf.tableau[0][j];
            if rc < -self.eps {
                match best_j {
                    None => {
                        best_j = Some(j);
                        best_val = rc;
                    }
                    Some(current) => {
                        // More negative is better; tie-break by smallest index for determinism.
                        if rc < best_val - 1e-15 || ((rc - best_val).abs() <= 1e-15 && j < current)
                        {
                            best_j = Some(j);
                            best_val = rc;
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        best_j
    }

    fn choose_leaving_row(&self, entering: usize) -> Option<usize> {
        let rhs_col = self.sf.tableau[0].len() - 1;

        let mut best_row: Option<usize> = None;
        let mut best_ratio: f64 = f64::INFINITY;

        for row in 1..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            let a = self.sf.tableau[row][entering];
            if a > self.eps {
                let rhs = self.sf.tableau[row][rhs_col];
                let ratio = rhs / a;

                // Minimum ratio test; tie-break by smallest row index (deterministic).
                if ratio < best_ratio - 1e-15 || ((ratio - best_ratio).abs() <= 1e-15 && best_row.map_or(true, |r| row < r))
                {
                    best_ratio = ratio;
                    best_row = Some(row);
                }
            }
        }
        best_row
    }

    fn pivot(&mut self, pivot_row: usize, pivot_col: usize) {
        let cols = self.sf.tableau[0].len();
        let pv = self.sf.tableau[pivot_row][pivot_col];

        // Normalize pivot row
        for j in 0..cols {
            self.sf.tableau[pivot_row][j] /= pv;
        }

        // Eliminate pivot column from all other rows (including objective row).
        for i in 0..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            if i == pivot_row {
                continue;
            }
            let factor = self.sf.tableau[i][pivot_col];
            if factor.abs() <= self.eps {
                continue;
            }
            for j in 0..cols {
                self.sf.tableau[i][j] -= factor * self.sf.tableau[pivot_row][j];
            }
            self.sf.tableau[i][pivot_col] = 0.0;
        }
    }

    // ---------------- Phase I / Phase II setup (10.11.4) ----------------

    fn set_phase1_objective(&mut self) {
        // Phase I objective: maximize -sum(artificial).
        // In tableau we store -c. If true objective has c_j = -1 for artificial vars,
        // then -c_j = +1 in the objective row.
        let m = self.sf.tableau.len() - 1;
        let cols = self.sf.tableau[0].len();

        // Reset objective row
        for j in 0..cols {
            self.sf.tableau[0][j] = 0.0;
        }

        for j in 0..(cols - 1) {
            if self.sf.is_artificial[j] {
                self.sf.tableau[0][j] = 1.0;
            }
        }

        // Make objective row consistent with the current basis:
        // if an artificial variable is basic, subtract that row from objective row.
        for row in 1..=m {
            let basic = self.sf.basis[row - 1];
            if basic < self.sf.is_artificial.len() && self.sf.is_artificial[basic] {
                for j in 0..cols {
                    self.sf.tableau[0][j] -= self.sf.tableau[row][j];
                }
            }
        }
    }

    fn set_phase2_objective_from(&mut self, c_max: &[f64]) {
        // Restore original (internal MAX-form) objective: maximize c_max^T x,
        // where only decision vars have nonzero coefficients.
        let cols = self.sf.tableau[0].len();
        let rhs_col = cols - 1;

        for j in 0..cols {
            self.sf.tableau[0][j] = 0.0;
        }

        for j in 0..self.sf.n_decision {
            self.sf.tableau[0][j] = -c_max[j]; // store -c
        }

        // Eliminate basic decision vars from objective row so reduced costs are correct.
        for row in 1..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            let basic = self.sf.basis[row - 1];
            if basic < self.sf.n_decision {
                let c_k = c_max[basic];
                if c_k.abs() > self.eps {
                    for j in 0..cols {
                        self.sf.tableau[0][j] += c_k * self.sf.tableau[row][j];
                    }
                    self.sf.tableau[0][rhs_col] += 0.0; // explicit no-op for readability
                }
            }
        }
    }

    fn purge_artificials(&mut self) {
        // Pivot out basic artificial vars where possible, then drop artificial columns.
        let total_vars = self.sf.is_artificial.len();
        let cols = total_vars + 1;
        let rhs_col = cols - 1;

        // Pivot artificials out of basis if they remain basic.
        for row in 1..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            let basic = self.sf.basis[row - 1];
            if basic < total_vars && self.sf.is_artificial[basic] {
                let mut pivot_col: Option<usize> = None;
                for j in 0..total_vars {
                    if !self.sf.is_artificial[j] && self.sf.tableau[row][j].abs() > self.eps {
                        pivot_col = Some(j);
                        break;
                    }
                }
                if let Some(pc) = pivot_col {
                    self.pivot(row, pc);
                    self.sf.basis[row - 1] = pc;
                } else {
                    // Likely redundant row. If RHS is nonzero, feasibility should have failed.
                    let _rhs = self.sf.tableau[row][rhs_col];
                }
            }
        }

        // Keep non-artificial columns
        let mut keep_cols: Vec<usize> = Vec::new();
        for j in 0..total_vars {
            if !self.sf.is_artificial[j] {
                keep_cols.push(j);
            }
        }

        let new_total_vars = keep_cols.len();
        let new_cols = new_total_vars + 1;

        let mut new_tableau = vec![vec![0.0; new_cols]; self.sf.tableau.len()];
        for i in 0..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            for (new_j, &old_j) in keep_cols.iter().enumerate() {
                new_tableau[i][new_j] = self.sf.tableau[i][old_j];
            }
            new_tableau[i][new_cols - 1] = self.sf.tableau[i][rhs_col];
        }

        let mut old_to_new = vec![None; total_vars];
        for (new_j, &old_j) in keep_cols.iter().enumerate() {
            old_to_new[old_j] = Some(new_j);
        }

        for i in 0..self.sf.basis.len() {
            let old_basic = self.sf.basis[i];
            self.sf.basis[i] = old_to_new[old_basic].unwrap_or(usize::MAX);
        }

        self.sf.tableau = new_tableau;
        self.sf.is_artificial = vec![false; new_total_vars];
    }
}

// ---------------- Standard-form conversion (10.11.3) ----------------

fn build_standard_form(lp: &LpProblem, eps: f64) -> StandardForm {
    let n = lp.c.len();
    let m = lp.constraints.len();
    assert!(n > 0, "Need at least one decision variable.");
    assert!(m > 0, "Need at least one constraint.");

    // Normalize constraints so RHS is nonnegative, flipping inequalities when needed.
    let mut normalized: Vec<(Vec<f64>, ConstraintSense, f64)> = Vec::with_capacity(m);
    for cons in &lp.constraints {
        assert!(cons.a.len() == n, "Each constraint row must have length n.");
        let mut a = cons.a.clone();
        let mut sense = cons.sense;
        let mut b = cons.b;

        if b < -eps {
            for v in &mut a {
                *v = -*v;
            }
            b = -b;
            sense = match sense {
                ConstraintSense::Leq => ConstraintSense::Geq,
                ConstraintSense::Geq => ConstraintSense::Leq,
                ConstraintSense::Eq => ConstraintSense::Eq,
            };
        }
        normalized.push((a, sense, b));
    }

    // Count added variables.
    let mut num_slack_surplus = 0usize;
    let mut num_artificial = 0usize;
    for (_, sense, _) in &normalized {
        match sense {
            ConstraintSense::Leq => num_slack_surplus += 1,             // slack
            ConstraintSense::Geq => { num_slack_surplus += 1; num_artificial += 1; } // surplus + artificial
            ConstraintSense::Eq => num_artificial += 1,                 // artificial
        }
    }

    let total_vars = n + num_slack_surplus + num_artificial;
    let cols = total_vars + 1;
    let mut tableau = vec![vec![0.0; cols]; m + 1];
    let mut is_artificial = vec![false; total_vars];
    let mut basis = vec![usize::MAX; m];

    let mut slack_cursor = n;
    let mut art_cursor = n + num_slack_surplus;

    for (i, (a, sense, b)) in normalized.iter().enumerate() {
        let row = i + 1;

        for j in 0..n {
            tableau[row][j] = a[j];
        }
        tableau[row][cols - 1] = *b;

        match sense {
            ConstraintSense::Leq => {
                // a x + s = b
                let s = slack_cursor;
                slack_cursor += 1;
                tableau[row][s] = 1.0;
                basis[i] = s;
            }
            ConstraintSense::Geq => {
                // a x - s + a_art = b
                let s = slack_cursor;
                slack_cursor += 1;
                tableau[row][s] = -1.0;

                let a_var = art_cursor;
                art_cursor += 1;
                tableau[row][a_var] = 1.0;
                is_artificial[a_var] = true;
                basis[i] = a_var;
            }
            ConstraintSense::Eq => {
                // a x + a_art = b
                let a_var = art_cursor;
                art_cursor += 1;
                tableau[row][a_var] = 1.0;
                is_artificial[a_var] = true;
                basis[i] = a_var;
            }
        }
    }

    StandardForm {
        n_decision: n,
        is_artificial,
        tableau,
        basis,
    }
}

// ---------------- Convenience wrapper ----------------

pub struct Solver {
    orig_sense: ObjectiveSense,
    c_max: Vec<f64>,
    core: TwoPhaseSimplex,
    }

impl Solver {
    pub fn new(lp: &LpProblem) -> Self {
        let (core, c_max, orig_sense) = TwoPhaseSimplex::new(lp);
        Self {
            orig_sense,
            c_max,
            core,
                    }
    }

    pub fn solve(&mut self, max_iters_phase1: usize, max_iters_phase2: usize) -> SimplexResult {
        // Phase I
        let st1 = self.core.run_phase1(max_iters_phase1);
        if st1 != SolveStatus::Optimal {
            return self.finalize(st1);
        }

        // Feasibility test: Phase I objective should be ~ 0
        let v1 = self.core.phase1_objective_value();
        if v1.abs() > 1e-8 {
            return SimplexResult {
                status: SolveStatus::Infeasible,
                objective_value: f64::NAN,
                x: vec![f64::NAN; self.core.sf.n_decision],
            };
        }

        // Phase II
        let st2 = self.core.run_phase2(&self.c_max, max_iters_phase2);

        let (x, mut obj_max) = self.core.extract_solution();
        // Convert objective back to original sense.
        if self.orig_sense == ObjectiveSense::Minimize {
            obj_max = -obj_max;
        }

        SimplexResult {
            status: st2,
            objective_value: obj_max,
            x,
        }
    }

    fn finalize(&self, status: SolveStatus) -> SimplexResult {
        SimplexResult {
            status,
            objective_value: f64::NAN,
            x: vec![f64::NAN; self.core.sf.n_decision],
        }
    }
}

// ---------------- Demonstration ----------------

fn main() {
    // Mixed-constraint example (forces Phase I):
    //
    // maximize   3 x1 + 2 x2
    // subject to
    //   (1)  x1 +  x2 <= 4
    //   (2) 2x1 +  x2 >= 5
    //   (3)  x1 -  x2  = 1
    //   x1, x2 >= 0
    //
    // Expected optimum: x1 = 2.5, x2 = 1.5, objective = 10.5

    let lp = LpProblem {
        sense: ObjectiveSense::Maximize,
        c: vec![3.0, 2.0],
        constraints: vec![
            Constraint {
                a: vec![1.0, 1.0],
                sense: ConstraintSense::Leq,
                b: 4.0,
            },
            Constraint {
                a: vec![2.0, 1.0],
                sense: ConstraintSense::Geq,
                b: 5.0,
            },
            Constraint {
                a: vec![1.0, -1.0],
                sense: ConstraintSense::Eq,
                b: 1.0,
            },
        ],
    };

    let mut solver = Solver::new(&lp);
    let res = solver.solve(50_000, 50_000);

    println!("Status: {}", res.status);
    if res.status == SolveStatus::Optimal {
        println!("Objective value: {:.10}", res.objective_value);
        for (j, val) in res.x.iter().enumerate() {
            println!("x_{} = {:.10}", j + 1, val);
        }
    }
}
```

Program 10.11.2 demonstrates how the simplex method described in Section 10.11.2 can be implemented as an explicit sequence of reduced-cost evaluations, ratio tests, and pivot operations. Each iteration corresponds to a basis exchange, moving from one BFS to an adjacent BFS while maintaining feasibility and improving the objective. The use of the most-negative reduced cost rule reflects the classical simplex strategy of selecting an improving edge direction, while the minimum ratio test ensures that the path remains within the feasible polyhedron by enforcing the nonnegativity constraints.

The program also provides a complete realization of the standard-form conversion procedure of Section 10.11.3 and the two-phase simplex framework of Section 10.11.4. By introducing slack, surplus, and artificial variables, the solver can handle general constraint structures where an initial BFS is not immediately available. Phase I isolates feasibility by minimizing the artificial variables, and Phase II then optimizes the true objective from the feasible vertex obtained. This separation of feasibility and optimality reflects the conceptual clarity and robustness of the two-phase simplex approach.

Finally, the tableau pivot operations provide a transparent computational interpretation of the algebraic pivot mechanics discussed in Section 10.11.5. Although the basis inverse $A_B^{-1}$ is not formed explicitly, each pivot updates the tableau in a manner equivalent to maintaining the transformation $A_B^{-1}$ applied to the constraint system. The reduced costs stored in the objective row correspond to the values $u_j$ in (10.11.5), and the ratio test reflects the feasibility evolution described in (10.11.6). In this way, the program establishes a clear bridge between the geometric vertex interpretation of simplex and its linear-algebraic foundation, forming a natural basis for later discussion of sparse basis factorizations and industrial-scale simplex implementations.

## 10.11.6. Degeneracy, Cycling, and Anti-Cycling Rules

A degenerate basic feasible solution (BFS) is one in which at least one basic variable has value zero. From a geometric perspective, degeneracy occurs when the current solution lies at the intersection of more than $n$ active constraints in an $n$-dimensional decision space. In such cases, multiple bases correspond to the same vertex of the feasible polyhedron, and the simplex method may move between these bases without changing the location of the iterate in the primal space.

Degeneracy can lead to zero step lengths during pivot operations. Specifically, when the leaving variable is already zero, the ratio test produces a step of length zero, so the objective value does not improve even though the basis changes. While such pivots preserve feasibility, they do not advance the algorithm toward optimality. Repeated zero-length pivots increase the risk of cycling, a pathological situation in which the simplex method revisits the same sequence of bases indefinitely without making progress.

Cycling is a theoretical concern rather than a common practical problem, but it highlights subtle interactions between algebraic pivot rules and the geometry of the feasible region. In highly degenerate linear programs, especially those arising from certain network flow or combinatorial formulations, the simplex method may experience long sequences of degenerate pivots. Although these sequences eventually terminate in most practical cases, they can significantly slow convergence by producing many iterations with little or no objective improvement.

To eliminate the possibility of cycling, several anti-cycling rules have been developed. Bland’s rule is the most well-known example. It prescribes selecting the entering variable with the smallest index among all eligible candidates and, similarly, choosing the leaving variable with the smallest index that satisfies the ratio test. This deterministic ordering guarantees finite termination of the simplex algorithm, regardless of degeneracy. However, because it restricts the freedom to choose more aggressive improving directions, Bland’s rule can lead to slower convergence in practice.

For this reason, most modern simplex implementations do not use strict anti-cycling rules by default. Instead, they rely on heuristic pivot selection strategies that perform well on typical problems, combined with safeguards such as perturbations, lexicographic ordering, or occasional restarts to handle rare degenerate cases. The general consensus reflected in the text is that while cycling is extremely rare, degeneracy can still cause temporary stalling, with many pivots yielding minimal or no objective progress before the algorithm resumes meaningful improvement.

## 10.11.7. Efficiency, Sparsity, and Implementation Heuristics

From a computational perspective, each pivot involves solving linear systems associated with the basis matrix, that is, updating $A_B^{-1}$ or more commonly updating an LU factorization of $A_B$. Naively, this can be $O(n^3)$ per pivot, but practical implementations use factor updates and exploit sparsity to reduce total cost substantially.

In large sparse LPs, maintaining sparse factors is crucial. The Markowitz criterion is used as a heuristic pivot selection strategy in LU factorization to reduce fill-in, by preferring pivots that minimize the number of nonzeros introduced into the factors. Pricing strategies also matter: rather than computing all reduced costs at each pivot, partial pricing and multiple pricing evaluate subsets or blocks of variables to find entering candidates more efficiently.

The text notes that modern solvers may implement both primal simplex and dual simplex, and often also provide a barrier (interior-point) method as an alternative. Simplex remains highly competitive, especially for medium-size and even large instances, particularly when warm-starting from a previous solution or when an optimal basis is needed for sensitivity analysis. It cites a recent CACM article emphasizing simplex’s continued practical importance.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion of the simplex method in Sections 10.11.2–10.11.5, the analysis of degeneracy and cycling in Section 10.11.6, and the practical efficiency considerations introduced in Section 10.11.7, Program 10.11.3 provides a complete Rust implementation of the two-phase simplex algorithm using a tableau-based representation. While the fundamental simplex framework moves from one basic feasible solution (BFS) to another by pivoting along edges of the feasible polyhedron, real LP problems often exhibit degeneracy, in which multiple bases correspond to the same vertex and pivot steps may produce no improvement in the objective. This program therefore augments the standard pivot loop with explicit degeneracy detection, cycling safeguards, and optional anti-cycling pivot selection. In addition, it introduces a simplified form of partial pricing, reflecting how modern solvers reduce computational overhead by limiting reduced-cost scans when the number of variables becomes large. The result is a solver that not only implements the classical simplex steps but also demonstrates the practical mechanisms required to ensure robustness and predictable termination in degenerate cases.

The program begins by defining the core problem representation through the `LpProblem` and `Constraint` structs. These store the objective coefficients $\mathbf{c}$, the optimization sense (maximize or minimize), and a list of linear constraints. Each constraint includes its coefficient vector $a_i$, right-hand side $b_i$, and a `ConstraintSense` indicating whether the constraint is of type $\le$, $\ge$, or equality. This structure directly corresponds to the general LP formulation in (10.11.1) and supports the mixed constraint types that motivate the Phase I construction described in Section 10.11.4.

The conversion to standard form is implemented in the function `build_standard_form`, which follows the procedure described in Section 10.11.3. Constraints of the form $a_i^T x \le b_i$ are converted into equalities by introducing slack variables $s_i \ge 0$. Constraints of the form $a_i^T x \ge b_i$ are rewritten using surplus variables with coefficient $-1$, but because surplus variables do not yield an immediate identity basis, an artificial variable is also added. Equality constraints similarly receive an artificial variable. This construction produces a standard-form tableau in which the system can be expressed in the partitioned basis form $A_B x_B + A_N x_N = b$ of (10.11.2), where basic variables correspond to identity columns and nonbasic variables are set to zero at a BFS.

The tableau representation is stored in the `StandardForm` struct, which contains the full tableau matrix and a `basis` vector describing which variable is basic in each constraint row. At a BFS, all nonbasic variables satisfy $x_N = 0$, and the basic variable values are read directly from the right-hand side column. This is equivalent to computing $x_B = A_B^{-1}b$ as in (10.11.3), but without explicitly forming $A_B^{-1}$. Instead, the tableau is maintained by Gauss–Jordan elimination steps during pivot operations, so that each pivot implicitly updates the effect of multiplying by $A_B^{-1}$.

The main solver behavior is controlled through the `SimplexConfig` struct, which encapsulates parameters corresponding to the implementation concerns of Sections 10.11.6 and 10.11.7. In particular, it allows selection of a pivot rule through the `PivotRule` enum. Under `PivotRule::Dantzig`, the entering variable is chosen as the one with the most negative reduced cost, corresponding to the reduced-cost criterion $u_j$ defined in (10.11.5). Under `PivotRule::Bland`, the smallest-index eligible entering variable is selected, implementing Bland’s anti-cycling rule as described in Section 10.11.6. The configuration also supports optional partial pricing through `pricing_window`, reflecting the observation in Section 10.11.7 that scanning all reduced costs can be expensive in large problems. When partial pricing is enabled, the solver examines only a window of reduced costs at each iteration and shifts the window when no improving variable is found.

Phase I and Phase II are implemented within the `Solver` struct. The method `set_phase1_objective` constructs the auxiliary objective described in Section 10.11.4 by maximizing the negative sum of artificial variables, which is equivalent to minimizing their sum. This objective provides a direct feasibility measure: if the Phase I optimum is nonzero, then at least one artificial variable remains positive and the original LP is infeasible. After Phase I terminates successfully, `purge_artificials` removes the artificial-variable columns from the tableau and attempts to pivot artificial variables out of the basis if they remain basic. This step produces a feasible basis for the original constraints, which becomes the initialization for Phase II. The method `set_phase2_objective` then restores the true objective function and eliminates contributions from basic variables so that reduced costs are correctly expressed in the objective row.

The simplex pivot iteration itself is implemented in `run_simplex`, which directly mirrors the algorithmic outline of Section 10.11.2. At each iteration, the solver first chooses an entering variable by scanning reduced costs in the objective row. In the algebraic formulation of Section 10.11.5, these reduced costs correspond to the values $u_N$ in (10.11.5), and a negative reduced cost indicates that increasing the corresponding nonbasic variable decreases the objective in a minimization problem or improves it in the internal maximization representation. Once an entering variable is selected, the solver applies the minimum ratio test through `choose_leaving_row_with_step`, selecting the leaving row that produces the smallest admissible ratio $b_i/a_{ik}$. This implements the feasibility condition implied by (10.11.6), ensuring that increasing the entering variable does not drive any basic variable negative.

The tableau update is performed by the method `pivot`, which carries out the Gauss–Jordan elimination step that replaces the leaving basic variable with the entering variable. This is the explicit computational counterpart of updating the basis matrix $A_B$ and recomputing the effect of $A_B^{-1}$ on the system. Each pivot therefore generates a new BFS, consistent with the vertex-to-vertex movement described in Section 10.11.2.

To address the degeneracy issues discussed in Section 10.11.6, the solver explicitly monitors the step length returned by the ratio test. If the minimum ratio is zero, the pivot is classified as degenerate, meaning the algorithm changes basis but does not move to a new vertex in primal space. Such pivots are counted in the `degenerate_pivots` diagnostic. To safeguard against cycling, the solver also computes a compact hash signature of the basis using `basis_signature` and stores a history of recently encountered bases. If a repeated basis is detected, cycling is flagged and, if configured, the solver switches automatically to Bland’s rule. This reflects the theoretical guarantee described in Section 10.11.6: Bland’s deterministic ordering prevents infinite repetition of bases and ensures finite termination even in highly degenerate cases.

The `main` function demonstrates the full implementation on a mixed-constraint LP containing $\le$, $\ge$, and equality constraints, ensuring that slack, surplus, and artificial variables are all required. The printed diagnostics report not only the final solution and objective value but also the number of iterations, the number of degenerate pivots, the pivot rule ultimately used, and whether cycling was detected. These outputs provide direct computational evidence of the degeneracy and cycling concepts discussed in Section 10.11.6, while the optional partial pricing window illustrates the pricing heuristics introduced in Section 10.11.7.

```rust
// Program 10.11.3 (Two-Phase Simplex with Degeneracy Handling and Anti-Cycling Rules)
//
// Covers Sections 10.11.6–10.11.7.
//
// 10.11.6 Degeneracy, cycling, and anti-cycling rules
// - Detects degenerate pivots (zero step length from the ratio test).
// - Supports two pivot rules:
//     * Dantzig rule: entering variable has the most negative reduced cost.
//     * Bland rule: smallest-index entering and smallest-index leaving (anti-cycling).
// - Includes a safeguard against cycling by tracking basis signatures. If a repeat is detected,
//   the solver automatically switches to Bland's rule to guarantee termination.
//
// 10.11.7 Efficiency, sparsity, and implementation heuristics (pedagogical hooks)
// - Implements "partial pricing" as an optional heuristic: instead of scanning all reduced costs,
//   scan a window of columns and advance the window when no improving candidate is found.
// - The tableau is dense and therefore not a sparse, factor-update implementation. The code is
//   meant to make pivot mechanics explicit. The partial pricing hook mirrors how modern solvers
//   reduce work during pricing without changing the underlying simplex logic.
//
// Supported model:
//   maximize or minimize   c^T x
//   subject to             a_i^T x (<=, >=, =) b_i
//                          x >= 0
//
// Notes:
// - Phase I maximizes -sum(artificial), equivalent to minimizing sum(artificial).
// - Floating-point tolerances are used throughout. For textbook use, the defaults are conservative.

use std::collections::{HashSet, VecDeque};
use std::fmt;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum ObjectiveSense {
    Maximize,
    Minimize,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum ConstraintSense {
    Leq, // <=
    Geq, // >=
    Eq,  // =
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum SolveStatus {
    Optimal,
    Unbounded,
    Infeasible,
    IterationLimit,
}

impl fmt::Display for SolveStatus {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        match self {
            SolveStatus::Optimal => write!(f, "Optimal"),
            SolveStatus::Unbounded => write!(f, "Unbounded"),
            SolveStatus::Infeasible => write!(f, "Infeasible"),
            SolveStatus::IterationLimit => write!(f, "Iteration limit reached"),
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum PivotRule {
    /// Entering variable: most negative reduced cost. Leaving: minimum ratio test.
    Dantzig,
    /// Anti-cycling: smallest-index entering and smallest-index leaving among ratio ties.
    Bland,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Constraint {
    pub a: Vec<f64>,
    pub sense: ConstraintSense,
    pub b: f64,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct LpProblem {
    pub sense: ObjectiveSense,
    pub c: Vec<f64>,
    pub constraints: Vec<Constraint>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct SimplexResult {
    pub status: SolveStatus,
    pub objective_value: f64,
    pub x: Vec<f64>,
    // Diagnostics relevant to 10.11.6–10.11.7
    pub iterations: usize,
    pub degenerate_pivots: usize,
    pub rule_used: PivotRule,
    pub cycled: bool,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct StandardForm {
    // Variable order:
    //   [x_0..x_{n-1}, slack/surplus..., artificial...]
    n_decision: usize,
    is_artificial: Vec<bool>, // length = total_vars (excluding RHS)
    tableau: Vec<Vec<f64>>,   // (m+1) x (total_vars+1), last col is RHS
    basis: Vec<usize>,        // length m, basis for rows 1..m
}

// ---------------- Standard-form conversion (10.11.3) ----------------

fn build_standard_form(lp: &LpProblem, eps: f64) -> StandardForm {
    let n = lp.c.len();
    let m = lp.constraints.len();
    assert!(n > 0, "Need at least one decision variable.");
    assert!(m > 0, "Need at least one constraint.");

    // Normalize constraints so RHS is nonnegative, flipping inequalities when needed.
    let mut normalized: Vec<(Vec<f64>, ConstraintSense, f64)> = Vec::with_capacity(m);
    for cons in &lp.constraints {
        assert!(cons.a.len() == n, "Each constraint row must have length n.");
        let mut a = cons.a.clone();
        let mut sense = cons.sense;
        let mut b = cons.b;

        if b < -eps {
            for v in &mut a {
                *v = -*v;
            }
            b = -b;
            sense = match sense {
                ConstraintSense::Leq => ConstraintSense::Geq,
                ConstraintSense::Geq => ConstraintSense::Leq,
                ConstraintSense::Eq => ConstraintSense::Eq,
            };
        }
        normalized.push((a, sense, b));
    }

    // Count added variables.
    let mut num_slack_surplus = 0usize;
    let mut num_artificial = 0usize;
    for (_, sense, _) in &normalized {
        match sense {
            ConstraintSense::Leq => num_slack_surplus += 1, // slack
            ConstraintSense::Geq => {
                num_slack_surplus += 1; // surplus
                num_artificial += 1; // artificial
            }
            ConstraintSense::Eq => num_artificial += 1, // artificial
        }
    }

    let total_vars = n + num_slack_surplus + num_artificial;
    let cols = total_vars + 1;
    let mut tableau = vec![vec![0.0; cols]; m + 1];
    let mut is_artificial = vec![false; total_vars];
    let mut basis = vec![usize::MAX; m];

    let mut slack_cursor = n;
    let mut art_cursor = n + num_slack_surplus;

    for (i, (a, sense, b)) in normalized.iter().enumerate() {
        let row = i + 1;

        for j in 0..n {
            tableau[row][j] = a[j];
        }
        tableau[row][cols - 1] = *b;

        match sense {
            ConstraintSense::Leq => {
                // a x + s = b
                let s = slack_cursor;
                slack_cursor += 1;
                tableau[row][s] = 1.0;
                basis[i] = s;
            }
            ConstraintSense::Geq => {
                // a x - s + a_art = b
                let s = slack_cursor;
                slack_cursor += 1;
                tableau[row][s] = -1.0;

                let a_var = art_cursor;
                art_cursor += 1;
                tableau[row][a_var] = 1.0;
                is_artificial[a_var] = true;
                basis[i] = a_var;
            }
            ConstraintSense::Eq => {
                // a x + a_art = b
                let a_var = art_cursor;
                art_cursor += 1;
                tableau[row][a_var] = 1.0;
                is_artificial[a_var] = true;
                basis[i] = a_var;
            }
        }
    }

    StandardForm {
        n_decision: n,
        is_artificial,
        tableau,
        basis,
    }
}

// ---------------- Two-phase simplex core ----------------

pub struct SimplexConfig {
    pub pivot_rule: PivotRule,
    /// If Some(k), use partial pricing with a window of k columns for entering-variable search.
    pub pricing_window: Option<usize>,
    /// Maximum number of basis signatures to remember for cycling detection.
    pub cycle_memory: usize,
    /// If true, when a repeated basis is detected, switch to Bland's rule.
    pub switch_to_bland_on_cycle: bool,
    /// Tolerances
    pub eps: f64,
    pub ratio_eps: f64,
    pub feasibility_tol: f64,
}

impl Default for SimplexConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            pivot_rule: PivotRule::Dantzig,
            pricing_window: None, // full pricing by default
            cycle_memory: 50_000,
            switch_to_bland_on_cycle: true,
            eps: 1e-12,
            ratio_eps: 1e-12,
            feasibility_tol: 1e-8,
        }
    }
}

pub struct Solver {
    orig_sense: ObjectiveSense,
```
