```rust
    c_max: Vec<f64>,
    sf: StandardForm,
    cfg: SimplexConfig,

    // Diagnostics
    iterations: usize,
    degenerate_pivots: usize,
    rule_used: PivotRule,
    cycled: bool,

    // Pricing state
    pricing_start: usize,

    // Cycling detection
    basis_seen: HashSet<u64>,
    basis_fifo: VecDeque<u64>,
}

impl Solver {
    pub fn new(lp: &LpProblem, cfg: SimplexConfig) -> Self {
        let mut c_max = lp.c.clone();
        if lp.sense == ObjectiveSense::Minimize {
            for v in &mut c_max {
                *v = -*v;
            }
        }

        // Save pivot rule BEFORE moving cfg into the struct.
        let rule_used = cfg.pivot_rule;

        let sf = build_standard_form(lp, cfg.eps);

        Self {
            orig_sense: lp.sense,
            c_max,
            sf,
            cfg,
            iterations: 0,
            degenerate_pivots: 0,
            rule_used,
            cycled: false,
            pricing_start: 0,
            basis_seen: HashSet::new(),
            basis_fifo: VecDeque::new(),
        }
    }

    pub fn solve(&mut self, max_iters_phase1: usize, max_iters_phase2: usize) -> SimplexResult {
        // Phase I
        self.set_phase1_objective();
        let st1 = self.run_simplex(max_iters_phase1);
        if st1 != SolveStatus::Optimal {
            return self.finish(st1);
        }

        // Feasibility test: Phase I objective should be ~ 0.
        let v1 = self.objective_value();
        if v1.abs() > self.cfg.feasibility_tol {
            return self.finish(SolveStatus::Infeasible);
        }

        // Phase II
        self.purge_artificials();
        self.set_phase2_objective();
        let st2 = self.run_simplex(max_iters_phase2);

        self.finish(st2)
    }

    // ---------------- Objective and extraction ----------------

    fn objective_value(&self) -> f64 {
        let rhs_col = self.sf.tableau[0].len() - 1;
        self.sf.tableau[0][rhs_col]
    }

    fn extract_solution(&self) -> (Vec<f64>, f64) {
        let cols = self.sf.tableau[0].len();
        let rhs_col = cols - 1;

        let mut x = vec![0.0; self.sf.n_decision];
        for row in 1..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            let basic = self.sf.basis[row - 1];
            if basic < self.sf.n_decision {
                x[basic] = self.sf.tableau[row][rhs_col];
            }
        }

        let mut obj = self.sf.tableau[0][rhs_col];
        if self.orig_sense == ObjectiveSense::Minimize {
            obj = -obj;
        }

        (x, obj)
    }

    fn finish(&self, status: SolveStatus) -> SimplexResult {
        let (x, obj) = self.extract_solution();
        SimplexResult {
            status,
            objective_value: obj,
            x,
            iterations: self.iterations,
            degenerate_pivots: self.degenerate_pivots,
            rule_used: self.rule_used,
            cycled: self.cycled,
        }
    }

    // ---------------- Phase I / Phase II setup (10.11.4) ----------------

    fn set_phase1_objective(&mut self) {
        // Phase I objective: maximize -sum(artificial).
        let m = self.sf.tableau.len() - 1;
        let cols = self.sf.tableau[0].len();

        for j in 0..cols {
            self.sf.tableau[0][j] = 0.0;
        }
        for j in 0..(cols - 1) {
            if self.sf.is_artificial[j] {
                self.sf.tableau[0][j] = 1.0;
            }
        }

        // Make objective row consistent with current basis.
        for row in 1..=m {
            let basic = self.sf.basis[row - 1];
            if basic < self.sf.is_artificial.len() && self.sf.is_artificial[basic] {
                for j in 0..cols {
                    self.sf.tableau[0][j] -= self.sf.tableau[row][j];
                }
            }
        }

        self.pricing_start = 0;
    }

    fn set_phase2_objective(&mut self) {
        // Restore original (internal MAX-form) objective: maximize c_max^T x.
        let cols = self.sf.tableau[0].len();
        let rhs_col = cols - 1;

        for j in 0..cols {
            self.sf.tableau[0][j] = 0.0;
        }
        for j in 0..self.sf.n_decision {
            self.sf.tableau[0][j] = -self.c_max[j];
        }

        // Eliminate basic decision vars from objective row.
        for row in 1..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            let basic = self.sf.basis[row - 1];
            if basic < self.sf.n_decision {
                let c_k = self.c_max[basic];
                if c_k.abs() > self.cfg.eps {
                    for j in 0..cols {
                        self.sf.tableau[0][j] += c_k * self.sf.tableau[row][j];
                    }
                    self.sf.tableau[0][rhs_col] += 0.0; // explicit no-op
                }
            }
        }

        self.pricing_start = 0;
    }

    fn purge_artificials(&mut self) {
        // Pivot out basic artificial variables where possible, then drop artificial columns.
        let total_vars = self.sf.is_artificial.len();
        let cols = total_vars + 1;
        let rhs_col = cols - 1;

        // Pivot artificials out of basis if they remain basic.
        for row in 1..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            let basic = self.sf.basis[row - 1];
            if basic < total_vars && self.sf.is_artificial[basic] {
                let mut pivot_col: Option<usize> = None;
                for j in 0..total_vars {
                    if !self.sf.is_artificial[j] && self.sf.tableau[row][j].abs() > self.cfg.eps {
                        pivot_col = Some(j);
                        break;
                    }
                }
                if let Some(pc) = pivot_col {
                    self.pivot(row, pc);
                    self.sf.basis[row - 1] = pc;
                } else {
                    let _rhs = self.sf.tableau[row][rhs_col];
                }
            }
        }

        // Keep non-artificial columns.
        let mut keep_cols: Vec<usize> = Vec::new();
        for j in 0..total_vars {
            if !self.sf.is_artificial[j] {
                keep_cols.push(j);
            }
        }

        let new_total_vars = keep_cols.len();
        let new_cols = new_total_vars + 1;

        let mut new_tableau = vec![vec![0.0; new_cols]; self.sf.tableau.len()];
        for i in 0..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            for (new_j, &old_j) in keep_cols.iter().enumerate() {
                new_tableau[i][new_j] = self.sf.tableau[i][old_j];
            }
            new_tableau[i][new_cols - 1] = self.sf.tableau[i][rhs_col];
        }

        let mut old_to_new = vec![None; total_vars];
        for (new_j, &old_j) in keep_cols.iter().enumerate() {
            old_to_new[old_j] = Some(new_j);
        }

        for i in 0..self.sf.basis.len() {
            let old_basic = self.sf.basis[i];
            self.sf.basis[i] = old_to_new[old_basic].unwrap_or(usize::MAX);
        }

        self.sf.tableau = new_tableau;
        self.sf.is_artificial = vec![false; new_total_vars];

        // Clear cycling memory since columns changed.
        self.basis_seen.clear();
        self.basis_fifo.clear();
    }

    // ---------------- Simplex iteration (10.11.2, 10.11.5, 10.11.6) ----------------

    fn run_simplex(&mut self, max_iters: usize) -> SolveStatus {
        for _ in 0..max_iters {
            self.iterations += 1;

            // Cycling detection uses a basis signature (hash) of the current basis vector.
            if self.cfg.cycle_memory > 0 {
                let sig = basis_signature(&self.sf.basis);
                if self.basis_seen.contains(&sig) {
                    self.cycled = true;
                    if self.cfg.switch_to_bland_on_cycle {
                        self.rule_used = PivotRule::Bland;
                    }
                } else {
                    self.basis_seen.insert(sig);
                    self.basis_fifo.push_back(sig);
                    if self.basis_fifo.len() > self.cfg.cycle_memory {
                        if let Some(old) = self.basis_fifo.pop_front() {
                            self.basis_seen.remove(&old);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }

            // Entering variable
            let entering = match self.choose_entering_variable() {
                Some(j) => j,
                None => return SolveStatus::Optimal,
            };

            // Leaving variable + step length (ratio)
            let (leaving_row, step) = match self.choose_leaving_row_with_step(entering) {
                Some(v) => v,
                None => return SolveStatus::Unbounded,
            };

            // Degeneracy detection: step length == 0 implies a degenerate pivot.
            if step.abs() <= self.cfg.feasibility_tol {
                self.degenerate_pivots += 1;
                if self.cycled && self.cfg.switch_to_bland_on_cycle {
                    self.rule_used = PivotRule::Bland;
                }
            }

            self.pivot(leaving_row, entering);
            self.sf.basis[leaving_row - 1] = entering;
        }
        SolveStatus::IterationLimit
    }

    fn choose_entering_variable(&mut self) -> Option<usize> {
        let rhs_col = self.sf.tableau[0].len() - 1;

        match self.rule_used {
            PivotRule::Bland => {
                // Smallest-index reduced cost < 0.
                for j in 0..rhs_col {
                    if self.sf.tableau[0][j] < -self.cfg.eps {
                        return Some(j);
                    }
                }
                None
            }
            PivotRule::Dantzig => {
                if let Some(win) = self.cfg.pricing_window {
                    self.choose_entering_dantzig_partial(rhs_col, win)
                } else {
                    self.choose_entering_dantzig_full(rhs_col)
                }
            }
        }
    }

    fn choose_entering_dantzig_full(&self, rhs_col: usize) -> Option<usize> {
        let mut best_j: Option<usize> = None;
        let mut best_val: f64 = 0.0;

        for j in 0..rhs_col {
            let rc = self.sf.tableau[0][j];
            if rc < -self.cfg.eps {
                match best_j {
                    None => {
                        best_j = Some(j);
                        best_val = rc;
                    }
                    Some(cur) => {
                        if rc < best_val - 1e-15 || ((rc - best_val).abs() <= 1e-15 && j < cur) {
                            best_j = Some(j);
                            best_val = rc;
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        best_j
    }

    fn choose_entering_dantzig_partial(&mut self, rhs_col: usize, win: usize) -> Option<usize> {
        if rhs_col == 0 {
            return None;
        }
        let win = win.clamp(1, rhs_col);

        let mut attempts = 0usize;
        while attempts * win < rhs_col + win {
            let start = self.pricing_start % rhs_col;
            let end = (start + win).min(rhs_col);

            let mut best_j: Option<usize> = None;
            let mut best_val: f64 = 0.0;

            for j in start..end {
                let rc = self.sf.tableau[0][j];
                if rc < -self.cfg.eps {
                    match best_j {
                        None => {
                            best_j = Some(j);
                            best_val = rc;
                        }
                        Some(cur) => {
                            if rc < best_val - 1e-15 || ((rc - best_val).abs() <= 1e-15 && j < cur)
                            {
                                best_j = Some(j);
                                best_val = rc;
                            }
                        }
                    }
                }
            }

            if best_j.is_some() {
                self.pricing_start = end % rhs_col;
                return best_j;
            }

            self.pricing_start = end % rhs_col;
            attempts += 1;
        }

        None
    }

    fn choose_leaving_row_with_step(&self, entering: usize) -> Option<(usize, f64)> {
        let rhs_col = self.sf.tableau[0].len() - 1;

        let mut best_row: Option<usize> = None;
        let mut best_ratio: f64 = f64::INFINITY;

        for row in 1..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            let a = self.sf.tableau[row][entering];
            if a > self.cfg.ratio_eps {
                let rhs = self.sf.tableau[row][rhs_col];
                let ratio = rhs / a;

                match self.rule_used {
                    PivotRule::Bland => {
                        if ratio < best_ratio - 1e-15 {
                            best_ratio = ratio;
                            best_row = Some(row);
                        } else if (ratio - best_ratio).abs() <= 1e-15 {
                            let cur_row = best_row.unwrap();
                            let basic_cur = self.sf.basis[cur_row - 1];
                            let basic_new = self.sf.basis[row - 1];
                            if basic_new < basic_cur {
                                best_ratio = ratio;
                                best_row = Some(row);
                            }
                        }
                    }
                    PivotRule::Dantzig => {
                        if ratio < best_ratio - 1e-15
                            || ((ratio - best_ratio).abs() <= 1e-15
                                && best_row.map_or(true, |r| row < r))
                        {
                            best_ratio = ratio;
                            best_row = Some(row);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        best_row.map(|r| (r, best_ratio))
    }

    fn pivot(&mut self, pivot_row: usize, pivot_col: usize) {
        let cols = self.sf.tableau[0].len();
        let pv = self.sf.tableau[pivot_row][pivot_col];

        // Normalize pivot row
        for j in 0..cols {
            self.sf.tableau[pivot_row][j] /= pv;
        }

        // Eliminate pivot column from all other rows (including objective row).
        for i in 0..self.sf.tableau.len() {
            if i == pivot_row {
                continue;
            }
            let factor = self.sf.tableau[i][pivot_col];
            if factor.abs() <= self.cfg.eps {
                continue;
            }
            for j in 0..cols {
                self.sf.tableau[i][j] -= factor * self.sf.tableau[pivot_row][j];
            }
            self.sf.tableau[i][pivot_col] = 0.0;
        }
    }
}

// Basis signature for cycling detection (stable, lightweight).
fn basis_signature(basis: &[usize]) -> u64 {
    let mut h: u64 = 1469598103934665603; // FNV offset basis
    for &v in basis {
        h ^= v as u64;
        h = h.wrapping_mul(1099511628211);
    }
    h
}

// ---------------- Demonstration ----------------

fn main() {
    // Mixed-constraint example (forces Phase I):
    //
    // maximize   3 x1 + 2 x2
    // subject to
    //   (1)  x1 +  x2 <= 4
    //   (2) 2x1 +  x2 >= 5
    //   (3)  x1 -  x2  = 1
    //   x1, x2 >= 0
    //
    // Expected optimum: x1 = 2.5, x2 = 1.5, objective = 10.5

    let lp = LpProblem {
        sense: ObjectiveSense::Maximize,
        c: vec![3.0, 2.0],
        constraints: vec![
            Constraint {
                a: vec![1.0, 1.0],
                sense: ConstraintSense::Leq,
                b: 4.0,
            },
            Constraint {
                a: vec![2.0, 1.0],
                sense: ConstraintSense::Geq,
                b: 5.0,
            },
            Constraint {
                a: vec![1.0, -1.0],
                sense: ConstraintSense::Eq,
                b: 1.0,
            },
        ],
    };

    // Configure:
    // - Use Dantzig rule initially.
    // - Enable partial pricing window (10.11.7 heuristic hook).
    // - Switch to Bland automatically if cycling is detected (10.11.6 safeguard).
    let mut cfg = SimplexConfig::default();
    cfg.pivot_rule = PivotRule::Dantzig;
    cfg.pricing_window = Some(8);
    cfg.switch_to_bland_on_cycle = true;

    let mut solver = Solver::new(&lp, cfg);
    let res = solver.solve(50_000, 50_000);

    println!("Status: {}", res.status);
    if res.status == SolveStatus::Optimal {
        println!("Objective value: {:.10}", res.objective_value);
        for (j, val) in res.x.iter().enumerate() {
            println!("x_{} = {:.10}", j + 1, val);
        }
    } else {
        println!("Objective value: {:.10}", res.objective_value);
    }

    // Diagnostics relevant to 10.11.6–10.11.7
    println!("Iterations: {}", res.iterations);
    println!("Degenerate pivots: {}", res.degenerate_pivots);
    println!("Pivot rule used: {:?}", res.rule_used);
    println!("Cycling detected: {}", res.cycled);
}
```

Program 10.11.3 demonstrates how the simplex method can be extended beyond its basic pivot framework to address the practical complications of degeneracy and cycling discussed in Section 10.11.6. Although simplex theory guarantees that optimal solutions occur at vertices, degenerate vertices may correspond to multiple bases, allowing the algorithm to pivot without changing the primal solution. By explicitly detecting zero-length pivots through the ratio test and recording degenerate pivot counts, the program illustrates how degeneracy can lead to temporary stalling even when feasibility is preserved.

The implementation also incorporates a robust anti-cycling safeguard by monitoring repeated bases and switching automatically to Bland’s rule when cycling is detected. This directly reflects the theoretical role of Bland’s ordering in guaranteeing termination. While Dantzig’s rule often converges faster in typical cases, the availability of Bland’s rule provides a reliable fallback for pathological degenerate instances, demonstrating how practical simplex solvers balance aggressive improvement strategies with termination guarantees.

Finally, the inclusion of partial pricing provides a simplified demonstration of the efficiency heuristics described in Section 10.11.7. Rather than recomputing reduced costs across all variables at every iteration, partial pricing restricts attention to a window of candidates, reducing per-iteration work and mirroring the pricing strategies used in large-scale solvers. Although the tableau approach remains dense and does not exploit sparsity or basis factorization updates, the modular design of the solver makes it a natural foundation for later extensions toward sparse LU factor maintenance, Markowitz-style pivot selection, and more advanced simplex variants used in modern optimization software.

## 10.11.8. Advanced Features in Professional Solvers

Professional simplex solvers incorporate a wide range of enhancements that go well beyond the basic textbook algorithm, reflecting decades of accumulated theoretical insight and practical engineering. One of the most important components is preprocessing, often referred to as *presolve*. During this stage, the solver analyzes the constraint system to identify and eliminate redundancies, detect fixed or implied variable bounds, remove linearly dependent constraints, and simplify the overall problem structure. Presolve routines frequently reduce problem size dramatically before any simplex iterations begin, which can have a decisive impact on performance for large-scale applications (Walaa, 2023).

Numerical scaling is another critical feature integrated into modern solvers. Poorly scaled constraint matrices can lead to large disparities in coefficient magnitudes, amplifying floating-point errors during pivot operations. By rescaling rows and columns to comparable ranges, solvers improve numerical conditioning and reduce the likelihood of instability, infeasible pivots, or premature termination. These techniques are essential for robust performance on real-world data, where coefficients often arise from heterogeneous physical or economic units.

Beyond preprocessing, professional solvers implement multiple pivot and pricing strategies and may switch between them dynamically as the algorithm progresses. Dantzig’s original pricing rule selects the entering variable with the most negative reduced cost, maximizing immediate objective improvement per unit change in the variable. While conceptually simple, this rule does not account for the geometry of the feasible region and may lead to inefficient progress in high-dimensional or ill-conditioned problems.

To address this limitation, more advanced pricing rules such as Devex and steepest-edge pricing are widely used. These methods attempt to approximate the true improvement in the objective per pivot step by incorporating estimates of edge lengths in the transformed space defined by the current basis. Steepest-edge pricing, in particular, uses information related to the norms of the columns of the inverse basis matrix to favor directions that yield larger reductions in the objective function. Devex pricing offers a computationally cheaper approximation to steepest-edge methods, striking a balance between accuracy and per-iteration cost. These heuristics are central to the efficiency of simplex on large-scale problems and are a key reason why modern solvers vastly outperform naive implementations.

Finally, contemporary linear programming solvers increasingly combine classical simplex technology with complementary algorithmic frameworks. Interior-point methods are often used to obtain high-quality starting points, after which simplex is applied for final refinement and exact optimality. Recent surveys emphasize that this hybrid strategy remains dominant in practice, even as new large-scale methods continue to emerge (Gondzio, 2025). At the same time, advances in large-scale optimization, including parallel and first-order approaches, highlight the ongoing evolution of LP solvers in response to growing problem sizes and modern computing architectures (Lu and Applegate, 2024).

Taken together, these advanced features explain why professional simplex solvers remain highly competitive and widely used. Far from being a static algorithm, simplex has evolved into a sophisticated framework that integrates preprocessing, numerical safeguards, geometric heuristics, and hybrid strategies to deliver reliable performance on problems of unprecedented scale.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion of degeneracy and cycling behavior in Section 10.11.6 and the computational efficiency considerations of sparse pivoting and pricing strategies in Section 10.11.7, Program 10.11.4 presents a compact Rust implementation that extends the basic two-phase simplex method with several features characteristic of professional solvers. While the core simplex mechanism remains a sequence of reduced-cost tests, ratio tests, and tableau pivots, practical large-scale performance depends heavily on preprocessing, numerical conditioning, and more sophisticated entering-variable selection rules. This program therefore incorporates a lightweight presolve stage, numerical scaling to improve coefficient conditioning, and multiple pricing heuristics that approximate the geometric effectiveness of candidate pivot directions. The resulting implementation demonstrates how the classical simplex algorithm evolves into a more robust solver framework when confronted with real-world LP models.

The program begins by defining the fundamental LP representation through the `Lp` and `Constraint` structs. These store the objective coefficients $\mathbf{c}$, the optimization sense, and a list of constraints with relations $\le$, $\ge$, or equality. This directly matches the general LP formulation in (10.11.1) and provides the flexibility required for mixed constraint systems. The `Config` struct then defines solver parameters, including pivot rules, pricing heuristics, feasibility tolerances, and cycling safeguards. This separation between problem data and solver configuration reflects how professional solvers are typically structured, enabling different numerical strategies to be selected without changing the core simplex machinery.

The first enhancement corresponding to Section 10.11.8 is implemented in the presolve routine `presolve_empty_rows`. This function detects trivial constraints whose coefficient vector is essentially zero. Such constraints either contribute nothing to the feasible set, for example $0 \le b$ or $0 = 0$, or they immediately imply infeasibility, for example $0 \ge b$ with $b > 0$. By removing redundant rows before simplex begins, the program reduces tableau size and eliminates constraints that would otherwise waste pivot iterations. The presolve stage in this compact implementation is intentionally minimal, but it demonstrates the key principle emphasized in Section 10.11.8: reducing problem size prior to optimization can have a decisive effect on overall performance.

A second important enhancement is numerical scaling, implemented by the function `scale_lp`. Poorly scaled LP data can produce large disparities in coefficient magnitudes, increasing floating-point roundoff during pivot operations. The scaling procedure in this program performs column scaling on the decision variables by computing the maximum absolute coefficient in each column and rescaling so that the largest entry is of order one. This mirrors the scaling strategies discussed in Section 10.11.8, where solvers attempt to improve numerical conditioning before factor updates or tableau pivots occur. The scaling factors are stored in the `Scaling` struct, allowing the solver to recover the solution in the original variable units after the scaled simplex computation terminates.

The standard-form conversion described in Section 10.11.3 is implemented in `build_standard_form`. This function converts all constraints into equalities with nonnegative variables by introducing slack variables for $\le$ constraints and surplus variables for $\ge$ constraints. Since surplus variables alone do not generate an identity basis column, $\ge$ and equality constraints also receive artificial variables, producing a tableau compatible with the two-phase approach described in Section 10.11.4. The resulting tableau representation is stored in the `SF` struct, which contains the tableau matrix, a basis index array, and a marker vector `is_art` that identifies artificial columns. This data structure encodes the standard-form decomposition $A_B x_B + A_N x_N = b$ in (10.11.2) and supports BFS extraction by treating nonbasic variables as zero.

Phase I is implemented through the method `set_phase1`, which constructs the auxiliary objective described in Section 10.11.4. Artificial variables are assigned a cost contribution so that the Phase I objective is equivalent to minimizing their sum. The method then eliminates contributions from artificial variables currently in the basis by subtracting their constraint rows from the objective row, ensuring that reduced costs are computed correctly. The simplex iteration is then performed by `run_simplex`, and feasibility is assessed by checking whether the Phase I objective value is zero within a prescribed tolerance. If the Phase I optimum is positive, the original LP is infeasible, matching the feasibility criterion described in Section 10.11.4.

If Phase I succeeds, the method `purge_artificials` removes artificial variables and their columns from the tableau and attempts to pivot artificial variables out of the basis if they remain basic. This produces a valid BFS for the original constraints and initializes Phase II. The Phase II objective is restored by `set_phase2`, which inserts the true objective coefficients into the tableau and eliminates contributions from basic decision variables so that the reduced costs in the objective row correspond to the reduced-cost expression $u_N$ in (10.11.5). The solver then continues simplex iterations exactly as in Phase II of the standard two-phase algorithm.

The core simplex pivot loop is implemented in `run_simplex` and follows the outline of Section 10.11.2. At each iteration, the entering variable is selected by `choose_entering`, which evaluates reduced costs stored in the objective row. If all reduced costs satisfy the optimality condition, the solver terminates with an optimal BFS. Otherwise, the leaving row is determined by `choose_leaving`, which performs the minimum ratio test, ensuring feasibility as described by the step relation (10.11.6). The basis update itself is performed by the `pivot` method, which carries out a Gauss–Jordan elimination step. This tableau update corresponds algebraically to updating the implicit effect of $A_B^{-1}$ on the constraint system, yielding a new BFS consistent with (10.11.3).

To reflect the pricing and pivot-selection enhancements discussed in Sections 10.11.7 and 10.11.8, the solver supports multiple pricing strategies through the `Pricing` enum. Under `Pricing::Dantzig`, the entering variable is selected as the one with the most negative reduced cost, corresponding to Dantzig’s classical pricing rule. Under `Pricing::Devex`, the reduced cost is scaled by a dynamically updated weight vector `devex_w`, implemented in `devex_update`, which approximates the behavior of Devex pricing by penalizing directions likely to produce weak pivots. Under `Pricing::SteepestEdge`, the reduced cost is normalized by a proxy for the edge length computed from the tableau column norm in `col_norm2`, providing a simplified steepest-edge heuristic. While these approximations do not compute exact steepest-edge norms based on $A_B^{-1}$, they capture the essential idea emphasized in Section 10.11.8: improved pricing rules attempt to estimate objective improvement relative to the geometry of the feasible region rather than relying solely on reduced-cost magnitude.

The program also includes a simplified partial pricing mechanism implemented in `scan_range`. Instead of scanning all reduced costs at every pivot, the solver optionally inspects only a fixed-size window of columns and shifts the window forward across iterations. This reflects the partial and multiple pricing strategies described in Section 10.11.7, where solvers reduce computational overhead by limiting reduced-cost evaluation. The solver records diagnostic information such as the number of degenerate pivots, the pivot rule used, the pricing rule used, and whether cycling was detected. Cycling safeguards are implemented by hashing the basis indices using `sig` and tracking previously visited bases. If a repeated basis is detected, the solver optionally switches to Bland’s rule, reflecting the anti-cycling guarantee described in Section 10.11.6.

Finally, the `main` function provides a demonstration LP with mixed constraint types, forcing the solver to execute Phase I and Phase II. The output prints the final decision variables, the objective value, and a diagnostic summary including iteration counts, degeneracy, and cycling detection. This example illustrates how presolve, scaling, and advanced pricing heuristics integrate into the simplex framework while preserving the fundamental pivot mechanics described in Sections 10.11.2–10.11.5.

```rust
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Program 10.11.4
// Two-Phase Simplex Method with Presolve, Scaling, and Advanced Pricing Heuristics
//
// Problem Statement
// -----------------
// This program implements a compact tableau-based simplex solver for linear
// programming problems of the form (10.11.1), including mixed constraint types.
//
// We consider linear programs with decision variables x_1,...,x_n and constraints
// of type <=, >=, and equality:
//
//     maximize or minimize    ζ = c^T x
//     subject to             A x (<=, >=, =) b
//                            x >= 0
//
// The solver follows the conceptual simplex outline of Section 10.11.2:
//
//   1. Initialization at a basic feasible solution (BFS). If no BFS is available,
//      the solver constructs one using a Phase I auxiliary problem.
//   2. Pivot iteration: reduced costs are used to select an entering variable.
//   3. Ratio test: feasibility is preserved by selecting a leaving variable using
//      the minimum ratio rule.
//   4. Basis update: a Gauss–Jordan pivot updates the tableau to obtain a new BFS.
//   5. Termination: when no negative reduced costs remain, the BFS is optimal.
//
// The implementation supports the standard-form conversion of Section 10.11.3 by
// introducing slack variables for <= constraints and surplus variables for >=
// constraints. Equality and >= constraints may require artificial variables, which
// motivates the two-phase simplex method described in Section 10.11.4.
//
// Advanced features inspired by Section 10.11.8 are also included:
//
//   • Presolve: remove trivial redundant constraints such as 0 <= b or 0 = 0.
//   • Scaling: rescale columns to reduce coefficient magnitude disparities.
//   • Pricing heuristics: Dantzig, Devex, and Steepest-Edge style scoring.
//   • Partial pricing: optionally scan reduced costs over a moving window.
//   • Degeneracy detection: count zero-step pivots produced by the ratio test.
//   • Cycling safeguards: detect repeated bases and optionally switch to Bland’s rule.
//
// A mixed-constraint test LP is included in main(), forcing Phase I feasibility
// resolution before Phase II optimization, demonstrating the full solver pipeline.
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

use std::collections::{HashSet, VecDeque};

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum Sense {
    Max,
    Min,
}
#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum Rel {
    Le,
    Ge,
    Eq,
}
#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum Status {
    Optimal,
    Unbounded,
    Infeasible,
    IterLimit,
    PresolveInfeasible,
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum PivotRule {
    Dantzig,
    Bland, // anti-cycling
}
#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
#[allow(dead_code)]
enum Pricing {
    Dantzig,
    Devex,
    SteepestEdge,
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
struct Constraint {
    a: Vec<f64>,
    rel: Rel,
    b: f64,
}
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
struct Lp {
    sense: Sense,
    c: Vec<f64>,
    cons: Vec<Constraint>,
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
struct Config {
    pivot_rule: PivotRule,
    pricing: Pricing,
    partial_window: Option<usize>, // partial pricing window
    eps: f64,
    ratio_eps: f64,
    feas_tol: f64,
    cycle_memory: usize,
    switch_to_bland_on_cycle: bool,
    enable_presolve: bool,
    enable_scaling: bool,
}
impl Default for Config {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            pivot_rule: PivotRule::Dantzig,
            pricing: Pricing::Dantzig,
            partial_window: None,
            eps: 1e-12,
            ratio_eps: 1e-12,
            feas_tol: 1e-8,
            cycle_memory: 50_000,
            switch_to_bland_on_cycle: true,
            enable_presolve: true,
            enable_scaling: true,
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug)]
struct ResultReport {
    status: Status,
    obj: f64,
    x: Vec<f64>,
    iters: usize,
    degenerate: usize,
    pivot_rule_used: PivotRule,
    pricing_used: Pricing,
    cycling: bool,
    presolve_removed_rows: usize,
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
struct Scaling {
    col_gamma: Vec<f64>, // x_orig = gamma * x_scaled
}

#[derive(Debug)]
struct SF {
    n_dec: usize,
    is_art: Vec<bool>,
    tab: Vec<Vec<f64>>, // (m+1) x (vars+1), last col RHS
    basis: Vec<usize>,  // length m
}

struct Solver {
    cfg: Config,
    orig_sense: Sense,
    c_max: Vec<f64>, // internal maximize form (after scaling/presolve)
    scaling: Option<Scaling>,
    obj_shift: f64, // presolve constant shift (kept minimal: 0 in this short version)
    sf: SF,

    iters: usize,
    degenerate: usize,
    pivot_rule_used: PivotRule,
    pricing_used: Pricing,
    cycling: bool,
    presolve_removed_rows: usize,

    pricing_start: usize,
    devex_w: Vec<f64>,
    seen: HashSet<u64>,
    fifo: VecDeque<u64>,
}

fn presolve_empty_rows(mut lp: Lp, eps: f64) -> Result<(Lp, usize), Status> {
    let mut kept = Vec::with_capacity(lp.cons.len());
    let mut removed = 0usize;

    for c in lp.cons.into_iter() {
        let max_abs = c.a.iter().fold(0.0_f64, |m, &v| m.max(v.abs()));
        if max_abs <= eps {
            // 0 rel b
            match c.rel {
                Rel::Le => {
                    if 0.0 <= c.b + eps {
                        removed += 1;
                        continue;
                    } else {
                        return Err(Status::PresolveInfeasible);
                    }
                }
                Rel::Ge => {
                    if 0.0 + eps >= c.b {
                        removed += 1;
                        continue;
                    } else {
                        return Err(Status::PresolveInfeasible);
                    }
                }
                Rel::Eq => {
                    if c.b.abs() <= eps {
                        removed += 1;
                        continue;
                    } else {
                        return Err(Status::PresolveInfeasible);
                    }
                }
            }
        } else {
            kept.push(c);
        }
    }
    lp.cons = kept;
    Ok((lp, removed))
}

fn scale_lp(mut lp: Lp, eps: f64) -> (Lp, Scaling) {
    let n = lp.c.len();
    // Column scaling by max abs in each decision-variable column (row scaling omitted to keep short).
    let mut gamma = vec![1.0_f64; n];
    for j in 0..n {
        let mut mx: f64 = 0.0_f64;
        for c in lp.cons.iter() {
            mx = mx.max(c.a[j].abs());
        }
        if mx > eps {
            gamma[j] = 1.0 / mx;
        }
    }
    for c in lp.cons.iter_mut() {
        for j in 0..n {
            c.a[j] *= gamma[j];
        }
    }
    for j in 0..n {
        lp.c[j] *= gamma[j];
    }
    (lp, Scaling { col_gamma: gamma })
}

fn build_standard_form(lp: &Lp, eps: f64) -> SF {
    let n = lp.c.len();
    let m = lp.cons.len();
    assert!(m > 0 && n > 0);

    // Normalize b>=0 by multiplying row by -1 (flip inequality if needed).
    let mut rows: Vec<(Vec<f64>, Rel, f64)> = Vec::with_capacity(m);
    for con in lp.cons.iter() {
        let mut a = con.a.clone();
        let mut rel = con.rel;
        let mut b = con.b;
        if b < -eps {
            for v in a.iter_mut() {
                *v = -*v;
            }
            b = -b;
            rel = match rel {
                Rel::Le => Rel::Ge,
                Rel::Ge => Rel::Le,
                Rel::Eq => Rel::Eq,
            };
        }
        rows.push((a, rel, b));
    }

    let mut s_cnt = 0usize;
    let mut a_cnt = 0usize;
    for (_, rel, _) in rows.iter() {
        match rel {
            Rel::Le => s_cnt += 1,
            Rel::Ge => {
                s_cnt += 1;
                a_cnt += 1;
            }
            Rel::Eq => a_cnt += 1,
        }
    }
    let total = n + s_cnt + a_cnt;
    let cols = total + 1;
    let mut tab = vec![vec![0.0_f64; cols]; m + 1];
    let mut is_art = vec![false; total];
    let mut basis = vec![usize::MAX; m];

    let mut s_cur = n;
    let mut a_cur = n + s_cnt;

    for (i, (a, rel, b)) in rows.iter().enumerate() {
        let r = i + 1;
        for j in 0..n {
            tab[r][j] = a[j];
        }
        tab[r][cols - 1] = *b;

        match rel {
            Rel::Le => {
                let s = s_cur;
                s_cur += 1;
                tab[r][s] = 1.0;
                basis[i] = s;
            }
            Rel::Ge => {
                let s = s_cur;
                s_cur += 1;
                tab[r][s] = -1.0;
                let av = a_cur;
                a_cur += 1;
                tab[r][av] = 1.0;
                is_art[av] = true;
                basis[i] = av;
            }
            Rel::Eq => {
                let av = a_cur;
                a_cur += 1;
                tab[r][av] = 1.0;
                is_art[av] = true;
                basis[i] = av;
            }
        }
    }

    SF {
        n_dec: n,
        is_art,
        tab,
        basis,
    }
}

fn sig(basis: &[usize]) -> u64 {
    let mut h: u64 = 1469598103934665603;
    for &v in basis {
        h ^= v as u64;
        h = h.wrapping_mul(1099511628211);
    }
    h
}

impl Solver {
    fn new(lp_in: &Lp, cfg: Config) -> Result<Self, Status> {
        let mut lp = lp_in.clone();
        let mut removed_rows = 0usize;

        if cfg.enable_presolve {
            let (lp2, removed) = presolve_empty_rows(lp, cfg.eps)?;
            lp = lp2;
            removed_rows = removed;
        }

        let mut scaling = None;
        if cfg.enable_scaling {
            let (lp2, scl) = scale_lp(lp, cfg.eps);
            lp = lp2;
            scaling = Some(scl);
        }

        // Internal maximize objective
        let mut c_max = lp.c.clone();
        if lp_in.sense == Sense::Min {
            for v in c_max.iter_mut() {
                *v = -*v;
            }
        }

        let sf = build_standard_form(&lp, cfg.eps);

        let pivot_rule_used = cfg.pivot_rule;
        let pricing_used = cfg.pricing;

        let var_cols = sf.tab[0].len() - 1;
        let devex_w = vec![1.0_f64; var_cols];

        Ok(Self {
            cfg,
            orig_sense: lp_in.sense,
            c_max,
            scaling,
            obj_shift: 0.0,
            sf,
            iters: 0,
            degenerate: 0,
            pivot_rule_used,
            pricing_used,
            cycling: false,
            presolve_removed_rows: removed_rows,
            pricing_start: 0,
            devex_w,
            seen: HashSet::new(),
            fifo: VecDeque::new(),
        })
    }

    fn solve(&mut self, it1: usize, it2: usize) -> ResultReport {
        self.set_phase1();
        let s1 = self.run_simplex(it1);
        if s1 != Status::Optimal {
            return self.finish(s1);
        }
        if self.obj_value().abs() > self.cfg.feas_tol {
            return self.finish(Status::Infeasible);
        }

        self.purge_artificials();
        self.set_phase2();
        let s2 = self.run_simplex(it2);
        self.finish(s2)
    }

    fn finish(&self, status: Status) -> ResultReport {
        let (mut x, mut obj) = self.extract_solution();

        if let Some(scl) = &self.scaling {
            for j in 0..x.len() {
                x[j] *= scl.col_gamma[j];
            }
        }

        obj += self.obj_shift;
        ResultReport {
            status,
            obj,
            x,
            iters: self.iters,
            degenerate: self.degenerate,
            pivot_rule_used: self.pivot_rule_used,
            pricing_used: self.pricing_used,
            cycling: self.cycling,
            presolve_removed_rows: self.presolve_removed_rows,
        }
    }

    fn obj_value(&self) -> f64 {
        let rhs = self.sf.tab[0].len() - 1;
        self.sf.tab[0][rhs]
    }

    fn extract_solution(&self) -> (Vec<f64>, f64) {
        let rhs = self.sf.tab[0].len() - 1;
        let mut x = vec![0.0_f64; self.sf.n_dec];
        for r in 1..self.sf.tab.len() {
            let b = self.sf.basis[r - 1];
            if b < self.sf.n_dec {
                x[b] = self.sf.tab[r][rhs];
            }
        }
        let mut obj = self.sf.tab[0][rhs];
        if self.orig_sense == Sense::Min {
            obj = -obj;
        }
        (x, obj)
    }

    // ---------- Phase objectives ----------
    fn set_phase1(&mut self) {
        // Maximize -sum(artificial)  <=> objective row has +1 on artificials then fixed by basis
        let m = self.sf.tab.len() - 1;
        let cols = self.sf.tab[0].len();
        let vars = cols - 1;

        for j in 0..cols {
            self.sf.tab[0][j] = 0.0;
        }
        for j in 0..vars {
            if self.sf.is_art[j] {
                self.sf.tab[0][j] = 1.0;
            }
        }
        // Eliminate basic artificials from objective
        for r in 1..=m {
            let b = self.sf.basis[r - 1];
            if b < vars && self.sf.is_art[b] {
                for j in 0..cols {
                    self.sf.tab[0][j] -= self.sf.tab[r][j];
                }
            }
        }

        self.reset_runtime();
    }

    fn set_phase2(&mut self) {
        // Maximize c^T x (internal c_max), so tableau stores -c in row 0.
        let cols = self.sf.tab[0].len();
        let rhs = cols - 1;
        let vars = cols - 1;

        for j in 0..cols {
            self.sf.tab[0][j] = 0.0;
        }
        for j in 0..self.sf.n_dec {
            self.sf.tab[0][j] = -self.c_max[j];
        }
        // Eliminate basic decision variables from objective row
        for r in 1..self.sf.tab.len() {
            let b = self.sf.basis[r - 1];
            if b < self.sf.n_dec {
                let ck = self.c_max[b];
                if ck.abs() > self.cfg.eps {
                    for j in 0..vars {
                        self.sf.tab[0][j] += ck * self.sf.tab[r][j];
                    }
                    self.sf.tab[0][rhs] += ck * self.sf.tab[r][rhs];
                }
            }
        }

        self.reset_runtime();
    }

    fn reset_runtime(&mut self) {
        self.iters = 0;
        self.degenerate = 0;
        self.cycling = false;
        self.pivot_rule_used = self.cfg.pivot_rule;
        self.pricing_used = self.cfg.pricing;
        self.pricing_start = 0;
        self.seen.clear();
        self.fifo.clear();
    }

    fn purge_artificials(&mut self) {
        let total = self.sf.is_art.len();
        let cols = total + 1;
        let rhs = cols - 1;

        // Pivot artificial basics out if possible
        for r in 1..self.sf.tab.len() {
            let b = self.sf.basis[r - 1];
            if b < total && self.sf.is_art[b] {
                let mut pc = None;
                for j in 0..total {
                    if !self.sf.is_art[j] && self.sf.tab[r][j].abs() > self.cfg.eps {
                        pc = Some(j);
                        break;
                    }
                }
                if let Some(j) = pc {
                    self.pivot(r, j);
                    self.sf.basis[r - 1] = j;
                } else {
                    let _ = self.sf.tab[r][rhs];
                }
            }
        }

        // Keep non-artificial columns
        let mut keep = Vec::new();
        for j in 0..total {
            if !self.sf.is_art[j] {
                keep.push(j);
            }
        }

        let new_total = keep.len();
        let new_cols = new_total + 1;
        let mut new_tab = vec![vec![0.0_f64; new_cols]; self.sf.tab.len()];

        for i in 0..self.sf.tab.len() {
            for (nj, &oj) in keep.iter().enumerate() {
                new_tab[i][nj] = self.sf.tab[i][oj];
            }
            new_tab[i][new_cols - 1] = self.sf.tab[i][rhs];
        }

        let mut map = vec![None; total];
        for (nj, &oj) in keep.iter().enumerate() {
            map[oj] = Some(nj);
        }
        for i in 0..self.sf.basis.len() {
            self.sf.basis[i] = map[self.sf.basis[i]].unwrap_or(usize::MAX);
        }

        self.sf.tab = new_tab;
        self.sf.is_art = vec![false; new_total];
        self.devex_w = vec![1.0_f64; new_total];
    }

    // ---------- Main simplex loop ----------
    fn run_simplex(&mut self, max_it: usize) -> Status {
        for _ in 0..max_it {
            self.iters += 1;

            // cycling detection on basis
            if self.cfg.cycle_memory > 0 {
                let s = sig(&self.sf.basis);
                if self.seen.contains(&s) {
                    self.cycling = true;
                    if self.cfg.switch_to_bland_on_cycle {
                        self.pivot_rule_used = PivotRule::Bland;
                    }
                } else {
                    self.seen.insert(s);
                    self.fifo.push_back(s);
                    if self.fifo.len() > self.cfg.cycle_memory {
                        if let Some(old) = self.fifo.pop_front() {
                            self.seen.remove(&old);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }

            let enter = match self.choose_entering() {
                Some(j) => j,
                None => return Status::Optimal,
            };
            let (leave_row, step) = match self.choose_leaving(enter) {
                Some(v) => v,
                None => return Status::Unbounded,
            };
            if step.abs() <= self.cfg.feas_tol {
                self.degenerate += 1;
            }

            self.pivot(leave_row, enter);
            self.sf.basis[leave_row - 1] = enter;

            if self.pricing_used == Pricing::Devex {
                self.devex_update();
            }
        }
        Status::IterLimit
    }

    fn choose_entering(&mut self) -> Option<usize> {
        let vars = self.sf.tab[0].len() - 1;

        // Bland: smallest index with negative reduced cost
        if self.pivot_rule_used == PivotRule::Bland {
            for j in 0..vars {
                if self.sf.tab[0][j] < -self.cfg.eps {
                    return Some(j);
                }
            }
            return None;
        }

        match self.pricing_used {
            Pricing::Dantzig => self.enter_dantzig(vars),
            Pricing::Devex => self.enter_devex(vars),
            Pricing::SteepestEdge => self.enter_steepest(vars),
        }
    }

    fn scan_range(&mut self, vars: usize) -> (usize, usize, bool) {
        if let Some(w) = self.cfg.partial_window {
            let w = w.clamp(1, vars.max(1));
            let start = self.pricing_start % vars.max(1);
            let end = (start + w).min(vars);
            // advance window for next call
            self.pricing_start = if vars == 0 { 0 } else { end % vars };
            (start, end, true)
        } else {
            (0, vars, false)
        }
    }

    fn enter_dantzig(&mut self, vars: usize) -> Option<usize> {
        // most negative reduced cost
        let (start, end, partial) = self.scan_range(vars);
        let mut best: Option<(usize, f64)> = None;

        for j in start..end {
            let rc = self.sf.tab[0][j];
            if rc < -self.cfg.eps {
                best = match best {
                    None => Some((j, rc)),
                    Some((bj, bv)) => {
                        if rc < bv { Some((j, rc)) } else { Some((bj, bv)) }
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        if best.is_some() || !partial {
            return best.map(|p| p.0);
        }

        // if partial pricing finds nothing, fall back to full scan once
        let mut best2: Option<(usize, f64)> = None;
        for j in 0..vars {
            let rc = self.sf.tab[0][j];
            if rc < -self.cfg.eps {
                best2 = match best2 {
                    None => Some((j, rc)),
                    Some((bj, bv)) => if rc < bv { Some((j, rc)) } else { Some((bj, bv)) },
                }
            }
        }
        best2.map(|p| p.0)
    }

    fn enter_devex(&mut self, vars: usize) -> Option<usize> {
        // minimize rc / sqrt(w_j) among rc<0
        let (start, end, partial) = self.scan_range(vars);
        let mut best: Option<(usize, f64)> = None;

        for j in start..end {
            let rc = self.sf.tab[0][j];
            if rc < -self.cfg.eps {
                let w = self.devex_w[j].max(1e-18);
                let sc = rc / w.sqrt();
                best = match best {
                    None => Some((j, sc)),
                    Some((bj, bs)) => if sc < bs { Some((j, sc)) } else { Some((bj, bs)) },
                }
            }
        }

        if best.is_some() || !partial {
            return best.map(|p| p.0);
        }

        let mut best2: Option<(usize, f64)> = None;
        for j in 0..vars {
            let rc = self.sf.tab[0][j];
            if rc < -self.cfg.eps {
                let w = self.devex_w[j].max(1e-18);
                let sc = rc / w.sqrt();
                best2 = match best2 {
                    None => Some((j, sc)),
                    Some((bj, bs)) => if sc < bs { Some((j, sc)) } else { Some((bj, bs)) },
                }
            }
        }
        best2.map(|p| p.0)
    }

    fn enter_steepest(&mut self, vars: usize) -> Option<usize> {
        // minimize rc / ||A_B^{-1} a_j|| among rc<0 (proxy via tableau column norm)
        let (start, end, partial) = self.scan_range(vars);
        let mut best: Option<(usize, f64)> = None;

        for j in start..end {
            let rc = self.sf.tab[0][j];
            if rc < -self.cfg.eps {
                let nrm2 = self.col_norm2(j).max(1e-18);
                let sc = rc / nrm2.sqrt();
                best = match best {
                    None => Some((j, sc)),
                    Some((bj, bs)) => if sc < bs { Some((j, sc)) } else { Some((bj, bs)) },
                }
            }
        }

        if best.is_some() || !partial {
            return best.map(|p| p.0);
        }

        let mut best2: Option<(usize, f64)> = None;
        for j in 0..vars {
            let rc = self.sf.tab[0][j];
            if rc < -self.cfg.eps {
                let nrm2 = self.col_norm2(j).max(1e-18);
                let sc = rc / nrm2.sqrt();
                best2 = match best2 {
                    None => Some((j, sc)),
                    Some((bj, bs)) => if sc < bs { Some((j, sc)) } else { Some((bj, bs)) },
                }
            }
        }
        best2.map(|p| p.0)
    }

    fn choose_leaving(&self, enter: usize) -> Option<(usize, f64)> {
        let rhs = self.sf.tab[0].len() - 1;
        let mut best_row: Option<usize> = None;
        let mut best_ratio: f64 = f64::INFINITY;

        for r in 1..self.sf.tab.len() {
            let a = self.sf.tab[r][enter];
            if a > self.cfg.ratio_eps {
                let ratio = self.sf.tab[r][rhs] / a;
                if ratio < best_ratio - 1e-15 {
                    best_ratio = ratio;
                    best_row = Some(r);
                } else if (ratio - best_ratio).abs() <= 1e-15 {
                    // tie-break
                    match self.pivot_rule_used {
                        PivotRule::Bland => {
                            let cur = best_row.unwrap();
                            let bc = self.sf.basis[cur - 1];
                            let bn = self.sf.basis[r - 1];
                            if bn < bc {
                                best_row = Some(r);
                            }
                        }
                        PivotRule::Dantzig => {
                            if r < best_row.unwrap() {
                                best_row = Some(r);
                            }
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        best_row.map(|r| (r, best_ratio))
    }

    fn pivot(&mut self, pr: usize, pc: usize) {
        let cols = self.sf.tab[0].len();
        let pv = self.sf.tab[pr][pc];

        for j in 0..cols {
            self.sf.tab[pr][j] /= pv;
        }

        for i in 0..self.sf.tab.len() {
            if i == pr {
                continue;
            }
            let f = self.sf.tab[i][pc];
            if f.abs() <= self.cfg.eps {
                continue;
            }
            for j in 0..cols {
                self.sf.tab[i][j] -= f * self.sf.tab[pr][j];
            }
            self.sf.tab[i][pc] = 0.0;
        }
    }

    fn col_norm2(&self, j: usize) -> f64 {
        let mut s = 0.0_f64;
        for r in 1..self.sf.tab.len() {
            let v = self.sf.tab[r][j];
            s += v * v;
        }
        s
    }

    fn devex_update(&mut self) {
        // Mild weight refresh based on tableau column norms (keeps code short and stable).
        for j in 0..self.devex_w.len() {
            let target = 1.0 + self.col_norm2(j);
            self.devex_w[j] = 0.9 * self.devex_w[j] + 0.1 * target;
        }
    }
}

// ---------------- Demo main ----------------

fn main() {
    // Mixed-constraint LP (forces Phase I):
    // maximize 3 x1 + 2 x2
    // s.t.  x1 + x2 <= 4
    //      2x1 + x2 >= 5
    //       x1 - x2  = 1
    // x>=0
    let lp = Lp {
        sense: Sense::Max,
        c: vec![3.0, 2.0],
        cons: vec![
            Constraint { a: vec![1.0, 1.0], rel: Rel::Le, b: 4.0 },
            Constraint { a: vec![2.0, 1.0], rel: Rel::Ge, b: 5.0 },
            Constraint { a: vec![1.0, -1.0], rel: Rel::Eq, b: 1.0 },
        ],
    };

    let mut cfg = Config::default();
    cfg.enable_presolve = true;
    cfg.enable_scaling = true;
    cfg.pricing = Pricing::Devex;       // try Dantzig / Devex / SteepestEdge
    cfg.partial_window = Some(8);       // optional partial pricing
    cfg.switch_to_bland_on_cycle = true;

    let mut solver = match Solver::new(&lp, cfg) {
        Ok(s) => s,
        Err(st) => {
            println!("Status: {:?}", st);
            return;
        }
    };

    let res = solver.solve(50_000, 50_000);

    println!("Status: {:?}", res.status);
    if res.status == Status::Optimal {
        println!("Objective value: {:.10}", res.obj);
        for (j, v) in res.x.iter().enumerate() {
            println!("x_{} = {:.10}", j + 1, v);
        }
    } else {
        println!("Objective value: {:.10}", res.obj);
    }

    println!("Iterations: {}", res.iters);
    println!("Degenerate pivots: {}", res.degenerate);
    println!("Pivot rule used: {:?}", res.pivot_rule_used);
    println!("Pricing rule used: {:?}", res.pricing_used);
    println!("Cycling detected: {}", res.cycling);
    println!("Presolve removed rows: {}", res.presolve_removed_rows);
}
```

Program 10.11.4 demonstrates how the simplex method can be extended into a more solver-like framework by incorporating the preprocessing, numerical safeguards, and geometric heuristics discussed in Section 10.11.8. While the pivot mechanics remain rooted in reduced-cost tests, ratio tests, and tableau elimination, practical performance depends strongly on the quality of the starting formulation. The presolve routine illustrates how redundant constraints may be removed and infeasibility detected before any simplex iterations begin, reducing computational cost and improving robustness.

The scaling procedure further highlights the importance of numerical conditioning in simplex computations. By rescaling variable columns so that coefficients remain in comparable ranges, the program reduces the likelihood of floating-point instability during pivot operations. This reflects the practical necessity of scaling in professional solvers, where poorly conditioned problems can otherwise lead to unreliable feasibility decisions or degraded convergence behavior.

The program also demonstrates how professional solvers improve entering-variable selection through advanced pricing strategies. Dantzig pricing focuses only on reduced costs, while Devex and steepest-edge style scoring incorporate additional geometric information that approximates the effect of moving along transformed edges of the feasible polyhedron. The inclusion of partial pricing provides a further illustration of how reduced-cost computation can be restricted to manageable subsets of variables, mirroring the efficiency heuristics described in Section 10.11.7. Together, these features emphasize that modern simplex solvers are not merely implementations of a textbook algorithm but engineered systems that combine preprocessing, numerical stabilization, and adaptive pivot heuristics to achieve high performance on large-scale real-world linear programs.

## 10.11.9. Real-World Examples and Interpretation of Output

The simplex method is used extensively in industrial, commercial, and public-sector planning, where decisions must be made subject to limited resources and competing objectives. Classical application areas include manufacturing and blending problems, in which raw materials must be combined to meet quality or demand constraints at minimum cost; transportation and logistics problems, where goods are routed through networks to minimize shipping costs or delivery times; and diet or resource allocation problems, which model the selection of activities or commodities to satisfy nutritional, budgetary, or policy requirements. These canonical examples continue to serve as benchmarks for understanding how linear programming translates complex operational decisions into a mathematically precise and computationally solvable framework (Walaa, 2023).

In real-world applications, the value of simplex extends beyond producing an optimal vector of decision variables. The final tableau and associated basis provide rich economic and operational insight into the structure of the solution. In particular, the dual variables associated with the constraints, often referred to as shadow prices, measure the marginal change in the objective value resulting from a small relaxation of a constraint’s right-hand side. A nonzero shadow price indicates that the corresponding constraint is binding and actively limits optimal performance, while a zero shadow price indicates the presence of slack. This dual interpretation is central to sensitivity analysis and supports informed decision-making when problem parameters are uncertain or subject to change.

The composition of the final basis also plays an important interpretive role. Basic variables identify activities or decisions that are active at the optimum, while nonbasic variables at zero represent excluded or dominated options under the current objective. By examining which constraints are binding and which resources are fully utilized, practitioners can identify bottlenecks, inefficiencies, and potential areas for system redesign. This ability to interpret solutions economically, rather than merely numerically, is one of the enduring strengths of the simplex method.

In modern large-scale applications, simplex is often embedded within broader optimization pipelines. Interior-point methods or first-order approaches may be used to generate high-quality approximate solutions, after which simplex is applied to refine feasibility, recover exact optimal bases, and extract interpretable dual information. Surveys of contemporary optimization practice emphasize that this complementary use of methods remains common in professional solvers (Gondzio, 2025), even as alternative large-scale approaches continue to develop (Lu and Applegate, 2024).

From a broader perspective, the continued relevance of linear programming and simplex-based analysis reflects a general trend in optimization toward methods that balance mathematical rigor, computational scalability, and interpretability. Popular science and practitioner-oriented discussions highlight that, despite advances in machine learning and heuristic optimization, classical optimization tools remain foundational in many decision-critical systems (Nadis, 2025).

## 10.11.10. Concluding Remarks

The simplex algorithm is a cornerstone of numerical optimization for linear programs. Its geometric interpretation as a walk along edges between adjacent vertices, its algebraic basis representation, and its practical refinements through pivot rules, sparse linear algebra, and presolve strategies explain why it remains highly effective in real applications. Despite exponential worst-case constructions, practical performance is typically excellent, and ongoing research continues to clarify the reasons behind simplex’s observed efficiency. The method remains a default choice in many settings, particularly where warm-starting, reoptimization, or sensitivity analysis is important (Nadis, 2025).

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/nH2hBL8P8hyfOOXnqJ4p.8","tags":[]}

# 10.12. Linear Programming: Interior-Point Methods

While the simplex method remains highly effective in practice, its exponential worst-case complexity motivated the development of alternative approaches to linear programming. Interior-point methods address this concern by traversing the interior of the feasible region rather than moving along its boundary. Instead of progressing from vertex to vertex, these methods approach the optimal solution along a continuous path within the feasible polyhedron, offering a fundamentally different geometric perspective on linear optimization.

The modern era of interior-point methods began with the polynomial-time algorithm introduced by Karmarkar (1984), which established that linear programs could be solved in time bounded by a polynomial in the size of the problem. This result resolved a long-standing theoretical question regarding the computational complexity of linear programming. Early interior-point methods, however, were largely of theoretical interest, as their initial implementations faced challenges related to numerical stability and practical efficiency.

Subsequent advances in algorithm design, numerical linear algebra, and implementation techniques significantly improved the robustness and performance of interior-point methods. As a result, they are now a standard component of professional linear programming solvers. In modern practice, interior-point algorithms are often competitive with simplex methods on large-scale problems and are frequently used in a complementary role, for example to obtain high-quality feasible solutions that can later be refined or interpreted using simplex-based techniques.

## 10.12.1. Primal–Dual Formulation

Interior-point methods are most naturally expressed within the primal–dual formulation of linear programming, which treats the primal and dual problems symmetrically and exploits their close mathematical relationship. Rather than focusing on extreme points of the feasible region, as simplex does, interior-point methods operate on iterates that remain strictly within the feasible sets of both problems, approaching optimality from the interior. This primal–dual perspective underlies most modern interior-point algorithms and is central to their numerical robustness and theoretical analysis (Gondzio, 2025).

Consider the primal problem in standard form:

$$
\min \ \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}, \qquad
A \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}, \qquad
\mathbf{x} \ge 0
\tag{10.12.1}
$$

and its associated dual,

$$\max \ \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{y}, \qquad A^T \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{s} = \mathbf{c}, \qquad\mathbf{s} \ge 0 \tag{10.12.2}$$

Here $\mathbf{y}$ represents the dual variables associated with the equality constraints of the primal problem, while $\mathbf{s}$ are the dual slack variables corresponding to the nonnegativity constraints on $\mathbf{x}$. The primal and dual formulations encode the same optimization problem from complementary perspectives, linking feasibility, optimality, and sensitivity through duality theory.

Optimality in linear programming is characterized by three conditions: primal feasibility, dual feasibility, and the complementarity conditions:

$$x_i s_i = 0, \qquad i = 1,\ldots,n \tag{10.12.3}$$

Complementarity enforces consistency between the primal and dual solutions, ensuring that for each variable either the primal constraint is active or the corresponding dual slack vanishes. At optimality, these conditions imply that the solution lies on the boundary of the feasible region, where active constraints and zero-valued variables coincide.

Interior-point methods relax the complementarity conditions during the iterative process by allowing strictly positive products $x_i s_i > 0$. By maintaining feasibility while gradually reducing these products toward zero, the algorithm traces a smooth trajectory through the interior of the feasible region toward optimality. This controlled relaxation avoids the combinatorial behavior associated with boundary-based methods and forms the basis for barrier and central-path approaches developed in later sections (Gondzio, 2025).

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.12.1 on the primal–dual formulation of linear programming and the relaxation of complementarity conditions in Equation (10.12.3), Program 10.12.1 provides a practical implementation of a basic primal–dual interior-point method for linear programs in standard form. Rather than moving along the boundary of the feasible polyhedron as simplex does, the algorithm maintains strictly positive primal and dual slack variables and iteratively drives their complementarity products toward zero. This approach reflects the geometric interpretation introduced earlier in the section, where optimality is approached through a smooth interior trajectory. The program implements a dense path-following strategy based on the perturbed KKT system, demonstrating how primal feasibility, dual feasibility, and approximate complementarity can be enforced simultaneously through Newton corrections. Although simplified for pedagogical clarity, the implementation captures the essential numerical structure of modern interior-point solvers, including residual-based convergence monitoring and step-size control to preserve interior feasibility.

At the core of the implementation is the `LP` struct, which stores the problem data $A$, $\mathbf{b}$, and $\mathbf{c}$ corresponding directly to the primal problem (10.12.1). The current primal–dual iterate is represented by the `PDState` struct, which contains the primal variables $\mathbf{x}$, the dual variables $\mathbf{y}$, and the dual slack variables $\mathbf{s}$ from the dual formulation (10.12.2). These variables form the natural state vector of the primal–dual method, and their evolution reflects the interior-point philosophy: the program enforces strict positivity of $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{s}$ throughout the iterations, so that complementarity is relaxed as $x_i s_i > 0$ rather than being forced to satisfy Equation (10.12.3) exactly at intermediate stages.

Several small helper routines provide the numerical infrastructure required for the solver. The function `inf_norm` computes the infinity norm of a residual vector, which is used to assess convergence of primal feasibility and dual feasibility. This is consistent with the stopping criteria of interior-point methods, which require that $A\mathbf{x} \approx \mathbf{b}$ and $A^T\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{s} \approx \mathbf{c}$ hold to within tolerance. The function `duality_gap` evaluates $\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{s}$, which measures the degree of violation of complementarity in Equation (10.12.3). In primal–dual interior-point theory, this quantity provides a natural estimate of the current suboptimality and defines the barrier parameter $\mu = (\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{s})/n$, which the algorithm drives toward zero.

A crucial aspect of interior-point methods is ensuring that the iterates remain in the strict interior of the feasible cone. The function `clamp_strictly_positive` enforces this by replacing any nonpositive component of $\mathbf{x}$ or $\mathbf{s}$ with a small floor value. While this adjustment is not part of the theoretical model, it is a common practical safeguard against floating-point underflow and prevents numerical breakdown when a step would otherwise drive a component slightly negative. The related routine `min_ratio_step` computes a maximum feasible step size $\alpha$ such that $\mathbf{x} + \alpha \Delta \mathbf{x} > 0$ and $\mathbf{s} + \alpha \Delta \mathbf{s} > 0$. This implements the classical fraction-to-the-boundary rule used in interior-point methods, ensuring that the algorithm remains consistent with the requirement that iterates stay strictly inside the domain of the logarithmic barrier.

The main computational procedure is implemented in `primal_dual_ipm`, which solves the primal–dual KKT system associated with (10.12.1)–(10.12.2). At each iteration, the function forms the primal residual $r_p = A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}$ and the dual residual $r_d = A^T\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{s} - \mathbf{c}$. These correspond directly to violations of primal feasibility and dual feasibility. Complementarity is handled through a perturbed residual vector $r_c$, defined componentwise as $x_i s_i - \mu_{\text{target}}$, which represents the relaxed complementarity condition replacing Equation (10.12.3). The parameter $\mu_{\text{target}}$ is chosen as a fraction of the current $\mu$, producing a simple path-following strategy in which the iterates are repeatedly pulled back toward the central path while $\mu$ decreases.

To compute the Newton correction, the program derives and solves the reduced normal equations system associated with the primal–dual KKT conditions. This reduction eliminates $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ and $\Delta \mathbf{s}$ to obtain a symmetric system in $\Delta \mathbf{y}$, based on the diagonal scaling matrix $D = X S^{-1}$. The matrix $A D A^T$ is assembled explicitly, and a small diagonal regularization term is added to improve numerical stability. This reflects the practical necessity of stabilizing the linear system when the scaling becomes ill-conditioned as $\mu$ decreases. Once $\Delta \mathbf{y}$ is obtained, the program reconstructs $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ and $\Delta \mathbf{s}$ using the eliminated equations, producing a full Newton direction for the primal–dual variables.

After computing the direction, the algorithm determines a step size $\alpha$ that preserves positivity of both $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{s}$. The update rule then advances the primal and dual iterates simultaneously. Convergence is checked using the infinity norms of $r_p$ and $r_d$, together with the magnitude of $\mu$, which is derived from the duality gap $\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{s}$. This combined stopping criterion is consistent with the primal–dual interpretation of optimality, where feasibility and complementarity must all be satisfied, and it ensures that the computed solution approaches the conditions of Equation (10.12.3) as the iterations proceed.

The `main` function provides a compact demonstration of the method by solving a small dense linear program in standard form. The example includes equality constraints and nonnegativity, matching the structure of Equation (10.12.1). A strictly positive initial guess is constructed for $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{s}$, while $\mathbf{y}$ is initialized at zero. The program then calls `primal_dual_ipm` and prints the final primal solution, dual variables, and slack variables, together with diagnostic quantities including the infinity norms of the primal and dual residuals and the final duality gap. These diagnostics confirm that the computed solution satisfies primal feasibility and dual feasibility to machine precision while driving the complementarity products toward zero, illustrating in numerical form the relaxation and gradual enforcement of Equation (10.12.3) described earlier in the section.

Add the following dependencies to cargo.toml:

```rust
[dependencies]
nalgebra = "0.32"
```

```rust
// Program 10.12.1: A basic primal–dual interior-point method for linear programs in standard form.
//
// Solves (10.12.1)–(10.12.2):
//   minimize    c^T x
//   subject to  A x = b,  x >= 0
//   maximize    b^T y
//   subject to  A^T y + s = c,  s >= 0
//
// The method follows the primal–dual central path by enforcing the perturbed complementarity
//   X S e = mu e
// instead of (10.12.3), and drives mu -> 0.
//
// Dependencies (add to Cargo.toml):
//   [dependencies]
//   nalgebra = "0.32"
//
// Notes:
// - Dense pedagogical implementation. For large-scale LPs, use sparse A and sparse linear algebra.
// - Feasibility is approached via residual reduction (not maintained exactly at every step).
// - Step sizes preserve strict positivity of x and s so iterates remain interior.

use nalgebra::{DMatrix, DVector};

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct LP {
    pub a: DMatrix<f64>, // m x n
    pub b: DVector<f64>, // m
    pub c: DVector<f64>, // n
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct PDState {
    pub x: DVector<f64>, // n, primal variables (x > 0)
    pub y: DVector<f64>, // m, dual variables
    pub s: DVector<f64>, // n, dual slacks (s > 0)
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct IPSolution {
    pub x: DVector<f64>,
    pub y: DVector<f64>,
    pub s: DVector<f64>,
    pub objective: f64,
    pub iterations: usize,
    pub status: String,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct IPOptions {
    pub max_iter: usize,
    pub tol: f64,
    pub mu_reduction: f64,    // in (0,1), typical 0.1
    pub alpha_safety: f64,    // in (0,1), typical 0.99
    pub regularization: f64,  // small diagonal stabilization for normal equations
    pub min_pos: f64,         // positivity floor to avoid underflow
}

impl Default for IPOptions {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            max_iter: 80,
            tol: 1e-8,
            mu_reduction: 0.1,
            alpha_safety: 0.99,
            regularization: 1e-10,
            min_pos: 1e-14,
        }
    }
}

fn inf_norm(v: &DVector<f64>) -> f64 {
    v.iter().map(|x| x.abs()).fold(0.0, f64::max)
}

fn clamp_strictly_positive(v: &mut DVector<f64>, eps: f64) {
    for i in 0..v.len() {
        if v[i] <= eps {
            v[i] = eps;
        }
    }
}

fn min_ratio_step(pos: &DVector<f64>, dir: &DVector<f64>, safety: f64) -> f64 {
    // Max alpha in (0,1] such that pos + alpha*dir > 0 componentwise.
    // If dir_i < 0, require alpha < -pos_i/dir_i.
    let mut alpha = 1.0;
    for i in 0..pos.len() {
        let di = dir[i];
        if di < 0.0 {
            let bound = -pos[i] / di;
            if bound.is_finite() && bound < alpha {
                alpha = bound;
            }
        }
    }
    (safety * alpha).min(1.0)
}

fn duality_gap(x: &DVector<f64>, s: &DVector<f64>) -> f64 {
    x.dot(s)
}

pub fn primal_dual_ipm(lp: &LP, mut st: PDState, opt: &IPOptions) -> Result<IPSolution, String> {
    let m = lp.a.nrows();
    let n = lp.a.ncols();
    if lp.b.len() != m || lp.c.len() != n {
        return Err("Dimension mismatch in LP data.".to_string());
    }
    if st.x.len() != n || st.y.len() != m || st.s.len() != n {
        return Err("Dimension mismatch in initial state.".to_string());
    }

    // Ensure strict positivity of x and s (interior start).
    clamp_strictly_positive(&mut st.x, opt.min_pos);
    clamp_strictly_positive(&mut st.s, opt.min_pos);

    let mut status = "max_iter_reached".to_string();
    let mut it_done = 0usize;

    for k in 0..opt.max_iter {
        it_done = k + 1;

        // Residuals for KKT conditions:
        // r_p = A x - b
        // r_d = A^T y + s - c
        let r_p = &lp.a * &st.x - &lp.b;
        let r_d = lp.a.transpose() * &st.y + &st.s - &lp.c;

        // Duality gap and barrier parameter.
        let gap = duality_gap(&st.x, &st.s);
        let mu = gap / (n as f64);

        // Stopping test: primal and dual residuals, and duality gap.
        let rp_norm = inf_norm(&r_p);
        let rd_norm = inf_norm(&r_d);
        let mu_abs = mu.abs();
        if rp_norm <= opt.tol && rd_norm <= opt.tol && mu_abs <= opt.tol {
            status = "converged".to_string();
            break;
        }

        // Target mu for the next Newton step (path-following).
        let mu_target = opt.mu_reduction * mu;

        // Complementarity residual for perturbed system:
        // r_c = X S e - mu_target e, i.e. r_c[i] = x_i s_i - mu_target.
        let mut r_c = DVector::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            r_c[i] = st.x[i] * st.s[i] - mu_target;
        }

        // Newton system for perturbed KKT:
        // A dx               = -r_p
        // A^T dy + ds        = -r_d
        // S dx + X ds        = -r_c
        //
        // Eliminate ds and dx -> normal equations:
        // (A D A^T) dy = -r_p - A S^{-1}(-r_c + X r_d), with D = X S^{-1}.

        // D = diag(d), d_i = x_i / s_i.
        let mut d = DVector::<f64>::zeros(n);
```
