```rust
        for i in 0..n {
            d[i] = st.x[i] / st.s[i];
        }

        // w = -r_c + X r_d  (componentwise)
        let mut w = DVector::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            w[i] = -r_c[i] + st.x[i] * r_d[i];
        }

        // u = S^{-1} w (componentwise)
        let mut u = DVector::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            u[i] = w[i] / st.s[i];
        }

        // rhs = -r_p - A u
        let rhs = -&r_p - &lp.a * u;

        // Form M = A D A^T using B = A * diag(sqrt(d)) so M = B B^T.
        let mut bmat = lp.a.clone();
        for j in 0..n {
            let sj = d[j].max(0.0).sqrt();
            for i in 0..m {
                bmat[(i, j)] *= sj;
            }
        }

        let mut mmat = &bmat * bmat.transpose();
        for i in 0..m {
            mmat[(i, i)] += opt.regularization;
        }

        // Solve normal equations for dy.
        let lu = mmat.lu();
        let dy = lu
            .solve(&rhs)
            .ok_or_else(|| "Linear solve failed (normal equations singular).".to_string())?;

        // Recover dx:
        // dx = S^{-1}(-r_c + X r_d + X A^T dy)
        let at_dy = lp.a.transpose() * &dy;
        let mut dx = DVector::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            let num = -r_c[i] + st.x[i] * r_d[i] + st.x[i] * at_dy[i];
            dx[i] = num / st.s[i];
        }

        // Recover ds from dual equation: ds = -r_d - A^T dy
        let ds = -r_d - at_dy;

        // Step sizes to preserve x > 0 and s > 0.
        let alpha_pri = min_ratio_step(&st.x, &dx, opt.alpha_safety);
        let alpha_dual = min_ratio_step(&st.s, &ds, opt.alpha_safety);
        let alpha = alpha_pri.min(alpha_dual);

        // Update.
        st.x += alpha * &dx;
        st.y += alpha * &dy;
        st.s += alpha * &ds;

        // Guard against numerical underflow / loss of strict positivity.
        clamp_strictly_positive(&mut st.x, opt.min_pos);
        clamp_strictly_positive(&mut st.s, opt.min_pos);
    }

    let obj = lp.c.dot(&st.x);
    Ok(IPSolution {
        x: st.x,
        y: st.y,
        s: st.s,
        objective: obj,
        iterations: it_done,
        status,
    })
}

fn main() {
    // Example LP (small, dense) in standard form:
    //
    // minimize    2 x1 + 1 x2 + 0 x3
    // subject to  x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
    //             x1           = 0.3
    //             x >= 0
    //
    // This is already in equality form with a slack-like variable x3.
    // The optimal solution is x1 = 0.3, x2 = 0.0, x3 = 0.7, objective = 0.6.
    let a = DMatrix::<f64>::from_row_slice(
        2,
        3,
        &[
            1.0, 1.0, 1.0, //
            1.0, 0.0, 0.0, //
        ],
    );
    let b = DVector::<f64>::from_row_slice(&[1.0, 0.3]);
    let c = DVector::<f64>::from_row_slice(&[2.0, 1.0, 0.0]);

    let lp = LP { a, b, c };

    // A strictly positive starting point (x > 0, s > 0) is required.
    // We allow infeasibility in Ax=b and A^T y + s = c initially; Newton steps reduce residuals.
    let x0 = DVector::<f64>::from_row_slice(&[0.33, 0.33, 0.34]);
    let y0 = DVector::<f64>::from_element(2, 0.0);
    let s0 = DVector::<f64>::from_element(3, 1.0);

    let st0 = PDState { x: x0, y: y0, s: s0 };

    let opt = IPOptions {
        max_iter: 60,
        tol: 1e-10,
        mu_reduction: 0.1,
        alpha_safety: 0.99,
        regularization: 1e-12,
        min_pos: 1e-14,
    };

    match primal_dual_ipm(&lp, st0, &opt) {
        Ok(sol) => {
            println!("Status: {}", sol.status);
            println!("Iterations: {}", sol.iterations);
            println!("Objective: {:.12}", sol.objective);
            println!("x = {}", sol.x.transpose());
            println!("y = {}", sol.y.transpose());
            println!("s = {}", sol.s.transpose());

            let rp = &lp.a * &sol.x - &lp.b;
            let rd = lp.a.transpose() * &sol.y + &sol.s - &lp.c;
            println!("||r_p||_inf = {:.3e}", inf_norm(&rp));
            println!("||r_d||_inf = {:.3e}", inf_norm(&rd));
            println!("duality gap = {:.3e}", duality_gap(&sol.x, &sol.s));
        }
        Err(e) => {
            eprintln!("Solver error: {e}");
        }
    }
}
```

Program 10.12.1 demonstrates how the primal–dual interior-point framework introduced in Section 10.12 can be translated into a complete computational solver using only the KKT conditions and Newton’s method. The implementation highlights the central numerical principle of interior-point algorithms: instead of enforcing the complementarity constraints of Equation (10.12.3) exactly, the method solves a perturbed system that maintains strictly positive iterates and reduces the duality gap progressively. This produces a smooth convergence process that avoids the combinatorial boundary-walking behavior characteristic of simplex.

The program also illustrates the role of numerical linear algebra in interior-point methods. Even in this simplified dense setting, the dominant computational cost arises from solving the normal equations system $A D A^T\Delta \mathbf{y} = \text{rhs}$, which reflects the coupling between primal and dual variables. In large-scale applications, this step becomes the primary performance bottleneck and motivates the use of sparse matrix formats, preconditioned Krylov solvers, and advanced factorization strategies. Nonetheless, the dense formulation presented here captures the essential structure of the method and provides a transparent foundation for later extensions to sparse and large-scale solvers.

Finally, the modular design of the implementation makes it straightforward to incorporate more advanced interior-point features discussed in modern solver literature, including predictor–corrector strategies, adaptive barrier updates, and more robust step-length selection rules. In this way, Program 10.12.1 serves as a minimal but complete reference implementation of the primal–dual interior-point philosophy and provides a concrete basis for understanding how theoretical optimality conditions translate into stable and efficient numerical algorithms.

## 10.12.2. Barrier Functions and Central Path

A central idea in interior-point methods is the use of barrier functions to enforce inequality constraints implicitly rather than explicitly. For linear programming problems with nonnegativity constraints, the most common choice is the logarithmic barrier, which penalizes iterates that approach the boundary of the feasible region. By incorporating this penalty into the objective function, the constrained optimization problem is transformed into a sequence of smooth problems whose solutions remain strictly feasible.

Specifically, the constrained linear program is replaced by a family of barrier subproblems of the form:

$$\min \ \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} - \mu \sum_{i=1}^n \ln x_i, \qquad A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b},\tag{10.12.4}$$

where $\mu > 0$ is the barrier parameter. The logarithmic terms grow without bound as any component $x_i$ approaches zero, thereby preventing violation of the nonnegativity constraints. For fixed $\mu$, the barrier problem is smooth and strictly convex over the feasible set, ensuring the existence of a unique minimizer under mild assumptions. As $\mu \to 0$, the influence of the barrier diminishes, and the minimizer of the barrier problem approaches the solution of the original linear program.

The collection of minimizers of the barrier subproblems, parameterized by $\mu$, forms a smooth curve known as the central path. This path lies entirely within the interior of the feasible region and converges to an optimal solution as the barrier parameter tends to zero. From a primal–dual perspective, points on the central path satisfy perturbed complementarity conditions of the form,

$$x_i s_i = \mu, \qquad i = 1,\ldots,n \tag{10.12.5}$$

which provide a continuous relaxation of the exact complementarity conditions introduced earlier. This interpretation highlights the close connection between barrier methods and primal–dual interior-point algorithms.

Interior-point methods aim to follow the central path by successively reducing the barrier parameter $\mu$ and applying Newton’s method to the first-order optimality conditions of the barrier problem. At each iteration, Newton steps are computed to correct both primal and dual variables simultaneously, balancing progress toward feasibility and optimality. Rather than tracking the central path exactly, practical algorithms take approximate steps that remain within a neighborhood of the path, allowing for larger steps and improved efficiency.

This barrier-based viewpoint provides a geometric and analytical framework for interior-point methods. By replacing the combinatorial movement along the boundary characteristic of simplex with smooth motion through the interior, interior-point algorithms achieve polynomial-time complexity and strong numerical performance on large-scale problems. Modern treatments emphasize that the effectiveness of these methods depends not on exact path-following, but on maintaining proximity to the central path while driving the barrier parameter toward zero in a controlled manner, a principle that underlies most contemporary primal–dual interior-point solvers (Gondzio, 2025).

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion of the logarithmic barrier formulation in Equation (10.12.4) and the perturbed complementarity conditions in Equation (10.12.5), Program 10.12.2 provides a concrete implementation of a primal–dual path-following interior-point method for linear programming in standard form. Rather than enforcing the nonnegativity constraints $x \ge 0$ explicitly through active-set logic, the program maintains strict feasibility by ensuring that both primal variables $x$ and dual slack variables $s$ remain positive throughout the iteration. At each step, Newton’s method is applied to the first-order optimality conditions of the barrier subproblem, producing simultaneous corrections to the primal variables, the dual multipliers, and the slack variables. By repeatedly reducing the barrier parameter $\mu$ while staying close to the central path, the algorithm illustrates the fundamental geometric mechanism of interior-point methods, namely smooth motion through the interior of the feasible region toward an optimal vertex solution.

At the core of the implementation is the function `pdip_path_follow`, which realizes a primal–dual interior-point algorithm based directly on the barrier subproblem in Equation (10.12.4) and the perturbed complementarity relation in Equation (10.12.5). The function maintains three sets of unknowns: the primal decision vector $x$, the dual equality multiplier vector $y$, and the dual slack vector $s$. At each iteration, the residual vectors $r_p = Ax - b$ and $r_d = A^T y + s - c$ are formed to measure primal feasibility and dual feasibility, respectively. The complementarity residual is constructed as $r_c = x_i s_i - \sigma \mu$, where $\mu = (x^T s)/n$ is the current barrier parameter and $\sigma$ is a centering constant. This directly enforces the idea that the iterates should remain in a neighborhood of the central path, where all products $x_i s_i$ are approximately equal to the barrier value $\mu$, consistent with Equation (10.12.5).

The Newton correction step is obtained by linearizing the KKT system associated with the barrier problem. This produces a coupled system for $(dx,dy,ds)$ involving the constraint Jacobian $A$ and the diagonal matrices $X$ and $S$ formed from the current vectors $x$ and $s$. Rather than solving the full saddle-point system directly, the program eliminates $dx$ and $ds$ to obtain a reduced normal-equation system in the dual step $dy$. This reduction yields a symmetric system with coefficient matrix $M = A\,\mathrm{diag}(x ./ s)\,A^{T}$, which is assembled explicitly in the code as `mmat`. The right-hand side incorporates the residual vectors $r_p$, $r_d$, and $r_c$, ensuring that the Newton step simultaneously improves feasibility and decreases the complementarity gap. Once $dy$ is obtained, the remaining corrections $ds$ and $dx$ are recovered through explicit back-substitution, thereby producing a full primal–dual Newton direction.

The function `gauss_solve` provides the dense linear algebra routine used to solve the reduced normal equations. It implements Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting, which is numerically adequate for small demonstration problems and has the advantage of being self-contained, avoiding reliance on external factorization libraries. Although modern interior-point solvers typically use sparse Cholesky or $LDL^{T}$ factorizations to handle large-scale constraint matrices efficiently, this dense solver is sufficient for illustrating the computational structure of a primal–dual method and for verifying the central-path convergence behavior predicted by the barrier theory.

To preserve strict positivity of the iterates, the step size is computed using the fraction-to-the-boundary strategy implemented in `min_ratio_step`. This function determines the largest feasible step length $\alpha$ such that $x + \alpha dx \ge 0$ (and similarly $s + \alpha ds \ge 0$) componentwise, then reduces it by a safety factor $\tau$. This ensures that no component of $x$ or $s$ crosses the boundary $0$, thereby keeping the logarithmic barrier term well-defined and enforcing the interior-point requirement that iterates remain strictly feasible. The final step length is chosen as the minimum of the primal and dual admissible step sizes, ensuring feasibility is maintained simultaneously for both variable sets.

The `main` function serves as a compact numerical demonstration of the theory behind Equations (10.12.4) and (10.12.5). It constructs a simple linear program with two variables and one equality constraint, selects an initial strictly feasible point $x_0$ satisfying $Ax=b$, and initializes $y_0$ and $s_0$ so that the dual feasibility condition $A^T y + s = c$ holds with $s>0$. This guarantees that the first iterate lies in the interior of the feasible region, allowing the barrier method to proceed without requiring a separate feasibility restoration phase. As the iteration progresses, the printed diagnostics show the monotone reduction of the complementarity measure $\mu$, demonstrating numerically how the iterates follow the central path and converge toward the optimal vertex solution as $\mu \to 0$.

Add the following dependencies to cargo.toml:

```rust
[dependencies]
ndarray = "0.15"
```

```rust
// Program 10.12.2: Barrier functions and central path (primal–dual path-following)
//
// Solve the standard-form LP:
//   minimize    c^T x
//   subject to  A x = b,  x >= 0
//
// Dual:
//   maximize    b^T y
//   subject to  A^T y + s = c,  s >= 0
//
// Central-path (perturbed complementarity) condition (10.12.5):
//   x_i s_i = mu   (or x_i s_i = sigma*mu for centering)
//
// Newton system for one primal–dual step:
//   A dx            = -r_p
//   A^T dy + ds     = -r_d
//   S dx + X ds     = -r_c
//
// with residuals:
//   r_p = A x - b
//   r_d = A^T y + s - c
//   r_c = X s - sigma*mu*e  (componentwise: x_i s_i - sigma*mu)
//
// Eliminating (dx, ds) yields normal equations:
//   (A * diag(x./s) * A^T) dy = -r_p - A * S^{-1} * (-r_c + X r_d)
//
// IMPORTANT FIX (vs. the earlier version):
// When forming dx after solving for dy, the correct substitution is
//   S dx = -r_c + X r_d + X A^T dy
// so the X A^T dy term enters with a PLUS sign.
//
// This code is educational and intended for small dense problems.

use ndarray::{Array1, Array2};

fn dot(a: &Array1<f64>, b: &Array1<f64>) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn inf_norm(v: &Array1<f64>) -> f64 {
    v.iter().fold(0.0, |acc, &x| acc.max(x.abs()))
}

fn min_ratio_step(x: &Array1<f64>, dx: &Array1<f64>, tau: f64) -> f64 {
    // Maximum step alpha in (0,1] such that x + alpha*dx >= 0, then shrink by tau.
    let mut alpha: f64 = 1.0f64;
    for (&xi, &dxi) in x.iter().zip(dx.iter()) {
        if dxi < 0.0f64 {
            alpha = alpha.min(-xi / dxi);
        }
    }
    (tau * alpha).min(1.0f64)
}

fn gauss_solve(mut a: Array2<f64>, mut b: Array1<f64>) -> Result<Array1<f64>, String> {
    // Dense Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting.
    let n = a.nrows();
    if a.ncols() != n || b.len() != n {
        return Err("Dimension mismatch in gauss_solve".to_string());
    }

    for k in 0..n {
        // Pivot
        let mut piv = k;
        let mut best = a[(k, k)].abs();
        for i in (k + 1)..n {
            let val = a[(i, k)].abs();
            if val > best {
                best = val;
                piv = i;
            }
        }
        if best <= 1e-14 {
            return Err("Singular or nearly singular system in gauss_solve".to_string());
        }
        if piv != k {
            // swap rows in A
            for j in k..n {
                let tmp = a[(k, j)];
                a[(k, j)] = a[(piv, j)];
                a[(piv, j)] = tmp;
            }
            // swap in b
            let tmpb = b[k];
            b[k] = b[piv];
            b[piv] = tmpb;
        }

        // Eliminate
        for i in (k + 1)..n {
            let m = a[(i, k)] / a[(k, k)];
            a[(i, k)] = 0.0;
            for j in (k + 1)..n {
                a[(i, j)] -= m * a[(k, j)];
            }
            b[i] -= m * b[k];
        }
    }

    // Back substitution
    let mut x = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for i in (0..n).rev() {
        let mut s = b[i];
        for j in (i + 1)..n {
            s -= a[(i, j)] * x[j];
        }
        x[i] = s / a[(i, i)];
    }
    Ok(x)
}

fn pdip_path_follow(
    a: &Array2<f64>,
    b: &Array1<f64>,
    c: &Array1<f64>,
    mut x: Array1<f64>,
    mut y: Array1<f64>,
    mut s: Array1<f64>,
    max_iter: usize,
) -> Result<(Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>), String> {
    let m = a.nrows();
    let n = a.ncols();
    if b.len() != m || c.len() != n || x.len() != n || y.len() != m || s.len() != n {
        return Err("Dimension mismatch in pdip_path_follow inputs".to_string());
    }

    // Parameters: neighborhood control and step length.
    let tau: f64 = 0.995; // fraction-to-the-boundary
    let sigma: f64 = 0.5; // centering parameter (more stable than 0.10 without corrector)
    let tol: f64 = 1e-10;

    for it in 0..max_iter {
        // Residuals
        let r_p = a.dot(&x) - b;                 // Ax - b
        let r_d = a.t().dot(&y) + &s - c;        // A^T y + s - c

        let mu = dot(&x, &s) / (n as f64);
        let mut r_c = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);   // x_i s_i - sigma*mu
        for i in 0..n {
            r_c[i] = x[i] * s[i] - sigma * mu;
        }

        let rp = inf_norm(&r_p);
        let rd = inf_norm(&r_d);
        let rc = inf_norm(&r_c);

        if rp.max(rd).max(rc) < tol && mu < 1e-12 {
            println!(
                "Converged at iter {it}: ||r_p||inf={rp:.3e}, ||r_d||inf={rd:.3e}, ||r_c||inf={rc:.3e}, mu={mu:.3e}"
            );
            return Ok((x, y, s));
        }

        // Build D = x ./ s and s_inv.
        let mut d = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        let mut s_inv = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            if s[i] <= 0.0 || x[i] <= 0.0 {
                return Err("Lost strict feasibility (x>0, s>0)".to_string());
            }
            s_inv[i] = 1.0 / s[i];
            d[i] = x[i] * s_inv[i];
        }

        // Form M = A * diag(D) * A^T (m x m).
        let mut mmat = Array2::<f64>::zeros((m, m));
        for i in 0..m {
            for j in 0..m {
                let mut sum = 0.0;
                for k in 0..n {
                    sum += a[(i, k)] * d[k] * a[(j, k)];
                }
                mmat[(i, j)] = sum;
            }
        }

        // rhs = -r_p - A * S^{-1} * (-r_c + X r_d)
        let mut w = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            w[i] = -r_c[i] + x[i] * r_d[i];
        }
        let mut q = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            q[i] = s_inv[i] * w[i];
        }
        let aq = a.dot(&q);
        let rhs = -&r_p - &aq;

        // Solve for dy: M dy = rhs
        let dy = gauss_solve(mmat, rhs)?;

        // ds = -r_d - A^T dy
        let at_dy = a.t().dot(&dy);
        let ds = -&r_d - &at_dy;

        // dx = S^{-1} ( -r_c + X r_d + X A^T dy )   <-- corrected sign
        let mut dx = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            dx[i] = s_inv[i] * (-r_c[i] + x[i] * r_d[i] + x[i] * at_dy[i]);
        }

        // Step sizes to maintain positivity.
        let alpha_pri = min_ratio_step(&x, &dx, tau);
        let alpha_dual = min_ratio_step(&s, &ds, tau);
        let alpha = alpha_pri.min(alpha_dual);

        // Update
        x = &x + &(dx.mapv(|v| alpha * v));
        y = &y + &(dy.mapv(|v| alpha * v));
        s = &s + &(ds.mapv(|v| alpha * v));

        // Progress log
        println!(
            "iter {it:3}: ||r_p||inf={rp:.3e}, ||r_d||inf={rd:.3e}, ||r_c||inf={rc:.3e}, mu={mu:.3e}, alpha_pri={alpha_pri:.3e}, alpha_dual={alpha_dual:.3e}, alpha={alpha:.3e}"
        );
    }

    Err("Maximum iterations reached without convergence".to_string())
}

fn main() -> Result<(), String> {
    // Example LP:
    //   minimize  x1 + 2 x2
    //   subject   x1 + x2 = 1
    //             x1, x2 >= 0
    //
    // Optimal solution is x = [1, 0], objective = 1.
    let a = Array2::from_shape_vec((1, 2), vec![1.0, 1.0]).unwrap();
    let b = Array1::from_vec(vec![1.0]);
    let c = Array1::from_vec(vec![1.0, 2.0]);

    // Strictly feasible start (Ax=b and x>0). Choose x = [0.5, 0.5].
    let x0 = Array1::from_vec(vec![0.5, 0.5]);

    // Choose y0 and s0 so that A^T y + s = c with s>0.
    // Here A^T y = [y, y]. Pick y=0, then s=c=[1,2] which is strictly positive.
    let y0 = Array1::from_vec(vec![0.0]);
    let s0 = Array1::from_vec(vec![1.0, 2.0]);

    let (x, y, s) = pdip_path_follow(&a, &b, &c, x0, y0, s0, 80)?;

    let obj = dot(&c, &x);
    println!("\nSolution:");
    println!("x = {:?}", x);
    println!("y = {:?}", y);
    println!("s = {:?}", s);
    println!("objective c^T x = {:.12}", obj);

    // Sanity checks
    let rp = a.dot(&x) - &b;
    let rd = a.t().dot(&y) + &s - &c;
    println!("Ax - b = {:?}", rp);
    println!("A^T y + s - c = {:?}", rd);

    // Complementarity products x_i s_i
    let mut xs = Vec::new();
    for i in 0..x.len() {
        xs.push(x[i] * s[i]);
    }
    println!("x_i s_i = {:?}", xs);

    Ok(())
}
```

Program 10.12.2 demonstrates how the barrier formulation in Equation (10.12.4) leads naturally to a primal–dual Newton iteration that follows the central path defined by the perturbed complementarity conditions in Equation (10.12.5). By maintaining strict positivity of both $x$ and $s$ through fraction-to-the-boundary step control, the method avoids the combinatorial pivot operations characteristic of simplex and instead progresses smoothly through the interior of the feasible polytope. The numerical output confirms the expected behavior: feasibility residuals are driven toward zero while the complementarity gap $\mu$ decreases steadily, illustrating the geometric principle that interior-point methods converge by shrinking the barrier parameter while remaining close to the central path.

The program also highlights the computational structure that underlies practical large-scale solvers. The elimination of the full KKT system to a symmetric normal-equation system demonstrates why interior-point methods can exploit efficient linear algebra kernels and why modern implementations rely heavily on sparse factorizations and preconditioned iterative solvers. Although the dense Gaussian elimination routine used here is intended only for small educational examples, the overall framework reflects the same primal–dual path-following strategy employed in industrial solvers. In this way, the implementation provides a foundation for extending the discussion to predictor–corrector variants, adaptive barrier updates, and sparse numerical linear algebra techniques that enable interior-point methods to solve large-scale optimization problems efficiently.

## 10.12.3. Newton System and Search Directions

Applying Newton’s method to the primal–dual optimality conditions yields a large but highly structured linear system at each interior-point iteration. In contrast to simplex, which updates a basis combinatorially, primal–dual interior-point methods compute continuous search directions by linearizing the optimality conditions around the current iterate. The resulting Newton step simultaneously updates the primal variables $\mathbf{x}$, the dual variables $\mathbf{y}$, and the dual slack variables $\mathbf{s}$, reflecting the tightly coupled nature of feasibility and optimality in the primal–dual framework.

For a primal–dual interior-point method, the Newton system typically takes the block form:

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
0 & A^T & I \\
A & 0 & 0 \\
S & 0 & X
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
\Delta \mathbf{y} \\
\Delta \mathbf{x} \\
\Delta \mathbf{s}
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
r_d \\
r_p \\
r_c
\end{pmatrix}
\tag{10.12.5}
$$

\
where $X = \mathrm{diag}(x_i)$ and $S = \mathrm{diag}(s_i)$. The vectors $r_d$, $r_p$, and $r_c$ denote the residuals associated with the dual feasibility conditions, the primal feasibility conditions, and the perturbed complementarity conditions, respectively. These residuals measure how far the current iterate deviates from satisfying the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions exactly.

The structure of this system highlights the defining feature of primal–dual interior-point methods: feasibility and complementarity are corrected simultaneously rather than sequentially. The diagonal blocks $X$ and $S$ encode curvature information induced by the barrier terms and ensure that the iterates remain in the interior of the feasible region. As the barrier parameter decreases, these diagonal matrices become increasingly ill-conditioned, making the accurate solution of the Newton system progressively more challenging.

In practice, the full system in (10.12.5) is rarely solved directly. By eliminating $\Delta \mathbf{s}$ using the third block row and substituting into the remaining equations, the system can be reduced to a smaller system involving only $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ and $\Delta \mathbf{y}$. Further elimination leads to the so-called normal equations, which form a symmetric positive-definite linear system under standard assumptions. This reduction significantly lowers computational cost while preserving the essential coupling between primal and dual variables.

The efficient solution of these reduced linear systems is central to the performance of interior-point solvers. Because the constraint matrix $A$ is typically large and sparse, modern implementations rely heavily on sparse linear algebra techniques, including sparse Cholesky factorizations, preconditioned iterative solvers, and sophisticated ordering strategies to control fill-in. The quality of these linear solves often dominates the overall runtime of the algorithm, especially for large-scale problems.

Thus, while interior-point methods are conceptually smooth and continuous, their practical efficiency depends critically on numerical linear algebra. The design of robust Newton systems and efficient solution strategies is therefore a defining aspect of modern interior-point algorithms and a key reason for their success in large-scale linear programming.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion of the block Newton system in Equation (10.12.5) and the role of Newton directions in primal–dual interior-point methods, Program 10.12.3 provides a practical implementation of search-direction computation for linear programming based on the linearization of the KKT optimality conditions. In contrast to simplex, which progresses by combinatorial basis updates, interior-point methods generate continuous steps $(\Delta \mathbf{x}, \Delta \mathbf{y}, \Delta \mathbf{s})$ by solving a structured linear system that couples primal feasibility, dual feasibility, and complementarity corrections. This program explicitly constructs the Newton system corresponding to the primal–dual conditions and demonstrates how the full KKT system can be solved directly or reduced to a smaller symmetric system through elimination. The implementation thereby illustrates the key numerical linear algebra mechanism that underlies modern interior-point solvers, namely that each iteration is dominated by the solution of a highly structured linear system whose stability and efficiency determine overall performance.

At the core of the implementation is the function `compute_residuals`, which evaluates the primal–dual KKT residuals used as the right-hand side of the Newton system in Equation (10.12.5). It computes the dual feasibility residual $r_d = \mathbf{c} - A^T\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{s}$ and the primal feasibility residual $r_p = \mathbf{b} - A\mathbf{x}$, which measure how far the current iterate deviates from satisfying the primal and dual constraints. It also constructs the complementarity residual $r_c = \sigma \mu \mathbf{e} - X\mathbf{s}$, where $\mu = (\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{s})/n$ represents the current complementarity gap and $\sigma$ is a centering parameter. This term enforces the perturbed complementarity relationship that defines the central path, consistent with the discussion surrounding Equation (10.12.5), and ensures that the Newton step attempts to reduce complementarity simultaneously with feasibility errors.

The function `solve_full_kkt` implements a direct dense solution of the Newton system in Equation (10.12.5) by explicitly assembling the block KKT matrix. The unknowns are ordered as $(\Delta \mathbf{y}, \Delta \mathbf{x}, \Delta \mathbf{s})$, and the coefficient matrix is constructed using the blocks $A^T$, $A$, the identity matrix $I$, and the diagonal matrices $X = \mathrm{diag}(x_i)$ and $S = \mathrm{diag}(s_i)$. This explicit assembly makes the structure of the Newton system transparent: the first block row encodes the linearization of dual feasibility, the second encodes primal feasibility, and the third encodes the complementarity correction. The resulting system is then solved using Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting through the helper function `gauss_solve`. Although this approach is computationally expensive for large problems, it is valuable pedagogically because it provides a direct implementation of the block system shown in Equation (10.12.5) and produces a reference solution against which reduced formulations can be verified.

The function `solve_normal_equations` demonstrates the standard reduction strategy used in practical interior-point solvers. Instead of solving the full KKT system, it eliminates $\Delta \mathbf{s}$ from the dual feasibility block equation and substitutes into the complementarity block equation, yielding an expression for $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ in terms of $\Delta \mathbf{y}$. Substituting this relation into the primal feasibility equation produces the reduced normal-equation system $M \Delta \mathbf{y} = \text{rhs}$, where $M = A\,\mathrm{diag}\!\left(\frac{x_i}{s_i}\right) A^{T}$. Under standard assumptions this matrix is symmetric positive definite, which makes it suitable for efficient factorization or iterative solution methods. This reduction captures the essential numerical advantage of modern interior-point algorithms: the large indefinite KKT system is replaced by a smaller symmetric system that can be solved more reliably and with significantly lower cost, especially when $A$ is sparse.

The linear algebra backbone of the program is provided by the function `gauss_solve`, which implements dense Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting. This routine is used both for solving the full KKT system and for solving the reduced normal equations. While modern solvers typically replace this approach with sparse Cholesky or sparse $LDL^{T}$ factorization, the algorithm implemented here preserves the essential numerical logic while remaining self-contained and easy to inspect. In this sense, it provides a clear computational illustration of why the efficiency of interior-point methods is closely tied to the performance of the underlying linear system solver.

The `main` function provides a small numerical experiment that verifies the equivalence between the full Newton system and its reduced normal-equation form. It constructs a simple two-variable linear program and selects an interior feasible point $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$, then chooses $\mathbf{y}$ and defines $\mathbf{s}$ so that $A^T\mathbf{y}+\mathbf{s}=\mathbf{c}$ holds exactly. This ensures that the primal and dual residuals $r_p$ and $r_d$ are initially near machine precision, while the complementarity residual $r_c$ remains nonzero, meaning the point is feasible but not on the central path. The program then computes Newton search directions using both the full KKT solve and the reduced normal-equation solve, and prints the norm of the difference between the resulting directions. The observed agreement, up to floating-point roundoff, confirms that elimination preserves the Newton direction and validates the reduction strategy described in the section.

Add the following dependencies to cargo.toml:

```rust
[dependencies]
ndarray = "0.15"
```

```rust
// Program 10.12.3: Newton System and Search Directions for Primal–Dual Interior-Point Methods
//
// Standard-form LP:
//   minimize    c^T x
//   subject to  A x = b,  x >= 0
//
// Dual variables: y and s with A^T y + s = c, s >= 0.
//
// Residual convention used here (matches the printed right-hand side in the program):
//   r_d = c - A^T y - s
//   r_p = b - A x
//   r_c = sigma*mu*e - X s    (componentwise: sigma*mu - x_i s_i)
//
// Newton system (consistent with these residuals):
//   A^T Δy + Δs           = r_d
//   A Δx                 = r_p
//   S Δx + X Δs          = r_c
//
// In block-matrix form (Equation 10.12.5 style):
//   [ A^T   0   I ] [Δy]   [ r_d ]
//   [  0    A   0 ] [Δx] = [ r_p ]
//   [  0    S   X ] [Δs]   [ r_c ]
//
// The code computes (Δy, Δx, Δs) in two ways:
//   1) Full KKT solve (dense Gaussian elimination).
//   2) Reduction to normal equations by eliminating Δs and Δx.

use ndarray::{Array1, Array2};

fn dot(a: &Array1<f64>, b: &Array1<f64>) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn inf_norm(v: &Array1<f64>) -> f64 {
    v.iter().fold(0.0, |acc, &x| acc.max(x.abs()))
}

fn gauss_solve(mut a: Array2<f64>, mut b: Array1<f64>) -> Result<Array1<f64>, String> {
    let n = a.nrows();
    if a.ncols() != n || b.len() != n {
        return Err("Dimension mismatch in gauss_solve".to_string());
    }

    for k in 0..n {
        // Partial pivoting
        let mut piv = k;
        let mut best = a[(k, k)].abs();
        for i in (k + 1)..n {
            let val = a[(i, k)].abs();
            if val > best {
                best = val;
                piv = i;
            }
        }
        if best <= 1e-14 {
            return Err("Singular or nearly singular system in gauss_solve".to_string());
        }
        if piv != k {
            for j in k..n {
                let tmp = a[(k, j)];
                a[(k, j)] = a[(piv, j)];
                a[(piv, j)] = tmp;
            }
            let tmpb = b[k];
            b[k] = b[piv];
            b[piv] = tmpb;
        }

        // Eliminate
        for i in (k + 1)..n {
            let m = a[(i, k)] / a[(k, k)];
            a[(i, k)] = 0.0;
            for j in (k + 1)..n {
                a[(i, j)] -= m * a[(k, j)];
            }
            b[i] -= m * b[k];
        }
    }

    // Back substitution
    let mut x = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for i in (0..n).rev() {
        let mut s = b[i];
        for j in (i + 1)..n {
            s -= a[(i, j)] * x[j];
        }
        x[i] = s / a[(i, i)];
    }
    Ok(x)
}

fn compute_residuals(
    a: &Array2<f64>,
    b: &Array1<f64>,
    c: &Array1<f64>,
    x: &Array1<f64>,
    y: &Array1<f64>,
    s: &Array1<f64>,
    sigma: f64,
) -> (Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>, f64) {
    let n = x.len();
    let mu = dot(x, s) / (n as f64);

    // r_d = c - A^T y - s
    let r_d = c - &(a.t().dot(y) + s);

    // r_p = b - A x
    let r_p = b - &a.dot(x);

    // r_c = sigma*mu*e - X s
    let mut r_c = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for i in 0..n {
        r_c[i] = sigma * mu - x[i] * s[i];
    }

    (r_d, r_p, r_c, mu)
}

fn solve_full_kkt(
    a: &Array2<f64>,
    x: &Array1<f64>,
    s: &Array1<f64>,
    r_d: &Array1<f64>,
    r_p: &Array1<f64>,
    r_c: &Array1<f64>,
) -> Result<(Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>), String> {
    // Variable order: [Δy (m); Δx (n); Δs (n)]
    // Equation order: [dual (n); primal (m); comp (n)]
    // Matrix:
    //   [ A^T   0   I ]
    //   [  0    A   0 ]
    //   [  0    S   X ]

    let m = a.nrows();
    let n = a.ncols();
    let dim = m + n + n;

    let mut kkt = Array2::<f64>::zeros((dim, dim));
    let mut rhs = Array1::<f64>::zeros(dim);

    // RHS = [r_d; r_p; r_c]
    for i in 0..n {
        rhs[i] = r_d[i];
    }
    for i in 0..m {
        rhs[n + i] = r_p[i];
    }
    for i in 0..n {
        rhs[n + m + i] = r_c[i];
    }

    // Row block 1 (dual): A^T Δy + Δs = r_d
    // A^T goes in Δy columns (offset 0), size n x m
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..m {
            kkt[(i, j)] = a[(j, i)];
        }
    }
    // I goes in Δs columns (offset m+n)
    for i in 0..n {
        kkt[(i, m + n + i)] = 1.0;
    }

    // Row block 2 (primal): A Δx = r_p
    // A goes in Δx columns (offset m)
    for i in 0..m {
        for j in 0..n {
            kkt[(n + i, m + j)] = a[(i, j)];
        }
    }

    // Row block 3 (comp): S Δx + X Δs = r_c
    // S in Δx columns, X in Δs columns
    for i in 0..n {
        kkt[(n + m + i, m + i)] = s[i];
        kkt[(n + m + i, m + n + i)] = x[i];
    }

    let sol = gauss_solve(kkt, rhs)?;

    let dy = sol.slice(ndarray::s![0..m]).to_owned();
    let dx = sol.slice(ndarray::s![m..(m + n)]).to_owned();
    let ds = sol.slice(ndarray::s![(m + n)..(m + n + n)]).to_owned();

    Ok((dy, dx, ds))
}

fn solve_normal_equations(
    a: &Array2<f64>,
    x: &Array1<f64>,
    s: &Array1<f64>,
    r_d: &Array1<f64>,
    r_p: &Array1<f64>,
    r_c: &Array1<f64>,
) -> Result<(Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>), String> {
    // Eliminate Δs from: A^T Δy + Δs = r_d  =>  Δs = r_d - A^T Δy
    // Substitute into:  S Δx + X Δs = r_c
    //   S Δx + X(r_d - A^T Δy) = r_c
    //   S Δx = r_c - X r_d + X A^T Δy
    //   Δx = S^{-1}(r_c - X r_d + X A^T Δy)
    //
    // Enforce A Δx = r_p:
    //   A S^{-1} X A^T Δy = r_p - A S^{-1}(r_c - X r_d)
    //
    // Let D = X./S, then M = A diag(D) A^T.

    let m = a.nrows();
    let n = a.ncols();

    let mut s_inv = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    let mut d = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for i in 0..n {
        if s[i] <= 0.0 || x[i] <= 0.0 {
            return Err("Lost strict positivity (x>0, s>0) required for reduction".to_string());
        }
        s_inv[i] = 1.0 / s[i];
        d[i] = x[i] * s_inv[i]; // x_i / s_i
    }

    // M = A * diag(D) * A^T
    let mut mmat = Array2::<f64>::zeros((m, m));
    for i in 0..m {
        for j in 0..m {
            let mut sum = 0.0;
            for k in 0..n {
                sum += a[(i, k)] * d[k] * a[(j, k)];
            }
            mmat[(i, j)] = sum;
        }
    }

    // rhs = r_p - A * S^{-1} * (r_c - X r_d)
    let mut t = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for i in 0..n {
        t[i] = r_c[i] - x[i] * r_d[i];
    }
    let mut q = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for i in 0..n {
        q[i] = s_inv[i] * t[i];
    }
    let rhs = r_p - &a.dot(&q);

    // Solve for Δy
    let dy = gauss_solve(mmat, rhs)?;

    // Recover Δs = r_d - A^T Δy
    let at_dy = a.t().dot(&dy);
    let ds = r_d - &at_dy;

    // Recover Δx = S^{-1}(r_c - X r_d + X A^T Δy)
    let mut dx = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for i in 0..n {
        dx[i] = s_inv[i] * (r_c[i] - x[i] * r_d[i] + x[i] * at_dy[i]);
    }

    Ok((dy, dx, ds))
}

fn main() -> Result<(), String> {
    // Tiny example for verifying the Newton system and the normal-equation reduction:
    //
    //   minimize  x1 + 2 x2
    //   subject   x1 + x2 = 1
    //             x >= 0
    let a = Array2::from_shape_vec((1, 2), vec![1.0, 1.0]).unwrap();
    let b = Array1::from_vec(vec![1.0]);
    let c = Array1::from_vec(vec![1.0, 2.0]);

    // Strictly feasible interior point: Ax=b and x>0
    let x = Array1::from_vec(vec![0.5, 0.5]);

    // Pick some y, then define s = c - A^T y so dual feasibility holds exactly.
    let y = Array1::from_vec(vec![0.2]);
    let s = &c - &a.t().dot(&y);
    if s[0] <= 0.0 || s[1] <= 0.0 {
        return Err("Chosen y produced nonpositive s; adjust y in main".to_string());
    }

    let sigma = 0.5;
    let (r_d, r_p, r_c, mu) = compute_residuals(&a, &b, &c, &x, &y, &s, sigma);

    println!("Initial residual norms (inf-norm):");
    println!("||r_d||_inf = {:.3e}", inf_norm(&r_d));
    println!("||r_p||_inf = {:.3e}", inf_norm(&r_p));
    println!("||r_c||_inf = {:.3e}", inf_norm(&r_c));
    println!("mu          = {:.3e}", mu);

    // Solve Newton directions
    let (dy_full, dx_full, ds_full) = solve_full_kkt(&a, &x, &s, &r_d, &r_p, &r_c)?;
    let (dy_red, dx_red, ds_red) = solve_normal_equations(&a, &x, &s, &r_d, &r_p, &r_c)?;

    // Compare directions
    println!("\nNewton direction comparison (inf-norm of differences):");
    println!("||Δy_full - Δy_red||_inf = {:.3e}", inf_norm(&(&dy_full - &dy_red)));
    println!("||Δx_full - Δx_red||_inf = {:.3e}", inf_norm(&(&dx_full - &dx_red)));
    println!("||Δs_full - Δs_red||_inf = {:.3e}", inf_norm(&(&ds_full - &ds_red)));

    // Verify KKT Newton equations numerically:
    // 1) A^T Δy + Δs = r_d
    let eq1_full = a.t().dot(&dy_full) + &ds_full - &r_d;
    let eq1_red = a.t().dot(&dy_red) + &ds_red - &r_d;

    // 2) A Δx = r_p
    let eq2_full = a.dot(&dx_full) - &r_p;
    let eq2_red = a.dot(&dx_red) - &r_p;

    // 3) S Δx + X Δs = r_c
    let n = x.len();
    let mut eq3_full = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    let mut eq3_red = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for i in 0..n {
        eq3_full[i] = s[i] * dx_full[i] + x[i] * ds_full[i] - r_c[i];
        eq3_red[i] = s[i] * dx_red[i] + x[i] * ds_red[i] - r_c[i];
    }

    println!("\nKKT Newton equation residuals (inf-norm):");
    println!(
        "Full solve:  ||eq1||={:.3e}, ||eq2||={:.3e}, ||eq3||={:.3e}",
        inf_norm(&eq1_full),
        inf_norm(&eq2_full),
        inf_norm(&eq3_full)
    );
    println!(
        "Reduced:     ||eq1||={:.3e}, ||eq2||={:.3e}, ||eq3||={:.3e}",
        inf_norm(&eq1_red),
        inf_norm(&eq2_red),
        inf_norm(&eq3_red)
    );

    println!("\nNewton directions (full KKT):");
    println!("Δy = {:?}", dy_full);
    println!("Δx = {:?}", dx_full);
    println!("Δs = {:?}", ds_full);

    Ok(())
}
```

Program 10.12.3 demonstrates how the block Newton system in Equation (10.12.5) translates directly into a computational procedure for generating primal–dual search directions in interior-point methods. By explicitly assembling the full KKT matrix and solving it as a dense linear system, the program provides a transparent reference implementation of the Newton step, revealing how feasibility and complementarity are corrected simultaneously through a coupled update of $\mathbf{x}$, $\mathbf{y}$, and $\mathbf{s}$. This directly reflects the defining interior-point principle that progress toward optimality and feasibility must be balanced at every iteration rather than enforced sequentially.

The program also illustrates the practical motivation for reducing the KKT system to normal equations. The elimination process yields a symmetric positive-definite system involving the matrix $A\,\mathrm{diag}\!\left(\frac{x_i}{s_i}\right) A^{T}$, which is far better suited to efficient numerical linear algebra than the original indefinite block system. The numerical experiment confirms that solving the reduced system produces the same Newton direction as solving the full KKT system, up to roundoff error. This observation supports the central claim of modern interior-point theory: although the algorithm is conceptually expressed through a large block Newton system, its computational efficiency depends on exploiting structure to obtain reduced systems that can be solved rapidly and robustly. This reduction step forms the foundation for advanced implementations that employ sparse Cholesky factorization, fill-reducing orderings, and preconditioned iterative methods to handle large-scale linear programs efficiently.

## 10.12.4. Polynomial Complexity and Practical Performance

Interior-point methods enjoy polynomial-time complexity guarantees, in contrast to the simplex method’s exponential worst-case behavior. From a theoretical standpoint, this result is significant because it establishes linear programming as a problem class that can be solved efficiently in the worst case, independent of pathological instances that defeat boundary-based algorithms. These guarantees provided a major motivation for the development of interior-point methods and reshaped the complexity-theoretic understanding of linear programming (Gondzio, 2025).

In practice, interior-point methods typically converge in a modest number of iterations, often between 20 and 50, and this iteration count is largely independent of problem dimension. Each iteration is computationally intensive, as it requires the solution of large sparse linear systems arising from the primal–dual Newton equations. However, the predictability of the iteration count, combined with strong numerical stability, makes interior-point methods particularly effective for large, sparse linear programs. For many modern applications, especially those arising in network optimization and large-scale planning, interior-point methods are frequently the method of choice in contemporary solver implementations (Nadis, 2025; Lu and Applegate, 2024).

## 10.12.5. Comparison with the Simplex Method

Interior-point and simplex methods represent two fundamentally different philosophies for solving linear programs. The simplex method exploits the geometry of vertices and edges, moving along the boundary of the feasible polyhedron from one basic feasible solution to another. Interior-point methods, by contrast, traverse the interior of the feasible region along smooth trajectories defined by barrier functions and Newton steps.

Simplex has clear advantages in scenarios where warm starts, reoptimization, or detailed sensitivity analysis are required. Because the final basis encodes structural information about binding constraints, reduced costs, and shadow prices, simplex solutions are particularly valuable in decision-support and parametric analysis settings. Interior-point methods, on the other hand, are often superior for very large problems where maintaining or updating a basis becomes computationally expensive or numerically fragile. Reflecting these complementary strengths, modern solvers typically include both approaches and may switch dynamically between them depending on problem size, structure, and the stage of optimization (Gondzio, 2025).

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.12.4 on polynomial-time complexity guarantees and the observed practical efficiency of interior-point methods, Program 10.12.4 provides a computational experiment that illustrates how primal–dual path-following algorithms behave as problem size increases. While the theoretical results establish that linear programming can be solved in polynomial time in the worst case, practical performance is typically characterized by a relatively stable iteration count, often on the order of a few dozen steps, even for problems with large numbers of variables. This program implements a simplified primal–dual interior-point solver and applies it to a sequence of strictly feasible test problems of increasing dimension. By recording iteration counts, residual norms, and the final complementarity gap $\mu$, the experiment demonstrates the key empirical phenomenon emphasized in modern solver design: the overall cost of interior-point methods is dominated less by the number of iterations and more by the linear algebra required at each iteration.

At the core of the implementation is the function `pdip_path_follow`, which executes a primal–dual path-following interior-point method based on the Newton framework introduced earlier. Each iteration forms the primal feasibility residual $r_p = A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}$ and the dual feasibility residual $r_d = A^T\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{s} - \mathbf{c}$, which correspond directly to the feasibility conditions embedded in the Newton system of Equation (10.12.5). The function also computes the complementarity measure $\mu = (\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{s})/n$ and constructs the perturbed complementarity residual $r_c = x_i s_i - \sigma\mu$, enforcing a neighborhood of the central path described earlier. The iteration terminates when all residuals become sufficiently small and the complementarity gap has been driven close to zero, reflecting convergence toward an optimal primal–dual solution.

The dominant computation within `pdip_path_follow` is the construction and solution of the reduced normal-equation system derived from eliminating slack variables in the Newton system. The program forms the diagonal scaling vector $d_i = x_i/s_i$, which encodes the curvature effect of the barrier terms and corresponds to the role played by the diagonal matrices $X$ and $S$ in Equation (10.12.5). Using this scaling, the solver assembles the symmetric matrix $M = A \, \operatorname{diag}(d) \, A^{T}$, which is the normal-equation coefficient matrix that arises from eliminating $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ and $\Delta \mathbf{s}$. Under standard assumptions, this matrix is symmetric positive definite, making it suitable for efficient numerical solution. In the code, the system is solved using dense Gaussian elimination via the function `gauss_solve`, which is appropriate for the small demonstration problems used here, although real solvers replace this step with sparse factorizations or preconditioned iterative methods.

To improve numerical robustness, the program includes a diagonal regularization term applied to the normal-equation matrix before solving. This step reflects a practical issue emphasized in large-scale implementations: as the barrier parameter decreases, the scaling induced by $x_i/s_i$ can lead to ill-conditioning, and robust solvers often incorporate stabilization strategies to ensure reliable factorization. By adding a small multiple of the identity matrix, the program reduces sensitivity to near-singular behavior without altering the qualitative convergence profile. This illustrates the general principle that interior-point efficiency depends not only on Newton’s method itself, but also on careful numerical linear algebra design.

The functions `min_ratio_step` and `inf_norm` support the step control and convergence monitoring required for stable path-following. The function `min_ratio_step` implements a fraction-to-the-boundary rule that computes the largest step size $\alpha$ that preserves strict positivity of either $\mathbf{x}$ or $\mathbf{s}$, then reduces it by a safety factor $\tau$. This ensures that iterates remain strictly inside the feasible region, so that the barrier interpretation remains valid and the algorithm continues to approximate the central path. The function `inf_norm` provides a convenient measure of residual magnitude, allowing the solver to apply termination criteria based on the maximum componentwise violation of feasibility or complementarity.

The experimental framework is completed by the deterministic problem generator `generate_strictly_feasible_lp`, which constructs linear programs in a way that guarantees the existence of a strictly feasible starting point. It builds the constraint matrix $A$ in full-row-rank form and selects a positive initial vector $\mathbf{x}_0$, then defines $\mathbf{b}=A\mathbf{x}_0$ so that primal feasibility holds exactly at initialization. Similarly, a positive slack vector $\mathbf{s}_0$ is chosen and $\mathbf{c}$ is defined so that the dual feasibility condition holds exactly at the initial point. This construction ensures that the interior-point solver begins in the strict interior of the feasible set, thereby avoiding the need for a separate Phase I feasibility procedure and allowing the program to focus directly on the path-following behavior.

The `main` function performs the empirical study described in Section 10.12.4 by running the solver on a sequence of test problems with increasing values of $m$ and $n$. For each case, it reports the number of iterations required to reach convergence, the final complementarity gap $\mu$, and the infinity norms of the feasibility and complementarity residuals. The output confirms the practical performance pattern emphasized in the text: the iteration count remains roughly constant across different dimensions, while the computational burden per iteration grows because the reduced linear system has dimension $m$. In large-scale sparse settings, this cost is dominated by sparse factorization complexity and fill-in, but the demonstration captures the same underlying scaling principle in a simplified dense setting.

Add the following dependencies to cargo.toml:

```rust
[dependencies]
ndarray = "0.15"
```

```rust
// Program 10.12.4: Polynomial Complexity and Practical Performance (Empirical Iteration Profile)
//
// This program illustrates the practical behavior emphasized in Sections 10.12.4–10.12.5:
// interior-point methods often converge in a modest number of iterations, while each iteration
// is dominated by the solution of a linear system arising from the primal–dual Newton equations.
//
// We implement a small primal–dual path-following solver for standard-form LPs:
//   minimize    c^T x
//   subject to  A x = b,  x >= 0
//
// We then run multiple problem sizes and report iteration statistics.
//
// Educational notes:
// - This is a dense demonstration (Gaussian elimination), intended for small sizes.
// - Real solvers use sparse factorizations / iterative methods and robust regularization.

use ndarray::{Array1, Array2};

fn dot(a: &Array1<f64>, b: &Array1<f64>) -> f64 {
    a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()
}

fn inf_norm(v: &Array1<f64>) -> f64 {
    v.iter().fold(0.0, |acc, &x| acc.max(x.abs()))
}

fn min_ratio_step(x: &Array1<f64>, dx: &Array1<f64>, tau: f64) -> f64 {
    let mut alpha: f64 = 1.0;
    for (&xi, &dxi) in x.iter().zip(dx.iter()) {
        if dxi < 0.0 {
            alpha = alpha.min(-xi / dxi);
        }
    }
    (tau * alpha).min(1.0)
}

fn gauss_solve(mut a: Array2<f64>, mut b: Array1<f64>) -> Result<Array1<f64>, String> {
    let n = a.nrows();
    if a.ncols() != n || b.len() != n {
        return Err("Dimension mismatch in gauss_solve".to_string());
    }

    for k in 0..n {
        let mut piv = k;
        let mut best = a[(k, k)].abs();
        for i in (k + 1)..n {
            let val = a[(i, k)].abs();
            if val > best {
                best = val;
                piv = i;
            }
        }
        if best <= 1e-14 {
            return Err("Singular or nearly singular system in gauss_solve".to_string());
        }
        if piv != k {
            for j in k..n {
                let tmp = a[(k, j)];
                a[(k, j)] = a[(piv, j)];
                a[(piv, j)] = tmp;
            }
            let tmpb = b[k];
            b[k] = b[piv];
            b[piv] = tmpb;
        }

        for i in (k + 1)..n {
            let m = a[(i, k)] / a[(k, k)];
            a[(i, k)] = 0.0;
            for j in (k + 1)..n {
                a[(i, j)] -= m * a[(k, j)];
            }
            b[i] -= m * b[k];
        }
    }

    let mut x = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for i in (0..n).rev() {
        let mut s = b[i];
        for j in (i + 1)..n {
            s -= a[(i, j)] * x[j];
        }
        x[i] = s / a[(i, i)];
    }
    Ok(x)
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct IpmStats {
    iters: usize,
    final_mu: f64,
    rp_inf: f64,
    rd_inf: f64,
    rc_inf: f64,
    normal_eq_dim: usize,
}

fn pdip_path_follow(
    a: &Array2<f64>,
    b: &Array1<f64>,
    c: &Array1<f64>,
    mut x: Array1<f64>,
    mut y: Array1<f64>,
    mut s: Array1<f64>,
    max_iter: usize,
    tol: f64,
    sigma: f64,
    delta: f64, // diagonal regularization for normal equations
) -> Result<(Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>, IpmStats), String> {
    let m = a.nrows();
    let n = a.ncols();
    if b.len() != m || c.len() != n || x.len() != n || y.len() != m || s.len() != n {
        return Err("Dimension mismatch in pdip_path_follow".to_string());
    }

    let tau: f64 = 0.995;

    for it in 0..max_iter {
        let r_p = a.dot(&x) - b;
        let r_d = a.t().dot(&y) + &s - c;

        let mu = dot(&x, &s) / (n as f64);
        let mut r_c = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            r_c[i] = x[i] * s[i] - sigma * mu;
        }

        let rp = inf_norm(&r_p);
        let rd = inf_norm(&r_d);
        let rc = inf_norm(&r_c);

        if rp.max(rd).max(rc) < tol && mu < 1e-12 {
            let stats = IpmStats {
                iters: it,
                final_mu: mu,
                rp_inf: rp,
                rd_inf: rd,
                rc_inf: rc,
                normal_eq_dim: m,
            };
            return Ok((x, y, s, stats));
        }

        // D = x./s
        let mut s_inv = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        let mut d = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            if x[i] <= 0.0 || s[i] <= 0.0 {
                return Err("Lost strict feasibility: x>0 and s>0 required".to_string());
            }
            s_inv[i] = 1.0 / s[i];
            d[i] = x[i] * s_inv[i];
        }

        // M = A * diag(D) * A^T
        let mut mmat = Array2::<f64>::zeros((m, m));
        for i in 0..m {
            for j in 0..m {
                let mut sum = 0.0;
                for k in 0..n {
                    sum += a[(i, k)] * d[k] * a[(j, k)];
                }
                mmat[(i, j)] = sum;
            }
        }

        // Regularize: M <- M + delta I
        for i in 0..m {
            mmat[(i, i)] += delta;
        }

        // rhs = -r_p - A * S^{-1} * (-r_c + X r_d)
        let mut w = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            w[i] = -r_c[i] + x[i] * r_d[i];
        }
        let mut q = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            q[i] = s_inv[i] * w[i];
        }
        let rhs = -&r_p - &a.dot(&q);

        let dy = gauss_solve(mmat, rhs)?;

        let at_dy = a.t().dot(&dy);
        let ds = -&r_d - &at_dy;

        // Correct sign: S dx = -r_c + X r_d + X A^T dy
        let mut dx = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
        for i in 0..n {
            dx[i] = s_inv[i] * (-r_c[i] + x[i] * r_d[i] + x[i] * at_dy[i]);
        }

        let alpha_pri = min_ratio_step(&x, &dx, tau);
        let alpha_dual = min_ratio_step(&s, &ds, tau);
        let alpha = alpha_pri.min(alpha_dual);

        x = &x + &(dx.mapv(|v| alpha * v));
        y = &y + &(dy.mapv(|v| alpha * v));
        s = &s + &(ds.mapv(|v| alpha * v));
    }

    Err("Maximum iterations reached without convergence".to_string())
}

// -----------------------------
// Rank-safe LP instance generator
// -----------------------------
//
// Construct A = [I_m | B], where B is a deterministic dense block.
// This guarantees rank(A) = m when n >= m, so the normal equations matrix
// A diag(D) A^T is SPD (up to conditioning) for D > 0.

fn generate_full_row_rank_a(m: usize, n: usize) -> Result<Array2<f64>, String> {
    if n < m {
        return Err("Require n >= m to build A = [I | B] with full row rank".to_string());
    }
    let mut a = Array2::<f64>::zeros((m, n));

    // Identity block
    for i in 0..m {
        a[(i, i)] = 1.0;
    }

    // Dense block B in columns m..n
    for i in 0..m {
        for j in m..n {
            let jj = (j - m + 1) as f64;
            let ii = (i + 1) as f64;
            a[(i, j)] = 0.2 * (ii / (jj + 1.0)) + 0.01 * ((ii + jj).sin());
        }
    }
    Ok(a)
}

fn generate_strictly_feasible_lp(
    m: usize,
    n: usize,
) -> Result<(Array2<f64>, Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>, Array1<f64>), String> {
    let a = generate_full_row_rank_a(m, n)?;

    // Interior x0 > 0
    let mut x0 = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for j in 0..n {
        x0[j] = 0.5 + 0.02 * (j as f64);
    }
    let b = a.dot(&x0);

    // Interior slack s0 > 0, set y0=0, c=s0 ensures A^T y0 + s0 = c.
    let mut s0 = Array1::<f64>::zeros(n);
    for j in 0..n {
        s0[j] = 1.0 + 0.01 * (j as f64);
    }
    let y0 = Array1::<f64>::zeros(m);
    let c = s0.clone();

    Ok((a, b, c, x0, y0, s0))
}

fn main() -> Result<(), String> {
    let cases: Vec<(usize, usize)> = vec![
        (4, 10),
        (6, 15),
        (8, 20),
        (10, 25),
    ];

    let max_iter: usize = 80;
    let tol: f64 = 1e-10;
    let sigma: f64 = 0.5;

    // Small diagonal regularization for normal equations.
    // This is a common practical safeguard when systems become ill-conditioned.
    let delta: f64 = 1e-12;

    println!("Program 10.12.4: Empirical interior-point iteration profile\n");
    println!("Case format: (m constraints, n variables).");
    println!("We report iteration count and final complementarity mu.\n");

    for (m, n) in cases {
        let (a, b, c, x0, y0, s0) = generate_strictly_feasible_lp(m, n)?;

        let (_x, _y, _s, stats) =
            pdip_path_follow(&a, &b, &c, x0, y0, s0, max_iter, tol, sigma, delta)?;

        println!(
            "m={:2}, n={:3}  | iters={:3}  | final mu={:.3e}  | ||r_p||inf={:.1e}  ||r_d||inf={:.1e}  ||r_c||inf={:.1e}  | normal-eq dim={}",
            m,
            n,
            stats.iters,
            stats.final_mu,
            stats.rp_inf,
            stats.rd_inf,
            stats.rc_inf,
            stats.normal_eq_dim
        );
    }

    Ok(())
}
```

Program 10.12.4 demonstrates the practical interpretation of polynomial-time complexity results for interior-point methods by showing that convergence is typically achieved in a modest and predictable number of iterations. The empirical results illustrate that the iteration count remains largely stable as the number of variables increases, consistent with the observation that many primal–dual interior-point solvers converge in roughly 20 to 50 iterations in real applications. This reinforces the key point of Section 10.12.4: although worst-case polynomial bounds provide theoretical reassurance, practical efficiency arises from the combination of stable iteration behavior and robust Newton-based progress toward the central path.

The program also highlights why interior-point methods often outperform simplex on large sparse problems. The computational cost is dominated by the solution of the reduced normal-equation system, whose dimension scales with the number of constraints and whose conditioning becomes increasingly challenging as the barrier parameter decreases. This explains why modern solver performance depends critically on sparse numerical linear algebra, including fill-reducing orderings, stable factorization methods, and regularization strategies. By combining a path-following iteration with an explicit linear solve at each step, the implementation captures the fundamental computational structure that makes interior-point methods both theoretically appealing and practically powerful.

Finally, the experiment provides a natural basis for comparison with simplex as discussed in Section 10.12.5. Whereas simplex progresses by discrete basis changes and may require many pivots in the worst case, the interior-point method proceeds through smooth updates governed by Newton search directions. The stable iteration counts observed here help explain why interior-point algorithms are frequently preferred for very large-scale planning, network optimization, and sparse industrial models, while simplex remains advantageous in settings requiring warm starts and sensitivity analysis. In this way, the program complements the theoretical discussion by illustrating the computational trade-off between predictable iteration counts and expensive per-iteration linear algebra, which is central to the modern understanding of linear programming solvers.

## 10.12.6. Applications and Modern Solver Architectures

Interior-point methods are widely used in large-scale optimization problems, including telecommunications network design, energy systems planning, traffic assignment, and financial optimization. Beyond linear programming, they play a foundational role in convex optimization more broadly, forming the algorithmic basis for solvers that address quadratic programming, semidefinite programming, and other structured convex problems.

Professional solvers integrate interior-point methods within sophisticated software architectures that include extensive preprocessing, scaling, and numerical safeguards. Presolve routines simplify problem structure and improve conditioning, while adaptive strategies monitor numerical progress and adjust algorithmic parameters dynamically. In many modern implementations, hybrid strategies are employed, in which an interior-point phase rapidly produces a high-quality solution that is subsequently refined using simplex to recover an optimal basis and interpretable dual information. Surveys of contemporary optimization practice emphasize that such hybrid architectures represent the dominant paradigm in large-scale linear programming today (Gondzio, 2025; Lu and Applegate, 2024).

## 10.12.7. Interior-Point Methods in Modern Linear Optimization

Interior-point methods provide a powerful and conceptually distinct alternative to the simplex method for solving linear programs. By approaching the optimal solution through the interior of the feasible region, rather than traversing its boundary, these methods replace combinatorial pivot operations with smooth, continuous progress guided by barrier functions and Newton-type updates. This interior viewpoint allows feasibility and optimality to be enforced simultaneously within a unified primal–dual framework.

From a theoretical perspective, interior-point methods established polynomial-time complexity guarantees for linear programming, resolving long-standing questions about worst-case behavior. In practice, modern primal–dual algorithms exhibit remarkably stable and predictable convergence, typically requiring only a modest number of iterations even for very large problems. Although each iteration involves the solution of large linear systems, advances in sparse numerical linear algebra and solver engineering have made these costs manageable in large-scale applications.

Rather than supplanting simplex, interior-point methods complement it. Simplex remains invaluable when warm starts, reoptimization, and detailed sensitivity analysis are required, while interior-point methods excel in large, sparse, and highly structured problems where basis management becomes expensive or unreliable. Contemporary solvers routinely integrate both approaches, often using interior-point phases to rapidly reach high-quality solutions and simplex refinement to recover interpretable optimal bases and dual information.

Together, simplex and interior-point methods form a cohesive algorithmic toolkit that underpins modern linear optimization theory and software. Their complementary strengths, combined with continued advances in computational methods and solver architectures, ensure that linear programming remains a practical and foundational tool across scientific, industrial, and policy-driven applications (Nadis, 2025).

+++ {"oxa":"oxa:pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP/q7sacdxaCQJQAPpNyQJn.6","tags":[]}

# 10.13. Simulated Annealing Methods

Simulated annealing (SA) is a probabilistic global optimization technique inspired by thermodynamics. It is particularly effective for large-scale optimization problems in which the objective function possesses many local minima, making purely greedy descent methods prone to premature convergence. Rather than deterministically moving “downhill” at every step, simulated annealing occasionally accepts uphill moves, that is, transitions to worse solutions, in order to escape local minima. This stochastic acceptance mechanism enables the algorithm to explore the solution space more broadly and makes SA especially suitable for combinatorial optimization problems where the search space is discrete and extremely large, and where concepts such as gradients or descent directions may not be well defined (Milisav et al., 2025).

Simulated annealing has been successfully applied to classical NP-hard problems such as the traveling salesman problem, as well as to complex engineering and design tasks including circuit layout and large-scale scheduling. Despite the breadth and scale of these applications, the core SA algorithm remains relatively simple, contributing to its appeal as a general-purpose optimization method.

### Thermodynamic Analogy

The central idea underlying simulated annealing is an analogy with the physical process of annealing in metals or the freezing of liquids. In a physical system, high temperature allows atoms to move freely among many configurations. If the system is cooled slowly, the atoms can gradually settle into a highly ordered crystalline structure corresponding to the global minimum of energy. In contrast, rapid cooling, or quenching, often traps the system in disordered, higher-energy configurations corresponding to local minima.

Simulated annealing mimics slow cooling by introducing randomness into the optimization process and gradually reducing a control parameter referred to as the temperature. At high temperature, the algorithm explores the search space freely, including moves that increase the objective function. As the temperature decreases, the algorithm becomes increasingly biased toward downhill moves, refining the solution and converging toward a low-cost configuration.

Mathematically, the probability that a physical system occupies a state of energy $E$ at temperature $T$ is governed by the Boltzmann distribution:

$$
P(E) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right)
\tag{10.13.1}
$$

where $k$ is the Boltzmann constant. This distribution implies that even at low temperatures there remains a nonzero probability of occupying higher-energy states. In optimization terms, this corresponds to a small but crucial chance of accepting worse solutions, enabling escape from local minima.

Building on this principle, simulated annealing adopts a Metropolis-style acceptance rule. If the current state has objective value $E_{\text{old}}$ and a proposed state has value $E_{\text{new}}$, the new state is accepted with probability:

$$
P_{\text{accept}} =
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } E_{\text{new}} \le E_{\text{old}}, \\[1ex]
\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{\text{new}} - E_{\text{old}}}{kT}\right),
& \text{if } E_{\text{new}} > E_{\text{old}} 
\end{cases}
\tag{10.13.2}
$$

Thus, all downhill moves are accepted, while uphill moves are accepted with a probability that decreases exponentially with the increase in objective value and inversely with temperature. At high temperature, this probability is close to one even for significant increases in cost, allowing broad exploration. At low temperature, uphill moves are rarely accepted, and the algorithm behaves increasingly like a greedy local optimizer (Milisav et al., 2025).

### Core Components of Simulated Annealing

To apply simulated annealing to a specific optimization problem, four essential components must be specified.

*Configuration (State) Space: *The configuration space defines the set of all possible candidate solutions. In combinatorial problems such as the traveling salesman problem, a configuration may be a permutation of elements. In other applications, it may represent assignments, graphs, or continuous parameter vectors. The nature of this space strongly influences algorithmic performance.

*Random Move Generator: *A procedure is required to generate random perturbations of the current state. These moves define the neighborhood structure of the search and must ensure ergodicity, meaning that any configuration can eventually be reached from any other. Effective move design balances local refinement with the ability to explore distant regions of the space.

*Objective Function $E(x)$***:** The objective function assigns a scalar cost or energy to each configuration. Acceptance decisions depend only on differences in objective values, making simulated annealing applicable even when the objective is noisy, discontinuous, or non-differentiable. Constraints are often incorporated through penalty terms added to (E(x)).

*Temperature Schedule***:** The temperature controls the likelihood of accepting uphill moves and is gradually reduced according to an annealing schedule. The schedule must specify the initial temperature, the rate of cooling, the number of moves attempted at each temperature, and the stopping criterion. If cooling is too rapid, the algorithm may freeze into a suboptimal configuration. If cooling is too slow, computational cost may become prohibitive. Modern implementations increasingly employ adaptive strategies that adjust temperature based on observed acceptance rates or search progress (Qiu and Liang, 2025).

## 10.13.1. Combinatorial Minimization: The Traveling Salesman Problem

The traveling salesman problem (TSP) is one of the most extensively studied problems in combinatorial optimization and serves as a canonical example for illustrating the strengths of simulated annealing. The problem consists of determining the shortest possible closed tour that visits each of $N$ cities exactly once. The number of distinct tours grows factorially with $N$, and the resulting objective landscape contains an enormous number of local minima. Consequently, deterministic greedy or local improvement algorithms frequently converge to suboptimal tours and are unable to recover from poor early decisions.

Simulated annealing is well suited to this setting because it allows controlled uphill moves, enabling the search to escape local minima and explore alternative regions of the solution space.

### State Representation and Objective Function

In the simulated annealing formulation of the TSP, a candidate solution is represented as a permutation of the city indices. Each permutation defines a unique tour that visits all cities exactly once before returning to the starting city.

The objective function $E$ corresponds to the total length of the tour,

$$
E =
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}
\sqrt{(x_{i_k} - x_{i_{k+1}})^2 + (y_{i_k} - y_{i_{k+1}})^2},
\qquad i_N = i_0
\tag{10.13.3}
$$

where $(x_{i_k}, y_{i_k})$ denotes the coordinates of the $k$-th city in the tour. This formulation provides a natural scalar energy measure that directly reflects solution quality and fits seamlessly into the annealing framework.

### Neighborhood Structure and Move Operators

The effectiveness of simulated annealing for the TSP depends critically on the choice of **move** operators, which define the neighborhood structure of the search. Commonly used moves include segment reversals, city swaps, and relocation of subsequences within the tour. These operations modify the permutation while preserving feasibility, that is, the requirement that each city is visited exactly once.

Local moves enable fine-grained improvements to an existing tour, while larger rearrangements allow the algorithm to escape poor configurations and explore distant regions of the solution space. To ensure effective exploration, the move set must be sufficiently rich to guarantee ergodicity, meaning that any tour can be reached from any other tour through a finite sequence of moves.

### Temperature Effects and Search Dynamics

At high temperature, simulated annealing readily accepts moves that increase the tour length. This behavior allows the algorithm to escape unfavorable initial tours and traverse widely separated regions of the solution space. During this phase, the search behaves almost randomly, promoting global exploration rather than immediate improvement.

As the temperature decreases, the acceptance of uphill moves becomes less frequent, and the algorithm increasingly favors moves that shorten the tour. This gradual reduction in randomness induces a smooth transition from exploration to exploitation. Near the end of the annealing process, the algorithm behaves similarly to a local optimizer, refining a high-quality tour rather than restructuring it completely.

This temperature-controlled transition is crucial for navigating the rugged combinatorial landscape of the TSP and avoiding premature convergence to inferior local minima.

### Scalability, Parallelism, and Modern Variants

Recent studies have demonstrated that simulated annealing remains competitive for large-scale and constrained variants of the traveling salesman problem, particularly when combined with parallel execution or problem-specific heuristics. Parallel simulated annealing allows multiple tours to be explored simultaneously, increasing the probability of discovering high-quality solutions and improving robustness with respect to initialization (Karacan et al., 2023).

Applications to related network optimization and randomization problems show that annealing-based approaches can generate solutions of consistently high quality that are difficult to obtain using purely greedy or deterministic methods. In these contexts, simulated annealing’s flexibility and tolerance for complex objective landscapes continue to make it a valuable tool (Milisav et al., 2025).

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 10.13 on the thermodynamic interpretation of simulated annealing and the Metropolis acceptance mechanism defined in Equation (10.13.2), Program 10.13.1 provides a practical implementation of simulated annealing for a canonical NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem: the traveling salesman problem. In this setting, the optimization landscape is highly multimodal, and purely greedy local improvement strategies frequently become trapped in suboptimal tours. The program demonstrates how the probabilistic acceptance of uphill moves, controlled by a gradually decreasing temperature parameter, allows the algorithm to explore the configuration space broadly at high temperature and transition smoothly toward local refinement as the system cools. By combining a permutation-based tour representation, a feasible move generator, and a geometric cooling schedule, the implementation illustrates the core principles that make simulated annealing a robust global optimization method for rugged discrete search spaces.

At the foundation of the implementation is the `City` struct, which stores the Cartesian coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ for each city in the plane. The objective function of the traveling salesman problem is implemented in the function `tour_length`, which evaluates the total tour distance according to Equation (10.13.3). Given a permutation representing the visiting order, the function computes the Euclidean distance between consecutive cities and includes the final return edge from the last city back to the first.
